topics.title: Classification of Coach Work Types: Stylish Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Coach Work Types: Stylish Forms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coach Work** refers to the complete body shell and superstructure of a passenger vehicle (or commercial coach/bus), encompassing the structural framework, external panels, internal trim, glazing, and all stylistic contours that define its visual and aerodynamic identity. In KTU 2024 terminology, it represents the **integrated assembly of body-in-white (BIW), closures, and aesthetic styling cues** that transform a mechanical chassis into a finished automobile.

**Stylish Forms** in coach work denote the **classified aesthetic and geometric categories** of vehicle body shapes evolved through carriage-making traditions (horse-drawn era), early automobile craft, and modern automotive design language. These forms are characterized by specific proportions, silhouettes, rooflines, and section profiles that give each vehicle its distinctive market identity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 Focus:** The KTU 2024 syllabus (PEAUT741) lays emphasis on the *evolution of body shapes* from the early **box-on-chassis** carriage style to today's **monocoque aerodynamic** forms, classifying them under **functional**, **stylistic**, and **constructional** typologies.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of coach work like **tailoring for vehicles**:
- Just as a tailor classifies garments into formal suits, casual wear, sportswear, and ceremonial attire based on *cut, drape, and silhouette*—coach builders classify vehicle bodies into **sedans, coupes, convertibles, and station wagons** based on *roofline, pillar configuration, and proportion*.
- **Stylish Forms** = the "fashion trend" of automobiles, where each decade brings new aesthetic preferences (e.g., the tall, narrow 1910s Ford Model T vs. the long, low, aerodynamic 1960s Cadillacs vs. today's compact SUVs).

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic — "SHARPP C":** The seven primary stylish forms are:
> **S**aloon, **H**atchback, **A**erodynamic, **R**oadster, **P**haeton, **P**ickup, **C**oupe.

---

## 1.3 Why "Stylish Forms" Matter in Vehicle Body Engineering

1. **Market Segmentation:** Each stylish form targets a specific buyer demographic (luxury = long wheelbase sedans; utility = pickup/van; sport = low coupe).
2. **Aerodynamic Efficiency:** Modern stylish forms are dictated by **drag coefficient (Cd)** targets—typically **0.25 to 0.35** for passenger cars.
3. **Packaging Efficiency:** The form determines interior volume, glass area, ingress/egress angles, and crash safety zones.
4. **Brand Identity:** Manufacturers use form signatures (e.g., BMW kidney grille proportions, Porsche 911 silhouette) as recognizable trademarks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Recap (Bold):**
> - **Drag Coefficient (Cd):** typically **0.30 to 0.40** for conventional saloons
> - **Frontal Area (A):** typically **1.8 to 2.5 m²**
> - **Roof Height to Belt-Line Ratio:** design parameter for stylish proportions
> - **Wheelbase-to-Overall-Length Ratio:** typically **0.55 to 0.65** for passenger cars

---

## 1.4 Scope Boundary for KTU Module 1

| Aspect | Covered in Module 1? |
|---|---|
| Historical evolution of coach work | ✅ Yes |
| Classification by stylish form | ✅ Yes (this topic) |
| Constructional types (unitized, semi-monocoque) | ✅ Yes |
| Material selection | ✅ Yes |
| Detailed aerodynamic CFD analysis | ❌ No (Module 3) |
| Crashworthiness simulation | ❌ No (Module 4) |
| Paint and finishing processes | ❌ No (Module 5) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Stylish Form Classification

The classification of stylish forms rests on **three orthogonal axes** in vehicle body engineering pedagogy:

### Axis A: Roofline Geometry
1. **Notchback** — Distinct engine compartment, passenger cabin, and boot (3-box silhouette).
2. **Fastback** — Continuous slope from roof to rear (2-box silhouette, sports-oriented).
3. **Hatchback** — Vertical or near-vertical rear with liftgate (2-box, compact/utility).
4. **Squareback / Estate** — Extended roof to rear, vertical tailgate (wagon profile).

### Axis B: Number of Side Doors & Volume
1. **2-door Coupe** — Sporty, 2+2 seating, fixed roof.
2. **4-door Saloon (Sedan)** — Formal passenger car.
3. **2-door Roadster / Spyder** — Open-top, 2-seater.
4. **4-door Convertible** — Retractable soft/hard top.
5. **Multi-purpose Vehicle (MPV)** — 5+ doors, tall roof, mono-volume.

### Axis C: Proportional Aesthetics (Styling Parameters)
1. **Cab-Forward Proportion** — A-pillar pulled forward, reduced front overhang.
2. **Rear-Wheel-Drive Long Hood** — Classic GT proportion (hood = 2× windshield-to-axle distance).
3. **Three-Box Proportion** — Equal length for engine, cabin, boot modules.
4. **Two-Box Proportion** — Cabin + cargo merged, no separate boot lid.
5. **One-Box Proportion** — Single volume (vans, MPVs).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drag coefficient | $C_d$ | **0.25 to 0.40** | dimensionless | Aerodynamic efficiency index |
| Frontal area | $A$ | **1.8 to 2.5** | $\text{m}^2$ | Drag force calculation |
| Aerodynamic drag force | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | varies | N | Power loss estimation |
| Wheelbase-to-length ratio | $r_{wb} = \dfrac{L_{wb}}{L_{OA}}$ | **0.55 to 0.65** | dimensionless | Style classification |
| Overhang ratio (front) | $r_{oh,f} = \dfrac{L_{oh,f}}{L_{OA}}$ | **0.25 to 0.35** | dimensionless | Cab-forward metric |
| Golden ratio styling index | $\phi = 1.618$ | **1.6 to 1.62** | dimensionless | Aesthetic proportion |
| Belt-line height ratio | $r_{bl} = \dfrac{h_{bl}}{h_{OA}}$ | **0.50 to 0.60** | dimensionless | Greenhouse proportion |
| Cabin efficiency | $\eta_{cab} = \dfrac{V_{cabin}}{V_{OA}}$ | **0.45 to 0.60** | dimensionless | Packaging efficiency |
| Hood length to windshield distance | $r_{hood} = \dfrac{L_{hood}}{L_{ws-axle}}$ | **1.5 to 2.5** | dimensionless | GT proportion index |
| Glass area ratio | $r_{glass} = \dfrac{A_{glass}}{A_{OA,side}}$ | **0.35 to 0.50** | dimensionless | Visibility styling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Density Constant:** $\rho_{air} = \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$ at sea level, **15°C**, standard atmosphere. Use this in all drag force calculations for KTU problems.

---

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter (Real-World Engineering Utility)

1. **Drag Force Equation ($F_d$):** Determines fuel consumption penalty at highway speeds. A reduction in $C_d$ from **0.40 to 0.25** at 120 km/h saves approximately **15–20%** fuel.
2. **Wheelbase Ratio ($r_{wb}$):** Critical for **handling balance** and **crash deformation zone** allocation.
3. **Cabin Efficiency ($\eta_{cab}$):** Used by packaging engineers to maximize occupant volume per unit footprint—a key KTU 2024 evaluation metric.
4. **Golden Ratio ($\phi$):** Styling designers use this to position the visual "focal point" of the car (often at the B-pillar or front axle).

---

## 2.4 The "Three-Box" Classical Styling Doctrine

The classical coach-builder's doctrine (originating from **horse-drawn carriage work**) classified vehicles into **three boxes**:

$$
\text{Vehicle} = \underbrace{\text{Box 1}}_{\text{Engine Compartment}} + \underbrace{\text{Box 2}}_{\text{Passenger Cabin}} + \underbrace{\text{Box 3}}_{\text{Luggage Boot}}
$$

Modern aerodynamic styling has reduced this to:

- **Two-box form:** Box 1 (engine) + Box 2+3 (unified cabin-cargo).
- **One-box form:** Single volume (MPVs, vans).
- **Zero-box (cab-forward):** Engine bay fully integrated under windshield base.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "classify" stylish forms, always reference the **silhouette topology** (number of visible boxes in side view) and **roofline type** (notchback, fastback, hatchback) simultaneously.

---

## 2.5 Detailed Classification Matrix — Stylish Forms

| Stylish Form | Roofline | Box Count | Doors | Typical Use | Aerodynamic Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Saloon / Sedan** | Notchback | 3 | 4 | Family executive | $C_d \approx 0.28$–$0.34$ |
| **Coupe** | Fastback or notchback | 3 (short) | 2 | Sports/luxury | $C_d \approx 0.25$–$0.32$ |
| **Convertible** | Open-top | 3 | 2 or 4 | Leisure | $C_d \approx 0.32$–$0.40$ |
| **Hatchback** | Hatchback | 2 | 3 or 5 | Compact urban | $C_d \approx 0.30$–$0.36$ |
| **Estate / Wagon** | Squareback | 2 (long) | 5 | Family utility | $C_d \approx 0.32$–$0.38$ |
| **Roadster** | Open-top, low | 2 | 2 | Pure sport | $C_d \approx 0.30$–$0.36$ |
| **SUV / Crossover** | Tall, upright | 2 | 5 | Off-road/family | $C_d \approx 0.35$–$0.42$ |
| **MPV / Minivan** | One-box | 1 | 5 | Multi-purpose | $C_d \approx 0.32$–$0.40$ |
| **Pickup** | Cabin + open bed | 2 + open | 2 or 4 | Utility | $C_d \approx 0.40$–$0.50$ |
| **Limousine** | Notchback extended | 3 (long) | 4 or 6 | Luxury/chauffeur | $C_d \approx 0.32$–$0.38$ |
| **Phaeton** | Open-top, formal | 3 | 4 | Vintage/classic | $C_d \approx 0.38$–$0.45$ |
| **Landaulet** | Partial open-top (rear) | 3 | 4 | Ceremonial | $C_d \approx 0.40$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Aerodynamic Drag Force from Stylish Form Parameters

Since stylish forms directly influence aerodynamic performance, KTU frequently tests the relationship between form, drag, and fuel economy. Below is the **complete derivation** of aerodynamic drag force as it relates to vehicle form classification.

### Step 1: Fundamental Definition of Aerodynamic Drag

Aerodynamic drag force is the resistance experienced by a body moving through a fluid (air). It is fundamentally derived from the **momentum transfer principle** and the **Bernoulli–Euler flow theory**.

The general expression for drag force is:

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot A \cdot C_d
$$

where:
- $F_d$ = aerodynamic drag force, in Newtons (N)
- $\rho$ = air density, in kg/m³
- $v$ = vehicle velocity, in m/s
- $A$ = frontal projected area, in m²
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient, dimensionless

---

### Step 2: Express the Formula in Terms of Stylish Form Parameters

The **drag coefficient $C_d$** is a direct function of the vehicle's stylish form. Empirically, $C_d$ is decomposed as:

$$
C_d = C_{D,0} + \Delta C_{D,form} + \Delta C_{D,detail}
$$

where:
- $C_{D,0} = 0.15$ — base pressure drag of a "perfectly streamlined" body
- $\Delta C_{D,form}$ — form-dependent drag increment (depends on roofline type)
- $\Delta C_{D,detail}$ — detail drag from mirrors, wheels, gaps, ornaments (typically **0.05 to 0.10**)

---

### Step 3: Compute Form-Dependent Drag Increment

Empirically validated values for $\Delta C_{D,form}$ from the literature (commonly referenced in KTU 2024 module content):

$$
\Delta C_{D,form} = k_1 \cdot (1 - r_{wb}) + k_2 \cdot (r_{oh,f}) + k_3 \cdot \tan(\alpha_{roof})
$$

where:
- $k_1 = 0.20$ (wheelbase ratio sensitivity coefficient)
- $k_2 = 0.15$ (overhang sensitivity coefficient)
- $k_3 = 0.10$ (roof rake angle sensitivity coefficient, in degrees)
- $r_{wb}$ = wheelbase-to-overall-length ratio
- $r_{oh,f}$ = front overhang ratio
- $\alpha_{roof}$ = rear roofline rake angle (in degrees)

---

### Step 4: Sample Numerical Evaluation (KTU-Style Problem)

**Given:** A mid-size saloon with:
- Wheelbase $L_{wb} = 2.7$ m
- Overall length $L_{OA} = 4.7$ m
- Front overhang $L_{oh,f} = 0.95$ m
- Rear roofline rake $\alpha_{roof} = 25°$
- Frontal area $A = 2.2$ m²
- Velocity $v = 120$ km/h = $\dfrac{120}{3.6} = 33.33$ m/s
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**Find:** Total drag force $F_d$.

---

#### Step 4a: Compute Wheelbase Ratio

$$
r_{wb} = \frac{L_{wb}}{L_{OA}} = \frac{2.7}{4.7} = 0.5745
$$

This falls within the typical stylish form range of **0.55 to 0.65** (saloon classification). ✅

---

#### Step 4b: Compute Front Overhang Ratio

$$
r_{oh,f} = \frac{L_{oh,f}}{L_{OA}} = \frac{0.95}{4.7} = 0.2021
$$

This is on the **low end** (cab-forward styling influence) → reduced form drag contribution. ✅

---

#### Step 4c: Compute Form-Dependent Drag Increment

$$
\Delta C_{D,form} = 0.20 \cdot (1 - 0.5745) + 0.15 \cdot (0.2021) + 0.10 \cdot \tan(25°)
$$

Evaluate term by term:

$$
\Delta C_{D,form} = 0.20 \cdot (0.4255) + 0.15 \cdot (0.2021) + 0.10 \cdot (0.4663)
$$

$$
\Delta C_{D,form} = 0.0851 + 0.0303 + 0.0466
$$

$$
\Delta C_{D,form} = 0.1620
$$

---

#### Step 4d: Add Detail Drag Increment

Assume standard detail drag for a modern saloon with flush glazing, wheel covers, and concealed wipers:

$$
\Delta C_{D,detail} = 0.08
$$

---

#### Step 4e: Compute Total Drag Coefficient

$$
C_d = C_{D,0} + \Delta C_{D,form} + \Delta C_{D,detail}
$$

$$
C_d = 0.15 + 0.1620 + 0.08 = 0.392
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $C_d$ value is in the **upper saloon range (0.39)**—indicating a more traditional, less aerodynamic styling. A teardrop coupe might achieve $C_d = 0.27$, while a pickup would be $C_d = 0.45$.

---

#### Step 4f: Compute Aerodynamic Drag Force

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot A \cdot C_d
$$

Substitute values:

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot (33.33)^2 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 0.392
$$

Compute the squared velocity term:

$$
v^2 = (33.33)^2 = 1110.89 \;\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

Compute the multiplicative chain step by step:

$$
F_d = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 1110.89 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 0.392
$$

$$
F_d = 0.6125 \cdot 1110.89 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 0.392
$$

$$
F_d = 680.42 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 0.392
$$

$$
F_d = 1496.92 \cdot 0.392
$$

$$
F_d = 586.79 \;\text{N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $F_d \approx \mathbf{587 \; N}$ at 120 km/h for this saloon with $C_d = 0.392$.
> **Power required to overcome drag:** $P_d = F_d \cdot v = 587 \cdot 33.33 = 19{,}565 \; \text{W} \approx 19.6 \; \text{kW}$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Stylish Form vs. Performance Trade-Offs

| Stylish Form | Cabin Efficiency $\eta_{cab}$ | Aerodynamic Class | Crash Safety Margin | Manufacturing Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Saloon | **0.50** | Medium | **High** (3-box crumple zones) | 1.0× (baseline) |
| Coupe | **0.40** | Low (best) | Medium (short rear overhang) | 1.3× |
| Hatchback | **0.55** | Medium | Medium (rear crumple short) | 0.9× |
| Estate | **0.60** | Medium-high | High (long wheelbase) | 1.1× |
| Roadster | **0.30** | Low | **Low** (no roof structure) | 1.5× |
| SUV | **0.55** | High (worst) | High (high mass) | 1.4× |
| MPV | **0.65** | High | High (tall greenhouse) | 1.2× |
| Pickup | **0.40** | Very high | Variable | 1.1× |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** This matrix is the **most-likely-asked** 14-mark question in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the **cabin efficiency** and **Cd range** for each form.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
