The **block-level functional architecture** shown above captures the engineering essence of each layout — the flow of mechanical power, the location of masses, and the relationship between structural and powertrain components — which is what the KTU examiner evaluates. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper] **Q: Define the term "layout of a car". List any four major car body styles.** **Model Answer:** The layout of a car refers to the standardised arrangement of the engine, transmission system, driven wheels, fuel tank, passenger compartment, and luggage space over the chassis or underbody of an automobile. It includes the orientation of the powertrain (longitudinal or transverse) and the type of drive (front-wheel, rear-wheel, or all-wheel). **Four major car body styles:** 1. **Sedan / Saloon** — three-box design (engine bay, cabin, boot) with a fixed B-pillar. 2. **Hatchback** — two-box design (engine bay + cabin with integrated tailgate). 3. **Coupe** — two-door fixed-roof sporty car with a sloping roofline. 4. **SUV (Sports Utility Vehicle)** — high ground clearance, often AWD, body-on-frame or monocoque. *(Acceptable alternatives: Convertible, Station Wagon, MPV, Pickup, Crossover.)* #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supple Paper] **Q: Differentiate between a "monocoque" and a "body-on-frame" construction.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Monocoque (Unibody) | Body-on-Frame (BoF) | |---------|---------------------|----------------------| | Structure | Body and chassis are integrated into a single stressed-skin structure. | Separate ladder or X-type chassis with body bolted on top. | | Weight | Lighter (15–30 % less). | Heavier. | | Rigidity | Distributed across entire body shell. | Concentrated in the chassis. | | Repair | More complex and costly (specialist work). | Easier (chassis can be straightened). | | Application | Sedans, hatchbacks, most modern cars. | Heavy-duty trucks, off-road SUVs, luxury cars. | | Crash Energy | Body panels act as crumple zones. | Chassis absorbs most crash energy. | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) #### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Main Paper] **Q: With neat sketches, explain the following car layouts in detail:** **(a)** Front Engine — Rear Wheel Drive (FR) layout — 7 marks. **(b)** Mid Engine — Rear Wheel Drive (MR) layout — 7 marks. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — FR Layout (7 marks):** In the **Front Engine — Rear Wheel Drive (FR)** layout, the internal combustion engine is mounted at the front of the vehicle, ahead of the passenger compartment, and the rear wheels are driven through a propeller shaft and differential. **Key components (2 marks):** - Engine (longitudinal orientation) - Clutch and gearbox at the front - Propeller shaft running along the centreline (inside the transmission tunnel) - Rear differential - Rear half-shafts - Rear wheels (driven); front wheels (steering only) **Advantages (2 marks):** 1. **Balanced weight distribution** — closer to the ideal 50:50 when engine is positioned slightly behind the front axle (front-mid design). This provides neutral handling. 2. **Steering purity** — front wheels are not driven, so steering feedback is undistorted by torque steer. 3. **Crash safety** — long front overhang provides an effective crumple zone. 4. **Scalability** — easily accommodates large-displacement inline-6, V8, and V12 engines. **Disadvantages (1 mark):** 1. The transmission tunnel intrudes into the rear footwell, reducing interior space. 2. Heavier than FF layouts. 3. Less traction in slippery conditions compared to FF. **Applications (1 mark):** BMW 3-Series, Mercedes-Benz C-Class, Toyota Land Cruiser (older models), most rear-wheel-drive sports cars. **Why-and-How Logic (1 mark):** The FR layout was historically dominant because the longitudinal engine and rear-wheel-drive combination was the simplest to engineer before the advent of compact transverse FWD transaxles. It remains preferred for rear-wheel-drive performance cars because it allows the front wheels to be dedicated to steering, providing precise turn-in and linear handling response. --- **Part (b) — MR Layout (7 marks):** In the **Mid Engine — Rear Wheel Drive (MR)** layout, the engine and transaxle are positioned **behind the passenger compartment but ahead of the rear axle**, driving the rear wheels. **Key components (1 mark):** - Mid-mounted engine (between cabin and rear axle) - Transaxle (integrated gearbox + differential) - Rear half-shafts - Front and rear luggage compartments (small, due to packaging) **Advantages (2 marks):** 1. **Lowest polar moment of inertia** — the engine's mass is concentrated near the car's centre, making the car change direction very quickly and predictably. 2. **Near-perfect 50:50 weight distribution** is achieved almost by default. 3. **Excellent cornering capability** — high lateral grip and balanced handling at the limit. 4. **Superior braking balance** — the front and rear axle loads do not shift dramatically under deceleration. **Disadvantages (2 marks):** 1. **Poor rear crashworthiness** — the engine sits in the rear crumple zone. 2. **Limited rear visibility** — rear window is small. 3. **Small luggage capacity** — fuel tank and oil tank occupy the side pods. 4. **Complex cooling** — radiators must be side-mounted. 5. **Expensive** — only used in supercars. **Applications (1 mark):** Ferrari 488 GTB, Lamborghini Huracán, Ford GT (2005), Audi R8 (mid-engine variant), McLaren 720S. **Why-and-How Logic (1 mark):** The MR layout is the optimum packaging for a track-focused performance car because it minimises the polar moment of inertia (a key factor in transient cornering response) while maintaining 50:50 weight distribution. The cost and packaging penalties (no rear boot, no rear seats, engine in the crumple zone) are accepted in exchange for the ultimate handling performance. --- #### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Paper] **Q: With neat sketches, explain the following car layouts in detail:** **(a)** Front Engine — Front Wheel Drive (FF) layout — 7 marks. **(b)** All-Wheel Drive (AWD) layout — 7 marks. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — FF Layout (7 marks):** In the **Front Engine — Front Wheel Drive (FF)** layout, the engine is mounted transversely (or sometimes longitudinally) at the front, and the front wheels are driven through an integrated transaxle (gearbox + differential). **Key components (2 marks):** - Transverse engine (crankshaft perpendicular to the car's length) - Integrated transaxle (gearbox + differential in one housing) - Two drive half-shafts with CV (constant-velocity) joints - Front wheels (drive + steering + braking) - Rear wheels (free rolling, non-driven) **Advantages (2 marks):** 1. **Compact packaging** — entire powertrain fits in the engine bay; no propeller shaft or transmission tunnel. 2. **Flat floor** — maximises cabin space, rear legroom, and boot volume. 3. **Lower mass** — 50–100 kg lighter than comparable FR cars. 4. **Superior traction in wet/snow** — driven wheels carry ~60 % of static weight. 5. **Lower manufacturing cost** — fewer parts, simpler assembly. **Disadvantages (1 mark):** 1. **Front-heavy weight distribution** (~60:40) causes **understeer**. 2. Front tyres perform three roles (drive, steer, brake) → faster wear. 3. **Torque steer** in high-power applications (worse with unequal half-shaft lengths). 4. Limited to smaller engines due to packaging constraints. **Applications (1 mark):** Maruti Suzuki Swift, Honda City, Hyundai Creta, Volkswagen Polo, Toyota Etios, Tata Tiago. **Why-and-How Logic (1 mark):** The FF layout dominates the global passenger car market because it offers the best compromise between packaging efficiency, cost, and everyday usability. The understeer tendency (a safety-favourable handling characteristic) makes FF cars predictable and forgiving for average drivers, which is the primary reason for its mass-market adoption post-1959 (Mini Cooper, Citroën Traction Avant). --- **Part (b) — AWD Layout (7 marks):** In the **All-Wheel Drive (AWD)** layout, all four wheels are driven, either continuously (full-time AWD) or on-demand (part-time AWD). The powertrain can be front-engine or mid-engine, with a transfer case distributing torque to both axles. **Key components (2 marks):** - Engine (front or mid) - Gearbox - Transfer case (or electronic multi-plate clutch coupling) - Front differential - Propeller shaft to rear axle - Rear differential - Four drive half-shafts (or two in the rear + two in the front) **Types (1 mark):** 1. **Part-time 4WD** — driver-selectable; used in off-road vehicles (e.g., Mahindra Thar, Jeep Wrangler). 2. **Full-time AWD** — always engaged; uses a centre differential (Torsen, viscous, or electronic). Example: Subaru Symmetrical AWD. 3. **On-demand AWD** — automatically engages rear wheels when front slip is detected. Example: Haldex clutch in VW/Audi vehicles. **Advantages (2 marks):** 1. **Maximum traction** in all weather conditions (snow, mud, wet tarmac). 2. **Superior launch performance** — no wheelspin. 3. **Improved high-speed stability** in slippery conditions. 4. **Safer cornering exit** — torque vectoring possible with electronically controlled AWD. **Disadvantages (1 mark):** 1. **Mass penalty** of 80–150 kg over equivalent 2WD. 2. **Higher fuel consumption** (~5–10 % worse). 3. **Increased mechanical complexity** and maintenance cost. 4. **Higher purchase price**. **Applications (1 mark):** Subaru Forester, Audi Q5, Toyota RAV4 AWD, Lamborghini Urus, Bugatti Chiron (M4 layout). **Why-and-How Logic (1 mark):** AWD is the optimal layout when maximum traction is required. The traction advantage can be quantified: on a $\mu = 0.3$ surface, an AWD car with 50:50 distribution can use 100 % of its weight for traction, whereas a FF car can only use 60 %. This ~67 % increase in available traction translates directly to safer launches and better corner-exit acceleration. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Conflating "layout" with "body style":** Many students incorrectly use "sedan" and "hatchback" as layouts. They are **body styles**, not layouts. The layout refers to engine and drive placement. (Lose 2 marks.) > 2. **Forgetting to mention the orientation of the engine** (longitudinal vs transverse) in FF vs FR descriptions. This is a frequent omission. (Lose 1 mark.) > 3. **Missing the "Why-and-How" reasoning:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasises Outcome-Based Education (OBE). Just listing advantages without explaining the underlying physics (e.g., "FR has 50:50 distribution *because* the engine is front-mid mounted and the differential is at the rear") leads to partial scoring. (Lose 2–3 marks.) > 4. **No sketches:** Always include a labelled block diagram or layout sketch. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a clear diagram. > 5. **Incorrect example cars:** Do not write "Tata Nano uses FR layout" — it uses RR. Do not write "Maruti Swift uses FR" — it uses FF. Verifying real-world examples is essential. > 6. **Mixing up understeer and oversteer:** FF = understeer, RR = oversteer, MR/FR = neutral. Getting this wrong is a classic 1-mark loss. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Layout** = packaging of engine + driven wheels + body style over the chassis. It is **not** synonymous with body style. - The **5 standard layout codes** are **FR, FF, RR, MR, and AWD (4WD)**. - **FR** → 50:50 distribution, longitudinal engine, propeller shaft, transmission tunnel, used in luxury sedans and sports cars (BMW, Mercedes). - **FF** → 60:40 distribution, transverse engine, integrated transaxle, flat floor, dominant in mass-market cars (Maruti, Honda, Hyundai). - **RR** → 40:60 distribution, rear-engine + rear-drive, oversteer tendency, used in Porsche 911, classic VW Beetle, Tata Nano. - **MR** → 50:50 distribution, mid-mounted engine, lowest polar moment of inertia, used in supercars (Ferrari, Lamborghini, McLaren). - **AWD** → all four wheels driven, maximum traction, used in SUVs and rally cars, +80 to +150 kg mass penalty. - **Body styles** = Sedan, Hatchback, Coupe, Convertible, Station Wagon, SUV, MPV, Pickup, Crossover. - **Construction types** = Monocoque (unibody) vs Body-on-Frame. - **Static weight distribution formula:** $\%F = \dfrac{W_f}{W_f + W_r} \times 100$. - **Polar moment of inertia:** $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$. Minimised by centralising masses (MR layout principle). - **Understeer** = front tyres lose grip first (FF cars). - **Oversteer** = rear tyres lose grip first (RR cars). - **AWD traction advantage** ≈ 67 % over FF on a $\mu = 0.3$ surface. - **Weight distribution targets:** FF ≈ 60:40, FR ≈ 50:50–55:45, RR ≈ 40:60, MR ≈ 50:50, AWD ≈ 50:50–60:40. - Always include a **labelled block diagram** in 14-mark answers. - Always mention **why** the layout suits its application — not just the facts. ---

topics.title: Buses and Coaches with Different Seating and Loading Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Buses and Coaches with Different Seating and Loading Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Buses and Coaches (AIS-052 / IS 14654 Definitions):**
> A **Bus** is a motor vehicle constructed or adapted to carry more than **10 persons (including the driver)**, primarily designed for the carriage of seated or standing passengers, with seats and gangways (aisles) that permit frequent passenger movement. A **Coach** is a specialized bus designed for long-distance travel, characterized by higher seating comfort, luggage hold capacity, and structural refinement for sustained high-speed cruising.

**Seating Capacity** is defined as the maximum number of designated passenger seats (excluding the driver) that can be permanently installed in the vehicle while satisfying the minimum per-passenger space envelope prescribed under **AIS-052 (Rev. 1)** and the **Automotive Industry Standards** notified by the Government of India.

**Loading Capacity (Gross Vehicle Weight – GVW)** is the maximum legally permissible total weight of the fully laden vehicle, comprising the **Kerb Weight (unladen mass)**, the **Passenger Payload**, the **Crew & Luggage Payload**, and any auxiliary fluid/fuel masses.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a bus body as a **"vertical apartment block on wheels."**
- The **floor plan** is like the apartment layout — you decide how many rooms (seats) fit, how wide the corridors (gangways) are, and where the elevators (doors) are placed.
- The **seating arrangement** is the furniture layout: a *2×2 layout* is like a 4-seater dining table (comfort), a *3×2 layout* is like a cinema hall (high capacity), and a *2×1 sleeper* is like a first-class airline cabin (luxury).
- The **loading capacity** is the building's foundation — it dictates the structural strength of the chassis, the size of the axles, and the choice of suspension. A double-decker bus is like a 2-story building; it needs a much stronger foundation (chassis) than a single-story mini-bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus specifically distinguishes between **"Seating"** and **"Loading"** capacities. Seating is a *geometric/ergonomic* problem, while Loading is a *structural/legal-weight* problem. Both must be solved **simultaneously**, and the governing (smaller) value dictates the final design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Standard passenger mass (per person) | **68 kg** | AIS-052 / CMVR |
| Crew mass (driver + conductor) | **68 kg + 68 kg** | AIS-052 |
| Minimum seat cushion width | **400 mm** | AIS-052 |
| Minimum seat back height | **450 mm (urban) / 600 mm (long-distance)** | AIS-052 |
| Minimum gangway (aisle) width | **450 mm (urban) / 350 mm (limited access)** | AIS-052 |
| Standard headroom | **1900 mm (urban) / 2000 mm (coach)** | AIS-052 |
| Maximum GVW range (India) | **5.5 t (mini) → 25 t (articulated/double-decker)** | CMVR Rule 93 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Hierarchy of Buses & Coaches

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to identify the vehicle category *first* before any design calculation, because each category carries its own **seating pitch**, **aisle width**, and **floor height** rules.

### A. By Length & Mass (CMVR Classification)

| Class | Length (m) | Typical GVW (t) | Typical Seats | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mini-Bus (Type A)** | ≤ 5.5 | 4.0 – 5.5 | 10 – 19 | School, Feeder, Office shuttle |
| **Midi-Bus (Type B)** | 5.5 – 8.0 | 7.0 – 10.0 | 20 – 32 | City routes, Pilgrimage |
| **Standard Bus (Type C)** | 8.0 – 12.0 | 12.0 – 18.0 | 33 – 55 | Inter-city, Public transit |
| **Articulated Bus** | 16.0 – 18.75 | 18.0 – 28.0 | 50 – 80 | BRT, High-density city |
| **Double-Decker** | 9.0 – 12.0 | 18.0 – 25.0 | 70 – 90 | Tourist, High-capacity transit |

### B. By Service Type (Function-Based)

1. **Urban / City Bus:** High doors, low floor, vertical stanchions, standee allowance.
2. **Inter-City Bus:** 2×2 reclining seats, luggage racks, fewer doors.
3. **Coach (Long-Distance):** Single door, AC, high floor, lavatory, parcel racks.
4. **Sleeper Coach:** 2×1 berths, semi-bunk or full-bunk, individual curtains.
5. **Luxury / Multi-Axle Coach:** 2×1 or 1×1, air-suspension, premium interior.

## 2.2 Seating Layouts (L × W Notation)

The notation **"L × W"** describes the number of seats on the **left side of the gangway (L)** and the **right side of the gangway (W)**.

| Layout | Seats per Row | Gangway Count | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **3 × 2** | 5 | 1 | High-capacity city/inter-city |
| **2 × 2** | 4 | 1 | Standard coach, school bus |
| **2 × 1** | 3 | 1 | Luxury / Sleeper (extra wide seat) |
| **3 × 0** | 3 (no aisle) | 0 | Mini-bus (driver-side entry) |
| **2 × 1 + 1** | 4 (one face-to-face) | 1 | Tour bus (club-class) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **algebraically verified** and aligned with **AIS-052 / CMVR** derivation patterns. Units are in **mm, kg, and tonnes (t)** unless otherwise stated.

**Formula 1 — Seating Capacity from Floor Geometry**

$$N_{seats} \;=\; \left\lfloor\, \frac{L_{usable}}{p_{seat}} \,\right\rfloor \times N_{per\,row}$$

Where:
- $L_{usable}$ = usable internal floor length (subtract front bulkhead, rear engine bay, door swing zones)
- $p_{seat}$ = **seat pitch** (longitudinal spacing between two consecutive seat rows)
- $N_{per\,row}$ = total seats per row from the chosen layout

**Formula 2 — Seat Pitch (KTU standard values)**

$$p_{seat} \;=\; p_{body} + t_{cushion\,front} + t_{cushion\,back} + \delta_{recline}$$

Standard values: **700 mm (urban)**, **750 mm (inter-city)**, **800–850 mm (luxury coach)**.

**Formula 3 — Payload Mass from Seating & Luggage**

$$m_{payload} \;=\; N_{seats} \cdot m_{pass} + m_{crew} + m_{luggage}$$

With **$m_{pass} = 68 \text{ kg}$** and **$m_{crew} = 2 \times 68 = 136 \text{ kg}$** (driver + conductor).

**Formula 4 — Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)**

$$M_{GVW} \;=\; M_{kerb} + m_{payload} + m_{fuel} + m_{fluids}$$

**Formula 5 — Axle Load Distribution (Static)**

$$W_{front} \;=\; \frac{M_{GVW} \cdot (L - a)}{L} \qquad\qquad W_{rear} \;=\; \frac{M_{GVW} \cdot a}{L}$$

Where:
- $L$ = wheelbase
- $a$ = longitudinal distance from **front axle** to the **center of gravity (c.g.)** of the fully laden bus

**Formula 6 — Floor Area & Standing Allowance (Urban Buses)**

$$A_{floor} \;=\; L_{usable} \times W_{internal} \qquad N_{standee} \;=\; \left\lfloor\, \frac{A_{floor} - A_{seats}}{A_{per\,standee}} \,\right\rfloor$$

**Formula 7 — Per-Passenger Floor Space (standing)**

$$A_{per\,standee} \;=\; 0.30 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (urban) to } 0.45 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (suburban)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **seating capacity** (a *count*) with **payload** (a *mass*). The mark scheme strictly tests both. If asked for "seating capacity," you give a *number*; if asked for "loading capacity," you give a *weight in kg or tonnes*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the commercial-vehicle industry (Ashok Leyland, Tata, Eicher, VECV, BYD), the **seating × pitch × aisle** matrix is the *starting point* of every body-builder's design brief. A mismatch between **seating capacity** (regulatory) and **GVW** (structural) leads to either:
- **Over-loading** → chassis frame stress, axle failure, and accidents (regulatory risk).
- **Under-utilization** → lower revenue per kilometer (commercial risk).

Hence, the engineer must iterate the **layout → payload → GVW → axle load** loop until all three constraints are simultaneously satisfied.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Standard Inter-City Bus Seating Calculation

**Given (KTU-Style Problem Statement):**
> Design the seating layout of a standard inter-city bus with the following data:
> - Total external length $L_{ext} = 9.0$ m
> - Front overhang + rear overhang = 1.2 m + 1.8 m
> - Wheelbase $L = 6.0$ m
> - Chassis frame width (internal cabin width) $W_{int} = 2.35$ m
> - Aisle width $w_{aisle} = 0.45$ m
> - Choose **2 × 2 layout** with seat pitch $p_{seat} = 0.75$ m
> - Front bulkhead (driver zone) = 1.5 m; Rear engine cover (after last row) = 0.6 m
> - Door swing zone (single front door) = 0.4 m at the front

**Required:** Total seating capacity, total payload mass, and check against a maximum GVW of **16.0 tonnes**. Assume kerb weight = 9.5 t, fuel + fluids = 200 kg, and luggage allowance = 15 kg per passenger.

### Step 1 — Usable Internal Floor Length

$$L_{usable} \;=\; L_{ext} - (\text{front overhang}) - (\text{rear overhang}) - (\text{front bulkhead}) - (\text{rear engine cover}) - (\text{door zone})$$

$$L_{usable} \;=\; 9.0 - 1.2 - 1.8 - 1.5 - 0.6 - 0.4 \;=\; 3.5 \text{ m}$$

> **Logic:** The total external length of **9.0 m** is reduced by every non-passenger zone. The chassis frame's external length usually equals external body length, so we simply subtract geometric exclusions. The 3.5 m result is the *pure passenger cabin* length.

### Step 2 — Number of Seat Rows

$$N_{rows} \;=\; \left\lfloor\, \frac{L_{usable}}{p_{seat}} \,\right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor\, \frac{3.5}{0.75} \,\right\rfloor$$

$$\frac{3.5}{0.75} \;=\; 4.667 \;\Rightarrow\; N_{rows} \;=\; 4 \text{ rows}$$

> **Logic:** We use the **floor function** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ because you cannot install a *fraction* of a row. Four full rows occupy $4 \times 0.75 = 3.0$ m, leaving 0.5 m unused (which can accommodate a half-row or be left as a buffer).

### Step 3 — Seats per Row (2 × 2 Layout)

$$N_{per\,row} \;=\; 2 + 2 \;=\; 4 \text{ seats}$$

### Step 4 — Total Seating Capacity

$$N_{seats} \;=\; N_{rows} \times N_{per\,row} \;=\; 4 \times 4 \;=\; 16 \text{ seats}$$

### Step 5 — Passenger Payload Mass

$$m_{pax} \;=\; N_{seats} \times 68 \;=\; 16 \times 68 \;=\; 1088 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{crew} \;=\; 2 \times 68 \;=\; 136 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{luggage} \;=\; N_{seats} \times 15 \;=\; 16 \times 15 \;=\; 240 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{payload} \;=\; 1088 + 136 + 240 \;=\; 1464 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 6 — Total Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)

$$M_{GVW} \;=\; M_{kerb} + m_{payload} + m_{fluids}$$

$$M_{GVW} \;=\; 9500 + 1464 + 200 \;=\; 11164 \text{ kg} \;\approx\; 11.16 \text{ t}$$

### Step 7 — Regulatory Compliance Check

$$\text{Maximum permissible GVW} \;=\; 16.0 \text{ t}$$

$$\text{Check:} \quad M_{GVW} \;=\; 11.16 \text{ t} \;<\; 16.0 \text{ t} \;\;\checkmark$$

> **Interpretation:** The 16-seat design is **safe and regulatory-compliant**. The chassis has **16.0 − 11.16 = 4.84 t** of residual capacity, which means the manufacturer *could* add more rows if the floor length allowed — but the geometric constraint (3.5 m) is now the binding limit.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Different Seating Layouts at Same Floor Length)

Using $L_{usable} = 3.5$ m and $p_{seat} = 0.75$ m (4 rows fixed), here is how layout choice changes the outcome:

| Layout | Seats/Row | Total Seats | Payload (kg) | Total GVW (t) | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3 × 0 (no aisle) | 3 | 12 | 1092 | 10.79 | School / mini-van |
| 2 × 1 (luxury) | 3 | 12 | 1092 | 10.79 | Luxury coach (1 extra-wide seat) |
| 2 × 2 (standard) | 4 | 16 | 1464 | 11.16 | Inter-city bus (most common) |
| 3 × 2 (high-density) | 5 | 20 | 1820 | 11.52 | Long-distance volume carrier |
| 2 × 1 sleeper | 3 (berths) | 12 | 1092 | 10.79 | Overnight sleeper coach |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Observation:** Moving from 2×2 to 3×2 *gains* 4 seats but adds only **356 kg** to GVW, well within the 16 t limit. This is the **engineering "sweet spot"** for high-density inter-city transport.

## 3.3 Axle Load Verification (Same Example)

Assume the c.g. of the laden bus is at distance $a = 2.8$ m from the front axle.

$$W_{front} \;=\; \frac{11.16 \times 10^3 \times (6.0 - 2.8)}{6.0} \;=\; \frac{11160 \times 3.2}{6.0}$$

$$W_{front} \;=\; \frac{35712}{6.0} \;=\; 5952 \text{ kg} \;\approx\; 5.95 \text{ t}$$

$$W_{rear} \;=\; \frac{11160 \times 2.8}{6.0} \;=\; \frac{31248}{6.0} \;=\; 5208 \text{ kg} \;\approx\; 5.21 \text{ t}$$

> **Engineering Check (CMVR Rule 93):** Front axle max load on a 6-t axle ≈ 6.0 t ✓; Rear axle max load on a 10-t axle ≈ 5.21 t ✓. **Both axles are safe.**

## 3.4 Standing Capacity (Urban Bus Variant)

For an urban **3 × 2** bus with $L_{usable} = 7.0$ m and $W_{int} = 2.4$ m, $p_{seat} = 0.70$ m:

$$N_{rows} \;=\; \left\lfloor\, \frac{7.0}{0.70} \,\right\rfloor \;=\; 10 \text{ rows}$$

$$N_{seats} \;=\; 10 \times 5 \;=\; 50 \text{ seats}$$

$$A_{floor} \;=\; 7.0 \times 2.4 \;=\; 16.8 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{seats} \;=\; N_{seats} \times (0.5 \times 0.45) \;\approx\; 50 \times 0.225 \;=\; 11.25 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{available} \;=\; 16.8 - 11.25 \;=\; 5.55 \text{ m}^2$$

$$N_{standee} \;=\; \left\lfloor\, \frac{5.55}{0.30} \,\right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor\, 18.5 \,\right\rfloor \;=\; 18 \text{ standees}$$

$$N_{total} \;=\; 50 + 18 \;=\; 68 \text{ passengers (peak)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bus Classification & Capacity Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bus Body Design] --> B{Use Case?}
    B -->|Short-distance| C[Urban / City Bus]
    B -->|Mid-distance| D[Inter-City Bus]
    B -->|Long-distance| E[Coach / Sleeper]

    C --> F[Low floor, wide doors]
    C --> G[Standing allowance: 0.30 m²/person]
    D --> H[2x2 or 3x2 layout]
    D --> I[pitch: 750 mm]
    E --> J[2x1 luxury / Sleeper berth]
    E --> K[pitch: 800-850 mm]

    F --> L[Seating Capacity]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[Payload Mass = N x 68 kg]
    M --> N[GVW = Kerb + Payload + Fluids]
    N --> O{Axle Load Safe?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Design Approved]
    O -->|No| Q[Reduce Seats or Strengthen Chassis]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Seating Layout Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[3x2 Layout] --> A1[5 seats per row]
    A1 --> A2[High density, 20 seats per 3.5m]
    B[2x2 Layout] --> B1[4 seats per row]
    B1 --> B2[Standard, 16 seats per 3.5m]
    C[2x1 Layout] --> C1[3 wide seats per row]
    C1 --> C2[Luxury, 12 seats per 3.5m]
    D[2x1 Sleeper] --> D1[3 berths per row]
    D1 --> D2[Overnight, 12 berths per 3.5m]
    E[3x0 Layout] --> E1[3 seats per row no aisle]
    E1 --> E2[Mini bus, 12 seats per 3.5m]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Coach Body)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FrontZone[Front Zone: Driver and Door]
        DRV[Driver Seat 1 Person]
        DOOR[Front Door 700mm]
    end
    subgraph CabinZone[Cabin Zone: Passenger Seating]
        L1[Row 1: 2x2 Layout]
        L2[Row 2: 2x2 Layout]
        L3[Row 3: 2x2 Layout]
        L4[Row 4: 2x2 Layout]
    end
    subgraph RearZone[Rear Zone: Luggage and Engine]
        LUG[Luggage Bay 1.5 cu.m]
        ENG[Engine Cover or Lavatory]
    end
    FrontZone --> CabinZone
    CabinZone --> RearZone
    LUG --> ENG
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between a **Bus** and a **Coach** with reference to the AIS-052 standards.

**Model Answer:**
A **Bus** is a motor vehicle designed to carry more than 10 persons, with seats and gangways that permit frequent passenger movement; typically used for **short to medium-distance** urban or inter-city routes. A **Coach** is a long-distance, high-comfort variant of a bus characterized by a **single front door**, **AC cabin**, **lavatory**, **luggage hold**, and **reclining seats with greater pitch (800–850 mm)**. Coaches follow stricter AIS-052 provisions for headroom (≥ 2000 mm) and seat-back height (≥ 600 mm), while city buses follow lower thresholds (1900 mm and 450 mm respectively). *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for comfort comparison, 1 for AIS-052 reference.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **Seating Pitch** and state its standard value for an urban bus and a long-distance coach.

**Model Answer:**
Seating pitch is the **longitudinal center-to-center distance** between two consecutive seat rows, measured along the vehicle floor. It is the key geometric parameter that determines the number of seat rows that can be fitted into a given floor length. The standard values are:
- **Urban bus: 700 mm** (high density, frequent stops)
- **Inter-city bus: 750 mm** (balance of comfort and capacity)
- **Long-distance coach: 800–850 mm** (reclining luxury seats)

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for standard values.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A standard inter-city bus has the following specifications:
- External length = 10.5 m, Wheelbase = 6.5 m
- Front overhang = 1.4 m, Rear overhang = 2.0 m
- Front bulkhead (driver zone) = 1.6 m
- Rear engine cover = 0.8 m
- Door swing zone = 0.5 m
- Chosen layout: **3 × 2**
- Seat pitch = 0.75 m
- Standard passenger mass = 68 kg
- Kerb weight = 11.0 t; Fuel and fluids = 250 kg
- Luggage = 20 kg per passenger
- Crew (driver + conductor) = 2 persons

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the seating capacity and the gross payload mass.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Usable floor length
$$L_{usable} = 10.5 - 1.4 - 2.0 - 1.6 - 0.8 - 0.5 = 4.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Number of seat rows
$$N_{rows} = \left\lfloor \frac{4.2}{0.75} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 5.6 \rfloor = 5 \text{ rows}$$

**Step 3:** Seats per row (3 × 2)
$$N_{per\,row} = 3 + 2 = 5 \text{ seats}$$

**Step 4:** Total seating capacity
$$N_{seats} = 5 \times 5 = 25 \text{ seats}$$

**Step 5:** Payload mass
$$m_{pax} = 25 \times 68 = 1700 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{crew} = 2 \times 68 = 136 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{luggage} = 25 \times 20 = 500 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{payload} = 1700 + 136 + 500 = 2336 \text{ kg}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the GVW and verify whether the design complies with a maximum permissible GVW of 18.0 tonnes. If the c.g. is at 3.0 m from the front axle, also check the rear-axle load against a 10-tonne rear-axle limit.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Gross Vehicle Weight
$$M_{GVW} = 11000 + 2336 + 250 = 13586 \text{ kg} \approx 13.59 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2:** Compliance check
$$13.59 \text{ t} < 18.0 \text{ t} \;\;\checkmark \;\; \text{Design is compliant.}$$

**Step 3:** Front axle load
$$W_{front} = \frac{13586 \times (6.5 - 3.0)}{6.5} = \frac{13586 \times 3.5}{6.5} = \frac{47551}{6.5} \approx 7316 \text{ kg} \approx 7.32 \text{ t}$$

**Step 4:** Rear axle load
$$W_{rear} = \frac{13586 \times 3.0}{6.5} = \frac{40758}{6.5} \approx 6270 \text{ kg} \approx 6.27 \text{ t}$$

**Step 5:** Compliance check for rear axle
$$6.27 \text{ t} < 10.0 \text{ t} \;\;\checkmark \;\; \text{Rear axle is safe.}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> - Many students compute $W_{rear}$ but forget to verify against the **legal axle limit** (10 t for a single rear axle). This costs 1–2 marks.
> - Some students confuse $a$ as measured from the **rear axle** instead of the **front axle**. Re-read the formula: $a$ is the distance from the **front axle to the c.g.**
> - The "compliance check" step is **mandatory** for full marks; merely stating the GVW is not enough.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — **Alternative Choice**

**Q:** Compare and contrast the design considerations for a **city bus** and a **long-distance coach**, focusing on:
(i) Seating layout and pitch,
(ii) Standing capacity allowance,
(iii) Floor height and door configuration,
(iv) GVW and payload distribution.

Provide a **tabular comparison** and justify the differences using AIS-052 standards.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Construct a tabular comparison covering (i), (ii), and (iii).**

**Model Solution:**

| Design Parameter | City Bus | Long-Distance Coach |
|---|---|---|
| **Seating layout** | 3 × 2 (5 seats/row) | 2 × 2 or 2 × 1 (3–4 seats/row) |
| **Seat pitch** | 700 mm | 800–850 mm |
| **Seat cushion width** | 400 mm minimum | 450 mm preferred |
| **Seat back height** | 450 mm minimum | 600 mm minimum |
| **Standing allowance** | Yes (0.30 m²/person) | No (no standing permitted) |
| **Floor height** | Low floor (≤ 400 mm) or semi-low (550 mm) | High floor (850–1100 mm) for luggage bay |
| **Door configuration** | 2 or 3 wide doors (≥ 1100 mm) | 1 single front door (700–800 mm) |
| **Aisle width** | 450 mm (allows passenger flow) | 350 mm (limited access) |
| **Headroom** | 1900 mm | 2000 mm |
| **Luggage** | Roof rack optional | Dedicated luggage bay below floor |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss GVW and payload distribution differences, with numerical reasoning.**

**Model Solution:**

A **city bus** typically has a **lower GVW (12–16 t)** because:
- Passengers alight frequently, so the *average* load is lower even at peak hours.
- Standing passengers are counted, but their *effective weight* is bounded by the floor area, not just seat count.
- The chassis is optimized for **maneuverability**, so the wheelbase is shorter and axles are lighter.

A **long-distance coach** typically has a **higher GVW (16–25 t)** because:
- All passengers are *seated*, so payload scales linearly with seat count.
- Heavy luggage holds below the floor add to the **kerb weight** (typically 12–14 t before passengers).
- Multi-axle configurations (tag-axle or twin rear axles) are used to distribute the higher GVW safely.

**Example numerical justification:**
- City bus: 50 seated + 20 standees = 70 × 68 = **4760 kg** payload, GVW ≈ 13 t.
- Coach: 45 seated × 68 = **3060 kg** payload, GVW ≈ 18 t (due to heavier kerb).

The **kerb weight** dominates the coach's GVW, while the **payload mass** dominates the city bus's GVW. This is why the **axle load distribution** calculation yields a more rear-heavy load for a coach (luggage bay at the rear) and a more uniform load for a city bus (passengers spread across the cabin).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss (Q-B):**
> - Students often write a *generic* comparison without **numbers**. KTU 2024 valuation awards marks for **quantitative justification** (e.g., GVW numbers, payload kg, AIS-052 references).
> - The phrase *"long-distance coach"* must be tied to **multi-axle / tag-axle** configurations for full structural-mechanics marks.
> - Avoid vague terms like *"better comfort"*; use **specific dimensions** (e.g., "headroom 2000 mm vs 1900 mm").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read before every KTU exam):**

- [x] **Bus** = > 10 persons, frequent passenger movement; **Coach** = long-distance, high comfort, single door.
- [x] **Seating Pitch** = longitudinal center-to-center distance between seat rows (700 / 750 / 850 mm).
- [x] **Layout notation "L × W"** = seats on left of aisle × seats on right of aisle.
- [x] **Standard passenger mass** = **68 kg** (AIS-052 / CMVR).
- [x] **Seating capacity formula:** $N_{seats} = \lfloor L_{usable} / p_{seat} \rfloor \times N_{per\,row}$.
- [x] **Usable floor length** = External length − (front bulkhead + rear engine cover + door zones + overhangs).
- [x] **GVW = Kerb + Payload + Fluids**; check against **CMVR Rule 93** maximum.
- [x] **Axle load formula:** $W_{front} = M_{GVW} \cdot (L - a) / L$ where $a$ is from **front axle** to c.g.
- [x] **Standing capacity** is allowed only in **urban buses** at **0.30 m²/person**.
- [x] **City bus:** 2–3 doors, low floor, 3×2 layout. **Coach:** 1 door, high floor, 2×2 or 2×1.
- [x] Always perform **three checks:** (1) GVW < legal max, (2) front axle < 6 t, (3) rear axle < 10 t (single) or 18 t (tandem).
- [x] Use the **floor function** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ for seat counts — never a fraction of a row.
- [x] **Mini-bus** (≤ 5.5 m) → 10–19 seats; **Standard bus** (8–12 m) → 33–55 seats; **Articulated/Double-decker** (16–18.75 m) → 50–90 seats.
- [x] Always mention **AIS-052** standard reference in design answers for KTU 2024 scheme compliance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Vehicle Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Commercial Vehicle Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commercial Vehicle (CV):** A motor vehicle designed and constructed primarily for the carriage of goods (cargo) or passengers for hire or reward, having a **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)** exceeding **3.5 tonnes (3500 kg)** in the European/KTU context, or used exclusively for commercial purposes beyond personal transport.

In the Indian Automotive Research Association (ARAI) and Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) classification adopted by KTU, commercial vehicles are broadly categorized into **Goods Carriers (Transport vehicles / Trucks)** and **Passenger Carriers (Buses)**. They are distinguished from passenger cars by their chassis-frame construction, multi-axle configurations, and load-bearing body architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heavy Lifter" of the Road

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the automobile world as a family of working professionals:
> - **Personal Cars** are like office workers — they carry a few people and small items, traveling short distances in comfort.
> - **Commercial Vehicles** are like **freight train trucks of the road** — built ruggedly, with a strong ladder-frame chassis, multiple axles to distribute massive loads, and a body purpose-built for a specific cargo (liquid, solid, containerized, or human).

Just as a courier delivery van, a school bus, and a fuel tanker all have totally different "bodies" designed around their cargo, commercial vehicles follow the same logic — **the body is dictated by what it carries**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standardized metrics govern CV classification:

- **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)** = Kerb Weight + Payload + Crew + Fuel
- **Gross Combination Weight (GCW)** = GVW + Trailer Laden Weight (for articulated vehicles)
- **Axle Load Limit:** **Single axle ≤ 10.2 tonnes**, **Tandem axle ≤ 18.0 tonnes** (as per IRC standards in India)
- **Maximum Vehicle Length (CMV Rules):** **11 m** (2-axle rigid truck), **12 m** (3-axle rigid), **18.75 m** (articulated combinations)
- **Maximum Vehicle Width:** **2.5 m**, **Maximum Height:** **3.8 m** (without permit)

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Commercial Vehicles

Commercial vehicles are classified along **three independent axes**:

1. **By Purpose:** Goods Carriers vs. Passenger Carriers
2. **By GVW Class:** Light, Medium, Heavy, Extra-Heavy
3. **By Body/Application Type:** Tipper, Tanker, Box, Flatbed, Reefer, Bus, etc.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, **always classify a CV using all three axes** — examiners reward structured answers (e.g., "A TATA LPT 1109 is a Medium Commercial Vehicle (MCV), 4×2 rigid configuration, with a closed-box body, GVW of 11 tonnes.").

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification by Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW Class)

This is the **primary classification** recognized globally and by the Indian automotive industry:

| GVW Class | Weight Range (Tonnes) | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|
| Light Commercial Vehicle (LCV) | 3.5 – 7.5 | Mini-trucks, Tempo Travellers, Small delivery vans (e.g., TATA Ace, Mahindra Supro) |
| Light-Medium Commercial Vehicle (LMCV) | 7.5 – 12.0 | Medium cargo carriers (e.g., TATA LPT 1109) |
| Medium Commercial Vehicle (MCV) | 12.0 – 18.0 | Larger cargo trucks, mini-buses (e.g., TATA LPT 1612) |
| Heavy Commercial Vehicle (HCV) | 18.0 – 25.0 | Heavy haul trucks, intercity buses (e.g., TATA LPT 2516) |
| Extra-Heavy Commercial Vehicle (EHCV) | > 25.0 | Multi-axle trucks, trailers (e.g., TATA Prima 4028.S) |

## 2.2 Classification by Axle Configuration (n × m Notation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **n × m** notation is universally used:
> - **n** = Total number of wheels (or axles, colloquially)
> - **m** = Number of driven wheels (or axles)

| Configuration | Drive Type | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 4 × 2 | Single rear axle driven | LCV, MCV — light to medium loads |
| 6 × 2 | One axle of two driven (or pusher/tag) | City distribution, HCV |
| 6 × 4 | Tandem rear axle driven | Heavy haulage, tippers |
| 8 × 2 | Three-axle rigid, single driven | High-capacity city buses, rigid trucks |
| 8 × 4 | All rear axles driven | Mining dumpers, very heavy loads |
| 4 × 4 | All wheels driven | Off-road, defense, specialized applications |
| 6 × 6 | All wheels driven | Military, severe off-road |

## 2.3 Classification by Body/Application Type

### A. Goods Carriers (Trucks)

1. **Flatbed / Platform Body** — Open flat platform for loose cargo, machinery, steel coils
2. **Box Body / Container Body** — Enclosed for goods protection (FMCG, e-commerce)
3. **Tipper / Dump Body** — Hydraulic tilting for bulk loose material (sand, coal, aggregates)
4. **Tanker Body** — Cylindrical/elliptical pressurized tank for liquids (water, fuel, milk, chemicals)
5. **Reefer Body** — Insulated + refrigeration unit for perishables (cold chain logistics)
6. **Specialized Bodies** — Car carriers, livestock carriers, crane-mounted, concrete mixers, refuse compactors

### B. Passenger Carriers (Buses)

1. **Mini-Bus** — ≤ 24 passengers, on LCV chassis
2. **Type I Bus** — Single-deck, two-axle, ≤ 65 passengers (city/intercity)
3. **Type II Bus** — Single-deck, three-axle, ≥ 65 passengers
4. **Type III Bus** — Articulated bus (two rigid sections joined by a turntable)
5. **Double-Decker Bus** — Two passenger decks
6. **Coach** — Long-distance luxury bus (semi-monocoque body)

## 2.4 Structural Construction Types of Bus Bodies

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test this distinction — it appears in nearly every past paper.

1. **Separate Chassis with Body (Ladder Frame):** Engine, drivetrain on a separate chassis frame; body bolted on top. Used in trucks and most entry-level buses.
2. **Semi-Monocoque Construction:** Chassis frame retained but body panels share load-bearing duties. Used in long-distance coaches.
3. **Monocoque (Integral) Construction:** No separate frame — body shell itself carries all loads. Used in modern city buses and luxury coaches. Lower weight, better rigidity.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although "Commercial Vehicle Types" is largely descriptive, the following quantitative relationships are examinable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GVW} &= W_{\text{kerb}} + W_{\text{payload}} + W_{\text{crew}} + W_{\text{fuel}} \\
\text{GCW} &= \text{GVW}_{\text{tractor}} + W_{\text{trailer laden}} \\
\text{Payload Capacity} &= \text{GVW} - W_{\text{kerb}} \\
\text{Loading Density} &= \frac{W_{\text{payload}}}{V_{\text{body}}} \quad \left[\text{kg/m}^3\right] \\
\text{Axle Load} &= \frac{W_{\text{load on axle}}}{n_{\text{wheels on axle}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Vehicle Weight | GVW | tonnes (t) | $\leq$ 49 t (Indian legal max for multi-axle) |
| Kerb Weight | $W_{\text{kerb}}$ | kg | 30–45% of GVW |
| Payload Ratio | $\eta_p$ | — | $\eta_p \leq$ **70%** of GVW |
| Body Volume (Closed) | $V_b$ | m³ | Typically **10–60 m³** |
| Floor Loading | $q_f$ | kg/m² | $\leq$ **1500 kg/m²** for floor integrity |
| Turntable Capacity (Articulated Bus) | — | — | Up to **180 passengers** total |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Logistics & Freight:** CVs move **~60% of India's freight tonnage** by road (NITI Aayog data).
- **Public Transport:** Buses carry **>70% of public transit passengers** in urban India.
- **Construction & Mining:** Tipper trucks and dumpers are indispensable for bulk earth/ore movement.
- **Defense & Disaster Response:** 4×4 / 6×6 CVs serve as platforms for missiles, radars, and mobile hospitals.
- **Engineering Significance:** Body engineering of CVs directly affects payload efficiency, fuel economy (a 10% weight reduction = ~6% fuel saving), and crashworthiness — directly linked to KTU COs of this course.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Although classification is descriptive, KTU examiners often set **numerical problems** linking GVW, payload, axle distribution, and body volume. Below is a fully worked-out model problem typical of a 14-mark Part B question.

## 3.1 Problem Statement

> A three-axle rigid goods carrier has a kerb weight of **7,200 kg**, a payload of **12,800 kg**, driver + co-driver mass of **150 kg**, and fuel mass of **250 kg**. The front axle carries **45%** of the kerb weight and **20%** of the payload. Verify whether the vehicle complies with Indian axle-load regulations, and calculate the loading density if the closed-box body has external dimensions **7.0 m × 2.4 m × 2.5 m** and wall thickness **50 mm** on all sides. Take standard fuel density as **0.75 kg/L**.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

### Step 1 — Compute the Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{kerb}} &= 7200 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{payload}} &= 12800 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{crew}} &= 150 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{fuel}} &= 250 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{GVW} = 7200 + 12800 + 150 + 250 = 20400 \text{ kg} = 20.4 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> Classification Result: This places the vehicle in the **HCV (Heavy Commercial Vehicle)** category (18 – 25 t).

### Step 2 — Calculate Payload Ratio

$$
\eta_p = \frac{W_{\text{payload}}}{\text{GVW}} = \frac{12800}{20400} = 0.6276 \approx 62.76\%
$$

Since $\eta_p \leq 70\%$, the vehicle satisfies the design practice of keeping payload under 70% of GVW.

### Step 3 — Front Axle Load Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{kerb on front}} &= 0.45 \times 7200 = 3240 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{payload on front}} &= 0.20 \times 12800 = 2560 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming crew and fuel contribute fully to the rear (rear-engineered trucks), the front axle load is:

$$
W_{\text{front axle}} = 3240 + 2560 = 5800 \text{ kg} = 5.8 \text{ tonnes}
$$

### Step 4 — Rear Axle Load (Tandem Rear Axle)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{kerb on rear}} &= 7200 - 3240 = 3960 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{payload on rear}} &= 12800 - 2560 = 10240 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{crew + fuel on rear}} &= 150 + 250 = 400 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{rear (tandem)}} &= 3960 + 10240 + 400 = 14600 \text{ kg} = 14.6 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compliance Check Against IRC Axle Limits

| Axle | Computed Load | Legal Limit | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Front (Single) | **5.8 t** | ≤ **10.2 t** | ✅ Compliant |
| Rear (Tandem) | **14.6 t** | ≤ **18.0 t** | ✅ Compliant |

The vehicle is **legally compliant** in both axles.

### Step 6 — Internal Body Volume Calculation

External dimensions: $L = 7.0$ m, $W = 2.4$ m, $H = 2.5$ m

Wall thickness $t = 0.050$ m on all sides → Internal dimensions are $(L - 2t)$, $(W - 2t)$, $(H - t_{\text{floor}})$ assuming floor is $t$ thick.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i &= 7.0 - 2(0.050) = 6.90 \text{ m} \\
W_i &= 2.4 - 2(0.050) = 2.30 \text{ m} \\
H_i &= 2.5 - 0.050 = 2.45 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
V_{\text{body}} = 6.90 \times 2.30 \times 2.45 = 38.87 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 7 — Loading Density

$$
\rho_{\text{loading}} = \frac{W_{\text{payload}}}{V_{\text{body}}} = \frac{12800}{38.87} = 329.3 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

> Interpretation: A typical FMCG carton density is **150–250 kg/m³**, so this truck can be fully loaded with cartons and still have margin. If loading cement bags (≈ 1300 kg/m³), the truck would be volume-empty but weight-full.

### Step 8 — Floor Loading Check

$$
\text{Floor Area} = L_i \times W_i = 6.90 \times 2.30 = 15.87 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
q_f = \frac{W_{\text{payload}}}{\text{Floor Area}} = \frac{12800}{15.87} = 806.6 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

Since $q_f \leq 1500$ kg/m², the **plywood/metal floor is safe** under static load.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative CV Type Selection

| Requirement | Best CV Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Bulk grain transport | Tanker with pneumatic discharge | Hermetic, fast unloading |
| Refrigerated fish | Reefer (insulated + refrigeration) | Maintains 0–4 °C |
| Coal mine haulage | Tipper (rear-tilt) | Hydraulic dump, rugged |
| 80 km intercity route | Coach (semi-monocoque) | Comfort, luggage space |
| Urban transit 120 pax | Articulated Type III bus | High capacity, single driver |
| Heavy machinery transport | Flatbed with low-bed trailer | Easy loading via ramps |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Commercial Vehicles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Commercial Vehicle] --> A1[By Purpose]
    A1 --> A2[Goods Carrier]
    A1 --> A3[Passenger Carrier]

    A2 --> A21[By GVW Class]
    A21 --> A22[LCV 3.5 to 7.5 t]
    A21 --> A23[MCV 7.5 to 18 t]
    A21 --> A24[HCV 18 to 25 t]
    A21 --> A25[EHCV greater 25 t]

    A2 --> A30[By Body Type]
    A30 --> A31[Flatbed]
    A30 --> A32[Box Body]
    A30 --> A33[Tipper]
    A30 --> A34[Tanker]
    A30 --> A35[Reefer]
    A30 --> A36[Specialized Crane Mixer Dumper]

    A3 --> A40[By Capacity]
    A40 --> A41[Mini Bus up to 24 pax]
    A40 --> A42[Type I II City Bus]
    A40 --> A43[Articulated Type III]
    A40 --> A44[Double Decker]
    A40 --> A45[Coach Long Distance]

    A3 --> A50[By Construction]
    A50 --> A51[Ladder Frame Separate Chassis]
    A50 --> A52[Semi Monocoque]
    A50 --> A53[Monocoque Integral]
```

## 4.2 Axle Configuration Block Diagram

```mermaid

    B0[Axle Configuration n x m] --> B1[Rigid Truck]
    B0 --> B2[Articulated Truck]

    B1 --> B11[4x2 Light MCV]

    B2 --> B21[4x2 Tractor with Semi Trailer]

```

## 4.3 Bus Body Construction Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Bus Body Construction] --> C1[Ladder Frame]

    C1 --> C11[Body Mounted on Separate Chassis]
    C1 --> C12[Load Path Through Chassis Only]
    C1 --> C13[Used in Trucks and Entry Buses]

    C2 --> C21[Chassis Plus Body Panels Share Load]
    C2 --> C22[Used in Coaches]

    C3 --> C31[No Separate Frame]

```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Heavy Truck

```mermaid

    D0[Heavy Commercial Vehicle Functional Architecture] --> D1[Front Axle Assembly]

    D0 --> D6[Driver Cabin Module]

    D1 --> D11[Steering Knuckle]
    D1 --> D12[Brake Assembly]
    D1 --> D13[Suspension Leaf Spring]

    D2 --> D21[Longitudinal Rails]
    D2 --> D22[Cross Members]
    D2 --> D23[Outriggers]

    D3 --> D31[Engine Diesel BS6]
    D3 --> D32[Clutch and Gearbox]
    D3 --> D33[Propeller Shaft]
    D3 --> D34[Rear Axle Tandem]

    D4 --> D41[Floor Cross Members]
    D4 --> D42[Side Wall Posts]
    D4 --> D43[Roof Bows]

    D5 --> D51[Side Panels]
    D5 --> D52[Rear Door]
    D5 --> D53[Roof Sheet]

    D6 --> D61[Cabin Shell]
    D6 --> D62[Windshield]
    D6 --> D63[Instrument Panel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is preferred for descriptive/architectural topics in KTU, where a physical drawing would be too complex to render in Mermaid. The block-level view maps all major load-bearing and functional modules — directly aligned with the syllabus CO of "identifying CV body components."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Classify commercial vehicles based on Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW). List any three examples with their GVW ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Commercial vehicles are classified by GVW into:
1. **Light Commercial Vehicle (LCV):** 3.5 – 7.5 tonnes (e.g., TATA Ace, Mahindra Supro).
2. **Medium Commercial Vehicle (MCV):** 7.5 – 18 tonnes (e.g., TATA LPT 1109, Ashok Leyland ECOMET).
3. **Heavy Commercial Vehicle (HCV):** 18 – 25 tonnes (e.g., TATA LPT 2516, Ashok Leyland 2820).
4. **Extra-Heavy Commercial Vehicle (EHCV):** > 25 tonnes (e.g., TATA Prima multi-axle trucks).
*(Any 3 with examples = 3 marks)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the meaning of the axle configuration notation "6 × 4" used for commercial vehicles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In the n × m notation:
- **n = total number of wheels (or axles)** → 6 wheels = three axles
- **m = number of driven wheels (or axles)** → 4 driven wheels = two driven axles

Therefore, a **6 × 4** vehicle has **three axles with the rear two axles being driven** by the engine power via tandem rear axle. It is typically used for **heavy haulage, mining tippers, and long-distance cargo trucks** where high traction and load capacity are required.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B module question carries internal choice. **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of bus body constructions** used in commercial vehicles. Compare their **load path, weight, and applications.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4 × 2 light commercial vehicle has a kerb weight of **3,500 kg**, payload of **4,000 kg**, fuel mass of **120 kg**, and crew of **80 kg**. The front axle carries **55%** of the kerb weight and **30%** of the payload. Verify compliance with the **single-axle load limit of 10.2 tonnes**. Also compute the **payload ratio**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Bus Body Constructions (7 marks):**

Three main types:

1. **Ladder Frame with Separate Body (3 marks)**
   - Body bolted onto a separate ladder chassis.
   - Load path: Chassis frame carries all loads.
   - **Pros:** Easy body replacement, repair-friendly.
   - **Cons:** Higher weight, lower NVH comfort.
   - Application: Trucks, entry-level buses, mini-buses.

2. **Semi-Monocoque (2 marks)**
   - Chassis frame retained but **side panels, roof, and floor share load-bearing duties**.
   - Better stiffness-to-weight ratio.
   - Application: Long-distance **coaches** (e.g., Ashok Leyland Lynx, Volvo B7R coaches).

3. **Monocoque / Integral Construction (2 marks)**
   - **No separate frame** — body shell itself carries all structural loads.
   - Load path: Distributed throughout the body panels.
   - **Pros:** Lowest weight, highest stiffness, best NVH.
   - **Cons:** Expensive to repair, requires specialized fabrication.
   - Application: Modern city buses (e.g., Tata Starbus, BYD eBuzz K9).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — GVW calculation:

$$
\text{GVW} = 3500 + 4000 + 120 + 80 = 7700 \text{ kg} = 7.7 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Classification: **LCV / entry MCV**.

Step 2 — Payload Ratio:

$$
\eta_p = \frac{4000}{7700} = 0.5195 = 51.95\%
$$

Compliant — payload ratio is below 70%. **(1 mark)**

Step 3 — Front Axle Load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{kerb, front}} &= 0.55 \times 3500 = 1925 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{payload, front}} &= 0.30 \times 4000 = 1200 \text{ kg} \\
W_{\text{front axle}} &= 1925 + 1200 = 3125 \text{ kg} = 3.125 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Rear Axle Load:

$$
W_{\text{rear axle}} = 7700 - 3125 = 4575 \text{ kg} = 4.575 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Step 5 — Compliance Check:

| Axle | Load | Limit (Single) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Front | 3.125 t | ≤ 10.2 t | ✅ Compliant |
| Rear | 4.575 t | ≤ 10.2 t | ✅ Compliant |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Vehicle is fully compliant with Indian axle-load regulations.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the classification of **goods carrier trucks based on body type**. Explain the constructional features and applications of **tipper, tanker, and reefer bodies**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heavy commercial vehicle has a **box body of internal dimensions 6.5 m × 2.3 m × 2.2 m**. If it carries a **payload of 14 tonnes** uniformly distributed on the floor, calculate: **(i)** the floor loading pressure in **kg/m²**, **(ii)** the loading density in **kg/m³**, and **(iii)** comment on whether this loading suits **FMCG cartons (density ≈ 200 kg/m³)** or **cement bags (density ≈ 1300 kg/m³)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Goods Carrier Body Types (7 marks):**

1. **Tipper Body (2 marks)**
   - Open-top with **rear-hinged or three-way tipping** mechanism.
   - **Hydraulic cylinder** tilts the body to dump loose bulk material.
   - Construction: High-tensile steel plate floor with reinforced side walls.
   - Application: Sand, gravel, coal, ore, demolition debris.

2. **Tanker Body (2.5 marks)**
   - Cylindrical or elliptical **pressurized tank** mounted on chassis.
   - Sub-classes: Fuel tanker (safety baffled), water bowser, milk tanker (insulated stainless steel), chemical tanker (lined).
   - Construction: MS / SS / aluminium shells with **anti-surge baffles**, manholes, and discharge valves.
   - Application: Petroleum transport, water supply, liquid chemicals.

3. **Reefer Body (2.5 marks)**
   - **Insulated + refrigeration unit** mounted on front wall.
   - Walls: Sandwich panels of **PUF / EPS foam** between FRP / GI sheets.
   - Maintains temperature from **−25 °C to +25 °C**.
   - Application: Cold-chain logistics — seafood, meat, dairy, pharma, ice-cream.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: Internal dimensions $L_i = 6.5$ m, $W_i = 2.3$ m, $H_i = 2.2$ m. Payload $W_p = 14{,}000$ kg.

Step 1 — Floor Area and Volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{floor}} &= 6.5 \times 2.3 = 14.95 \text{ m}^2 \\
V_{\text{body}} &= 6.5 \times 2.3 \times 2.2 = 32.89 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Floor Loading:

$$
q_f = \frac{W_p}{A_{\text{floor}}} = \frac{14000}{14.95} = 936.5 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

Since $q_f \leq 1500$ kg/m², the **floor is safe**. **(2 marks)**

Step 3 — Loading Density:

$$
\rho_{\text{loading}} = \frac{W_p}{V_{\text{body}}} = \frac{14000}{32.89} = 425.7 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**(2 marks)**

Step 4 — Cargo Suitability Analysis:

For FMCG cartons (200 kg/m³), the **achievable payload by volume** is:

$$
W_{\text{FMCG, max}} = 32.89 \times 200 = 6578 \text{ kg} = 6.58 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Since truck can carry 14 t but only 6.58 t of cartons will fit → truck will be **volume-full but weight-empty**.

For cement bags (1300 kg/m³):

$$
W_{\text{cement, max}} = 32.89 \times 1300 = 42{,}757 \text{ kg} \approx 42.8 \text{ tonnes}
$$

But truck capacity = 14 t → truck will be **weight-full but volume-empty** (only 14/1300 = 10.77 m³ used out of 32.89 m³).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Neither cargo fully utilizes the truck. To optimize logistics, FMCG requires **larger body volume (high-cube)** and cement requires **higher GVW (multi-axle trailer)**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing n×m:** Many students write "6×4 = 6 wheels, 4 driven" — **WRONG**. It means **3 axles, 2 driven axles (4 driven wheels)**. *(Lose 1–2 marks)*
> 2. **GVW vs. Kerb Weight:** GVW includes payload + crew + fuel. **Kerb Weight is only the empty vehicle.** Mixing them gives incorrect payload ratio.
> 3. **Forgetting Units:** Always carry units in numerical answers — `kg/m²`, `kg/m³`, `tonnes`. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 4. **Body construction confusion:** "Monocoque = no chassis" — must explicitly state "**body shell carries all loads**" for full credit.
> 5. **Incomplete classification:** When asked to "classify," always classify along **all three axes** (purpose, GVW, body type). Single-axis answers score ≤ 50% marks.
> 6. **No sketches in Part B:** KTU rewards 1–2 marks for neat, labeled diagrams of bus/truck bodies. Always include one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Commercial Vehicle = GVW > **3.5 t** OR used for commercial transport of goods/passengers.
- ✅ **5 GVW Classes:** LCV (3.5–7.5 t), MCV (7.5–18 t), HCV (18–25 t), EHCV (>25 t), and ultra-class (>49 t).
- ✅ **n × m notation:** n = total wheels, m = driven wheels. Examples: 4×2, 6×4, 8×4.
- ✅ **6 truck body types:** Flatbed, Box, Tipper, Tanker, Reefer, Specialized (crane/mixer/dumper).
- ✅ **6 bus types:** Mini, Type I, Type II, Type III (articulated), Double-decker, Coach.
- ✅ **3 bus body constructions:** Ladder-frame, Semi-monocoque, Monocoque.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:**
  - GVW = Kerb + Payload + Crew + Fuel
  - Payload Ratio = Payload / GVW ≤ **70%**
  - Loading Density = Payload / Body Volume
  - Floor Loading ≤ **1500 kg/m²**
- ✅ **Legal Limits:** Single axle ≤ 10.2 t, Tandem axle ≤ 18 t, Max length 18.75 m, Max width 2.5 m.
- ✅ **Body type ↔ Cargo mapping:** Tipper ↔ Bulk, Tanker ↔ Liquid, Reefer ↔ Perishable, Flatbed ↔ Heavy machinery, Box ↔ FMCG.
- ✅ **Always classify** any CV using **Purpose + GVW Class + Axle Configuration + Body Type** in KTU answers.
- ✅ For numerical problems: **show every algebraic step** — KTU awards partial marks for the correct method even if the final answer has a small error.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Commercial Vehicle Types**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vans and Pickups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vans and Pickups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Vans
A **Van** is a light commercial vehicle (LCV) or passenger carrier characterized by an enclosed body, typically with a **box-type** or **panel-type** cargo/passenger compartment integrated with the driver's cabin. The body is usually built on a separate chassis or as a unitized monocoque structure. Vans are designed primarily for the transportation of **goods** (delivery vans) or **passengers** (passenger vans/minibuses) over short to medium distances.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEAUT741 Module 1):**
> Vans are light commercial vehicles (LCVs) with an enclosed body used for the carriage of goods or passengers, having a GVW typically between **1.5 tonnes and 3.5 tonnes**.

### 1.2 Definition of Pickups
A **Pickup Truck** is a light commercial vehicle featuring a separate, open or covered cargo bed (called the **pickup body**, **flatbed**, or **dropside body**) at the rear, with a distinct **driver/passenger cab** at the front. Pickups are engineered for flexible utility — hauling goods, equipment, and limited passengers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:**
> Pickups are classified as LCVs with **GVW up to 7.5 tonnes** (light-duty) or higher (medium-duty). The defining feature is the **separate load-carrying platform** behind the cab.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:**
> - A **Van** is like a **moving cupboard on wheels** — everything is locked inside a closed box. You use it when you want to keep your cargo (or passengers) protected from rain, dust, and theft (e.g., Amazon delivery vans, school buses, ice-cream vans).
> - A **Pickup** is like a **wheelbarrow with a windshield** — it has an open tray at the back for easy loading/unloading of bulky items like bricks, farm produce, or furniture.

### 1.4 Classification of Vans
| Van Type | Primary Use | Body Style |
|---|---|---|
| **Panel Van** | Goods delivery | Fully enclosed, no side/rear windows in cargo area |
| **Box Van** | Bulk cargo, removals | Large rectangular box body on chassis |
| **Refrigerated Van (Reefer)** | Perishable goods | Insulated + refrigeration unit |
| **Passenger Van / Minibus** | People transport | Multiple rows of seating, glazed sides |
| **Tipper Van** | Loose material dumping | Hydraulically tilted cargo box |
| **Camper Van** | Recreational/living | Converted interior with beds, kitchen |

### 1.5 Classification of Pickups
| Pickup Type | Cab Configuration | Body Style |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Cab Pickup** | 2 doors, 2–3 seats | Longest load bed |
| **Double Cab / Extended Cab** | 4 doors, 4–5 seats | Medium load bed |
| **Crew Cab** | 4 full-size doors, 5–6 seats | Shortest load bed |
| **Dropside Pickup** | Any cab | Hinged/tipping side panels |
| **Flatbed Pickup** | Any cab | Flat platform, no sides |
| **Chassis Cab** | Cab only | Customer fits custom body |
| **Off-road Pickup (4x4)** | Any cab | High ground clearance, AWD |

### 1.6 Key Physical Metrics
> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **GVW of Vans:** typically **1.5 t – 3.5 t**
> - **GVW of Pickups:** typically **1.5 t – 7.5 t** (light-duty) and up to **13 t** (medium-duty)
> - **Wheelbase range (Vans):** **2.5 m – 4.0 m**
> - **Wheelbase range (Pickups):** **2.8 m – 3.8 m**
> - **Standard body width:** **1.6 m – 2.0 m**
> - **Loading floor height (Pickups):** **0.7 m – 0.9 m** above ground

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Features of a Van Body
1. **Chassis Frame:** Ladder-type or space-frame chassis, providing the structural backbone.
2. **Cabin Module:** Front driver's cabin integrated with or attached to the cargo body.
3. **Cargo Compartment (Box):** A fully enclosed box with floor, four walls, and roof made from:
   - **Steel sheet** (1.0 mm – 1.6 mm) for body panels
   - **Aluminium sheet** for lightweight applications
   - **GRP/Fibreglass** for refrigerated vans
4. **Floor Reinforcement:** Cross members spaced **300 mm – 400 mm** apart to support point loads.
5. **Rear Door(s):** Hinged barn doors, roller shutter, or lift-up tailgate.
6. **Side Door:** Sliding door (most common) for kerbside loading.

### 2.2 Constructional Features of a Pickup Body
1. **Chassis:** Ladder frame with high torsional rigidity to handle off-road stresses.
2. **Cab:** Separate module — single, double, or crew configuration.
3. **Load Bed (Tray):** The defining component:
   - **Length:** 1.8 m (short), 2.4 m (standard), 3.0 m (long)
   - **Width:** 1.5 m – 1.8 m
   - **Depth of side walls:** 0.4 m – 0.5 m
4. **Drop-Side Panels:** Three hinged sides (rear + two sides) for easy loading.
5. **Tailgate:** Hinged at the bottom, can be removed.
6. **Tie-down Hooks:** For load securing (load rating typically **500 kg per hook**).
7. **Liner:** Plastic or rubber spray-on coating to protect the bed.

### 2.3 Design Considerations
> [!NOTE]
> **Why these parameters matter for KTU:**
> Body design of vans and pickups must balance **payload capacity**, **structural rigidity**, **aerodynamics**, **ergonomics**, and **legal regulations** (e.g., AIS-052 in India).

- **Payload Maximisation:** Keep kerb weight low, use lightweight materials (aluminium, composites).
- **Loading Height:** Should be **between 0.6 m and 0.9 m** to allow manual loading without excessive bending.
- **Cargo Volume:** Maximise internal dimensions within legal width (typically **2.0 m – 2.3 m**) and height limits.
- **Accessibility:** Sliding side doors + wide rear doors (vans) for quick loading/unloading.
- **Safety:** Reinforced cab, crumple zones, side impact protection, ESC (Electronic Stability Control).
- **Aerodynamics:** Vans have a high drag coefficient (**Cd ≈ 0.36 – 0.42**), so nose design matters.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Payload Capacity** | $P = GVW - W_k$ | kg or tonnes | $W_k$ = kerb weight |
| 2 | **Kerb Weight** | $W_k = W_c + W_b + W_f$ | kg | Chassis + body + fluids |
| 3 | **Cargo Volume** | $V_c = L_b \times W_b \times H_b$ | m³ | Internal body dimensions |
| 4 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_c}{V_o}$ | dimensionless | $V_o$ = outer envelope volume |
| 5 | **Load Density** | $\rho_L = \dfrac{P}{V_c}$ | kg/m³ | For cargo capacity check |
| 6 | **Weight Distribution (Front)** | $W_f = \dfrac{W_r \cdot L + P \cdot x}{L}$ | kg | Static axle load front |
| 7 | **Weight Distribution (Rear)** | $W_r^{total} = GVW - W_f$ | kg | Static axle load rear |
| 8 | **Floor Load Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{n \cdot A_s}$ | N/mm² | $n$ = number of cross members |
| 9 | **Centre of Gravity (Height)** | $h = \dfrac{\sum (W_i \cdot h_i)}{\sum W_i}$ | m | Composite CG of vehicle |
| 10 | **Bending Stress on Frame** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | N/mm² | $M$ = bending moment, $y$ = distance, $I$ = moment of inertia |
| 11 | **Torsional Rigidity** | $K_t = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{\theta}$ | N·m/rad | $T$ = torque, $\theta$ = angle of twist |
| 12 | **Drag Force on Van** | $F_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | N | $\rho$ = air density 1.225 kg/m³ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** All absolute values in the table above have been written using $\vert$ notation rather than the vertical pipe `|` character to prevent breaking the markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Logistics Industry:** Vans form the backbone of last-mile delivery (Amazon, DHL, FedEx fleets).
- **Agriculture:** Pickups used for transporting produce, livestock feed, and equipment.
- **Construction:** Tipper vans and pickups for material movement on small sites.
- **Public Transport:** Passenger vans (Tempo Traveller, Force Traveller) for tourism and employee transport.
- **Emergency Services:** Refrigerated vans for medical supply chains, mobile clinics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Payload Calculation from Vehicle Weight Data
**Problem Setup:** A delivery van has a Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW) rating of **3500 kg** and a kerb weight of **2200 kg**. Determine the maximum legal payload, the load density if the cargo area volume is **8.5 m³**, and the number of standard 25 kg sacks that can be loaded.

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters.**
We are given the Gross Vehicle Weight rating, which is the maximum allowable total mass of the vehicle when fully loaded. We are also given the kerb weight, which is the mass of the empty vehicle including all fluids, a full fuel tank, and standard equipment.

$$
GVW = 3500 \text{ kg}, \quad W_k = 2200 \text{ kg}, \quad V_c = 8.5 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the payload formula.**
The payload is the difference between the GVW and the kerb weight. This represents the maximum mass of cargo, passengers, and any additional equipment that the vehicle is legally permitted to carry.

$$
P = GVW - W_k
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
P = 3500 - 2200
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the result.**

$$
P = 1300 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the load density of the cargo.**
Load density is the payload per unit volume of cargo space. It tells us how heavy the cargo is relative to the available space.

$$
\rho_L = \dfrac{P}{V_c} = \dfrac{1300}{8.5}
$$

$$
\rho_L \approx 152.94 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the number of standard 25 kg sacks.**

$$
N = \dfrac{P}{m_{sack}} = \dfrac{1300}{25}
$$

$$
N = 52 \text{ sacks}
$$

**Final Answer:** Payload = **1300 kg**, Load density = **152.94 kg/m³**, Number of sacks = **52**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Front and Rear Axle Weight Distribution
**Problem Setup:** A pickup truck has a wheelbase of **3.2 m**. The kerb weight is 1800 kg with a centre of gravity at 1.4 m behind the front axle. When loaded, an additional payload of **1000 kg** is placed at **2.5 m** behind the front axle. Determine the load on each axle.

**Step 1 — Static front axle load (unloaded).**
Using the principle of moments about the rear contact point, taking counter-clockwise moments as positive.

$$
W_{f,\,unloaded} \cdot L = W_k \cdot (L - x_k)
$$

where $x_k$ is the distance of the kerb weight CG from the front axle, so the kerb weight CG is at distance $(L - x_k)$ from the rear axle.

Wait — let us re-derive from the front axle perspective. Taking moments about the rear axle contact point (so rear axle reaction is eliminated):

$$
W_f \cdot L = W_k \cdot (L - x_k)
$$

Here $x_k = 1.4$ m is the kerb CG behind the front axle, so the kerb CG is at $(L - x_k) = 3.2 - 1.4 = 1.8$ m from the rear axle.

**Step 2 — Solve for the unloaded front axle load.**

$$
W_{f,\,unloaded} = \dfrac{W_k \cdot (L - x_k)}{L} = \dfrac{1800 \times 1.8}{3.2}
$$

$$
W_{f,\,unloaded} = \dfrac{3240}{3.2} = 1012.5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute unloaded rear axle load.**

$$
W_{r,\,unloaded} = W_k - W_{f,\,unloaded} = 1800 - 1012.5
$$

$$
W_{r,\,unloaded} = 787.5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the payload contribution.**
The payload $P = 1000$ kg is located at $x_p = 2.5$ m behind the front axle, i.e., at $L - x_p = 0.7$ m from the rear axle.

**Step 5 — Front axle load (loaded).**
Taking moments about the rear axle contact point:

$$
W_{f,\,loaded} \cdot L = W_k \cdot (L - x_k) + P \cdot (L - x_p)
$$

$$
W_{f,\,loaded} = \dfrac{W_k \cdot (L - x_k) + P \cdot (L - x_p)}{L}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute values.**

$$
W_{f,\,loaded} = \dfrac{1800 \times 1.8 + 1000 \times 0.7}{3.2}
$$

$$
W_{f,\,loaded} = \dfrac{3240 + 700}{3.2} = \dfrac{3940}{3.2}
$$

$$
W_{f,\,loaded} = 1231.25 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 7 — Rear axle load (loaded).**

$$
W_{r,\,loaded} = (W_k + P) - W_{f,\,loaded} = 2800 - 1231.25
$$

$$
W_{r,\,loaded} = 1568.75 \text{ kg}
$$

**Final Answer:** Loaded front axle = **1231.25 kg**, Loaded rear axle = **1568.75 kg**, Total = **2800 kg** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** Sum of axle loads equals GVW (kerb + payload), confirming the moment balance is consistent.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Centre of Gravity Height of a Loaded Pickup
**Problem Setup:** A pickup has three major masses: chassis+cab (1600 kg at height 0.7 m), engine (250 kg at 0.55 m), and loaded cargo bed (1200 kg at 1.1 m). Find the composite CG height.

**Step 1 — Formula for composite CG height.**

$$
h = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot h_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the weighted moment sum.**

$$
\sum (W_i \cdot h_i) = (1600 \times 0.7) + (250 \times 0.55) + (1200 \times 1.1)
$$

$$
\sum (W_i \cdot h_i) = 1120 + 137.5 + 1320
$$

$$
\sum (W_i \cdot h_i) = 2577.5 \text{ kg·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total mass.**

$$
\sum W_i = 1600 + 250 + 1200 = 3050 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the CG height.**

$$
h = \dfrac{2577.5}{3050}
$$

$$
h \approx 0.845 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer:** The composite centre of gravity is at a height of **≈ 0.845 m** above the ground. This is critical for calculating **roll stability** and **anti-roll bar design**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Van vs Pickup Comparison

| Design Aspect | Van | Pickup |
|---|---|---|
| **Body Type** | Enclosed box | Open tray + separate cab |
| **Typical GVW** | 1.5 – 3.5 t | 1.5 – 7.5 t |
| **Cargo Protection** | Excellent (sealed from weather) | Limited (needs tarpaulin/canopy) |
| **Loading/Unloading** | Side sliding + rear doors | Drop-side + tailgate (3-side access) |
| **Loading Floor Height** | ~ 0.55 – 0.75 m | ~ 0.75 – 0.95 m |
| **Volumetric Capacity** | High (tall enclosed) | Lower (open bed) |
| **Aerodynamic Drag (Cd)** | 0.36 – 0.42 | 0.38 – 0.45 |
| **Off-road Capability** | Limited (2WD usually) | Good (4x4 common) |
| **Typical Use** | Urban delivery, passenger transport | Construction, agriculture, utility |
| **Structural Integration** | Often monocoque/unitized | Body-on-frame (ladder chassis) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
