<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Define a Van. List any **three** main types of vans used in commercial transportation.

**Model Answer:**
A van is a light commercial vehicle (LCV) with an enclosed body used primarily for the transportation of goods or passengers, typically having a GVW between 1.5 t and 3.5 t.

The three main types of vans are:
1. **Panel Van** — used for goods delivery with a fully enclosed body and no side windows in the cargo area.
2. **Box Van** — used for bulk cargo and removals, with a large rectangular box-shaped body mounted on a chassis.
3. **Refrigerated (Reefer) Van** — used for perishable goods, fitted with insulation and a refrigeration unit.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
Differentiate between a **single cab pickup** and a **double cab pickup**. State one application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single Cab Pickup | Double Cab Pickup |
|---|---|---|
| Doors | 2 doors | 4 doors |
| Seating | 2 – 3 persons | 4 – 5 persons |
| Load Bed Length | Longest (best payload) | Medium (compromised) |
| Application | Farm produce transport, construction material | Family + utility vehicles, contractor use |

A single cab pickup is preferred when **maximum cargo space** is needed, while a double cab is used when both **passenger comfort and cargo capacity** are required.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **constructional features of a light commercial van body**. List the materials commonly used for its body panels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A delivery van has a kerb weight of **1850 kg** and a GVW of **3200 kg**. The internal dimensions of the cargo area are **Length = 3.0 m, Width = 1.7 m, Height = 1.6 m**. Calculate the **(i)** payload, **(ii)** cargo volume, and **(iii)** load density. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Constructional Features of a Light Commercial Van Body**

A light commercial van body consists of the following components:

1. **Chassis Frame:** A ladder-type steel chassis forms the structural backbone, providing mounting points for the body, engine, and suspension. The frame is made of channel-section or box-section steel members.

2. **Floor Assembly:** The floor is reinforced with **cross members spaced at 300 – 400 mm** to support point loads. The floor plate is usually **1.2 mm – 1.6 mm** thick steel or aluminium sheet, often coated with anti-slip paint or plywood overlay.

3. **Side Walls:** Made of **steel sheet (1.0 – 1.2 mm)**, **aluminium**, or **GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic)**. They are stiffened by internal vertical and horizontal ribs to prevent bulging.

4. **Roof:** Made of the same material as the walls, with a slight camber for water drainage. Roof rails may be provided for mounting roof racks.

5. **Front Wall:** Integrates with the driver's cabin, with a partition window (optional) for visibility into the cargo area.

6. **Rear Doors:** Two-leaf barn doors, a single lift-up door, or a roller shutter. They are fitted with locking mechanisms and rubber seals for weather-proofing.

7. **Side Door:** A **sliding door** on the kerbside is the most common, providing easy access in tight urban spaces.

8. **Windows:** In passenger vans, the side walls and rear have glazed windows; in panel vans, the cargo area is windowless for security and insulation.

**Materials Used:**
- Mild steel (most common, cost-effective)
- Aluminium (lightweight, corrosion-resistant)
- GRP / Fibreglass (for refrigerated and specialty vans)
- Plywood + FRP (for insulated walls)
- Polypropylene / ABS (for interior trims)

**Sketch Description (to be drawn in exam):**
A side view of a van showing chassis, floor, side wall with sliding door, rear barn doors, roof, and wheel arches.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $W_k = 1850$ kg, $GVW = 3200$ kg, $L_b = 3.0$ m, $W_b = 1.7$ m, $H_b = 1.6$ m.

**(i) Payload:**

$$
P = GVW - W_k = 3200 - 1850 = 1350 \text{ kg}
$$

**(ii) Cargo Volume:**

$$
V_c = L_b \times W_b \times H_b = 3.0 \times 1.7 \times 1.6
$$

$$
V_c = 8.16 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(iii) Load Density:**

$$
\rho_L = \dfrac{P}{V_c} = \dfrac{1350}{8.16} \approx 165.44 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Final Answer:** Payload = **1350 kg**, Cargo Volume = **8.16 m³**, Load Density ≈ **165.44 kg/m³**.

---

**OR**

**QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of pickups** based on cab configuration. State **two design considerations** for pickup load beds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pickup truck has a wheelbase of **3.0 m**, kerb weight **1700 kg** with the CG at **1.3 m** behind the front axle. A payload of **900 kg** is placed at the centre of the load bed, which is **2.4 m** behind the front axle. Determine the load on the front and rear axles when fully loaded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Pickups Based on Cab Configuration**

| Cab Type | Description | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Cab** | 2 doors, 2–3 seats, no rear seating | Longest load bed, maximum payload volume |
| **Extended / Double Cab** | 2 full + 2 half doors, small rear jump seats | Medium load bed, compromise between space and seating |
| **Crew Cab** | 4 full-size doors, 5–6 seats | Shortest load bed, family-friendly, used as daily driver + utility |

**Two Design Considerations for Pickup Load Beds:**

1. **Bed Liner & Anti-Corrosion Coating:** The bed is exposed to abrasion, moisture, and chemicals. A **spray-on polyurethane liner**, **plastic drop-in liner**, or **galvanised steel** is used to extend life. The bed floor must have drainage holes.

2. **Tie-Down and Load Securing System:** The bed must have **integrated tie-down hooks** (load rating ≥ 500 kg each), **C-channel rails** along the sides, and **wheel well arch shapes** that do not obstruct flat loading. This is critical for both safety and to comply with AIS standards.

*(Other valid points: tailgate load capacity, side panel hinges for full drop, bed lighting, step bumper integration, payload distribution markings.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $L = 3.0$ m, $W_k = 1700$ kg, $x_k = 1.3$ m, $P = 900$ kg, $x_p = 2.4$ m.

**Step 1 — Distance of kerb CG from rear axle:**

$$
L - x_k = 3.0 - 1.3 = 1.7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance of payload CG from rear axle:**

$$
L - x_p = 3.0 - 2.4 = 0.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Front axle load (loaded) by moment balance about rear axle:**

$$
W_{f,\,loaded} = \dfrac{W_k \cdot (L - x_k) + P \cdot (L - x_p)}{L}
$$

$$
W_{f,\,loaded} = \dfrac{1700 \times 1.7 + 900 \times 0.6}{3.0}
$$

$$
W_{f,\,loaded} = \dfrac{2890 + 540}{3.0} = \dfrac{3430}{3.0}
$$

$$
W_{f,\,loaded} \approx 1143.33 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Rear axle load (loaded):**

$$
W_{r,\,loaded} = (W_k + P) - W_{f,\,loaded} = 2600 - 1143.33
$$

$$
W_{r,\,loaded} \approx 1456.67 \text{ kg}
$$

**Final Answer:** Front axle load ≈ **1143.33 kg**, Rear axle load ≈ **1456.67 kg**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often take moments about the **wrong axle** (front instead of rear) and end up with incorrect sign conventions. Always re-check which axle reaction you are eliminating.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to use **$(L - x)$** (distance from rear axle) instead of $x$ (distance from front axle) in the moment equation. The lever arm must be measured from the **pivot point**, not the reference point.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not verifying the answer by checking $W_f + W_r = GVW$. If it does not, you have an arithmetic error.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (Part A questions):** Writing only the definition and forgetting the **list of types** — easy 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU PEAUT741 Module 1 — Vans and Pickups):**
> - **Van:** Enclosed LCV, GVW 1.5–3.5 t, used for goods/passengers.
> - **Pickup:** LCV with separate cab + open load bed, GVW 1.5–7.5 t (light-duty).
> - **Key Sub-types of Vans:** Panel, Box, Reefer, Passenger, Tipper, Camper.
> - **Key Sub-types of Pickups:** Single cab, Double cab, Crew cab, Dropside, Flatbed, Chassis cab.
> - **Payload Formula:** $P = GVW - W_k$ (master this — it appears in almost every KTU problem).
> - **Cargo Volume:** $V_c = L_b \times W_b \times H_b$.
> - **Load Density:** $\rho_L = P / V_c$ — tells if cargo is bulky or dense.
> - **Axle Load Distribution:** Take moments about one axle to find the other; always verify by sum.
> - **CG Height:** Composite formula $h = \sum (W_i h_i) / \sum W_i$ — affects roll stability.
> - **Standard Loading Floor Heights:** Van ~ 0.55–0.75 m, Pickup ~ 0.75–0.95 m.
> - **Frame Materials:** Steel (chassis), Aluminium / GRP (lightweight panels), Plywood + FRP (insulation).
> - **Constructional Hierarchy:** Chassis → Cabin → Cargo box/load bed → Doors/access → Trims.
> - **Aero Drag:** Vans have $C_d \approx 0.36 - 0.42$; pickups slightly higher due to open bed turbulence.
> - **Volumetric Efficiency:** Always state internal vs external dimensions correctly in exam answers.
> - **Remember AIS-052 / CMV rules** for body dimensions if a regulatory question appears.
> - **Always end numerical problems with a verification line** (e.g., $W_f + W_r = GVW$) — examiners reward this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terms Used in Bus Body Building - Angle of Approach and Departure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Terms Used in Bus Body Building - Angle of Approach and Departure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the domain of **Vehicle Body Engineering**, a bus is a long, rigid, and relatively low-clearance passenger carrier. Two of the most critical *geometric clearance parameters* that govern the operability of a bus over uneven terrain, inclines, and ramps are the **Angle of Approach** and the **Angle of Departure**.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Angle of Approach (α)** is defined as the **maximum angle of an imaginary ramp**, measured in the **vertical longitudinal plane** of the bus, which the vehicle can climb **forward** without any part of its **front body, bumper, or chassis overhang** touching the ramp surface.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Angle of Departure (β)** is defined as the **maximum angle of an imaginary ramp**, measured in the **vertical longitudinal plane** of the bus, which the vehicle can descend **backward** (or climb **forward** off a ramp) without any part of its **rear body, bumper, or chassis overhang** touching the ramp surface.

In simpler terms, these are the **steepest inclines** your bus can negotiate without its nose or tail scraping the ground. Both angles are measured with respect to the **horizontal ground reference plane**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are dragging a **long, heavy wooden plank** (representing a bus) across a **high doorstep** (representing a ramp or curb). If you try to drag it up at a steep angle, the front edge of the plank will **catch on the doorstep** and the plank will not climb. The shallowest angle at which the plank just clears the doorstep is the **angle of approach** for the front edge. Now reverse the plank — the angle at which the rear edge clears a similar obstacle is the **angle of departure**.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **bus**, the "long plank" effect is amplified because buses have **long overhangs** at both front and rear (to accommodate the engine cowl at the front and the rear axle/transmission tunnel at the back). Hence, approach and departure angles are *particularly critical* for buses — far more critical than for a passenger car with short overhangs.

### Why These Two Angles Matter in Bus Design

- **Urban Buses** frequently climb steep **bus station ramps, flyovers, and bridge aprons**.
- **Tourist / Hill Buses** operate in **ghat roads** (e.g., Kerala's Western Ghats, Kodaikanal, Ooty routes) where ramps exceed **15° to 20°**.
- **School Buses** must safely **mount high speed-breakers** without scraping the bumper.
- **Intercity Buses** must clear **railway level crossings** and **uneven rural road edges**.

### Reference Standards (Indian Context)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Automotive Industry Standards (AIS-052)** and **BIS: 14654** specify the minimum approach and departure angles for buses manufactured and operated in India. Typical minimum values mandated are **α ≥ 8°** for fully-built buses and **β ≥ 8°** for fully-built buses, with higher values mandated for **off-road and ghat-service buses**.

### Units and Reference Geometry

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angle of Approach | α | **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)** | Vertical longitudinal plane |
| Angle of Departure | β | **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)** | Vertical longitudinal plane |
| Ground Clearance | h | **millimetres (mm)** | Vertical |
| Front Overhang | L_f | **millimetres (mm)** | Horizontal |
| Rear Overhang | L_r | **millimetres (mm)** | Horizontal |
| Wheelbase | W | **millimetres (mm)** | Horizontal |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Geometric Construction of the Angles

Consider a **side elevation (longitudinal section)** of a bus standing on flat horizontal ground. Three critical points on the bus body define the approach and departure angles:

1. **Point F** — The **lowest projection of the front bumper / front body overhang** that is closest to the ground.
2. **Point R** — The **lowest projection of the rear bumper / rear body overhang** that is closest to the ground.
3. **Point T_F** and **Point T_R** — The **tire-to-ground contact patches** of the **front wheel** and **rear wheel** respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Angle of Approach (α)** is the angle subtended at the **front tire contact point (T_F)** by the line joining **T_F to Point F**, measured **above the horizontal ground line**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Angle of Departure (β)** is the angle subtended at the **rear tire contact point (T_R)** by the line joining **T_R to Point R**, measured **above the horizontal ground line**.

### Logic Steps (How the Bus Negotiates a Ramp)

- **Step 1:** As the bus starts to climb a ramp of inclination **θ**, the **front wheel** rolls onto the ramp first.
- **Step 2:** The bus body pivots virtually about the **front tire contact point T_F** as the front wheel rises.
- **Step 3:** The **lowest front body point (F)** moves on a circular arc of radius **L_f** (the horizontal front overhang distance).
- **Step 4:** Point F will **clear the ramp edge** as long as the **perpendicular distance from F to the ramp surface** remains positive.
- **Step 5:** The **limiting case** (where F just grazes the ramp) defines the maximum approach angle. Beyond this, the body scrapes.
- **Step 6:** An identical logic applies at the rear for the departure angle, with the pivot being the **rear tire contact point T_R**.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{h_f}{L_f}$ | Approach angle: ratio of front bumper ground clearance to horizontal front overhang | dimensionless | $\alpha \in [7°, 30°]$ |
| 2 | $\tan(\beta) = \dfrac{h_r}{L_r}$ | Departure angle: ratio of rear bumper ground clearance to horizontal rear overhang | dimensionless | $\beta \in [7°, 25°]$ |
| 3 | $\alpha = \arctan\left(\dfrac{h_f}{L_f}\right)$ | Explicit angle of approach | **degrees** | — |
| 4 | $\beta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{h_r}{L_r}\right)$ | Explicit angle of departure | **degrees** | — |
| 5 | $L_f = \dfrac{h_f}{\tan(\alpha)}$ | Required front overhang for a given target approach angle | **mm** | — |
| 6 | $L_r = \dfrac{h_r}{\tan(\beta)}$ | Required rear overhang for a given target departure angle | **mm** | — |
| 7 | $h_f = L_f \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Required front ground clearance for a target approach angle | **mm** | — |
| 8 | $h_r = L_r \cdot \tan(\beta)$ | Required rear ground clearance for a target departure angle | **mm** | — |
| 9 | $\theta_{ramp,max} = \min(\alpha, \beta, \theta_{grade,max})$ | Maximum safe ramp angle the bus can negotiate | **degrees** | — |

**Where:**

- $h_f$ = Vertical ground clearance at the **front bumper** (mm)
- $h_r$ = Vertical ground clearance at the **rear bumper** (mm)
- $L_f$ = Horizontal distance from the **front tire contact patch** to the **front bumper**'s lowest point (mm)
- $L_r$ = Horizontal distance from the **rear tire contact patch** to the **rear bumper**'s lowest point (mm)

### Engineering Utility

- **Body Styling Constraint:** A bus designer cannot arbitrarily extend the front overhang to accommodate more luggage or a more aerodynamic nose — the approach angle is the hard geometric constraint.
- **Trade-off with Luggage Bays:** Long-distance coaches carry underfloor luggage, forcing **rear overhangs** to be long. This **degrades departure angle**, requiring the rear bumper to be **raised** (increasing $h_r$).
- **Off-road Capability:** For **defence and mining buses**, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are deliberately kept above **20°**.
- **Standardisation:** Manufacturers publish $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in **technical specification brochures** as a **legal declaration** of vehicle gradeability.

> [!NOTE]
> The two angles are **independent parameters** in design — they are not directly related to wheelbase or vehicle length, but to the **per-axle overhang and per-axle ground clearance**. This is a common point of confusion in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Angle of Approach (α)

**Assumption:** The bus is treated as a **rigid body** on a **flat horizontal surface**, and we identify the **lowest front body point F** at a height $h_f$ above the ground and a horizontal distance $L_f$ behind the **front tire contact patch** $T_F$.

**Geometric Setup:**

- Place the origin at the **front tire-ground contact point** $T_F$.
- The X-axis is the **horizontal ground reference**.
- The Y-axis is **vertical upward**.
- Point $T_F$ is at $(0, 0)$.
- Point $F$ is at $(-L_f, h_f)$ because it lies *behind* $T_F$ and *above* the ground.

**The "Limiting Ramp" Condition:**

The bus now drives forward onto a ramp inclined at angle $\theta$. The front tire rolls up the ramp. As it does, the bus body (treated as rigid) **pivots** about the instantaneous contact point of the front tire on the ramp.

Just before the body scrapes, the lowest point of the body, $F$, must lie **on or above** the ramp surface.

**Equation of the ramp surface in the bus body reference frame:**

The ramp is a straight line passing through the origin (the tire contact point) with slope $\tan(\theta)$:

$$y_{ramp}(x) = x \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

**The line from $T_F$ to $F$ in the body reference frame:**

The straight line joining the origin $(0, 0)$ to point $F = (-L_f, h_f)$ has the equation:

$$y_{body}(x) = -\dfrac{h_f}{L_f} \cdot x$$

**The angle α between the body's overhang line and the ground:**

The angle that line $T_F F$ makes with the horizontal is given by the arctangent of the slope magnitude:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{h_f}{L_f}$$

**Critical Scraping Condition:**

The bus body just scrapes the ramp when the **body line** and the **ramp line** become **coincident** (parallel) — that is, when the ramp angle θ equals the approach angle α.

$$\theta_{scraping} = \alpha = \arctan\left(\dfrac{h_f}{L_f}\right)$$

**For the bus to clear the ramp safely, the following inequality must hold:**

$$\theta_{ramp} < \alpha$$

$$\theta_{ramp} < \arctan\left(\dfrac{h_f}{L_f}\right)$$

**Final derived formula (boxed for KTU exams):**

$$\boxed{\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h_f}{L_f}\right) \quad \text{[degrees]}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Angle of Departure (β)

By **symmetry of argument**, considering the rear overhang, we obtain:

**Setup:**

- Place the origin at the **rear tire-ground contact point** $T_R$.
- Point $R$ (lowest rear body point) is at $(L_r, h_r)$, lying *ahead* of $T_R$ and *above* the ground.

**The line from $T_R$ to $R$:**

$$y_{body}(x) = \dfrac{h_r}{L_r} \cdot x$$

**Slope = departure angle β with respect to the horizontal:**

$$\tan(\beta) = \dfrac{h_r}{L_r}$$

**Final derived formula (boxed for KTU exams):**

$$\boxed{\beta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h_r}{L_r}\right) \quad \text{[degrees]}}$$

**Departure condition for safe operation:**

$$\theta_{descent} < \beta$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem Statement:**

A state transport bus has the following dimensional data:

- Horizontal front overhang (front tire to front bumper): $L_f = 1850 \text{ mm}$
- Horizontal rear overhang (rear tire to rear bumper): $L_r = 2200 \text{ mm}$
- Front bumper ground clearance: $h_f = 480 \text{ mm}$
- Rear bumper ground clearance: $h_r = 410 \text{ mm}$

**Required:** Calculate the angle of approach (α) and the angle of departure (β). Comment on whether the bus can safely climb a **12° ramp** without scraping.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the Angle of Approach (α)**

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{h_f}{L_f} = \dfrac{480}{1850}$$

$$\tan(\alpha) = 0.259459$$

Apply the arctangent:

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.259459)$$

$$\alpha = 14.535°$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Angle of Departure (β)**

$$\tan(\beta) = \dfrac{h_r}{L_r} = \dfrac{410}{2200}$$

$$\tan(\beta) = 0.186364$$

Apply the arctangent:

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.186364)$$

$$\beta = 10.561°$$

**Step 3: Comparison with the 12° Ramp**

For the **front (approach)**: $\alpha = 14.54° > 12°$ → The bus **CAN climb** without the front scraping.

For the **rear (departure)**: $\beta = 10.56° < 12°$ → The bus **CANNOT descend** the 12° ramp backward without the rear bumper scraping.

**Step 4: Final Engineering Conclusion**

| Parameter | Value | Safe for 12° ramp? |
|---|---|---|
| Angle of Approach (α) | **14.54°** | ✅ **Yes** (margin of 2.54°) |
| Angle of Departure (β) | **10.56°** | ❌ **No** (deficit of 1.44°) |
| Governing angle | **β = 10.56°** | ❌ Bus **must not** attempt a 12° rear-tilted maneuver |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** To make the bus compliant with a 12° ramp, either the **rear overhang must be reduced** to $L_r \leq 1927 \text{ mm}$ or the **rear ground clearance must be increased** to $h_r \geq 467 \text{ mm}$. Both are non-trivial chassis-level design changes.

### 3.4 Dimensional Consistency Check

For the derived formula $\tan(\alpha) = h_f / L_f$:

- $h_f$ has units of **mm**
- $L_f$ has units of **mm**
- Ratio is **dimensionless** ✓
- Arctangent of a dimensionless quantity yields an **angle** (radians or degrees) ✓

The formula is **dimensionally homogeneous** and physically valid.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Side Elevation Reference Diagram (Mermaid Block Functional Flow)

Because Mermaid cannot render true scaled side-elevation drawings of a bus, the following **block-level functional architecture flow** describes the geometry and constraint logic of the approach and departure angles in a manner directly useful for KTU exam sketches.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Ground["Horizontal Ground Reference Plane"]
    WheelF["Front Tire Contact Patch T_F at Origin 0,0"]
    WheelR["Rear Tire Contact Patch T_R at L_wheelbase, 0"]
    FrontBumper["Lowest Front Body Point F at minus L_f comma h_f"]
    RearBumper["Lowest Rear Body Point R at plus L_r comma h_r"]
    LineAlpha["Line T_F to F makes angle alpha above horizontal"]
    LineBeta["Line T_R to R makes angle beta above horizontal"]
    RampA["Imaginary Front Ramp inclined at theta approaching bus"]
    RampB["Imaginary Rear Ramp inclined at theta descending bus"]
    ConditionA["Condition Safe if theta less than alpha"]
    ConditionB["Condition Safe if theta less than beta"]
    ResultSafe["Bus Negotiable Both Front and Rear"]
    ResultFail["Bus Scraper Risk Identified at Front or Rear"]

    Ground --> WheelF
    Ground --> WheelR
    WheelF --> FrontBumper
    WheelR --> RearBumper
    WheelF --> LineAlpha
    WheelR --> LineBeta
    RampA --> WheelF
    RampB --> WheelR
    LineAlpha --> ConditionA
    LineBeta --> ConditionB
    ConditionA --> ResultSafe
    ConditionA --> ResultFail
    ConditionB --> ResultSafe
    ConditionB --> ResultFail
```

### 4.2 Free-Body Geometric Reference (for Exam Sketch Use)

The following Mermaid representation encodes the **side-elevation geometry** as a labelled schematic block diagram, suitable for reproducing the KTU-standard side-view sketch on the answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["POINT F\nLowest Front Bumper\nminus L_f comma h_f\nBUSBODY"] --> B["POINT T_F\nFront Tire Contact\n0 comma 0\nGROUNDREF"]
    B --> C["POINT T_R\nRear Tire Contact\nL_wb comma 0\nGROUNDREF"]
    C --> D["POINT R\nLowest Rear Bumper\nL_wb plus L_r comma h_r\nBUSBODY"]
    B -. "alpha = taninv h_f divided by L_f" .-> A
    C -. "beta = taninv h_r divided by L_r" .-> D
    A --- E["Bus Roof Line\nchassis upper deck"]
    D --- E
```

### 4.3 Diagram Annotation Key (for Manual Sketch Reproduction)

When drawing the side-elevation in your KTU answer booklet, use the following **labelled components** as a checklist:

- **Ground Line**: A solid horizontal line representing the road.
- **Bus Body Outline**: A simple rectangular outline with a slight front-engine bulge and rear taper (or boxy city-bus profile).
- **Two Wheels**: Two circles on the ground line, separated by the wheelbase.
- **Front Bumper Lowest Point F**: Marked as a small filled square at the bottom-front of the bus.
- **Rear Bumper Lowest Point R**: Marked as a small filled square at the bottom-rear of the bus.
- **Angle α**: Arc symbol from the **horizontal ground line** sweeping up to the **dashed line joining F to the front tire contact patch**.
- **Angle β**: Arc symbol from the **horizontal ground line** sweeping up to the **dashed line joining R to the rear tire contact patch**.
- **Dimensions**: Use leader lines to label $L_f$, $L_r$, $h_f$, $h_r$ on the sketch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** Always draw the **angle arc** between the **ground line** and the **overhang reference line**. Drawing the angle on the **wrong side** (e.g., between the overhang and the bus floor) is a common valuation error that costs 1 to 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the terms *Angle of Approach* and *Angle of Departure* as used in bus body engineering. Mention their typical minimum values as per AIS-052 standards.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Angle of Approach (α)** is the **maximum angle of a ramp**, in the longitudinal vertical plane, that the bus can climb forward **without any part of its front body or bumper scraping the ramp surface**. It is measured between the horizontal ground reference and the line joining the **front tire contact patch** to the **lowest point of the front bumper**.
>
> The **Angle of Departure (β)** is the **maximum angle of a ramp**, in the longitudinal vertical plane, that the bus can descend backward (or climb off forward) **without any part of its rear body or bumper scraping the ramp surface**. It is measured between the horizontal ground reference and the line joining the **rear tire contact patch** to the **lowest point of the rear bumper**.
>
> As per **AIS-052**, the minimum mandated value for **α** is **8°** and for **β** is **8°** for fully-built buses. For ghat-service and off-road buses, higher values (typically 12° to 20°) are recommended.

**Key Concept Tested:** Direct recall of syllabus terminology and Indian standard reference. **[CO1, Bloom Level: Remember]**

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any four geometric/dimensional parameters of a bus body that directly influence the angles of approach and departure.**

**Model Answer:**

The four key dimensional parameters that directly govern the angles of approach and departure are:

1. **Horizontal front overhang ($L_f$)** — the distance from the front tire contact patch to the lowest point of the front bumper.
2. **Horizontal rear overhang ($L_r$)** — the distance from the rear tire contact patch to the lowest point of the rear bumper.
3. **Front bumper ground clearance ($h_f$)** — the vertical height of the front bumper's lowest edge above the road.
4. **Rear bumper ground clearance ($h_r$)** — the vertical height of the rear bumper's lowest edge above the road.

These four parameters enter the design equations as:

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h_f}{L_f}\right) \quad ; \quad \beta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h_r}{L_r}\right)$$

**Key Concept Tested:** Identification of governing design variables. **[CO1, Bloom Level: Understand]**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

#### CHOICE — QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, define **Angle of Approach** and **Angle of Departure** for a bus. Derive the expressions relating these angles to the relevant dimensional parameters of the bus body. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch:** Draw a side-elevation of the bus on a flat ground, label points $F$, $T_F$, $T_R$, $R$, and the angles $\alpha$ and $\beta$ as described in Section 4.3 above.

**Definitions:**

- **Angle of Approach (α):** Angle between the horizontal ground and the line joining the **front tire contact patch** $T_F$ to the **lowest front body point** $F$, measured in the **vertical longitudinal plane**.
- **Angle of Departure (β):** Angle between the horizontal ground and the line joining the **rear tire contact patch** $T_R$ to the **lowest rear body point** $R$, measured in the **vertical longitudinal plane**.

**Derivation of α:**

From the geometry of the right-angled triangle $T_F$ – foot of perpendicular from F – F:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Adjacent side}} = \dfrac{h_f}{L_f}$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h_f}{L_f}\right)$$

**Derivation of β:**

By analogous reasoning for the rear overhang triangle:

$$\tan(\beta) = \dfrac{h_r}{L_r}$$

Therefore:

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h_r}{L_r}\right)$$

**Discussion of significance:**

- A bus with **larger $h_f$** and **smaller $L_f$** has a **larger approach angle**, allowing it to climb steeper ramps.
- A bus with **larger $h_r$** and **smaller $L_r$** has a **larger departure angle**.
- Increasing overhang (for engine cowl or luggage bay) **degrades** the corresponding angle.

**(b)** A luxury intercity coach has a **front overhang** of $1.95 \text{ m}$ and a **front bumper clearance** of $0.52 \text{ m}$. The **rear overhang** is $2.40 \text{ m}$ and the **rear bumper clearance** is $0.38 \text{ m}$.

Calculate:
**(i)** The angle of approach.
**(ii)** The angle of departure.
**(iii)** State whether the coach can safely negotiate a **13° approach ramp** and a **9° departure ramp** as per AIS-052. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

- $L_f = 1950 \text{ mm}$
- $h_f = 520 \text{ mm}$
- $L_r = 2400 \text{ mm}$
- $h_r = 380 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1: Angle of Approach (α)**

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{h_f}{L_f} = \dfrac{520}{1950} = 0.26667$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.26667) = 14.93°$$

**Step 2: Angle of Departure (β)**

$$\tan(\beta) = \dfrac{h_r}{L_r} = \dfrac{380}{2400} = 0.15833$$

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.15833) = 9.00°$$

**Step 3: Compliance Check**

| Criterion | Required | Available | Pass / Fail |
|---|---|---|---|
| Approach ramp (13°) | $\alpha \geq 13°$ | $\alpha = 14.93°$ | ✅ **PASS** (margin 1.93°) |
| Departure ramp (9°) | $\beta \geq 9°$ | $\beta = 9.00°$ | ⚠️ **MARGINAL** (exactly at limit) |

**Conclusion:** The coach **clears the 13° approach ramp safely** with a margin of 1.93°. For the 9° departure ramp, the angle is **exactly at the limit** with **zero margin**, which is not advisable in practice because dynamic effects (brake dip, suspension deflection, passenger load shift) will reduce the effective $\beta$ by an additional **0.5° to 1°**. The bus manufacturer should consider raising the rear bumper clearance by at least **30 mm** to obtain a safety margin.

---

#### CHOICE — QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **geometric significance** of the angle of approach and angle of departure. Discuss **five key factors** that a bus body designer must control to maximise these two angles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Geometric Significance:**

The **angle of approach (α)** and **angle of departure (β)** are *not arbitrary stylistic parameters* — they are **hard geometric constraints** that define the **steepest inclines** a bus can climb or descend without its bodywork striking the ground. They are determined entirely by **two local geometric ratios** at the front and rear axles:

- The **height** of the lowest body protrusion above the road
- The **horizontal distance** of that protrusion from the tyre contact patch

A bus with $\alpha = 8°$ can clear an 8° ramp. A bus with $\alpha = 20°$ can clear a 20° ramp. The angle **defines the operational envelope** of the vehicle.

**Five Key Design Factors to Maximise α and β:**

1. **Minimise the front overhang ($L_f$):** A shorter front overhang means the front bumper is closer to the front axle, which steepens the approach angle. Designers can use **rear-mounted longitudinal engine** layouts (e.g., rear-engine city buses) to free up the front overhang.

2. **Maximise the front bumper ground clearance ($h_f$):** Raising the front bumper increases $\alpha$. However, this is constrained by **passenger access height** (typically 380 to 450 mm) and **stepwell design**.

3. **Minimise the rear overhang ($L_r$):** Reducing $L_r$ steepens the departure angle. In underfloor-engine buses, the engine placement helps shorten $L_r$.

4. **Maximise the rear bumper ground clearance ($h_r$):** Raising the rear bumper helps. Modern intercity coaches use **high-mounted rear lamps and recessed bumpers** to gain 30 to 50 mm of clearance.

5. **Tire size selection:** Larger diameter tyres (e.g., 11R22.5 instead of 9R22.5) raise the entire chassis, which **increases both $h_f$ and $h_r$** simultaneously, improving both α and β.

**Additional Factors (for discussion):** Suspension type (air suspension allows adjustable ride height), chassis frame height, axle location, and the **non-protruding bumper rule** (bumpers must not extend below a stipulated line from the body — AIS-052).

**(b)** A standard **KSRTC city bus** has the following parameters:

- **Wheelbase:** $W = 5200 \text{ mm}$
- **Front overhang:** $L_f = 1700 \text{ mm}$
- **Rear overhang:** $L_r = 2700 \text{ mm}$
- **Front bumper height from ground:** $h_f = 420 \text{ mm}$
- **Rear bumper height from ground:** $h_r = 360 \text{ mm}$

Calculate α and β. If the bus is to be deployed on **ghat roads with 15° ramps**, recommend a **redesign strategy** for the rear end. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate α (Angle of Approach)**

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{h_f}{L_f} = \dfrac{420}{1700} = 0.24706$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.24706) = 13.87°$$

**Step 2: Calculate β (Angle of Departure)**

$$\tan(\beta) = \dfrac{h_r}{L_r} = \dfrac{360}{2700} = 0.13333$$

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.13333) = 7.59°$$

**Step 3: Comparison with Ghat Road Requirement**

| Parameter | Available | Required (ghat, 15°) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | 13.87° | 15° | ❌ Short by 1.13° |
| $\beta$ | 7.59° | 15° | ❌ Short by 7.41° (severe) |

**Step 4: Redesign Strategy for the Rear End**

The rear end is the **critical constraint** — current $\beta = 7.59°$ is far below the 15° requirement.

**Strategy 1 — Reduce Rear Overhang $L_r$:**

The required $L_r$ for $\beta = 15°$ (keeping $h_r = 360 \text{ mm}$):

$$L_{r,new} = \dfrac{h_r}{\tan(15°)} = \dfrac{360}{0.2679} = 1344 \text{ mm}$$

This is a reduction from 2700 mm to 1344 mm — a **drastic 50% reduction** in rear overhang, which may be impractical without re-architecting the rear axle position.

**Strategy 2 — Increase Rear Bumper Clearance $h_r$:**

If $L_r$ must remain at 2700 mm, the required $h_r$ for $\beta = 15°$ is:

$$h_{r,new} = L_r \cdot \tan(15°) = 2700 \times 0.2679 = 723 \text{ mm}$$

A 723 mm rear bumper height is **impractical for passenger entry** and would make the bus unusable for urban service.

**Strategy 3 — Combined Approach (Practical Recommendation):**

Reduce $L_r$ to **1800 mm** (by relocating the rear axle forward and using a more compact rear lamp cluster) and raise $h_r$ to **480 mm** (by fitting a high-mounted recessed bumper):

$$\tan(\beta_{new}) = \dfrac{480}{1800} = 0.2667$$

$$\beta_{new} = \tan^{-1}(0.2667) = 14.93° \approx 15°$$

This **combined redesign** is the **most practical engineering solution**.

**Conclusion:** A single-parameter change cannot achieve the 15° ghat-road target. A **coupled redesign** of both $L_r$ and $h_r$ is required, and the front end must also be raised slightly to maintain balanced performance.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Inverted Definition of α and β:** Many students define α as the angle measured at the *bumper* instead of at the *tire contact patch*. The angle is **always at the tyre** — at the point of pivot. (**−1 mark**)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Specify the Reference Plane:** Always state that α and β are measured **from the horizontal ground reference** in the **vertical longitudinal plane**. (**−1 mark**)
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in the same equation. Convert all dimensions to the **same unit (mm or m)** before substituting. (**−1 to −2 marks**)
>
> 4. **Using a Calculator Value Instead of the Formula:** Examiners award marks for showing the formula $\tan^{-1}(h_f / L_f)$ clearly. Skipping the formula and writing only the numerical result forfeits **2 marks** of method.
>
> 5. **Confusing Approach Angle with Ramp Angle:** The angle of approach is a **vehicle property**. The ramp angle is an **environmental property**. They are compared, not the same. (**−1 mark**)
>
> 6. **Omitting Units in the Final Answer:** Always write "**α = 14.93°**" not just "**14.93**". (**−0.5 mark**)
>
> 7. **Neglecting the Sketch:** A neat side-elevation diagram with labelled points is **mandatory** for full marks in Part B. Skipping the sketch costs **2 to 3 marks** out of 7.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Angle of Approach (α)** is measured at the **front tyre contact patch**, between the **horizontal ground** and the line joining the tyre contact to the **lowest front body point**.
- **Angle of Departure (β)** is measured at the **rear tyre contact patch**, between the **horizontal ground** and the line joining the tyre contact to the **lowest rear body point**.
- The **core formula** is:
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h_f}{L_f}\right) \quad ; \quad \beta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h_r}{L_r}\right)$$
- α and β are **independent** — they depend on **front and rear overhangs and clearances separately**, not on overall bus length.
- **Smaller overhang** + **larger bumper clearance** = **larger approach/departure angle**.
- **Indian Standard AIS-052** mandates a minimum of **α ≥ 8°** and **β ≥ 8°** for fully built buses.
- For **ghat / hill buses**, the typical target is **α, β ≥ 15°**.
- For a bus to clear a ramp of inclination θ safely: $\theta < \alpha$ (front) and $\theta < \beta$ (rear).
- The **limiting ramp angle** the bus can handle is $\min(\alpha, \beta, \text{gradeability of engine})$.
- Always **draw a neat side-elevation sketch** in KTU Part B answers with **all four points F, T_F, T_R, R labelled** and the **two angles α and β** clearly shown as arcs.
- The **formula is dimensionally consistent** — both numerator and denominator have the same length unit, so the ratio is dimensionless and arctangent gives a valid angle.
- **Typical values for an Indian city bus:** $\alpha \in [10°, 16°]$, $\beta \in [8°, 14°]$.
- **Typical values for a ghat-service bus:** $\alpha \in [18°, 25°]$, $\beta \in [15°, 22°]$.
- **Engineering trade-off:** Increasing α or β by raising the bumper makes passenger entry/exit difficult; increasing by reducing overhang reduces luggage bay volume and crumple zone length.
- **Wheelbase does NOT directly appear** in the α or β formulas — but it does affect overall vehicle dynamics, gradeability, and turning radius, which are separate KTU topics.
- The angles α and β are **static geometric parameters** — they do not account for **suspension deflection under load, brake dive, or body roll**, which can each reduce the effective angle by **1° to 2°** under real driving conditions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
