## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Define "Type Approval" in the context of Indian motor vehicle regulations. State the difference between type approval and self-certification.*

**Model Answer:**

**Type Approval** is a *prior-to-market* certification procedure in which a national authority (e.g., MoRTH in India) certifies that a *type* of vehicle (or component) satisfies all applicable technical and administrative requirements of the CMVR, based on testing conducted by a designated *Test Agency* (e.g., ARAI, iCAT, VRDE, CIRT). A single Type Approval Certificate (TAC) covers a production run of conforming vehicles.

**Type Approval vs. Self-Certification:**

| **Aspect** | **Type Approval (India, EU)** | **Self-Certification (USA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Testing | By an independent agency | By the manufacturer |
| Review | By a government committee (TAC) | None prior to market |
| Authority | Government issues the certificate | Manufacturer certifies compliance |
| Change of variant | Re-approval may be needed | Manufacturer's own determination |

India follows a *hybrid* model: a few vehicle categories (e.g., M1) require type approval; others follow self-certification under the **In-Use** and **CoP** regimes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*What is BNVSAP? List the three crash test protocols currently covered by BNVSAP and the corresponding test speeds.*

**Model Answer:**

**BNVSAP** stands for the **Bharat New Vehicle Safety Assessment Program**, also known as **Bharat NCAP**. It is India's *consumer information* crash test program, jointly administered by the **Global NCAP** and **ARAI**, launched in **2023** under the patronage of the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)**. It applies to **M1** category passenger cars manufactured in India.

The three test protocols are:

1. **Frontal Offset Deformable Barrier (ODB) Test:** Vehicle is propelled at **64 km/h** into a deformable barrier with a **40% overlap**.
2. **Side Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) Test:** A deformable trolley of mass **1,400 kg** strikes the stationary vehicle at **50 km/h** at an angle of **90°** to the vehicle's longitudinal axis.
3. **Pole Side Impact Test:** The vehicle is propelled laterally at **29 km/h** into a rigid pole of diameter **254 mm**, simulating a tree-pole crash.

Vehicles are awarded **1 to 5 stars** based on adult occupant protection scores.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the international framework of vehicle safety regulations. Compare the ECE (United Nations), FMVSS (United States) and AIS (India) frameworks with respect to their legislative basis, administering body, scope and adoption mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)** in the Indian homologation process. With the help of a flow diagram, describe the end-to-end Type Approval process for a new M1 passenger car as per **CMVR Rule 126**.

---

**Model Answer (a) — International Framework Comparison [7 Marks]:**

The international framework for vehicle safety regulations has three major pillars, each with distinct legislative and administrative characteristics.

**(i) Legislative Basis:**

- **ECE (UN WP.29) Regulations:** Issued under the **1958 Agreement** of the United Nations World Forum for Harmonization of Vehicle Regulations (WP.29). The 1958 Agreement is an *international treaty* that requires contracting parties to *accept* type approvals issued by the approval authority of any other contracting party. India acceded to the 1958 Agreement in **2006**, which is why E-marked components are acceptable in India.
- **FMVSS:** Codified in **Title 49 of the U.S. Code of Federal Regulations (49 CFR Part 571)**. Issued and enforced by the **National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA)**. The basis is the **National Traffic and Motor Vehicle Safety Act, 1966**.
- **AIS:** *Technical standards* developed by the **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)** under the **CMVR**, 1989. The CMVR is notified under the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988**, which is a *domestic statute* of the Parliament of India.

**(ii) Administering Body:**

- ECE: UNECE **Working Party 29 (WP.29)** + **GR (General Requirements)** sub-groups (e.g., GRSG, GRSP, GRRF, GRE).
- FMVSS: **NHTSA** (under the U.S. Department of Transportation).
- AIS: **ARAI** (industry body, with MoRTH as the policy authority).

**(iii) Scope:**

- ECE R-series: Component-level approvals (e.g., R14 seat-belt anchorages, R43 glazing).
- FMVSS: Predominantly *whole-vehicle* (e.g., FMVSS 208) but also component-level.
- AIS: Both component-level (AIS-001, AIS-008) and vehicle-level (AIS-072, AIS-097).

**(iv) Adoption Mechanism:**

- ECE: Type approval by a *designated approval authority* of a contracting party.
- FMVSS: **Self-certification** — the manufacturer certifies compliance; NHTSA performs *post-market surveillance*.
- AIS: Type approval for certain categories; conformity-of-production audit by ARAI.

---

**Model Answer (b) — Role of ARAI and Type Approval Flow [7 Marks]:**

**Role of ARAI:**

ARAI is a *cooperative research association* established in **1966** by the automotive industry in India, supported by the Government of India and MoRTH. Its functions relevant to body engineering include:

1. **Standards Development:** Drafting AIS standards through *industry working groups*.
2. **Testing:** Operating the **Vehicle Research Laboratory** and **Crash Test Facility** at Pune, with state-of-the-art **sled test, full vehicle crash, and component test** capabilities.
3. **Homologation Services:** Acting as the *test agency* for the purpose of Type Approval, issuing test reports that feed into the TAC's decision.
4. **Type Approval Committee (TAC) Secretariat:** Coordinating with MoRTH and the **Standing Committee on Automotive Standards (SCoAS)**.
5. **Training and Consultancy:** Supporting OEMs in design, prototyping, and CoP.
6. **R&D and Certification for EV / Hydrogen** vehicles under **AIS-156, AIS-100** etc.

**Type Approval Flow for an M1 Passenger Car (per CMVR Rule 126):**

The process comprises the following eight sequential stages:

1. **Identification of applicable standards** (AIS-072, AIS-097, AIS-098, AIS-008, AIS-026, AIS-052 if applicable, etc.).
2. **Preparation of the Compliance Matrix (CM)** mapping each requirement to test method, sample, and acceptance criterion.
3. **Design and CAE validation** (LS-DYNA / RADIOSS crash simulations).
4. **Prototype build and pre-compliance** in-house tests.
5. **Submission of samples and CM to ARAI** (or iCAT / VRDE).
6. **Independent testing** by the agency in accordance with the relevant AIS.
7. **Test reports** are generated and submitted to the **Type Approval Committee (TAC)**.
8. **Issue of TAC by MoRTH** → enables registration by RTO.

Post-approval, the OEM must conduct **Conformity of Production (CoP)** audits (typically **annual**) to ensure production units match the tested specimen.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various **frontal, side and rear impact** crash test protocols as per AIS / ECE / FMVSS. Discuss the **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** and the **3-ms cumulative acceleration** criteria with their typical acceptance limits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to **AIS-052 (Bus Body Code)**, discuss the structural and dimensional requirements for the construction of a Type-III city bus body. Explain the roll-over protection test (ECE R66 equivalent) and its design significance.

---

**Model Answer (a) — Crash Test Protocols and Injury Criteria [7 Marks]:**

**Frontal Impact (AIS-072 / ECE R94 / FMVSS 208):**

- The vehicle is propelled at **56 km/h** into either an **Offset Deformable Barrier (ODB, 40% overlap)** or — in the U.S. case — additionally a **Full-Width Rigid Barrier (FWRB)**.
- Test dummies: **Hybrid III 50th percentile male** (driver & front passenger) and **Hybrid III 5th percentile female** (rear seat in some protocols).
- The barrier is a **deformable honeycomb** of **6 honeycombs** representing the geometry and stiffness of a deformable car front.

**Side Impact (AIS-097 / ECE R95 / FMVSS 214):**

- A **Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB)** of mass **1,300 kg** (AIS-097: 950 kg) strikes the stationary vehicle at **50 km/h**, oriented such that the **front edge of the barrier** contacts the **B-pillar** of the test vehicle.
- The dummy is instrumented to record head, thorax, abdomen, pelvis, and femur loads.

**Pole Side Impact (AIS-184 / GTR 14):**

- The vehicle slides sideways at **32 km/h** (AIS-184: 32 ± 1 km/h) into a rigid vertical pole of **diameter 254 mm**, simulating a tree-pole crash.

**Rear Impact (AIS-098 / FMVSS 301):**

- A **moving barrier** strikes the rear of the stationary vehicle at **35–80 km/h** (protocol-dependent), focusing on fuel system integrity and whiplash prevention.

**Injury Criteria:**

1. **HIC (Head Injury Criterion):** A dimensionless number computed from the resultant head acceleration $a$ (in $g$) over the most critical 15 ms window. The AIS-072 / ECE R94 limit is $\text{HIC}_{15} \leq 700$. Exceeding this correlates with a high risk of severe or fatal head injury (AIS $\geq 4$).
2. **3-ms Cumulative Acceleration:** The maximum average head acceleration sustained over any 3 ms interval, with a limit of **80 g** in AIS-072.
3. **Chest Compression (Thoracic Deflection):** Lateral thorax compression in side impact tests is limited to **≤ 50 mm** to avoid multiple rib fractures.
4. **Femur Load:** Axial compressive force on the femur is limited to **≤ 10 kN** to prevent femur fracture.

---

**Model Answer (b) — AIS-052 Bus Body Code and Rollover Protection [7 Marks]:**

**AIS-052** is the **code of practice for constructional and functional requirements of bus bodies**. It is the *single most important* standard for a body engineer working on buses, coaches, and school buses in India.

**Classification under AIS-052:**

Buses are classified into **Type I (intercity / long-distance)** and **Type II / Type III (urban / city)**. The requirements differ in:

- **Floor height:** Type III low-floor urban buses have a floor height **≤ 650 mm** from the ground (for level boarding at bus stops).
- **Door width:** A minimum of **1,000 mm** clear opening for at least one service door.
- **Seat dimensions:** Seat width ≥ 400 mm, seat depth ≥ 400 mm, seat back height ≥ 700 mm (for high-back seats) or ≥ 450 mm (low-back).
- **Emergency exits:** Minimum **4 emergency exits** in a standard bus, including at least one roof hatch and one rear emergency window.
- **Gangway width:** ≥ 600 mm for non-urban buses and ≥ 850 mm for low-floor urban buses.
- **Structural integrity:** The superstructure must not collapse under the **ECE R66** rollover test load (in AIS-052, this is the *static equivalent* requirement on roof strength).

**Rollover Protection Test (ECE R66 / AIS-052 equivalent):**

The test is *quasi-static* in the AIS-052 framework. A bus body is placed on a tilted platform that is gradually rotated about a longitudinal axis until the body is **inverted (rolled onto its side)**. Alternatively, a **dynamic tilt-table** test rotates the bus to a 90° roll angle and then drops it. The pass criterion is that the **survival space** — the volume surrounding the driver's seat and passenger seats — must not be violated. The body must demonstrate:

- **Roof strength** to prevent crushing of the survival space.
- **Window retention** to prevent ejection.
- **A-pillar and B-pillar strength** to maintain the side structure.

**Design Significance:**

- **Buses have a high centre of gravity** and are therefore rollover-prone. A robust *superstructure* — typically a **tubular steel space frame** welded to the chassis — is essential.
- **ECE R66** prescribes a *quasi-static* side load equivalent to **1.5 times the bus's unladen mass** applied to the roof edge.
- AIS-052 adopts an *equivalent static load* method but accepts **finite element (FE) analysis** as an alternative to the physical test, provided it is *correlated* with at least one physical test.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing AIS with CMVR:** Writing *"AIS is the law"* — it is *not* law. AIS becomes binding only when notified under CMVR.
> 2. **Mixing up barrier types:** Stating *"FMVSS 208 uses a deformable barrier"* is *wrong* — FMVSS 208 uses a **rigid** barrier in its full-width form.
> 3. **Forgetting the units:** Writing *"HIC is 700 m/s²"* is *wrong* — HIC is **dimensionless**.
> 4. **Skipping the dummy:** A frontal crash test *always* uses a **Hybrid III dummy** — examiners expect this keyword.
> 5. **Omitting CoP:** A common omission. The TAC is *not* the end of the road; the **Conformity of Production (CoP)** audit is a *standing* requirement.
> 6. **Ignoring 1958 Agreement:** India accepts E-marks under the **1958 Agreement**; omitting this loses a mark in "international framework" questions.
> 7. **HIC formula not written:** In a 14-mark question on crashworthiness, the **HIC formula** is a *mandatory* item — writing only the limit (700) is insufficient.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- **Regulatory Stack (India):** Constitution → **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** → **CMVR, 1989** → **MoRTH Notifications** → **SCoAS** → **ARAI** → **AIS Standards** → **TAC** → **RTO Registration**.
- **Test Agency Names (memorize the 4):** **ARAI (Pune)**, **iCAT (Manesar, Haryana)**, **VRDE (Ahmednagar)**, **CIRT (Pune)**. Each is a designated Test Agency under CMVR.
- **Key Bus Standard:** **AIS-052** for bus body; **ECE R66** for rollover; **ECE R107** for general construction.
- **Key Car Standards:** **AIS-072** (frontal), **AIS-097** (side), **AIS-098** (rear), **AIS-075** (pedestrian), **AIS-184** (pole side), **AIS-026** (glazing), **AIS-069** (steering column), **AIS-068** (doors).
- **Crash Speeds (memorize):** **56 km/h** frontal, **50 km/h** side, **32 km/h** pole side, **29 km/h** BNVSAP pole, **64 km/h** BNVSAP frontal.
- **HIC Formula:** $\text{HIC} = \left[(t_2 - t_1)^{-1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a\,dt\right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$. **Limit: 700.**
- **3-ms Limit:** **80 g.**
- **Chest Deflection Limit:** **50 mm.**
- **Femur Load Limit:** **10 kN.**
- **Steering Column Axial Collapse:** **11.1 kN.**
- **Door Transverse Load (latched):** **9.0 kN.**
- **Roof Crush (FMVSS 216):** Failure at **≤ 3× vehicle weight** (i.e., load $\geq 1.5 \times W$ on each side must be sustained for **> 5 seconds** before failure).
- **International Frameworks:** **UNECE WP.29** (1958 Agreement + 1998 Agreement), **EU WVTA Regulation 2018/858**, **FMVSS (49 CFR 571)**, **GTR**, **ISO 26262 (Functional Safety)**.
- **BNVSAP Stars:** 1 star (<50%), 2 star (50–65%), 3 star (65–80%), 4 star (80–90%), 5 star (≥90%).
- **CoP is standing:** TAC is *not* a one-shot. **Conformity of Production** must be demonstrated *throughout* the production life.
- **1958 Agreement:** India is a contracting party. **E-marks** are accepted for many components.
- **Type Approval vs. Self-Certification:** India = type approval (for M1, N1) + CoP; USA = self-certification; EU = WVTA.
- **AIS Numbering Convention:** AIS-0xx is the older series (mirrors ECE); AIS-1xx is the modernized series; AIS-2xx onwards covers emerging tech (ADAS, EVs, hydrogen). For example, **AIS-156** = EV traction battery safety.
- **EV-Specific Body Standards:** **AIS-156** (lithium-ion traction battery), **AIS-038** (REESS general), **AIS-039** (REESS vibration), **AIS-040** (REESS thermal shock), **AIS-041** (REESS mechanical impact), **AIS-049** (REESS fire), **AIS-050** (REESS external short circuit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Cue:** When a question asks *"Discuss Industry Standards Affecting Vehicle Body Design,"* always structure your answer in **THREE BLOCKS**: (1) **International Framework** (ECE, FMVSS, GTR, ISO), (2) **Indian Framework** (CMVR + AIS + ARAI + TAC + CoP), and (3) **Body-Engineering Specific Standards** (AIS-072, AIS-097, AIS-098, AIS-184, AIS-075, AIS-052, AIS-026, AIS-068, AIS-069). Closing with a paragraph on **BNVSAP** and the **future of GTRs** (e.g., GTR 20 EV safety, autonomous vehicle regulations under SAE J3016) earns *bonus* examiner goodwill.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Body Materials - Aluminium Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Body Materials - Aluminium Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aluminium Alloys in Vehicle Body Engineering** are engineered metallic materials composed primarily of aluminium (Al ≥ 90%) with deliberate additions of alloying elements such as **Magnesium (Mg)**, **Silicon (Si)**, **Copper (Cu)**, **Zinc (Zn)**, and **Manganese (Mn)** to achieve specific combinations of low density, high strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance, formability, and crash-energy absorption required for modern automotive body-in-white (BIW) construction.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the automobile body material is classified as **lightweight non-ferrous material** under the broader domain of **Vehicle Body Materials & Selection**. The most commonly deployed aluminium alloys for body panels belong to the **5xxx (Al-Mg)** and **6xxx (Al-Mg-Si)** series, with **7xxx (Al-Zn)** alloys used selectively in high-strength structural members.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (Aligned with KTU 2024 OBE framework):**
> - **Body-in-White (BIW):** The stage in automotive manufacturing where the body sheet metal components have been joined together (by welding, riveting, or adhesive bonding) but before painting and final assembly of moving parts (doors, hoods, fenders, etc.).
> - **Lightweighting:** The systematic reduction of vehicle curb mass to improve fuel efficiency, reduce CO₂ emissions, and enhance dynamic performance.
> - **Alloy Designation:** Aluminium alloys are designated by a 4-digit system (e.g., AA 6016) standardized by the **Aluminium Association (AA)** and recognized globally.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Aluminium Alloy as a Reinforced Honeycomb"**
> Imagine you are building a lightweight bridge. Pure aluminium is like a piece of soft chalk — light but weak. Now imagine dipping that chalk into a series of strengthening "shells" (alloying elements). Magnesium wraps the grains to make them slide less easily (giving strength). Silicon forms tiny invisible glass-like particles (Mg₂Si) that act like embedded nails (precipitation hardening). Zinc creates ultra-strong interlocking crystals. Together, these create a **metal "honeycomb"** that is roughly **three times lighter than steel yet strong enough to carry passengers safely in a crash**. This is precisely why modern luxury cars like the Audi A8, Jaguar XJ, and Ford F-150 use extensive aluminium body structures — they shed weight without sacrificing the safety cage.

## 1.3 Why Aluminium Alloys? — The Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mandatory Engineering Drivers (High-Yield for KTU ESE):**
> 1. **Mass Reduction:** Density of Al ≈ **2.7 g/cm³** vs Steel ≈ **7.85 g/cm³** → ~**65% mass saving** for equivalent volume.
> 2. **Emission Compliance:** Every **100 kg** of mass reduction improves fuel economy by approximately **0.4–0.5 L/100 km** and reduces CO₂ emissions by ~**10 g/km**.
> 3. **Crash Performance:** Aluminium's higher specific energy absorption (per kg) makes it superior in crumple zone design when properly engineered.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

| Property | Aluminium Alloy (Typical 6xxx) | Mild Steel (DC04) |
|---|---|---|
| Density (ρ) | **2.70 g/cm³** | **7.85 g/cm³** |
| Young's Modulus (E) | **70 GPa** | **210 GPa** |
| Yield Strength (σ_y) | **120–350 MPa** | **140–300 MPa** |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u) | **220–400 MPa** | **270–410 MPa** |
| Elongation (A%) | **25–35 %** | **30–42 %** |
| Specific Strength (σ_y/ρ) | **44–130 kNm/kg** | **18–38 kNm/kg** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Aluminium Alloys for Automotive Bodies

Aluminium alloys are classified into **two broad categories** based on their response to heat treatment:

### A. Heat-Treatable Alloys (Precipitation Hardenable)
These alloys gain strength through **controlled precipitation** of intermetallic phases during artificial aging (T6 temper).

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Automotive Application |
|---|---|---|
| **2xxx** | Cu | Limited body use; aerospace legacy |
| **6xxx** | Mg + Si (forms Mg₂Si) | **Body panels (hoods, doors, fenders)** — most widely used |
| **7xxx** | Zn | High-strength structural members, B-pillars |

> [!NOTE]
> **6xxx Series — The Workhorse of Vehicle Body Engineering:**
> - **AA 6016-T4/T6:** Outer body panels (Audi A8, Jaguar XJ)
> - **AA 6111-T4:** Hoods, deck lids (North American vehicles)
> - **AA 6022-T6:** Closure panels (doors, lift gates)
> - **AA 6061-T6:** Extruded structural components
> - **AA 6082-T6:** Crash management systems

### B. Non-Heat-Treatable Alloys (Strain Hardenable)
Strength is achieved through **cold working** (rolling, stretching).

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Automotive Application |
|---|---|---|
| **1xxx** | Pure Al (≥99%) | Heat shields, decorative trim |
| **3xxx** | Mn | Heat exchangers, radiators |
| **4xxx** | Si | Brazing sheet, filler wires |
| **5xxx** | Mg | **Inner body panels, floors, structural panels** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5xxx Series — The Formability Champion:**
> Alloys like **AA 5182**, **AA 5754**, and **AA 5083** are renowned for their **excellent deep-drawing characteristics** and are typically used for **inner panels** that are not visible (e.g., floor pans, wheelhouse inner panels, dashboard reinforcements).

## 2.2 Designation System (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The **Aluminium Association (AA) Four-Digit Designation:**

$$
\text{AA} \; \underbrace{X}_{\text{Series}} \; \underbrace{XX}_{\text{Alloy variation}} \; - \; \underbrace{TXX}_{\text{Temper}}
$$

- **First digit (X):** Principal alloying element group (1–9)
- **Second digit (X):** Alloy modification (0 = original, 1–9 = variations)
- **Third & fourth digits (XX):** Specific alloy within the series
- **Temper suffix (TXX):** Mechanical/thermal treatment history

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **AA 6016-T4** = 6xxx series (Mg+Si) → alloy 016 → Solution heat-treated and naturally aged (T4 temper).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all **mathematically verifiable** formulas required for solving Module-2 numerical/proof-based questions in KTU ESE.

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass reduction (m) | $m = V \cdot \rho_{Al}$ | kg | $V$ = volume, $\rho_{Al}$ = 2700 kg/m³ |
| 2 | Percentage mass saving | $\Delta m\% = \dfrac{\rho_{steel} - \rho_{Al}}{\rho_{steel}} \times 100$ | % | ≈ 65.6% for full replacement |
| 3 | Specific strength | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | kNm/kg or Pam³/kg | Used for material selection |
| 4 | Stiffness-equivalent thickness | $t_{Al} = t_{steel} \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{E_{steel}}{E_{Al}}} = t_{steel} \cdot \sqrt[3]{3}$ | mm | To match bending stiffness |
| 5 | Springback ratio (angle) | $K_\theta = \dfrac{\theta' - \theta}{\theta}$ | dimensionless | $\theta'$ = unloaded, $\theta$ = loaded |
| 6 | Springback radius | $\dfrac{1}{\rho'} - \dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | 1/m | $M$ = bending moment, $I$ = 2nd moment of area |
| 7 | Bending stress (outer fibre) | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Pa | $c$ = distance from neutral axis |
| 8 | Energy absorption (axial crush) | $E_{abs} = \bar{F} \cdot d$ | J | $\bar{F}$ = mean crush force, $d$ = crush distance |
| 9 | Specific energy absorption (SEA) | $SEA = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{m}$ | kJ/kg | Higher SEA = better crash performer per kg |
| 10 | Buckling stress (plate) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | Pa | $k$ = buckling coefficient, $\nu$ = 0.33 for Al |
| 11 | Strain hardening (Hollomon) | $\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$ | Pa | $K$ = strength coefficient, $n$ = strain hardening exponent |
| 12 | Plastic strain ratio (Lankford) | $r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_w}{\varepsilon_t}$ | dimensionless | $r > 1$ → good drawability |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not abstract — they directly govern:

- **Springback (Eq. 5 & 6):** Aluminium's lower modulus (70 GPa) causes **~3× more springback** than steel, demanding compensated tooling geometry in stamping dies.
- **Stiffness-Equivalent Thickness (Eq. 4):** When replacing a 1 mm steel panel, an aluminium panel must be **~1.44 mm** thick to match the same flexural rigidity. This partially offsets the mass benefit.
- **Energy Absorption (Eq. 8 & 9):** Aluminium crumple tubes exhibit **SEA of 25–40 kJ/kg**, comparable to advanced high-strength steel (AHSS), validating its use in crash zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Springback Angle for a Pure-Bending Strip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is springback critical in aluminium body forming?**
> Because aluminium's elastic modulus is **3× lower** than steel, springback in aluminium stamping is **~3× larger**, leading to dimensional inaccuracies if not compensated.

**Problem Statement:** A 6016-T4 aluminium strip of width $b$, thickness $t$, and length $L$ is bent over a die radius $R_i$ (inner radius). Determine the springback angle $\Delta\theta$ when the bending moment is released.

### Step 1: Geometry of Pure Bending

For pure bending, the neutral axis radius $\rho_n$ and the inner fibre strain is:

$$
\varepsilon_{inner} = \dfrac{(\rho_n - t/2) - \rho_n}{\rho_n} = -\dfrac{t}{2\rho_n}
$$

### Step 2: Stress Distribution (Elastic-Perfectly Plastic Assumption)

The maximum elastic strain at the outer fibre (radius $\rho_n + t/2$) follows Hooke's law:

$$
\sigma_y = E \cdot \varepsilon_{y,max} = E \cdot \dfrac{t}{2\rho_n}
$$

Solving for the neutral axis radius at first yield:

$$
\rho_{n,yield} = \dfrac{E \cdot t}{2\sigma_y}
$$

### Step 3: Unloaded Curvature via Moment-Curvature Relation

When the bending moment $M$ is removed, the elastic portion recovers. Using the classical beam theory, the unloaded curvature change is:

$$
\dfrac{1}{\rho'} - \dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{M}{E \cdot I}
$$

Where:
- $I$ = second moment of area per unit width $= t^3 / 12$ (per unit width)
- $\rho$ = bent (tool) radius
- $\rho'$ = sprung-back radius

### Step 4: Springback Angle Formula

The springback angle for a length $L$ is:

$$
\Delta\theta = L \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho'}\right)
$$

Substituting the elastic recovery relation:

$$
\Delta\theta = \dfrac{12 \cdot M \cdot L}{E \cdot t^3}
$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Given:** AA 6016-T4 panel, $t = 1.2$ mm, $L = 800$ mm, $E = 70$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 180$ MPa, bend radius $R_i = 4$ mm.
> **Find:** Springback angle.

**Solution Path:**

$$
\rho_n \approx R_i + \dfrac{t}{2} = 4 + 0.6 = 4.6 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{4.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 217.39 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

For a strip elastically bent, the released moment per unit width using $\sigma_y = M \cdot c / I$:

$$
M = \sigma_y \cdot \dfrac{t^2}{6} = 180 \times 10^6 \cdot \dfrac{(1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{6} = 43.2 \text{ Nm/m}
$$

The change in curvature:

$$
\Delta\kappa = \dfrac{M}{EI} = \dfrac{43.2}{(70 \times 10^9) \cdot (1.44 \times 10^{-9})} = 0.4286 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

Springback angle:

$$
\Delta\theta = 0.4286 \times 0.800 = 0.343 \text{ rad} \approx 19.6^\circ
$$

**Conclusion:** A 19.6° springback is **3× higher** than a comparable steel panel (~6°). Tool designers must **overbend** the die by ~20° to achieve the final target geometry.

## 3.2 Derivation — Stiffness-Equivalent Thickness

When replacing a steel panel of thickness $t_s$ with aluminium, to maintain the same **flexural rigidity** $D = \dfrac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$:

$$
D_{steel} = D_{Al} \implies E_s t_s^3 = E_{Al} t_{Al}^3
$$

Solving for $t_{Al}$:

$$
t_{Al} = t_s \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{E_s}{E_{Al}}} = t_s \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{210}{70}} = t_s \cdot \sqrt[3]{3}
$$

$$
t_{Al} = 1.4422 \cdot t_s
$$

**Interpretation:** A 1.0 mm steel panel must be replaced by a **1.44 mm aluminium panel** for equivalent stiffness — not 1.0 mm. This is a classic KTU numerical problem!

## 3.3 Derivation — Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) Under Axial Crushing

For an axially crushed thin-walled aluminium tube of mass $m$ and crush distance $d$:

**Crush Force Mean:**

$$
\bar{F} = \dfrac{1}{d}\int_0^d F(x) \, dx
$$

**Energy Absorbed:**

$$
E_{abs} = \bar{F} \cdot d
$$

**Specific Energy Absorption:**

$$
SEA = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{m} = \dfrac{\bar{F} \cdot d}{m}
$$

For an aluminium crash-box of mean crush force $\bar{F} = 80$ kN, $d = 0.3$ m, $m = 2.5$ kg:

$$
E_{abs} = 80{,}000 \times 0.3 = 24{,}000 \text{ J} = 24 \text{ kJ}
$$

$$
SEA = \dfrac{24{,}000}{2.5} = 9600 \text{ J/kg} = 9.6 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (≈10 kJ/kg) is **competitive with AHSS crash-boxes**, confirming aluminium's suitability for **crumple-zone design**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Alloy Selection Logic for Body Components

| Component | Recommended Alloy | Temper | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bonnet (Hood) Outer | **AA 6016** | T4 / T6 | High surface quality, age-hardenable for dent resistance |
| Bonnet Inner | **AA 5182** | O | Excellent formability for deep draws |
| Door Outer | **AA 6022 / 6016** | T6 | Hemming capability, paint-bake response |
| Door Inner | **AA 5182 / 5754** | O | Deep drawing, structural reinforcement |
| Fender | **AA 6016** | T4 | Stretch formability + dent resistance |
| Floor Pan | **AA 5754 / 5182** | O / H32 | High ductility, weldability |
| B-Pillar Reinforcement | **AA 7075** | T6 | Ultra-high strength (570 MPa UTS) |
| Crush Box (Front Rail) | **AA 6061 / 6082** | T6 | Predictable buckling, high SEA |
| Battery Enclosure (EV) | **AA 6061 / 6005** | T6 / T5 | Extrudability, crashworthiness |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
