# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
**Q:** Define **alloy steel**. List any **four alloying elements** commonly added to steel and state **one specific function** of each.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** An *alloy steel* is a ferrous alloy in which one or more elements other than carbon (and the standard residuals Mn, Si, S, P) are added in significant, deliberate proportions to obtain specific mechanical, physical, or chemical properties.

| Element | Function |
|---|---|
| **Chromium (Cr)** | Forms hard carbides; provides corrosion resistance via passive $Cr_2O_3$ film. |
| **Nickel (Ni)** | Stabilises austenite; improves low-temperature toughness and impact resistance. |
| **Molybdenum (Mo)** | Enhances hot-strength; reduces temper-embrittlement; deep-hardening. |
| **Vanadium (V)** | Forms very hard, stable carbides; refines grain size (HSLA). |

*(Any four with one function each = 3 marks under KTU valuation scheme: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the table.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
**Q:** What is the **IIW Carbon Equivalent (CE)** formula? What is the **significance** of CE in vehicle body welding?

**Model Answer:**

> **Formula:**
> 
> $$CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

> **Significance:**
> 1. CE predicts the **cold-cracking susceptibility** of a steel weld joint.
> 2. It guides the **pre-heat and interpass temperature** selection.
> 3. For arc welding without pre-heating: $CE \leq 0.40$.
> 4. Mandatory pre-heat zone: $CE > 0.45$.
> 5. Critical for **resistance spot welding (RSW)** of automotive body-in-white assemblies where hundreds of welds per vehicle are made.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Classify **alloy steels** in detail. Explain the role of the following alloying elements with their **mechanism of action**:
> *(i) Chromium, (ii) Nickel, (iii) Molybdenum, (iv) Vanadium, (v) Boron.*

> **(b)** [7 Marks] With a **suitable diagram**, explain the **manufacturing route and microstructural evolution** of **Press-Hardened Steel (PHS 22MnB5)** used in automotive safety cages. Why is boron added in such small quantities?

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Classification of Alloy Steels:**

1. **Low-Alloy Steels** — Total alloy content $\leq 5\%$
   - *Examples:* 4140, 4340, HSLA 350
2. **High-Alloy Steels** — Total alloy content $\geq 5\%$
   - *Examples:* Stainless steel, high-speed steel

**Roles of alloying elements:**

| Element | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Cr** | Forms substitutional solid solution in ferrite; forms stable carbides ($Cr_{23}C_6$, $Cr_7C_3$); creates passive $Cr_2O_3$ film for corrosion resistance. |
| **Ni** | Austenite-stabiliser; lowers $M_s$ temperature; improves low-temperature toughness via solid-solution strengthening of ferrite. |
| **Mo** | Strong carbide former; reduces temper-embrittlement by gettering P and Sn to grain boundaries; enhances high-temperature creep strength. |
| **V** | Forms extremely hard, fine VC and V(CN) particles; pins austenite grain boundaries during hot-rolling; contributes to *secondary hardening* on tempering. |
| **B** | Segregates to prior-austenite grain boundaries; suppresses ferrite nucleation; **dramatically increases hardenability** (≈ 1 ppm B = 1% Cr equivalent). |

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Manufacturing route of PHS 22MnB5:** *(refer to the block diagram in Section 4.3)*

1. **Cold-rolled blank** with composition 0.22 C, 1.2 Mn, 0.3 Cr, 0.2 Mo, 0.002–0.005 B is heated to **900–950 °C** in a furnace (austenitisation, ~5 min).
2. The blank is **transferred to a water-cooled die** in 5–7 seconds (to avoid ferrite/pearlite formation).
3. The die is **closed** while the blank is still hot — this *forms* the geometry.
4. The water-cooled die extracts heat at **30–50 °C/s**, transforming austenite to **100% martensite** (the *in-die quenching* step).
5. Final operations: **trimming, piercing, shot-blasting** to remove scale.

**Why boron in trace amounts?**
- Boron is **highly effective at the grain boundaries** — even 10–30 ppm is sufficient to suppress the *nucleation of ferrite* at austenite boundaries.
- Adding more boron is **counter-productive** — it forms brittle **Fe–B compounds (Fe₂B / Fe₂₃(C,B)₆)** that drastically reduce toughness.
- Therefore, **optimum boron** = **0.001–0.003%** — the "Goldilocks zone."

> **Resulting properties:** UTS 1500–2000 MPa, YS ≈ 1100 MPa, used in A-pillar, B-pillar, roof rail, side impact beam.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Hall-Petch strengthening mechanism** in HSLA steels. A steel has $\sigma_0 = 75$ MPa and $k_y = 0.7$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$. Determine the **yield strength** when the ferrite grain diameter is refined to **3 $\mu$m**. What is the **percentage improvement** over a 20 $\mu$m grain structure?

> **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **role of Advanced High-Strength Steels (AHSS)** in modern vehicle body engineering. Compare the microstructures and applications of **DP, TRIP, and PHS** grades with suitable property tables.

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Hall-Petch mechanism:** The yield strength of a polycrystalline metal increases with the *inverse square root of the average grain diameter* because grain boundaries act as *barriers to dislocation motion*. In HSLA steels, micro-alloying elements like **Nb, V, and Ti** form fine carbides/nitrides that **pin** austenite grain boundaries during hot rolling, resulting in very fine ferrite grains on cooling.

**Calculation:**

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

**For d₁ = 3 $\mu$m:**

$$
\sigma_y(d_1) = 75 + 0.7 \times (3 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2}
$$

$$
= 75 + 0.7 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{3 \times 10^{-6}}} = 75 + 0.7 \times 577.35
$$

$$
\sigma_y(d_1) = 75 + 404.15 = 479.15~\text{MPa}
$$

**For d₂ = 20 $\mu$m:**

$$
\sigma_y(d_2) = 75 + 0.7 \times (20 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2}
$$

$$
= 75 + 0.7 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{20 \times 10^{-6}}} = 75 + 0.7 \times 223.6
$$

$$
\sigma_y(d_2) = 75 + 156.52 = 231.52~\text{MPa}
$$

**Percentage improvement:**

$$
\%~\text{improvement} = \frac{479.15 - 231.52}{231.52} \times 100 = 106.96\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** Grain refinement alone **more than doubles** the yield strength. This explains why micro-alloyed HSLA steels dominate *cost-sensitive* automotive structural applications.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Role of AHSS in vehicle body engineering:**

Modern passenger cars must satisfy **three conflicting requirements**:
1. **Light-weighting** (for fuel efficiency and EV range).
2. **Crashworthiness** (FMVSS 216, Euro NCAP).
3. **Affordability** (mass-market production).

**AHSS uniquely satisfies all three** by combining high strength with reasonable formability, enabling thinner gauges without compromising safety.

**Comparison Table (DP / TRIP / PHS):**

| Parameter | DP 780 | TRIP 780 | PHS 1500 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microstructure** | Ferrite (soft) + Martensite islands (hard) | Ferrite + Bainite + Retained Austenite | 100% Martensite |
| **Mechanism** | Hard martensite carries load; soft ferrite gives elongation | Strain-induced transformation of retained $\gamma \to$ martensite (TRIP effect) | Die-quenched from austenite |
| **UTS (MPa)** | 780 | 780 | 1500 |
| **YS (MPa)** | 450 | 450 | 1100 |
| **% Elongation** | 14 | 22 | 6 |
| **Formability** | Good (laser-weldable) | Very good (best of three) | Poor (must be hot-formed) |
| **Crash Role** | Energy absorption | Intrusion resistance | Intrusion barrier (max strength) |
| **Vehicle Zone** | Front rails, floor | B-pillar reinforcements | A/B-pillars, roof rails, sills |

> **Crash Energy Philosophy:** *Use TRIP/DP where you want progressive collapse; use PHS where you want absolute intrusion resistance.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2, Alloy Steels:**
> 1. **Confusing carbon as an alloying element** (carbon is the principal hardener, *not* an alloying addition by convention).
> 2. **Writing vague "improves properties"** without naming the *specific mechanism* (carbide formation vs. solid solution vs. grain refinement).
> 3. **Forgetting units** in Hall-Petch and hardenability calculations (always write MPa, m$^{-1/2}$, mm).
> 4. **Omitting the application column** — KTU board examiners award marks when the answer is *contextualised* to vehicle body engineering.
> 5. **Mixing up austenite-stabilisers (Ni, Mn) and ferrite-stabilisers (Cr, Mo, Si)** in classification questions.
> 6. **Writing the CE formula with `/` instead of fractions** — always use the **fractional form** for clarity in the answer script.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the KTU exam:**

- **Definition:** Alloy steel = Fe + C + **deliberate alloying addition** $\geq 1\%$ (low) or $\geq 5\%$ (high).
- **Four strengthening mechanisms:** Solid solution, Carbide dispersion, Hall-Petch grain refinement, Phase balance.
- **Austenite stabilisers:** **Ni, Mn, Cu, C, N** (expand $\gamma$-field).
- **Ferrite stabilisers:** **Cr, Mo, Si, V, Ti, W, B** (contract $\gamma$-field).
- **Boron trick:** 1 ppm B ≈ 1% Cr for hardenability; 22MnB5 = standard PHS grade.
- **Stainless steel rule:** **Cr $\geq 10.5\%$** → passive $Cr_2O_3$ film.
- **CE threshold:** $CE > 0.45$ → mandatory pre-heat before welding.
- **HSLA secret:** Nb, V, Ti micro-alloying + controlled rolling = *cheap strength*.
- **AHSS family acronym:** **D**ual **P**hase, **TRIP**, **C**omplex **P**hase, **M**artensitic, **P**ress **H**ardened.
- **Formability metric:** $\bar{r} \geq 2.0$ for deep-drawing IF grades.
- **Hall-Petch formula:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ — *grain refinement is free strength*.
- **Martensite start temp (Andrews):** $M_s = 539 - 423C - 30.4Mn - 17.7Ni - 12.1Cr - 7.5Mo$.
- **Modern BIW fact:** **55–75% AHSS by mass** in contemporary passenger vehicles.
- **Most-quoted KTU numbers:** 22MnB5 (PHS), 4140 (Cr-Mo alloy), AISI 304 (austenitic SS), HSLA 350 (BIW).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plastics** are defined as a broad class of **synthetic or semi-synthetic organic polymers** that are malleable, ductile, and can be molded into solid objects of diverse shapes and sizes. In the context of **Vehicle Body Engineering**, plastics refer to a family of high-performance polymeric materials (often reinforced with fillers, fibers, or elastomers) that are engineered to substitute or complement traditional metals (steel, aluminium) in both **structural** and **non-structural** automotive applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A plastic is a high-molecular-weight organic polymer (M.W. > 10,000 g/mol) compounded with additives such as plasticizers, stabilizers, fillers, and reinforcing agents to achieve desired mechanical, thermal, and aesthetic properties for automotive body applications.

### Standard Classification Metric

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Density ($\rho$)** | **0.85 – 1.80 g/cm³** | $\approx$ 5–8× lighter than mild steel |
| **Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$)** | **20 – 200 MPa** | Lower than metals, but high strength-to-weight ratio |
| **Glass Transition Temp ($T_g$)** | **80 – 200 °C** | Defines upper service limit |
| **Young's Modulus ($E$)** | **0.5 – 10 GPa** | Significantly lower than metals |
| **Specific Strength ($\sigma_u / \rho$)** | **15 – 120 kNm/kg** | Comparable to or higher than steel |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Molecular Spaghetti Bowl"**
> Think of a plastic as a **bowl of cooked spaghetti** trapped inside a sealed container. Each noodle represents a long polymer chain. If the noodles are just **tangled loosely** (think: cooked spaghetti), they slide past each other easily — this is a **thermoplastic**, which you can re-melt. If, however, the noodles are glued at countless junction points to form a single 3D web, the structure becomes rigid and **cannot be remelted** — this is a **thermoset plastic**, like a hardened epoxy. In a car dashboard, the soft, leather-like skin is a thermoplastic elastomer (loosely tangled), while the hardened rigid dashboard frame is a thermoset composite (frozen web).

### Why Plastics Matter in a Modern Vehicle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Motivation:** For every **100 kg** of plastic substituted for steel in a vehicle, the **fuel economy improves by ~0.5 L/100 km** and **CO₂ emissions drop by ~10 g/km**. This is the central reason plastics now constitute **~15–20% of a modern car's mass** (vs. <5% in the 1970s).

---

## 1.3 The Polymer Backbone — What Makes a Plastic "Plastic"?

A plastic is built from repeating small molecules called **monomers** linked into long **macromolecular chains**. The degree of polymerization ($DP$) and the molecular architecture dictate the bulk properties.

$$
n \cdot M_{\text{monomer}} \;\longrightarrow\; -(M_{\text{monomer}})_n-
$$

Where:
- $n$ = degree of polymerization (typically **10³ to 10⁵**)
- $M_{\text{monomer}}$ = molecular weight of the repeating unit (g/mol)

The key is that these long chains are held together by **secondary bonds** (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding) rather than primary covalent bonds, which is why plastics can deform, melt, and be remolded.

---

## 1.4 Two Grand Divisions of Plastics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** "Differentiate between thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics with examples used in vehicle bodies." — *This question appears almost every year.*

| Property | Thermoplastics | Thermosetting Plastics (Thermosets) |
|---|---|---|
| **Bonding** | Linear/Branched chains, secondary bonds | Cross-linked 3D network, primary bonds at junctions |
| **Heating Behaviour** | **Softens → melts → re-solidifies** (reversible) | **Decomposes; does NOT melt** (irreversible) |
| **Recyclability** | Easily recyclable | Cannot be remelted (only ground as filler) |
| **Mechanical Properties** | Tough, ductile, impact-resistant | Hard, rigid, brittle, high-temperature resistant |
| **Processing** | Injection molding, blow molding, thermoforming | Compression molding, RIM, resin transfer molding |
| **Vehicle Examples** | PP, ABS, PA (Nylon), PC, PVC, PE | Phenolic resins, Epoxy, Polyurethane (RIM), SMC |
| **Typical $T_g$** | 80 – 150 °C | 150 – 300 °C |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Molecular Architecture — The Hidden Driver of Properties

### 2.1.1 Chain Configuration

Plastics derive their macroscopic properties directly from their **microstructure**. Three key architectural features dominate:

1. **Chain Length (Degree of Polymerization, $DP$):**
   Longer chains = more chain entanglement = higher tensile strength, higher viscosity, and higher melting point.
   $$\sigma_u \;\propto\; (DP)^{0.5}$$
   (For most thermoplastics, this square-root relationship holds up to a critical $DP$.)

2. **Branching:** Side branches (as in **LDPE** vs. **HDPE**) reduce crystallinity and density but increase flexibility and transparency.

3. **Cross-linking Density ($\rho_{cl}$):** In thermosets, increasing cross-link density raises rigidity, $T_g$, and chemical resistance but **lowers elongation at break**.

### 2.1.2 Crystallinity vs. Amorphous Behaviour

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semicrystalline vs. Amorphous Plastics:**
> - **Semicrystalline plastics** (e.g., PP, PA, POM) have ordered regions (**crystallites**) embedded in an amorphous matrix. They exhibit a sharp melting point ($T_m$) and higher chemical resistance.
> - **Amorphous plastics** (e.g., ABS, PC, PMMA) have no long-range order. They soften gradually above $T_g$ and offer better dimensional stability and transparency.

The **degree of crystallinity** ($\chi_c$) directly affects density:
$$
\rho = \chi_c \cdot \rho_c + (1 - \chi_c) \cdot \rho_a
$$
Where $\rho_c$ = crystalline phase density, $\rho_a$ = amorphous phase density.

---

## 2.2 Key Engineering Properties for Vehicle Body Design

### 2.2.1 Specific Strength & Specific Modulus (Why plastics win)

The single most important metric in vehicle body engineering is the **specific property** — the property divided by density.

$$
\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_u}{\rho} \quad [\text{kNm/kg}]
$$

$$
\text{Specific Modulus} = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad [\text{MNm/kg or GPa/(g/cm³)}]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** A Class-A outer body panel made of **SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound)** can match the flexural stiffness of a steel panel at **40–50% lower mass** because the specific stiffness is competitive.

### 2.2.2 Creep Behaviour (Long-term loading)

Plastics **creep** under sustained load even at room temperature — a critical issue for load-bearing plastic parts (e.g., seat frames, bumper brackets).

The creep modulus $E_c(t)$ decays with time:
$$
E_c(t) = \frac{E_0}{1 + \left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^n}
$$
Where $E_0$ = instantaneous modulus, $\tau$ = characteristic relaxation time, $n$ = material constant (typically 0.1 – 0.4).

### 2.2.3 Thermal Expansion

Plastics have a **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** 5–10× higher than steel. This is critical for **class-A panel fit & finish**.

$$
\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$
Typical $\alpha$ values:
- **Steel:** $\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6} \; /\text{K}$
- **Aluminium:** $\alpha \approx 23 \times 10^{-6} \; /\text{K}$
- **PP:** $\alpha \approx 100 - 150 \times 10^{-6} \; /\text{K}$
- **SMC (composite):** $\alpha \approx 12 - 20 \times 10^{-6} \; /\text{K}$ (fiber-tuned to match steel!)

---

## 2.3 Common Plastics Used in Vehicle Bodies — Properties Cheat Sheet

| Plastic | Type | Density (g/cm³) | $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | $T_m$ / $T_g$ (°C) | Key Vehicle Body Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PP (Polypropylene)** | Thermoplastic (semicryst.) | 0.90 | 30 – 40 | 165 / -10 | Bumpers, dashboards, battery trays |
| **ABS** | Thermoplastic (amorphous) | 1.05 | 40 – 55 | / 105 | Interior trims, instrument panels, grilles |
| **PA6 (Nylon 6)** | Thermoplastic (semicryst.) | 1.13 | 70 – 85 | 220 / 50 | Engine covers, radiator tanks, mirror housings |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | Thermoplastic (amorphous) | 1.20 | 60 – 70 | / 145 | Headlamp lenses, sunroofs, panoramic roofs |
| **PMMA (Acrylic)** | Thermoplastic (amorphous) | 1.18 | 70 – 80 | / 105 | Tail-light lenses, light guides |
| **PVC** | Thermoplastic (amorphous) | 1.38 | 50 – 60 | / 80 | Wire harnesses, door/window seals |
| **PU (Polyurethane)** | Thermoset (elastomeric) | 1.05 – 1.25 | 30 – 50 | / -40 to 80 | Seat foam, steering wheels, bumper foams |
| **SMC** | Thermoset composite | 1.75 – 1.95 | 70 – 200 | / >200 | Class-A body panels (hood, fender, decklid) |
| **Phenolic Resin** | Thermoset | 1.30 – 1.45 | 35 – 55 | / >250 | Brake pads, clutch facings, electrical housings |

---

## 2.4 Plastics Manufacturing Processes for Body Components

| Process | Type of Plastic | Body Part Examples | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Injection Molding** | Thermoplastic | Bumpers, dashboards, brackets | High volume, complex geometry |
| **Blow Molding** | Thermoplastic (HDPE/PP) | Fuel tanks, air ducts | Hollow parts |
| **Thermoforming** | Thermoplastic sheet | Door inner panels, trim | Low-cost, large area |
| **Compression Molding** | Thermoset (SMC/BMC) | Hood, fender, decklid (Class-A) | Excellent surface finish |
| **Reaction Injection Molding (RIM)** | Polyurethane (PUR) | Bumpers, fenders, spoilers | Low pressure, large parts |
| **Rotational Molding** | Thermoplastic | Large hollow parts (truck ducts) | Uniform wall thickness |
| **Extrusion** | Thermoplastic | Weather strips, sealing profiles | Continuous cross-section |
| **Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)** | Thermoset composite | Structural panels, leaf springs | Fiber-reinforced, high strength |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case Study — Front Bumper System (PP + EPDM + PUR foam):**
> A modern car bumper is a **multi-material sandwich**:
> - **Outer skin:** PP/EPDM (tough, paintable, recyclable)
> - **Energy absorber:** PUR foam (EPP or EPS — energy-absorbing)
> - **Reinforcement beam:** Steel or GFRP (SMC) — pedestrian impact and low-speed crashworthiness
> This single assembly can absorb **~10 kJ** of impact energy at 4 km/h with **>50% mass savings** over an all-steel design.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Summary)

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{\text{polymer}} = n \cdot M_{\text{monomer}}$ | $n$ = DP, $M_{\text{monomer}}$ = monomer mass | Mass scaling of polymerization |
| 2 | $\sigma_u \propto (DP)^{0.5}$ | $DP$ = degree of polymerization | Strength control via chain length |
| 3 | $\rho = \chi_c \rho_c + (1-\chi_c)\rho_a$ | $\chi_c$ = crystallinity | Density prediction |
| 4 | $\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = CTE, $\Delta T$ = temp. change | Thermal fit & finish |
| 5 | $E_c(t) = E_0 / [1 + (t/\tau)^n]$ | $t$ = time, $\tau$ = relax. time, $n$ = const. | Long-term creep of plastic parts |
| 6 | $\text{Sp. Strength} = \sigma_u / \rho$ | $\sigma_u$ = ultimate stress, $\rho$ = density | Light-weighting index |
| 7 | $\text{Sp. Stiffness} = E / \rho$ | $E$ = Young's modulus | Light-weighting index |
| 8 | $\sigma_{cr} = K_{Ic} / (\sqrt{\pi a})$ | $K_{Ic}$ = fracture toughness, $a$ = crack length | Crack safety in plastic parts |
| 9 | $E_{\text{composite}} = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | $V_f$ = fiber vol. fraction (RoM) | Composite modulus prediction |
| 10 | $\rho_{\text{panel}} = (\rho_{skin} t_{skin} + \rho_{core} t_{core}) / t_{\text{total}}$ | Sandwich panel density | Sandwich panel mass |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize formulas 6, 7, and 9 — at least one specific-strength/specific-modulus or composite rule-of-mixtures calculation appears in nearly every end-semester paper.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Plastics Lighten Vehicles — The Mass-Savings Equation

**Problem Statement:**
A horizontal Class-A body panel of a passenger car is currently fabricated from **mild steel (MS)** and is to be **redesigned using a glass-fibre-reinforced SMC composite** of equivalent flexural stiffness. Compute the percentage mass reduction.

### Given Data
- Panel dimensions: $L \times b = 1200 \; \text{mm} \times 1000 \; \text{mm}$
- Steel: $E_s = 210 \; \text{GPa}$, $\rho_s = 7.85 \; \text{g/cm}^3$, thickness $t_s = 0.80 \; \text{mm}$
- SMC: $E_{smc} = 12 \; \text{GPa}$ (30% glass by weight), $\rho_{smc} = 1.85 \; \text{g/cm}^3$

### Step 1 — Equate Flexural Rigness

For a thin plate (or beam) of width $b$ and thickness $t$, the **flexural rigidity per unit width** is:
$$
D = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)} \quad \text{(per unit width)}
$$

For equivalent stiffness ($D_s = D_{smc}$), and assuming $\nu_s \approx \nu_{smc} \approx 0.30$:
$$
E_s \cdot t_s^3 = E_{smc} \cdot t_{smc}^3
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the SMC Thickness

$$
t_{smc}^3 = t_s^3 \cdot \frac{E_s}{E_{smc}}
$$

$$
t_{smc}^3 = (0.80)^3 \cdot \frac{210}{12} = 0.512 \cdot 17.5 = 8.96 \; \text{mm}^3
$$

$$
t_{smc} = (8.96)^{1/3} = 2.07 \; \text{mm}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The SMC panel must be **2.07 mm** thick to match the flexural stiffness of a **0.80 mm** steel panel.

### Step 3 — Compute Mass of Each Panel

**Mass of steel panel:**
$$
m_s = \rho_s \cdot L \cdot b \cdot t_s = 7.85 \cdot 120 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.080 = 7536 \; \text{g} = 7.54 \; \text{kg}
$$
*(Note: $L$, $b$ in cm, $t$ in cm)*

**Mass of SMC panel:**
$$
m_{smc} = 1.85 \cdot 120 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.207 = 4595 \; \text{g} = 4.60 \; \text{kg}
$$

### Step 4 — Mass Reduction Percentage

$$
\% \text{ Mass Saved} = \frac{m_s - m_{smc}}{m_s} \times 100 = \frac{7.54 - 4.60}{7.54} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ Mass Saved} = 38.99 \; \% \approx 39 \; \%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** This 39% figure is the *standard textbook answer* expected by examiners. Always show the cube-root computation clearly — examiners award partial marks for correct substitution even if the final cube-root is approximated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures (ROM) for a Fibre-Reinforced Composite

For a unidirectional fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP), assuming **iso-strain** (Voigt model — fibres and matrix deform equally):

### Step 1 — Load Sharing

Total load = Load carried by fibre + Load carried by matrix.
$$
F_{total} = F_f + F_m
$$
Divide by the total area $A = A_f + A_m$:
$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A} + \sigma_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Iso-Strain (compatibility)

Under iso-strain, $\epsilon_c = \epsilon_f = \epsilon_m = \epsilon$. Hooke's law gives $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$. Substituting:

$$
E_c \cdot \epsilon = E_f \cdot \epsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot \epsilon \cdot V_m
$$

### Step 3 — Cancel $\epsilon$ (non-zero) — Final Voigt Result

$$
\boxed{E_c = V_f \cdot E_f + V_m \cdot E_m = V_f E_f + (1 - V_f) E_m}
$$

Similarly, the **density** follows the **rule of mixtures** (mass additive):
$$
\boxed{\rho_c = V_f \cdot \rho_f + (1 - V_f) \cdot \rho_m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example:**
> For a glass-fibre (E-glass) reinforced PP composite with $V_f = 0.30$:
> $E_f = 72 \; \text{GPa}$, $E_m = 1.5 \; \text{GPa}$
> $E_c = 0.30 \cdot 72 + 0.70 \cdot 1.5 = 21.6 + 1.05 = 22.65 \; \text{GPa}$
> $\rho_f = 2.55$, $\rho_m = 0.90$ → $\rho_c = 0.30 \cdot 2.55 + 0.70 \cdot 0.90 = 1.395 \; \text{g/cm}^3$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Material Selection for Body Panels

| Selection Criterion | Steel (MS) | Aluminium (A6011) | SMC (GF + UP) | CFRP (PAN-based) | PP (unfilled) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ (g/cm³) | 7.85 | 2.70 | 1.85 | 1.55 | 0.90 |
| Young's $E$ (GPa) | 210 | 70 | 12 | 130 | 1.5 |
| Specific Stiffness $E/\rho$ | **26.8** | **25.9** | **6.5** | **83.9** | **1.67** |
| Tensile $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | 350 | 290 | 90 | 1500 | 35 |
| Specific Strength | 44.6 | 107.4 | 48.6 | 967.7 | 38.9 |
| Cost (rel. $/kg) | 1.0 | 3.5 | 2.0 | 30+ | 1.1 |
| Recyclability | Excellent | Excellent | Limited (filler use) | Very difficult | Excellent |
| Crash Energy Abs. (per kg) | Medium | Medium | High | High | Very High |
| Best Vehicle Use | Chassis, structure | Hood, panels | Body panels | F1, premium cars | Bumpers, trims |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the matrix:** CFRP wins on every performance metric except cost and recyclability — explaining why it stays in motorsport. SMC is the **commercial sweet-spot** for Class-A panels in volume production. PP is unbeatable for non-structural, ductile, recyclable parts.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: CTE Tailoring in SMC for Class-A Panels

A critical engineering requirement is that the plastic panel's CTE **matches the steel body-in-white (BIW)** to which it is bonded. This is achieved by tuning the fibre volume fraction.

### Composite CTE (Schapery model, simplified for unidirectional fibre):
$$
\alpha_c = \frac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m (1 - V_f)}{E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

### Goal
Set $\alpha_c = \alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \; /\text{K}$

### Data
- E-glass fibre: $\alpha_f = 5 \times 10^{-6} \; /\text{K}$, $E_f = 72 \; \text{GPa}$
- UP (unsaturated polyester) matrix: $\alpha_m = 80 \times 10^{-6} \; /\text{K}$, $E_m = 3.5 \; \text{GPa}$

### Solve for $V_f$

Let $x = V_f$. Then $(1-x)$ = matrix volume fraction.
$$
12 = \frac{5 \cdot 72 \cdot x + 80 \cdot 3.5 \cdot (1-x)}{72 \cdot x + 3.5 \cdot (1-x)}
$$

Multiply through:
$$
12 [72x + 3.5 - 3.5x] = 360x + 280 - 280x
$$

$$
12 [68.5x + 3.5] = 80x + 280
$$

$$
822x + 42 = 80x + 280
$$

$$
742x = 238 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 0.321
$$

$$
\boxed{V_f \approx 0.32 \; (32\%)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** With ~32% glass fibre by volume, the SMC panel's CTE matches that of steel — which is why mass-production SMC body panels (hoods, fenders) can be **flush-mounted and adhesively bonded** to the steel BIW without warping or gaps over the vehicle's 10-year service life.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Plastics in a Typical Passenger Car (Mass Distribution)

```mermaid
graph LR
    carA["TYPICAL PASSENGER CAR PLASTICS USAGE APPROX 15 TO 20 PERCENT OF TOTAL MASS"] --> extZ["EXTERIOR 35 PERCENT"]
    carA --> intZ["INTERIOR 40 PERCENT"]
    carA --> undZ["UNDERHOOD 15 PERCENT"]
    carA --> eleZ["ELECTRICAL AND FLUIDS 10 PERCENT"]

    extZ --> eX1["BUMPER BEAMS PP EPDM TPO"]
    extZ --> eX2["FENDERS HOOD SMC OR PA PP"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics. Give two examples of each used in vehicle body applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Thermoplastics | Thermosetting Plastics |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular structure | Linear/branched chains | 3D cross-linked network |
| Response to heat | Soften, melt, re-solidify (reversible) | Harden permanently; do not melt (irreversible) |
| Recyclability | Easily remelted and reprocessed | Cannot be remelted; ground for filler |
| Mechanical behaviour | Tough, ductile | Hard, rigid, brittle |
| Process | Injection molding, blow molding | Compression molding, RIM |

**Vehicle Body Examples:**
- **Thermoplastics:** PP (bumpers), ABS (interior trims), PC (headlamp lenses), PA6 (engine covers).
- **Thermosets:** SMC (Class-A body panels), Phenolic (brake pads), Polyurethane/RIM (bumper foams), Epoxy (composite leaf springs).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for structural difference, 1 mark for thermal/recyclability difference, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why are plastics increasingly used in modern vehicle body construction? List four key advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Light-weighting:** Plastics are 5–8× lighter than steel, improving fuel economy and reducing CO₂ emissions.
2. **Design flexibility:** Complex 3D shapes, integrated features, and Class-A surface finish are achievable in a single molding operation.
3. **Corrosion resistance:** Plastics do not rust, extending body-panel life and reducing warranty costs.
4. **Energy absorption / safety:** Plastics like PUR foams and PP/EPDM have high specific energy absorption, improving crashworthiness and pedestrian safety.
5. *(Bonus — ½ mark)* Reduced NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) due to damping properties.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per advantage, max 4 points, best 3 awarded.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse "thermoplastic" with "thermoset"** — examiners deduct 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Always state numerical values with units** — "high strength" scores 0; "70–90 MPa" scores full marks.
> 3. **In calculation questions, show the formula FIRST, then substitution, then answer** — partial marks are awarded stepwise.
> 4. **Draw diagrams wherever the question says "explain with sketch"** — unmarked diagrams lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Do not write generic essay-style answers** for "compare" questions — use a **comparison table** for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **injection molding** process for thermoplastic body parts. Discuss the **process parameters** that affect the quality of a molded bumper. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **compression molding** of **Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC)** for Class-A automotive body panels. Compare the properties of SMC with those of mild steel for use in body panels. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Injection Molding Process:**

**Working Principle (with sketch):**

The injection molding machine consists of three main units: (1) **injection unit** (hopper, barrel, screw, heater bands), (2) **clamping unit** (tie bars, platens, mold), and (3) **control unit**.

**Step-by-step process:**
1. **Feeding:** Thermoplastic granules (e.g., PP) are fed from the hopper into the heated barrel.
2. **Melting & Plasticizing:** Heater bands (200–240 °C) and the rotating screw melt the polymer. Shear heating supplements the band heaters.
3. **Injection:** The screw acts as a plunger, forcing the molten polymer (at 100–150 MPa injection pressure) through the nozzle into the closed mold cavity.
4. **Cooling & Solidification:** Water channels in the mold cool the part to below $T_g$ for amorphous or below $T_m$ for semicrystalline plastics. Cooling time is typically 30–60% of cycle time.
5. **Ejection:** The mold opens; ejector pins push the solidified part out.
6. **Trimming & Finishing:** Flash is removed; the part is deflashed, painted, and assembled.

**Key Process Parameters affecting bumper quality:**
- **Melt Temperature:** Affects viscosity, surface finish, and shrinkage.
- **Injection Pressure:** Affects packing density and weld-line strength.
- **Mold Temperature:** Affects crystallinity, surface gloss, and ejection.
- **Cooling Time:** Affects cycle time and warpage.
- **Injection Speed:** Affects weld-line quality and air entrapment.
- **Hold/Pack Pressure:** Compensates for shrinkage (typically 50–80% of injection pressure).

*(4 marks: process diagram + steps; 3 marks: process parameters with engineering reasoning.)*

---

**Part (b) — SMC Compression Molding:**

**Process Description:**
1. **SMC Preparation:** Glass-fibre rovings are chopped (25–50 mm length) and deposited on a resin paste (unsaturated polyester + filler CaCO₃ + styrene monomer + peroxide catalyst). The sandwich is kneaded, rolled, and matured for 24–48 hours to reach a tacky, leather-like B-stage sheet.
2. **Charge Placement:** SMC sheets are cut to charge pattern and placed in the heated mold (typically 140–160 °C).
3. **Mold Closing & Cure:** The press (500–2000 tons capacity) closes at 1–2 mm/s; pressure reaches 5–15 MPa. Curing occurs in 90–180 s.
4. **Ejection & Trimming:** The cured rigid panel is ejected and deflashed.

**Comparison Table: SMC vs Mild Steel for Body Panels:**

| Property | Mild Steel (MS) | SMC (GF + UP, 30% glass) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | 7.85 g/cm³ | 1.85 g/cm³ | **~76% mass reduction** |
| Young's Modulus | 210 GPa | 12 GPa | Lower stiffness — needs thickness compensation |
| Tensile Strength | 350 MPa | 90 MPa | Lower strength — needs design optimization |
| Corrosion Resistance | Poor (paints, galvanizing) | Excellent | Lower warranty cost |
| Ductility | High (20% elongation) | Brittle (1% elongation) | Energy absorption different |
| Tooling Cost | Low (stamping dies) | High (mold + press) | High-volume production required |
| Surface Finish | Excellent (Class-A) | Good to Excellent (in-mold coating) | Comparable with proper formulation |
| CTE | 12 × 10⁻⁶ /K | 12–20 × 10⁻⁶ /K (tunable) | Tunable to match steel |
| Recyclability | Excellent | Limited (filler / pyrolysis) | Sustainability disadvantage |

*(3 marks: SMC process steps; 4 marks: comparison table with engineering implications.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define **specific strength** and **specific stiffness**. A Class-A car hood is currently made of steel with thickness **0.75 mm** and is to be replaced by an SMC composite of equivalent flexural rigidity. Calculate the new SMC thickness. Take: $E_{steel} = 210 \; \text{GPa}$, $E_{SMC} = 14 \; \text{GPa}$, and assume Poisson's ratios are equal. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **applications of plastics in interior and exterior** parts of a passenger car. Explain the **advantages and limitations** of using plastics in vehicle bodies. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Specific Strength** = $\sigma_u / \rho$ — the ultimate tensile strength per unit density (kNm/kg).
- **Specific Stiffness** = $E / \rho$ — the Young's modulus per unit density (GPa·cm³/g or MNm/kg).

These are used in vehicle lightweighting to **compare materials on a mass-normalized basis**.

**Calculation (5 marks):**

For a flat panel under bending, flexural rigidity per unit width:
$$
D = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}
$$

Equating $D_{steel} = D_{SMC}$ and cancelling the common $1/(1 - \nu^2)$ factor:
$$
E_s \cdot t_s^3 = E_{SMC} \cdot t_{SMC}^3
$$

$$
t_{SMC} = t_s \cdot \left(\frac{E_s}{E_{SMC}}\right)^{1/3} = 0.75 \cdot \left(\frac{210}{14}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
t_{SMC} = 0.75 \cdot (15)^{1/3} = 0.75 \cdot 2.466 = \boxed{1.85 \; \text{mm}}
$$

**Mass check:**
- $m_{steel} = 7.85 \cdot 0.75 \times 10^{-3} \cdot A = 5.89 \times 10^{-3} \cdot A$ (kg, with A in m²)
- $m_{SMC} = 1.85 \cdot 1.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot A = 3.42 \times 10^{-3} \cdot A$
- **Mass saved** ≈ (5.89 − 3.42)/5.89 × 100 ≈ **42%**

---

**Part (b):**

**Applications of Plastics in Vehicle Bodies:**

**Interior (4 marks):**
- **Dashboard / Instrument Panel:** PP, ABS, TPE, PC/ABS blends — soft-touch, integrated HVAC ducts.
- **Door Trims:** PP with fabric/PVC lamination.
- **Seat Foams:** Polyurethane (PUR) flexible foam; PP/PET fibres for seat covers.
- **Steering Wheels:** PUR skin over a magnesium/steel insert.
- **Headliner:** PP / PET non-woven sandwich with PUR foam core.
- **Airbag Housings:** PA (Nylon) 6.6 with high impact strength.

**Exterior (3 marks):**
- **Bumpers & Fascias:** PP/EPDM (thermoplastic olefin), TPU.
- **Fenders, Hoods, Decklids (Class-A panels):** SMC, GMT (Glass Mat Thermoplastic), or PP-based nanocomposites.
- **Grilles:** ABS, PC/ABS.
- **Mirror Housings:** PA, PP, ASA.
- **Wheel Arch Liners:** PP-EPDM.
- **Fuel Filler Doors:** PA, PP.
- **Lighting:** PC (headlamp lens), PMMA (tail-light), BMC (lamp housing).

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Weight reduction (5–8× lighter than steel)** → better fuel economy and lower CO₂.
2. **Design freedom** → complex 3D geometries, integrated features, fewer parts.
3. **Corrosion resistance** → no rust, longer life, lower warranty cost.
4. **Crash performance** → high specific energy absorption; pedestrian-friendly bumpers.
5. **Reduced NVH** → better damping than metals.
6. **Lower tooling cost** for complex parts (vs. multi-stage stamping).

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Lower strength and modulus** than metals — not suitable for highly loaded structures.
2. **High CTE (5–10× steel)** — dimensional fit issues; needs fibre reinforcement.
3. **Creep at elevated temperature** — limits long-term load-bearing use.
4. **Recycling difficulty** for thermosets and fibre composites.
5. **UV degradation, thermal aging, and chemical attack** (petrol, oil) → need additives.
6. **Paint and adhesion challenges** — most plastics need special primers and in-mold coatings for Class-A finish.
7. **Higher material cost per kg** for engineering plastics (PA, PC, PBT).

*(Distribution: 2 marks for definitions, 5 marks for thickness calculation, 4 marks for interior apps, 3 marks for exterior apps, 3 marks for advantages, 3 marks for limitations. Examiner-friendly table format recommended.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Plastics in Vehicle Body Engineering**

- ✅ **Definition:** Plastics = high-MW organic polymers (M.W. > 10⁴ g/mol), compounded with additives.
- ✅ **Two grand families:** **Thermoplastics** (reversible, linear/branched) vs. **Thermosets** (irreversible, cross-linked).
- ✅ **Semicrystalline** (PP, PA, POM) have $T_m$; **Amorphous** (ABS, PC, PMMA) have $T_g$.
- ✅ **Five most-used automotive plastics:** PP, ABS, PA (Nylon), PC, PUR — memorize their primary vehicle applications.
- ✅ **SMC** is the **Class-A body panel** material of choice in mass production.
- ✅ **CFRP** is the highest-performance plastic but **cost-prohibitive** for volume cars.
- ✅ **Specific strength** ($\sigma_u / \rho$) and **specific stiffness** ($E / \rho$) are the **lightweighting indices** to use in calculations.
- ✅ **Flexural rigidity equation:** $D = E t^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$ — equating this is the standard "thickness replacement" calculation.
- ✅ **Rule of mixtures (Voigt, iso-strain):** $E_c = V_f E_f + (1-V_f) E_m$ — used for fibre-reinforced composites.
- ✅ **SMC CTE** can be **tuned to match steel** ($\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6} /K$) by adjusting $V_f$ (~30–35%).
- ✅ **Manufacturing processes:** Injection molding (bumper), blow molding (fuel tank), compression molding (SMC panels), RIM (PUR bumper covers), thermoforming (door trims), extrusion (seals).
- ✅ **Multi-material bumper:** PP/EPDM skin + PUR foam core + steel/GFRP beam — typical sandwich for crash energy absorption.
- ✅ **CTE mismatch is THE most common failure mode** in plastic-on-metal body assemblies — always check $\alpha$ compatibility.
- ✅ **Creep** in plastics: $E_c(t) = E_0 / [1 + (t/\tau)^n]$ — important for design verification of long-term load-bearing parts.
- ✅ **Recyclability hierarchy:** Thermoplastics > Thermosets (reused as filler only) > Fibre composites (very difficult).
- ✅ **For KTU:** When a question says "compare," **always use a table**; when it says "explain with neat sketch," **always include a labelled diagram**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Pitfall Alert — Frequently Confused Pairs:**
> 1. **$T_g$ vs. $T_m$** — $T_g$ = glass transition (amorphous); $T_m$ = melting (semicrystalline). Examiners deduct marks if swapped.
> 2. **"Thermoplastic" ≠ "Thermoforming"** — thermoforming is a process; thermoplastic is a material family.
> 3. **"SMC" ≠ "Carbon Fibre"** — SMC uses glass fibres + polyester; CFRP uses carbon fibres + epoxy.
> 4. **"RIM"** uses PUR (a thermoset) — *not* a thermoplastic, despite the "injection" in its name.

topics.title: Metal Matrix Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal Matrix Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Metal Matrix Composite (MMC)** is an engineered composite material consisting of a **metallic matrix** (the continuous phase) reinforced with one or more discontinuous phases (reinforcements) such as ceramics, organic compounds, or intermetallics. The metallic matrix provides ductility and toughness, while the reinforcement provides high strength and stiffness, producing a material with a combination of properties unattainable in either constituent alone.

In the context of **Vehicle Body Engineering**, MMCs are increasingly replacing conventional monolithic metals (steel, aluminium alloys) in body panels, frames, suspension components, brake rotors, drive shafts, and cylinder liners due to their superior **specific strength (strength-to-weight ratio)**, **specific stiffness (modulus-to-weight ratio)**, **wear resistance**, and **dimensional stability at elevated temperatures**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition Note:**
> According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT741 — Vehicle Body Engineering), MMCs are defined as *"Composite materials with a metal or alloy matrix reinforced with ceramic particles, whiskers, or fibres, used in vehicle bodies to achieve high specific strength, stiffness, and wear resistance while reducing weight."*

### 1.1.1 Constituent Phases of an MMC

| Phase | Role | Common Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Matrix** | Continuous phase that binds, transfers load to reinforcement, and provides ductility/toughness | Aluminium (Al), Titanium (Ti), Magnesium (Mg), Copper (Cu), Nickel (Ni) |
| **Reinforcement** | Discontinuous phase that carries the major portion of applied load and provides strength/stiffness | SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C, TiC, TiB₂, Carbon fibre, Al₂O₃ fibre, SiC whiskers |
| **Interface (Interphase)** | The bond region between matrix and reinforcement that governs load transfer | Formed naturally or by chemical surface treatments |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reinforced Concrete" of the Metals World

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Think of an MMC like a **brick wall reinforced with steel bars (rebar) in concrete**:
> - The **concrete** (matrix) is strong in compression but weak in tension. It holds everything together and gives shape.
> - The **steel rebar** (reinforcement) is strong in tension and adds tremendous strength.
> - Together, they form a structure that handles **both** compression and tension — far superior to either material on its own.
>
> In an MMC, the **metal matrix** is your "concrete" (it provides ductility, conducts heat/electricity, and supports the reinforcement), while the **ceramic reinforcement** is your "steel rebar" (it provides the stiffness and strength). The ceramic is normally **brittle** and would shatter on its own, but the metal matrix holds it in place and distributes loads evenly.

## 1.3 Physical & Performance Constants in MMC Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Density of pure aluminium matrix:** $\rho_{Al} \approx 2.7 \ \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Density of silicon carbide (SiC) reinforcement:** $\rho_{SiC} \approx 3.2 \ \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Young's modulus of Al matrix:** $E_{Al} \approx 70 \ \text{GPa}$
> - **Young's modulus of SiC:** $E_{SiC} \approx 410 \ \text{GPa}$
> - **Rule of Mixtures (upper bound, Voigt model):** A theoretical ceiling for MMC stiffness.

## 1.4 Classification of MMCs (KTU Board Favourite)

MMCs are classified based on the **type of reinforcement geometry**, as shown below:

1. **Particle-reinforced MMCs** — Reinforcement is in the form of equiaxed particles (e.g., SiC particles in Al for brake rotors).
2. **Short-fibre / Whisker-reinforced MMCs** — Reinforcement in the form of chopped fibres or whiskers (e.g., Al₂O₃ short fibres in Al).
3. **Continuous-fibre-reinforced MMCs** — Reinforcement in the form of long aligned fibres (e.g., Al₂O₃ fibre-reinforced Al for connecting rods).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse MMCs with **polymer matrix composites (PMCs)**. MMCs have a **metallic** matrix and operate at much higher temperatures (often 300 °C – 600 °C) than PMCs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Science Behind MMCs

The behaviour of an MMC is governed by the interaction between three key elements: the **matrix**, the **reinforcement**, and the **interfacial bond**. Understanding this interaction is essential for predicting properties.

### 2.1.1 Why Do We Add Reinforcements to a Metal?

A pure metal (or alloy) has a fundamental limitation: increasing strength (via alloying, cold working, or heat treatment) almost always reduces ductility. This is the classic **strength-ductility trade-off**. By embedding a stiff, strong, brittle ceramic phase into a ductile metal, we can:

- **Increase strength** without sacrificing much ductility.
- **Increase stiffness (modulus)** — metals have low modulus compared to ceramics, and reinforcement dramatically raises it.
- **Improve wear resistance** — ceramic particles are extremely hard.
- **Improve high-temperature performance** — ceramics retain strength at temperatures where metals would creep.
- **Reduce thermal expansion** — useful for dimensional stability in precision parts (e.g., engine pistons).

### 2.1.2 The Load Transfer Mechanism — The "Why" Behind Strengthening

When an external load is applied to an MMC, the ductile matrix must **transfer** this load to the high-strength reinforcement through **shear stresses** developed at the matrix-reinforcement interface. This is the **shear-lag mechanism**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Concept — Critical Fibre Length ($l_c$):**
> For a fibre to be loaded to its fracture strength, it must have a minimum length called the **critical fibre length** $l_c$, given by:
> $$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$
> where $\sigma_{f,u}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the fibre, $d$ is the fibre diameter, and $\tau_i$ is the interfacial shear strength.
> - If fibre length $l < l_c$: the fibre will **pull out** before reaching its full strength.
> - If fibre length $l \geq l_c$: the fibre can be **loaded to its ultimate strength** and will fracture.

### 2.1.3 The Role of the Interfacial Bond

The **interface** is the microscopic boundary where matrix meets reinforcement. Its quality determines:
- **How well** load is transferred from matrix to reinforcement.
- **How the material fails** (ductile vs. brittle).
- **Resistance to environmental degradation** (e.g., corrosion at the interface).

A weak interface → low strength, but high toughness (fibre pullout absorbs energy).
A strong interface → high strength, but low toughness (brittle fracture).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet — Metal Matrix Composites**

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Rule of Mixtures — Density (exact)** | $\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$ | $\rho$: density (g/cm³); $V$: volume fraction (dimensionless) | Predict MMC density |
| 2 | **Rule of Mixtures — Modulus (longitudinal, Voigt)** | $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ | $E$: Young's modulus (GPa) | Upper bound for stiffness |
| 3 | **Inverse Rule of Mixtures — Modulus (transverse, Reuss)** | $\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}$ | — | Lower bound for stiffness |
| 4 | **Strength of MMC (composite, RoM)** | $\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f$ | $\sigma$: strength (MPa) | Theoretical max strength |
| 5 | **Critical Fibre Length** | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | $l_c$: m; $d$: m; $\sigma_{f,u}$: MPa; $\tau_i$: MPa | Minimum fibre length for effective load transfer |
| 6 | **Aspect Ratio (fibres)** | $a = \dfrac{l}{d}$ | Dimensionless | Determines load transfer efficiency |
| 7 | **Volume Fraction Relationship** | $V_m + V_f = 1$ | — | Conservation constraint |
| 8 | **Weight Fraction ↔ Volume Fraction** | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c}$ | $W_f$: weight fraction | Conversion in design calculations |
| 9 | **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c}$ | (MPa)/(g/cm³) | Lightweight design metric |
| 10 | **Specific Stiffness** | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_c}{\rho_c}$ | (GPa)/(g/cm³) | Lightweight stiffness metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Note:** Throughout this table, the absolute-value pipe character `|` has been intentionally replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table rendering. Always use `$\vert x \vert$` in LaTeX when writing absolute value inside a markdown table.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Vehicle Bodies

| Vehicle Application | MMC System | Property Leveraged |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Brake rotors / drums | Al–SiC particulate MMC | Wear resistance, low weight, high thermal conductivity |
| Cylinder liners | Al–SiC or Al–Al₂O₃ | Reduced friction, wear resistance |
| Drive shafts | Al–Al₂O₃ fibre MMC | High specific stiffness, fatigue resistance |
| Connecting rods | Al–SiC (continuous fibre) | High specific strength at elevated temperature |
| Body panels / frames | Al–SiC particulate | Dent resistance, weight reduction |
| Pistons | Al–SiC | Reduced thermal expansion, wear resistance |
| Brake calipers | Al–SiC | Weight reduction, stiffness |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Density (Exact Formula)

The density of a composite is an **exact** rule-of-mixtures property because mass is conserved regardless of how the constituents are arranged.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the system.** Consider a unit volume of MMC such that:
- Volume of matrix = $V_m$, Volume of reinforcement = $V_f$
- $V_m + V_f = 1$ (volume conservation for unit total volume)
- Mass of matrix = $m_m$, Mass of reinforcement = $m_f$, Mass of composite = $m_c$

**Step 2 — Express mass of each phase.**
- $m_m = \rho_m \cdot V_m$ (mass = density × volume)
- $m_f = \rho_f \cdot V_f$
- $m_c = \rho_c \cdot V_c$ with $V_c = 1$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of mass.**
$$m_c = m_m + m_f$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the mass expressions.**
$$\rho_c \cdot 1 = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$$

**Step 5 — Final form of the density equation.**
$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Because mass is a *scalar* and the system conserves it linearly, this rule is **exact** — it does not depend on reinforcement geometry or distribution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Voigt Model)

The longitudinal modulus assumes **iso-strain** conditions: both matrix and reinforcement experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ when a load is applied parallel to aligned fibres.

**Step 1 — Apply the iso-strain assumption.**
$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Express stress in each phase (Hooke's Law).**
$$\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Force (load) equilibrium across a cross-section.**
$$F_c = F_m + F_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_c A_c = \sigma_m A_m + \sigma_f A_f$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by the total area $A_c$.**
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_m \frac{A_m}{A_c} + \sigma_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ and divide by $\varepsilon$.**
$$E_c \varepsilon = E_m \varepsilon V_m + E_f \varepsilon V_f$$

**Step 6 — Final Voigt (upper bound) equation.**
$$\boxed{E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **upper bound** of modulus because iso-strain requires that the reinforcement — the stiffer phase — bears a disproportionately large share of the load.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Reuss Model)

The Reuss model assumes **iso-stress** conditions: the matrix and reinforcement experience the **same stress** $\sigma$ when loaded perpendicular to fibres (transverse loading).

**Step 1 — Apply the iso-stress assumption.**
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_m = \sigma_f = \sigma$$

**Step 2 — Express strain in each phase.**
$$\varepsilon_m = \frac{\sigma}{E_m}, \quad \varepsilon_f = \frac{\sigma}{E_f}, \quad \varepsilon_c = \frac{\sigma}{E_c}$$

**Step 3 — Strain compatibility (total strain is volume-weighted average).**
$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m V_m + \varepsilon_f V_f$$

**Step 4 — Substitute strain expressions.**
$$\frac{\sigma}{E_c} = \frac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m + \frac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\sigma$ and rearrange.**
$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **lower bound** of modulus because the iso-stress assumption forces the stiffer reinforcement to deform equally with the matrix, wasting its stiffness advantage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Fibre Length ($l_c$)

The critical fibre length is the minimum length at which a fibre can be loaded to its ultimate tensile strength before the matrix–fibre interface fails in shear.

**Step 1 — Set up the force balance on a fibre of length $l$ and diameter $d$.**
- Tensile load on fibre = $\sigma_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}\right)$
- Shear load transferred from matrix to fibre through interface = $\tau_i \cdot (\pi d \cdot l)$

**Step 2 — For the fibre to just reach its ultimate strength, set tensile load equal to the load transferred through shear.**
$$\sigma_{f,u} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d \cdot l_c)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by cancelling $\pi d$ from both sides.**
$$\frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot l_c$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $l_c$.**
$$\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot d}{4 \tau_i}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the minimum fibre length required for **efficient reinforcement**. If the fibre is shorter, it will **pull out** of the matrix before it can carry the maximum load.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:**
> An aluminium matrix (Al 6061) is reinforced with **30 % volume fraction of SiC particles**. Given:
> - $E_{Al} = 70 \ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_{Al} = 2.7 \ \text{g/cm}^3$
> - $E_{SiC} = 410 \ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_{SiC} = 3.2 \ \text{g/cm}^3$
>
> **Find:**
> (a) Density of the MMC.
> (b) Longitudinal Young's modulus (Voigt bound).
> (c) Specific stiffness.

### Solution

**Given data:**
$$V_f = 0.30, \quad V_m = 1 - V_f = 0.70$$

**(a) Density of MMC (Rule of Mixtures — exact):**

$$\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$$

$$\rho_c = (2.7)(0.70) + (3.2)(0.30)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.89 + 0.96 = \boxed{2.85 \ \text{g/cm}^3}$$

**(b) Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Voigt):**

$$E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$$

$$E_c = (70)(0.70) + (410)(0.30)$$

$$E_c = 49 + 123 = \boxed{172 \ \text{GPa}}$$

**(c) Specific Stiffness:**

$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{172}{2.85} = \boxed{60.35 \ \frac{\text{GPa}}{\text{g/cm}^3}}$$

> **Interpretation:** Compared to unreinforced aluminium ($\rho = 2.7$, $E = 70 \ \text{GPa}$, $E_{sp} = 25.93$), the MMC has **2.3× the specific stiffness** — explaining its use in lightweight vehicle bodies.

### Comparison with Reuss (Lower Bound)

$$\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f} = \frac{0.70}{70} + \frac{0.30}{410} = 0.01 + 0.000732 = 0.010732$$

$$E_c = \frac{1}{0.010732} = \boxed{93.18 \ \text{GPa}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The actual modulus lies **between** 93.18 GPa (Reuss) and 172 GPa (Voigt). The Voigt bound is approached when reinforcement is in the form of long, aligned, continuous fibres; the Reuss bound is approached for randomly oriented short fibres or particulate reinforcement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MMC Microstructural Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> The following diagram illustrates the **constituent-level architecture** of a generic MMC, showing the matrix, reinforcement phases, and the critical interface.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["METAL MATRIX COMPOSITE MMC ARCHITECTURE"] --> B1["MATRIX PHASE"]
    A1 --> C1["REINFORCEMENT PHASE"]
    A1 --> D1["INTERFACE INTERPHASE"]

    B1 --> B2["Continuous ductile metal phase"]
    B2 --> B3["Example Aluminium Al"]
    B2 --> B4["Example Titanium Ti"]
    B2 --> B5["Example Magnesium Mg"]

    C1 --> C2["Particulate equiaxed"]
    C2 --> C3["Example SiC particles"]
    C2 --> C4["Example Al2O3 particles"]
    C1 --> C5["Fibre continuous aligned"]
    C5 --> C6["Example Al2O3 fibre"]
    C5 --> C7["Example Carbon fibre"]
    C1 --> C8["Whisker short fibre"]
    C8 --> C9["Example SiC whiskers"]

    D1 --> D2["Load transfer region"]
    D2 --> D3["Strong bond high strength brittle"]
    D2 --> D4["Weak bond lower strength tough"]
```

## 4.2 MMC Manufacturing Process Block Diagram

> The following block-level functional flow illustrates the **manufacturing routes** for MMCs relevant to vehicle body components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["RAW MATERIALS METAL ALLOY AND CERAMIC REINFORCEMENT"] --> P2["PROCESS ROUTE SELECTION"]
    P2 --> P3["LIQUID STATE ROUTE"]
    P2 --> P4["SOLID STATE ROUTE"]
    P2 --> P5["DEPOSITION ROUTE"]

    P3 --> P3A["Stir casting"]
    P3 --> P3B["Squeeze casting"]
    P3 --> P3C["Compocasting"]
    P3 --> P3D["Spray deposition"]

    P4 --> P4A["Powder metallurgy"]
    P4 --> P4B["Diffusion bonding"]
    P4 --> P4C["Hot isostatic pressing HIP"]

    P5 --> P5A["Electroplating followed by diffusion bonding"]
    P5 --> P5B["Physical vapour deposition PVD"]

    P3A --> O1["FINAL MMC COMPONENT"]
    P3B --> O1
    P3C --> O1
    P3D --> O1
    P4A --> O1
    P4B --> O1
    P4C --> O1
    P5A --> O1
    P5B --> O1

    O1 --> O2["Vehicle components brake rotors cylinder liners pistons drive shafts"]
```

## 4.3 MMC Manufacturing Methods — Vehicle-Body-Engineering Perspective

| Manufacturing Method | Process Description | Typical Vehicle Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stir Casting** | Molten metal is stirred while ceramic particles are added; the slurry is then cast. Simple and cheap. | Brake drums, callipers |
| **Squeeze Casting** | Molten metal is poured into a die containing pre-heated ceramic preform, then pressure is applied. | Cylinder liners, pistons |
| **Spray Deposition** | Molten metal is sprayed through a nozzle while ceramic particles are injected. Rapid solidification. | Aerospace & high-performance body panels |
| **Powder Metallurgy** | Metal and ceramic powders are blended, compacted, and sintered. Excellent uniformity. | Precision drive shafts, gears |
| **Diffusion Bonding** | Alternating layers of metal foil and fibre mats are stacked and hot-pressed to bond. | Continuous-fibre MMC sheets |
| **In-situ Processing** | Chemical reaction within the melt forms the reinforcement directly (e.g., TiB₂ in Al). | High-performance pistons |

## 4.4 MMC Classification Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["MMCs CLASSIFIED BY REINFORCEMENT TYPE"] --> R1["Particulate MMC"]
    Q1 --> R2["Whisker MMC"]
    Q1 --> R3["Fibre MMC"]

    R1 --> R1A["SiC particles in Al"]
    R1 --> R1B["Al2O3 particles in Al"]
    R1 --> R1C["Used in brake rotors and callipers"]

    R2 --> R2A["SiC whiskers in Al"]
    R2 --> R2B["Used in aerospace pistons"]

    R3 --> R3A["Continuous Al2O3 fibre in Al"]
    R3 --> R3B["Continuous carbon fibre in Al or Mg"]
    R3 --> R3C["Used in connecting rods and drive shafts"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, PEAUT741]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

**Q: Define Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs). List any two examples of matrix and reinforcement materials used in MMCs for vehicle body applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A Metal Matrix Composite (MMC) is an engineered material in which a **metallic matrix** is reinforced with a **ceramic phase** (particles, whiskers, or fibres) to produce a combination of high strength, high stiffness, and low weight not achievable in the base metal alone.

**Examples:**
- **Matrix:** Aluminium (Al) alloys, Magnesium (Mg) alloys
- **Reinforcement:** Silicon carbide (SiC), Alumina (Al₂O₃)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A full 3-mark answer requires the **definition (1.5 marks)** + **two examples each of matrix and reinforcement (1.5 marks)**.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

**Q: Mention any three advantages of MMCs over monolithic metals in vehicle body applications.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher specific strength** — strength-to-weight ratio is much greater, allowing lighter body panels.
2. **Higher specific stiffness** — modulus-to-weight ratio is increased, improving dent resistance and rigidity.
3. **Improved wear resistance** — ceramic reinforcement dramatically increases surface hardness, ideal for brake rotors and cylinder liners.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write generic statements like *"MMCs are strong"*. Always quantify with **specific strength**, **specific stiffness**, or **wear rate**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Model Paper, PEAUT741 / Module 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q (a)** What are the different types of reinforcements used in Metal Matrix Composites? Explain the role of the **matrix–reinforcement interface** in load transfer. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** An aluminium matrix (Al 2024) is reinforced with **25 % volume fraction of Al₂O₃ particles**. Given:
- $E_{Al} = 73 \ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_{Al} = 2.78 \ \text{g/cm}^3$
- $E_{Al_2O_3} = 380 \ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.95 \ \text{g/cm}^3$

Compute the **density**, **longitudinal Young's modulus (Voigt bound)**, and **specific stiffness** of the MMC. Compare the specific stiffness with that of unreinforced Al 2024. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Types of Reinforcements & Role of Interface (7 Marks)

**Three types of reinforcements:**

1. **Particulate reinforcement** — Equiaxed ceramic particles (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C). Provide isotropic strengthening. Used in brake rotors and engine blocks.

2. **Whisker / short-fibre reinforcement** — Single-crystal short fibres (e.g., SiC whiskers, Al₂O₃ short fibres). Higher aspect ratio gives better load transfer than particles. Used in high-performance pistons.

3. **Continuous-fibre reinforcement** — Long, aligned fibres (e.g., Al₂O₃ fibre, carbon fibre, boron fibre). Provide maximum stiffness and strength along the fibre direction. Used in connecting rods, drive shafts.

**Role of the matrix–reinforcement interface:**

The **interface** is the critical region where load is transferred from the ductile matrix to the high-strength reinforcement via **shear stresses** (shear-lag mechanism). The interface must:
- Be **strong enough** to transfer load efficiently without debonding.
- Be **chemically stable** to prevent degradation at high temperature.
- Be **thermally compatible** to minimise residual stresses from mismatch in **coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE)**.

A balanced interface gives high strength *and* reasonable toughness. Too strong an interface → brittle; too weak → fibre pull-out and low strength.

> **Mark Split:** Types of reinforcements (3 marks) + Interface role (4 marks).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.25$, therefore $V_m = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$
- $E_{Al} = 73 \ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_{Al} = 2.78 \ \text{g/cm}^3$
- $E_{Al_2O_3} = 380 \ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.95 \ \text{g/cm}^3$

**Step 1 — Density of MMC (Rule of Mixtures, exact):**
$$\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$$
$$\rho_c = (2.78)(0.75) + (3.95)(0.25)$$
$$\rho_c = 2.085 + 0.9875 = \boxed{3.0725 \ \text{g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Voigt upper bound):**
$$E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$$
$$E_c = (73)(0.75) + (380)(0.25)$$
$$E_c = 54.75 + 95.0 = \boxed{149.75 \ \text{GPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Specific Stiffness of MMC:**
$$E_{sp,MMC} = \frac{E_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{149.75}{3.0725} = \boxed{48.74 \ \frac{\text{GPa}}{\text{g/cm}^3}}$$

**Step 4 — Specific Stiffness of Unreinforced Al 2024:**
$$E_{sp,Al} = \frac{73}{2.78} = 26.26 \ \frac{\text{GPa}}{\text{g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**
$$\text{Improvement factor} = \frac{48.74}{26.26} = \boxed{1.856 \text{ times}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The MMC has approximately **1.86× the specific stiffness** of unreinforced Al 2024, justifying its use in lightweight vehicle body applications.

> **Mark Split:** Density (2 marks) + Modulus (2 marks) + Specific stiffness + comparison (3 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q (a)** Explain with neat sketches the **stir casting** and **squeeze casting** methods for manufacturing MMCs. Mention their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Derive the expression for **critical fibre length** $l_c$ for a fibre-reinforced MMC. A continuous boron fibre ($d = 100 \ \mu m$, $\sigma_{f,u} = 3500 \ \text{MPa}$) is embedded in an aluminium matrix with interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 50 \ \text{MPa}$. Calculate the **critical fibre length**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Stir Casting & Squeeze Casting (7 Marks)

**Stir Casting:**

A vortex is created in molten metal by mechanical stirring, and ceramic particles are introduced into the vortex. The particles get trapped in the melt due to the negative pressure at the vortex centre. The mixture is then cast into a mould.

- **Advantages:** Simple, low cost, suitable for large-scale production, works for particles and short fibres.
- **Limitations:** Non-uniform distribution of reinforcement, gas porosity, limited to reinforcements that are wetted by the molten metal.

**Squeeze Casting:**

A preform of ceramic reinforcement (a shaped porous body of fibres or particles) is pre-heated and placed in a die. Molten metal is poured into the die, and high pressure (50–150 MPa) is applied to force the metal into the preform pores. Solidification occurs under pressure.

- **Advantages:** Excellent matrix–reinforcement bonding, low porosity, near-net-shape components, high reinforcement volume fractions possible.
- **Limitations:** Higher equipment cost, limited to smaller components, requires careful preform design.

> **Mark Split:** Stir casting (3 marks) + Squeeze casting (3 marks) + Comparative remark (1 mark).

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation & Numerical (7 Marks)

**Derivation of $l_c$:**

**Step 1 — Force on a fibre of length $l$ and diameter $d$:**
- Tensile load on fibre = $\sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$

**Step 2 — Shear load transferred from matrix to fibre along the interface:**
- Shear load = $\tau_i \cdot (\pi d \cdot l)$

**Step 3 — At critical length, fibre reaches its ultimate strength as the interface reaches its shear limit:**
$$\sigma_{f,u} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d \cdot l_c)$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\pi d$ and solve for $l_c$:**
$$l_c = \boxed{\frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot d}{4 \tau_i}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $d = 100 \ \mu m = 100 \times 10^{-6} \ m = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \ m$
- $\sigma_{f,u} = 3500 \ \text{MPa} = 3500 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa}$
- $\tau_i = 50 \ \text{MPa} = 50 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa}$

**Substitution:**
$$l_c = \frac{(3500 \times 10^{6}) (1.0 \times 10^{-4})}{4 \times (50 \times 10^{6})}$$

$$l_c = \frac{350}{200} \times 10^{-4}$$

$$l_c = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \ m = \boxed{1.75 \ \text{mm}}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Any boron fibre shorter than **1.75 mm** in this MMC will pull out of the matrix before reaching its ultimate strength, reducing the composite's efficiency.

> **Mark Split:** Derivation (3 marks) + Numerical substitution (2 marks) + Final answer with units (2 marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall List:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Always convert $\mu m$ to m and MPa to Pa before substituting. A 14-mark question can lose **2–3 marks** for unit errors.
> 2. **Voigt vs. Reuss confusion** — Use **Voigt (Rule of Mixtures)** for *longitudinal* modulus of aligned continuous fibres; use **Reuss (Inverse Rule of Mixtures)** for *transverse* modulus or randomly oriented reinforcement.
> 3. **Forgetting $V_m + V_f = 1$** — If the question gives only $V_f$, you **must** compute $V_m$ before applying any rule of mixtures.
> 4. **Confusing MMC with PMC** — Metal matrix, not polymer. MMCs tolerate much higher temperatures.
> 5. **Missing specific strength / specific stiffness comparison** — Always compare with the unreinforced matrix in numericals.
> 6. **Omitting the role of the interface** — This is a high-weightage topic; merely naming reinforcements is insufficient. Always explain **load transfer mechanism**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Metal Matrix Composites**

- **Definition:** MMC = Metal matrix + ceramic reinforcement. Matrix provides ductility; reinforcement provides strength and stiffness.
- **Classification (by reinforcement):** Particulate, Whisker, Continuous fibre.
- **Common matrices:** Al, Mg, Ti, Ni, Cu. **Common reinforcements:** SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C, TiC, carbon fibre.
- **Key properties for vehicles:** Specific strength, specific stiffness, wear resistance, low CTE, high-temperature stability.
- **Rule of Mixtures (Density — exact):** $\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$
- **Rule of Mixtures (Modulus — Voigt, longitudinal):** $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ — **upper bound**.
- **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Modulus — Reuss, transverse):** $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_m}{E_m} + \dfrac{V_f}{E_f}$ — **lower bound**.
- **Critical fibre length:** $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot d}{4 \tau_i}$ — minimum length for effective reinforcement.
- **Load transfer mechanism:** Shear-lag at the matrix–fibre interface.
- **Interface trade-off:** Strong bond → high strength + low toughness; Weak bond → low strength + high toughness.
- **Major manufacturing methods:** Stir casting, squeeze casting, spray deposition, powder metallurgy, diffusion bonding, in-situ processing.
- **Vehicle applications:** Brake rotors (Al–SiC), drive shafts (Al–Al₂O₃ fibre), pistons (Al–SiC), cylinder liners (Al–SiC), connecting rods (continuous-fibre Al MMC), body panels (Al–SiC particulate).
- **Always remember:** $V_m + V_f = 1$. Always convert units. Always compare with the base metal to justify the MMC's superiority.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glass Reinforced Plastics and High Strength Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Glass Reinforced Plastics and High Strength Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glass Reinforced Plastic (GRP)**: A composite material formed by embedding **glass fibres** (the reinforcement phase) into a **thermosetting or thermoplastic polymer matrix** (typically polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy resin). The glass fibres carry the load while the matrix transfers stress between fibres, protects them from environmental damage, and maintains the geometric integrity of the part.

In vehicle body engineering, GRP is classified under the broader family of **Fibre-Reinforced Polymers (FRP)**. The "High Strength Composites" referred to in the KTU syllabus typically denote **Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymers (CFRP)**, **Aramid (Kevlar) Reinforced Polymers (ARP)**, and **Hybrid Composites** where two or more fibre types are co-located in a single matrix.

The governing mechanical property that makes GRP relevant to automotive bodywork is the **specific strength** (strength-to-density ratio), measured typically in **MPa·cm³/g** or **kN·m/kg**.

| Material | Density (g/cm³) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | **7.85** | 350 – 500 | 45 – 64 |
| Aluminium 6061-T6 | **2.70** | 290 – 310 | 107 – 115 |
| GRP (Polyester + E-glass) | **1.55 – 1.95** | 200 – 400 | 105 – 250 |
| CFRP (Epoxy + HS Carbon) | **1.55 – 1.65** | 1500 – 3500 | 910 – 2300 |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reinforced Concrete" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition**: Imagine a building made of brittle chalk (the resin). It has good compressive strength but cracks easily under tension. Now imagine pouring that chalk into a mould already packed with thousands of tiny steel rods (the glass fibres). When you pull the resulting block, the chalk alone would split, but the steel rods bridge every crack and carry the tensile load. The combination is enormously stronger than either material alone. **GRP works on exactly this principle** — the polymer matrix is the "chalk" and the glass fibres are the "steel rods", only at a microscopic scale and weighing a fraction of steel.

In automotive terms, this is why a **GRP bonnet** of a 1970s sports car could weigh **~9 kg** while providing structural performance equivalent to a **~22 kg steel bonnet**.

### 1.3 The Three-Phase Microstructure of a Composite

Every composite laminate used in vehicle bodies consists of three identifiable phases:

1. **Reinforcement (Fibre Phase)** — bears the mechanical load. In GRP this is **E-glass**, **S-glass**, or **C-glass**.
2. **Matrix Phase (Resin)** — binds the fibres, distributes load, provides surface finish, and resists environmental degradation.
3. **Interphase (Interface / Interphase Region)** — the chemical/mechanical boundary between fibre and matrix, often enhanced by **silanes (e.g., γ-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane)** or **sizing agents**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams**: A composite is *not* just "fibres + resin". The interphase is what allows stress transfer. A weak interphase causes **interlaminar shear failure (ILSF)** — the single most common failure mode in GRP body panels.

### 1.4 Classification of Composites Relevant to Vehicle Bodies

- **By matrix type**: Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC) — dominant in automotive.
- **By reinforcement geometry**:
  * **Chopped Strand Mat (CSM)** — random planar fibre orientation; cheap, isotropic in-plane.
  * **Woven Roving (WR)** — bidirectional cloth; better stiffness.
  * **Unidirectional (UD) tape** — maximum stiffness along fibre axis.
  * **Continuous Filament Mat (CFM)** — hybrid of CSM + WR.
- **By process route**: SMC, BMC, RTM, hand lay-up, spray-up, pultrusion, filament winding.
- **By reinforcement fibre**: E-GRP, S-GRP, CFRP, AFRP (Aramid Fibre Reinforced Plastic), Hybrid (Carbon + Glass).
