## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constituent Materials of GRP

#### 2.1.1 Glass Fibre Types (KTU High-Yield)

| Glass Type | Key Composition Marker | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Density (g/cm³) | Typical Vehicle Body Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E-glass** | Calcium alumino-borosilicate | 2000 – 3500 | **2.54 – 2.62** | Body panels, dashboards, bumper beams |
| **S-glass** | Magnesium alumino-silicate | 4000 – 4800 | **2.48 – 2.49** | High-performance chassis components |
| **C-glass** | Calcium-borosilicate (acid resistant) | 2000 – 3000 | **2.50** | Surface veils for chemical resistance |
| **AR-glass** | Alkali-resistant (zirconia) | 2500 – 3500 | **2.70** | Rare in vehicles; cement composites |

#### 2.1.2 Resin Systems

- **Unsaturated Polyester Resin (UPE)** — Most common in automotive GRP. Low cost, room-temperature cure, good chemical resistance.
- **Vinyl Ester Resin** — Bridges UPE and epoxy; superior mechanical and corrosion properties. Used in structural SMC.
- **Epoxy Resin** — Highest mechanical performance, low shrinkage, but expensive and requires elevated cure temperatures.
- **Phenolic Resin** — Excellent fire/smoke performance; used in interior panels where flammability is critical.
- **Polyurethane (PU) Resin** — Fast cure, used in **SRIM (Structural Reaction Injection Moulding)**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip**: The following equations are mandatory for solving any numerical/data-based question on GRP composites in KTU ESE.

**Table 2.1 — Master Formula Sheet for Composite Mechanics**

| # | Property | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fibre Volume Fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{v_f}{v_c} = \dfrac{v_f}{v_f + v_m}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Matrix Volume Fraction | $V_m = 1 - V_f$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Composite Density (ROM) | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | kg/m³ or g/cm³ |
| 4 | Longitudinal Modulus (ROM) | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Valid only for UD aligned loading |
| 5 | Transverse Modulus (Inverse ROM) | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse rule of mixtures |
| 6 | Longitudinal Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{cL} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Upper bound (iso-strain) |
| 7 | Major Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Approximation |
| 8 | Specific Strength | $\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}$ | kN·m/kg — key automotive metric |
| 9 | Specific Modulus | $\dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Used for stiffness-critical panels |
| 10 | Laminate Density | $\rho_{lam} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho_i \, t_i$ | $t_i$ = ply thickness |
| 11 | Weight Saving % | $\Delta W\% = \dfrac{W_{steel} - W_{GRP}}{W_{steel}} \times 100$ | Direct metric for designers |
| 12 | Fibre Weight Fraction | $W_f = \dfrac{w_f}{w_c}$ | Used for hand lay-up mass calculations |

**CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: All vertical bars (absolute values) and conditional separators in expressions above are written using `$\vert$` to preserve table integrity.

### 2.3 Why "High Strength Composites" Matter in Vehicle Bodies

The push from GRP to **CFRP** in modern vehicles (BMW 7-series, McLaren MonoCell, Alfa Romeo 4C) is driven by three quantified advantages:

1. **Mass reduction** — CFRP monocoques achieve **~50–65% mass saving** vs. steel equivalents.
2. **Crashworthiness** — Carbon/epoxy laminates absorb **~3× more specific energy** (energy per unit mass) than high-strength steel under axial crush.
3. **Stiffness** — A CFRP skin at **1.2 mm** can match the flexural rigidity of a **0.8 mm** steel panel at **1/4 the mass**.

### 2.4 High-Strength Composite Constituents

- **Carbon Fibres** — PAN-based HS (High Strength) fibre: tensile strength **3500 – 7000 MPa**, modulus **230 – 290 GPa**. HM (High Modulus) fibre: modulus **350 – 700 GPa**.
- **Aramid Fibres (Kevlar 49, Twaron)** — Tensile strength **2700 – 3600 MPa**, density **1.44 g/cm³**, excellent impact/toughness.
- **Hybrid Composites** — Combine carbon (high stiffness) with glass (low cost) in tailored ply stacks.
- **Sandwich Panels** — GRP/CFRP skins bonded to a **PVC foam**, **PMI foam**, **balsa**, or **honeycomb** core. Core shear stiffness $\tau_c$ governs panel bending:

$$\sigma_{bend} \approx \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d}{2}$$

where $d$ = distance between skin centroids (sandwich thickness minus skin thickness).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Density

The density of a composite is a **mass-weighted average** of the constituent densities. Mass of composite equals mass of fibre plus mass of matrix.

**Step 1 — Write the mass conservation statement.**

Mass conservation requires:

$$m_c = m_f + m_m$$

**Step 2 — Express each mass in terms of volume and density.**

$$m_c = \rho_c v_c \quad ; \quad m_f = \rho_f v_f \quad ; \quad m_m = \rho_m v_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the mass equation.**

$$\rho_c v_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m v_m$$

**Step 4 — Divide the entire equation by $v_c$ to introduce volume fractions.**

Dividing through by $v_c$ (assuming $v_c \neq 0$):

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \dfrac{v_f}{v_c} + \rho_m \dfrac{v_m}{v_c}$$

**Step 5 — Recognise that $\dfrac{v_f}{v_c} = V_f$ and $\dfrac{v_m}{v_c} = V_m$.**

Since $V_f + V_m = 1$, we obtain the **Rule of Mixtures (ROM) for density**:

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)**:

A GRP car bumper beam uses E-glass ($V_f = 0.45$) and unsaturated polyester resin. Given $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_m = 1.25 \text{ g/cm}^3$, find composite density.

$$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.45) + (1.25)(0.55) = 1.1475 + 0.6875 = \boxed{1.835 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

Mass saving vs. steel: $\Delta W\% = \dfrac{7.85 - 1.835}{7.85} \times 100 = 76.6\%$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Longitudinal Modulus ($E_L$) using Iso-Strain Assumption

This is the **fundamental derivation** that KTU examiners love. We assume:
- Fibres and matrix are perfectly bonded.
- Under axial load, both phases experience the **same strain** (iso-strain / Voigt model).
- Load is applied parallel to fibre direction.

**Step 1 — Apply iso-strain condition.**

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Express total load as sum of phase loads.**

For a cross-section of unit area, the load is shared:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law to each phase.**

$$\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon \quad ; \quad \sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the load-sharing equation.**

$$\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m = \varepsilon (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $\varepsilon$ to obtain composite modulus.**

$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

**Numerical Check**:
With $E_f = 72 \text{ GPa}$ (E-glass), $E_m = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$ (polyester), $V_f = 0.45$:

$$E_L = 72(0.45) + 3.5(0.55) = 32.4 + 1.925 = \boxed{34.325 \text{ GPa}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result is the **upper bound** of modulus. Real laminates with off-axis plies fall between $E_L$ and $E_T$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transverse Modulus ($E_T$) using Iso-Stress Assumption

When load is applied **perpendicular to the fibres**, both phases experience the **same stress** (iso-stress / Reuss model).

**Step 1 — Apply iso-stress condition.**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

**Step 2 — Express total strain as weighted sum.**

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute strain = stress/modulus.**

$$\dfrac{\sigma}{E_T} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \dfrac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\sigma$ from each term.**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}}$$

**Numerical Check** (same materials as above):

$$\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{0.45}{72} + \dfrac{0.55}{3.5} = 0.00625 + 0.15714 = 0.16339$$

$$E_T = \dfrac{1}{0.16339} = \boxed{6.12 \text{ GPa}}$$

Note that $E_L \gg E_T$, confirming the **anisotropic** nature of GRP — a key reason why vehicle body panels use **balanced / quasi-isotropic lay-ups**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Weight Saving Calculation for a Vehicle Panel

**Problem**: A rear floor pan of a passenger car measures **1200 mm × 1400 mm**. Original steel thickness = **0.8 mm**, $\rho_{steel} = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Replacement: GRP with equivalent bending stiffness, $t_{GRP} = 2.5 \text{ mm}$, $\rho_{GRP} = 1.65 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Calculate the weight saving.

**Step 1 — Calculate steel panel mass.**

$$V_{steel} = 1200 \times 1400 \times 0.8 = 1{,}344{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 1344 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$m_{steel} = 1344 \times 7.85 = 10{,}550.4 \text{ g} = 10.55 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate GRP panel mass.**

$$V_{GRP} = 1200 \times 1400 \times 2.5 = 4{,}200{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 4200 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$m_{GRP} = 4200 \times 1.65 = 6930 \text{ g} = 6.93 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage weight saving.**

$$\Delta W\% = \dfrac{10.55 - 6.93}{10.55} \times 100 = \boxed{34.3\%}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Manufacturing Processes vs. Production Volume

| Process | Glass Form | Resin | Production Volume | Vehicle Body Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hand Lay-Up | CSM / WR | Polyester (room cure) | 1 – 500 units/yr | Prototypes, low-volume sports cars |
| Spray-Up | Chopped fibre + resin spray | Polyester | 50 – 5000 units/yr | Truck cabs, recreational vehicles |
| SMC Compression | Sheet Moulding Compound | Polyester + filler | 10 000 – 200 000 units/yr | **Class-A body panels** (bonnets, roof) |
| BMC Injection | Bulk Moulding Compound | Polyester | 50 000 – 500 000 units/yr | Headlamp reflectors, small covers |
| RTM (Resin Transfer Moulding) | Preform (CSM/WR) | Vinyl ester / epoxy | 1000 – 50 000 units/yr | Structural panels, chassis parts |
| SRIM | Preform + PU | Polyurethane | 5 000 – 100 000 units/yr | Bumper beams, fenders |
| Pultrusion | Continuous roving | Polyester / epoxy | Continuous profile | Body frame rails, structural members |
| Filament Winding | Continuous roving | Epoxy | Specialised | Drive shafts, **propeller shafts** |
| Autoclave (CFRP) | Prepreg UD / fabric | Epoxy (high T cure) | 1 – 10 000 units/yr | F1 monocoques, supercar tubs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy Tip**: When asked "Why SMC is preferred over hand lay-up for mass production?" — answer with **cycle time** (< 2 min vs. > 4 hr), **class-A finish**, **dimensional reproducibility**, and **low labour cost**. These four points alone earn full marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define Glass Reinforced Plastic. List any two advantages of GRP over conventional steel for vehicle body applications.**

*Model Answer*:
GRP is a composite material in which glass fibres (typically E-glass) are embedded in a thermosetting polymer matrix such as unsaturated polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy resin. The fibres carry the tensile and flexural loads while the matrix binds them, distributes stress, and provides surface finish and environmental resistance.

**Two advantages over steel**:
1. **Lower density** ($\rho_{GRP} \approx 1.6$ g/cm³ vs. $\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ g/cm³), giving $70$–$80\%$ weight reduction.
2. **Corrosion resistance** — GRP does not rust, eliminating painting and anti-corrosion treatments in many applications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**What is SMC? Mention its typical application in vehicle body engineering.**

*Model Answer*:
**SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound)** is a ready-to-mould fibre-reinforced thermoset material consisting of chopped glass fibres (typically 25 mm long), unsaturated polyester resin, fillers (CaCO₃), thickener (MgO), and additives, all rolled into a sheet between two carrier films. After a maturation period (24–72 hours at 40 °C), the sheets are cut into charges and compression-moulded at 140–160 °C and 7–14 MPa pressure.

**Application**: SMC is widely used for **Class-A exterior body panels** such as bonnets, roof panels, door outer skins, decklids, and fenders in passenger cars and trucks (e.g., Chevrolet Corvette, SMC-bodied trucks of the 1970s–80s).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **rule of mixtures** for predicting the longitudinal modulus ($E_L$) and density ($\rho_c$) of a unidirectional GRP composite. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional GRP laminate has $V_f = 0.50$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³. Compute $E_L$, $E_T$, $\rho_c$, and the specific modulus $E_L/\rho_c$. Compare with mild steel ($E = 210$ GPa, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³). **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Rule of Mixtures — Theory (7 marks)**

The rule of mixtures is a micromechanics model that predicts the bulk properties of a composite by **volume-weighted averaging** of the constituent properties.

**Assumption 1 — Perfect bonding**: There is no slip at the fibre–matrix interface. Stress transfers fully.

**Assumption 2 — Iso-strain (for $E_L$)**: Both fibre and matrix experience identical strain when load is applied parallel to fibres.

**Assumption 3 — Homogeneous distribution**: Fibres are uniformly distributed and aligned.

**Derivation for $E_L$**:

Step 1: Iso-strain: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$

Step 2: Load sharing: $P_c = P_f + P_m \Rightarrow \sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$

Step 3: Dividing by $A_c$: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$

Step 4: Using Hooke's law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$:

$$E_L \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m$$

Step 5: Therefore, **$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$**

**Derivation for $\rho_c$**:

Mass conservation: $\rho_c v_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m v_m$

Dividing by $v_c$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$$

Both equations follow the **linear rule of mixtures** and are valid upper bounds (iso-strain / Voigt assumption). The transverse modulus $E_T$ uses the **inverse rule of mixtures** (iso-stress / Reuss model):

$$\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Longitudinal modulus $E_L$**:

$$E_L = (72)(0.50) + (3.5)(0.50) = 36 + 1.75 = \boxed{37.75 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Transverse modulus $E_T$**:

$$\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{0.50}{72} + \dfrac{0.50}{3.5} = 0.00694 + 0.14286 = 0.14980$$

$$E_T = \dfrac{1}{0.14980} = \boxed{6.676 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Composite density $\rho_c$**:

$$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.50) + (1.25)(0.50) = 1.275 + 0.625 = \boxed{1.90 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Specific modulus $E_L / \rho_c$**:

$$\dfrac{E_L}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{37.75}{1.90} = 19.87 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Specific modulus of steel**:

$$\dfrac{E_{steel}}{\rho_{steel}} = \dfrac{210}{7.85} = 26.75 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Comparison**: Steel has a **higher specific modulus** (26.75 vs. 19.87) in this calculation, but note that:
- GRP's $E_L$ is anisotropic; with proper **cross-ply lay-up**, the **effective in-plane modulus** can approach steel values.
- The **specific strength** of GRP (e.g., 250 vs. steel's 64 kN·m/kg) is the real advantage in vehicle body design where **crashworthiness** dominates over pure stiffness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students commonly forget that the rule of mixtures for $E_L$ uses the **linear** form, while $E_T$ uses the **inverse/reciprocal** form. Mixing these up leads to a **2-mark deduction** typically. Always write the form explicitly in your answer.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound) process** for manufacturing vehicle body panels. Compare it with the **hand lay-up** process on the basis of (i) cycle time, (ii) production volume suitability, (iii) surface finish, and (iv) fibre volume fraction achievable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car front fender measures **900 mm × 500 mm × 2.0 mm**. Calculate the mass if manufactured from (i) steel ($\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³) and (ii) GRP ($\rho = 1.75$ g/cm³). Determine the **percentage weight reduction** and the **fuel saving per year** assuming the vehicle runs 15,000 km/year with a fuel consumption reduction factor of 0.6 L per 100 kg of mass saved per 1000 km. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) SMC Process Description and Comparison (7 marks)**

**SMC Process**:

1. **Resin paste preparation** — Unsaturated polyester resin is mixed with calcium carbonate filler, styrene monomer, release agent, thickener (MgO), catalyst, and pigment in a high-speed mixer.
2. **Impregnation** — The paste is fed between two carrier films and chopped glass fibre strands (25 mm) are deposited on the resin film.
3. **Maturation** — The wet SMC sheet is wound on a roll and stored at 40 °C for 24–72 hours. The MgO thickener increases viscosity to a leather-like consistency.
4. **Moulding** — Cut charges are placed in a heated (140–160 °C) matched metal mould of a hydraulic press at 7–14 MPa pressure for 60–180 seconds.
5. **Demoulding** — The cured Class-A panel is ejected; flash is trimmed.

**Comparison Table**:

| Parameter | Hand Lay-Up | SMC |
|---|---|---|
| Cycle Time | 4 – 24 hours (room cure) | **60 – 180 seconds** |
| Production Volume | 1 – 500 units/year | **10 000 – 200 000 units/year** |
| Surface Finish | Gel-coat on one side, low quality | **Class-A on both sides, paintable** |
| Achievable $V_f$ | 0.20 – 0.35 | **0.25 – 0.45 (chopped)** |

**Conclusion**: SMC is **superior for medium-to-high volume** production of consistent, dimensionally accurate body panels.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Volume of the fender**:

$$V = 900 \times 500 \times 2.0 = 900{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 900 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Steel mass**:

$$m_{steel} = 900 \times 7.85 = \boxed{7065 \text{ g} = 7.065 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 3 — GRP mass**:

$$m_{GRP} = 900 \times 1.75 = \boxed{1575 \text{ g} = 1.575 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Mass saving**:

$$\Delta m = 7.065 - 1.575 = 5.49 \text{ kg}$$

$$\Delta W\% = \dfrac{5.49}{7.065} \times 100 = \boxed{77.7\%}$$

**Step 5 — Fuel saving calculation**:

Mass saved = 5.49 kg = **0.0549 tonnes**

Given rule: **0.6 L per 100 kg per 1000 km**

Fuel saving per km = $\dfrac{0.0549}{100} \times \dfrac{0.6}{1000}$ L/km = $3.294 \times 10^{-7}$ L/km

Annual fuel saving = $3.294 \times 10^{-7} \times 15{,}000$ = $\boxed{4.94 \text{ L/year}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: A very common error is forgetting to convert **mm³ → cm³** before multiplying by density in g/cm³. The factor is **1000 mm³ = 1 cm³**. Missing this conversion leads to a mass value off by **3 orders of magnitude**, costing up to **3 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Glass Reinforced Plastics and High Strength Composites**

- **GRP definition**: Glass fibre + thermoset/thermoplastic matrix; reinforcement carries load, matrix transfers stress.
- **E-glass** is the default automotive fibre ($\rho = 2.55$ g/cm³, $E = 72$ GPa, $\sigma_t = 2$–$3.5$ GPa).
- **S-glass** offers higher strength; **carbon fibre** offers highest specific modulus.
- **Volume fraction $V_f$** is the governing parameter for all composite properties.
- **Rule of mixtures**:
  * $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$
  * $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$  *(upper bound)*
  * $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$  *(inverse, lower bound)*
- **Specific strength** and **specific modulus** are the key automotive design metrics — NOT raw strength or modulus.
- **SMC** is the dominant process for medium-volume Class-A body panels; **RTM** for structural parts; **autoclave** for CFRP supercars.
- **Hand lay-up** remains relevant only for prototypes and ultra-low volume (sports cars, custom coachwork).
- **Sandwich panels** (CFRP/GRP skins + foam/honeycomb core) provide high flexural rigidity at low weight — used in floors, roofs, and tailgates.
- **Interphase** (silanes, sizing) is critical for stress transfer; weak interphase → interlaminar shear failure.
- **High strength composites** = CFRP, AFRP, hybrids; primarily used in F1, supercars, EVs, and luxury cars.
- **Mass saving** of 50–80% is achievable vs. steel; **fuel saving** scales linearly with mass removed (rule of thumb: 0.4–0.6 L/100 km per 100 kg).
- **Anisotropy** of GRP means body panels use **balanced / quasi-isotropic lay-ups** to avoid directional weakness.
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE)** of GRP can be tailored to match steel (~11 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) by adjusting $V_f$, important for mixed-material body-in-white.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoplastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermoplastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Thermoplastics

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PEAUT741 (Vehicle Body Engineering)**, Module 2 covers non-metallic materials used in modern automotive body construction, where **Thermoplastics** form the largest and most commercially significant class of polymers.

A **Thermoplastic** (Greek: *therme* = heat, *plastikos* = moldable) is defined as a **linear or slightly branched chain polymer** that softens upon heating and hardens upon cooling, with this physical transformation being **fully reversible and repeatable** without any significant change in the material's intrinsic chemical structure. The intermolecular bonding in thermoplastics consists of relatively weak **secondary bonds (Van der Waals forces, dipole-dipole interactions, and hydrogen bonds)** rather than primary covalent crosslinks, which is the molecular origin of their reprocessability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Thermoplastics are *recyclable, reprocessable, weldable, and thermoformable* polymers. They are the dominant choice for **non-structural and semi-structural** vehicle body components (bumpers, fenders, dashboards, door panels, fuel tanks, interior trims) due to their high specific strength, corrosion immunity, and design flexibility.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a bowl of **cooked spaghetti noodles** lying in a heap on a plate.

- The individual **noodles** represent long polymer chains (thermoplastic molecules).
- The **stickiness between noodles** (from sauce, oil, or just friction) represents the weak **secondary bonds** holding the chains together.
- When you **microwave the plate (apply heat)**, the noodles become slippery and slide past each other — the spaghetti heap can now be reshaped into any form. This is exactly what happens to a thermoplastic during heating: chains gain mobility.
- When the plate **cools down**, the noodles stiffen again, locking the new shape. Reheat? It melts once more. **No chemical reaction ever took place** — only physical rearrangement.

> [!NOTE]
> Compare this with a **thermoset**: a thermoset is like a bowl of *curdled, dried epoxy resin* — once the crosslinks form (chemical bonds), heating will **char and degrade** the material rather than re-melt it. This is the critical distinction examiners love to test.

### 1.3 Position of Thermoplastics in the Polymer Family Tree

| Polymer Class | Behavior on Heating | Bond Type | Recyclable? | Vehicle Body Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermoplastics** | Soften → Harden (reversible) | Weak secondary bonds | ✅ Yes | Bumpers, dashboards, tanks |
| **Thermosetting Plastics** | Harden permanently; char on reheating | Strong covalent crosslinks | ❌ No (shredded filler only) | Body panels (older SMC), brake linings |
| **Elastomers (Rubbers)** | Elastic deformation over wide T range | Light crosslinks + flexible chains | Limited | Seals, hoses, tires |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)**: The temperature at which amorphous regions of the polymer transition from a rigid, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state. Typical automotive thermoplastics: **Tg ≈ −40 °C to 150 °C**.
- **Melting Temperature (Tm)**: The temperature at which crystalline regions melt. For semi-crystalline automotive thermoplastics: **Tm ≈ 130 °C to 270 °C**.
- **Degree of Crystallinity (% Xc)**: Mass fraction of crystalline regions; strongly affects stiffness, density, and chemical resistance.
- **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT)**: The temperature at which a polymer sample deflects 0.25 mm under a 0.45 MPa load. Critical for under-hood applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, remember that thermoplastics are characterized by their **melt flow index (MFI / MFR)**, typically measured in **g/10 min** at a specified temperature and load (e.g., 2.16 kg at 230 °C for polypropylene).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Thermoplastics Used in Vehicle Bodies

Thermoplastics used in vehicle body engineering are classified based on **molecular structure** and **thermal-mechanical response**:

#### A. Based on Internal Structure

**1. Amorphous Thermoplastics**
- Polymer chains are arranged in a **random, disordered** manner.
- Exhibit a **gradual softening** over a temperature range (Tg).
- Properties: **Transparent**, low shrinkage, isotropic, poor chemical resistance, good dimensional stability.
- Vehicle examples: **ABS, PMMA (Acrylic), Polycarbonate (PC), Polystyrene (PS), PVC**.

**2. Semi-Crystalline Thermoplastics**
- Contain both **ordered crystalline regions** (lamellae/spherulites) and **disordered amorphous regions**.
- Exhibit a **sharp melting point (Tm)**.
- Properties: **Opaque**, higher density, better chemical resistance, higher wear resistance, anisotropic shrinkage.
- Vehicle examples: **Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene (PE), Polyamide (Nylon 6, 66), POM (Acetal), PET, PBT**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is the bumper fascia made of polypropylene (semi-crystalline) but the instrument cluster lens made of PMMA (amorphous)?"* The answer lies in **impact energy absorption** (semi-crystalline) versus **optical clarity** (amorphous).

#### B. Based on Engineering Performance (Industry Standard Classification)

| Category | Definition | Examples | Vehicle Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Commodity Plastics** | High volume, low cost | PE, PP, PVC, PS, ABS | Interior trim, battery cases |
| **Engineering Plastics** | Higher strength, thermal, dimensional stability | PA (Nylon), POM, PC, PBT, PET | Under-hood, structural |
| **High-Performance Plastics** | Extreme thermal/chemical/mechanical | PEEK, PPS, PSU, PEI | Aerospace, EV battery housings |

### 2.2 Detailed Properties of Key Automotive Thermoplastics

#### 1. Polypropylene (PP)
- **Density:** ≈ **0.905 g/cm³** (one of the lightest plastics).
- **Tg:** ≈ −20 °C; **Tm:** ≈ 165 °C.
- **Properties:** Excellent chemical resistance, fatigue resistance (hinge property), low cost, poor UV resistance (requires stabilization).
- **Vehicle Use:** Bumper fascias, dashboard carriers, door panels, battery trays, interior trim.
- **Form:** Usually **filled with talc, EPDM, or glass fibers** to enhance stiffness and impact strength.

#### 2. Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS)
- **Density:** ≈ **1.04 g/cm³**.
- **Tg:** ≈ 105 °C.
- **Properties:** Tough, hard, glossy, easy to electroplate (chrome-plated trim), good impact resistance.
- **Vehicle Use:** Instrument panels, center consoles, grilles, mirror housings, decorative interior/exterior trims.
- **Composition:** Acrylonitrile (chemical resistance) + Butadiene (toughness) + Styrene (rigidity + processability).

#### 3. Polycarbonate (PC)
- **Density:** ≈ **1.20 g/cm³**.
- **Tg:** ≈ 150 °C; **Amorphous**.
- **Properties:** Outstanding impact strength (250× glass), excellent transparency, high heat resistance, notch-sensitive.
- **Vehicle Use:** Headlamp lenses, sunroofs, panoramic roof panels, instrument cluster covers, EV battery module covers.
- **Blends:** PC/ABS (interior), PC/PBT (painted exterior body panels).

#### 4. Polyamide (Nylon - PA6, PA66)
- **Density:** ≈ **1.13–1.15 g/cm³**.
- **Tm:** PA6 ≈ 220 °C, PA66 ≈ 265 °C.
- **Properties:** High strength, high melting point, high moisture absorption (hygroscopic — must be dried before processing).
- **Vehicle Use:** Air intake manifolds, radiator end tanks, engine covers, door handles.
- **Form:** **Glass-fiber reinforced (GF-PA6)** for under-hood structural parts.

#### 5. Polyethylene (PE) — HDPE
- **Density:** ≈ **0.94–0.97 g/cm³** (HDPE).
- **Tm:** ≈ 130 °C.
- **Properties:** Excellent chemical resistance, impact strength, easy processing.
- **Vehicle Use:** **Plastic fuel tanks** (HDPE blow-molded with EVOH barrier layer for emission control), battery cases.

#### 6. Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)
- **Properties:** Flame retardant (self-extinguishing due to chlorine content), weather resistant, versatile (rigid or plasticized).
- **Vehicle Use:** Wiring harness insulation, dashboard skins, weatherstrips, underbody coatings (PVC plastisol).

#### 7. Polyoxymethylene (POM / Acetal)
- **Tm:** ≈ 165–175 °C.
- **Properties:** High stiffness, low friction, excellent dimensional stability, low moisture absorption.
- **Vehicle Use:** Fuel system components, clips, fasteners, window regulator gears.

#### 8. Polymethyl Methacrylate (PMMA / Acrylic)
- **Properties:** Outstanding optical clarity (>92% light transmission), UV resistant, scratch-prone.
- **Vehicle Use:** Tail lamp lenses, light guides, decorative panels.

### 2.3 Why Thermoplastics? — The Engineering "Why"

The automotive industry shifted from metals to thermoplastics for these reasons:

1. **Mass Reduction (Lightweighting)** — Plastics have **1/3 to 1/8 the density of steel**; this directly improves fuel efficiency and EV range.
2. **Corrosion Resistance** — No rust, no painting required for many parts.
3. **Design Freedom** — Complex shapes integrated into single parts (bumper fascia replaces 5–7 steel components).
4. **Energy Absorption** — Plastic bumpers absorb **5–10× more crash energy per unit mass** than steel.
5. **Recyclability** — Aligns with **ELV Directive (2000/53/EC)** mandating 85% recyclability.
6. **Cost Efficiency** — Lower tooling and assembly cost for high-volume parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU high-yield concept: **Total part cost = Raw material (40%) + Processing (30%) + Assembly (20%) + Finishing (10%)**. Plastics win on processing and assembly (one-shot molding vs. multi-stage metal stamping + welding + painting).

### 2.4 Limitations of Thermoplastics in Vehicle Bodies

- **Lower modulus and strength** compared to metals (must be glass/carbon fiber reinforced for structural use).
- **High coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE)** — 5–10× that of steel, causing **dimensional mismatch** in mixed-material bodies.
- **Temperature sensitivity** — Loss of stiffness above Tg.
- **Creep** — Long-term loading causes permanent deformation (must be checked with creep modulus data).
- **UV degradation** — Requires carbon black, UV stabilizers, or paint.
- **Flammability** — Most thermoplastics are combustible; require flame retardants.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Density of polymer** | $\rho$ in **g/cm³** | Mass calculation for lightweighting |
| 2 | **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_{uts} / \rho$ in **MPa·cm³/g** | Material selection index |
| 3 | **Specific Modulus** | $E / \rho$ in **GPa·cm³/g** | Stiffness-to-weight ratio |
| 4 | **Melt Flow Index** | **MFI** in **g/10 min** | Processability indicator |
| 5 | **Crystallinity (DSC method)** | $\%X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100$ | Where $\Delta H_f$ is sample heat of fusion, $\Delta H_f^0$ is 100% crystalline heat of fusion | Degree of crystallinity (semi-crystalline polymers) |
| 6 | **Tg (Fox equation for blends)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ | Predicts Tg of polymer blends (weights $w_i$) |
| 7 | **Heat Deflection Temperature** | **HDT** in **°C** at 0.45 MPa or 1.82 MPa | Service temperature limit |
| 8 | **Linear Thermal Expansion** | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | Where CTE $\alpha$ is in **/K** or **/°C** | Dimensional change with temperature |
| 9 | **Creep Modulus** | $E_c(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\epsilon(t)}$ | Long-term load-bearing capability |
| 10 | **Ashby Performance Index** (tie-rod) | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}^{2/3}}{\rho}$ | Optimal material for elastic light-weight tie |
| 11 | **Charpy Impact Energy** | $a_{cU}$ in **kJ/m²** | Energy absorption in crash |
| 12 | **Rule of Mixtures (filled polymer)** | $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ | Modulus of composite | 

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical pipe in Fox equation is replaced with mid-line notation; absolute value `|x|` is written as `\vert x\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above metrics directly guide **CAE (Computer Aided Engineering)** and **DFM (Design for Manufacturing)** decisions:
- **MFI** dictates **injection molding parameters** (temperature, pressure, cycle time).
- **Tg** dictates **paint oven temperature** (must exceed 80 °C for automotive e-coat; some plastics like PP need lower-temp primers).
- **CTE** dictates **joining tolerance** when plastic meets steel/aluminum in a **multi-material body (MMB)**.
- **Specific strength** drives the **lightweighting index** in EV battery enclosures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Degree of Crystallinity from DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)

**Problem Statement:** A sample of **Polypropylene (PP)** used in a bumper fascia is tested in DSC. The heat of fusion of the test sample is measured as $\Delta H_f = 80 \text{ J/g}$. The heat of fusion of 100% crystalline PP (literature value) is $\Delta H_f^0 = 207 \text{ J/g}$. Find the degree of crystallinity of the bumper material.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Write the fundamental definition of degree of crystallinity from DSC.

$$\%X_c = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100$$

This is the **ratio** of the actual latent heat absorbed by the sample during melting to the theoretical latent heat of a perfectly crystalline specimen of the same mass, expressed as a percentage.

**Step 2:** Identify the known values from the problem statement.

$$\Delta H_f = 80 \text{ J/g}, \quad \Delta H_f^0 = 207 \text{ J/g}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the formula.

$$\%X_c = \frac{80}{207} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Perform the numerical evaluation.

$$\%X_c = 0.3865 \times 100 = 38.65\%$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.

$$\%X_c \approx 38.65\%$$

This means the bumper PP has approximately **38.65% crystalline regions** and **61.35% amorphous regions**. Such a moderate crystallinity is **typical for impact-modified bumper-grade PP** — a higher crystallinity would increase stiffness but reduce impact resistance, defeating the purpose of a crash-absorbing fascia.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Board Tip: Always state the units and the **practical interpretation** of the result. Examiners award the "application mark" for connecting the math to engineering reality.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Glass Transition Temperature of a Polymer Blend (Fox Equation)

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle interior dashboard blend is composed of:
- **ABS** (weight fraction $w_1 = 0.60$, $T_{g,1} = 105 \text{ °C} = 378 \text{ K}$)
- **PC** (weight fraction $w_2 = 0.40$, $T_{g,2} = 150 \text{ °C} = 423 \text{ K}$)

Find the glass transition temperature of the PC/ABS blend using the Fox equation.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Recall the Fox equation (1956) for the glass transition of miscible polymer blends. Note: temperatures **must be in Kelvin**.

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \sum_i \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

**Step 2:** Convert Celsius to Kelvin (since Fox equation is derived from free-volume theory, which is thermodynamic).

$$T_{g,1} = 105 + 273 = 378 \text{ K}$$
$$T_{g,2} = 150 + 273 = 423 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values into the Fox equation.

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.60}{378} + \frac{0.40}{423}$$

**Step 4:** Compute each term.

$$\frac{0.60}{378} = 0.001587 \text{ K}^{-1}$$
$$\frac{0.40}{423} = 0.000946 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Sum the terms.

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 0.001587 + 0.000946 = 0.002533 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6:** Take the reciprocal.

$$T_g = \frac{1}{0.002533} = 394.79 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7:** Convert back to Celsius.

$$T_g = 394.79 - 273 = 121.79 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 8:** Final result and interpretation.

$$\boxed{T_g \approx 121.8 \text{ °C}}$$

The PC/ABS blend has a Tg **higher than pure ABS (105 °C)** and **lower than pure PC (150 °C)**, exactly as expected. This Tg is sufficient for the dashboard to survive the **paint-bake oven at 140 °C** without softening, and the blend is widely used in **automotive interior applications** for this reason.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Strength Comparison for Lightweighting

**Problem Statement:** Compare the specific strength of a **glass-fiber reinforced Nylon 66 (GF-PA66)** bumper beam versus **mild steel (MS)** used in older bumpers.

Given:
- GF-PA66: $\rho_p = 1.45 \text{ g/cm³}$, $\sigma_{uts} = 180 \text{ MPa}$
- Mild Steel: $\rho_s = 7.85 \text{ g/cm³}$, $\sigma_{uts} = 400 \text{ MPa}$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Write the specific strength formula.

$$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$$

**Step 2:** Compute for GF-PA66.

$$\left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho}\right)_{GF-PA66} = \frac{180 \text{ MPa}}{1.45 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 124.1 \text{ MPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3 / \text{g}$$

**Step 3:** Compute for Mild Steel.

$$\left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho}\right)_{MS} = \frac{400 \text{ MPa}}{7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 50.96 \text{ MPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3 / \text{g}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio.

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{124.1}{50.96} \approx 2.44$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.

The GF-PA66 has a **specific strength 2.44× that of mild steel**, meaning a structural bumper beam can be made **2.44 times stronger for the same mass**, or **equally strong at less than half the mass**. This is the mathematical foundation of automotive **lightweighting** for ICE and EV platforms.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Key Automotive Thermoplastics

| Property | PP (Talc-filled) | ABS | PC | PA66 (GF 30%) | POM | HDPE | PMMA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 0.97 | 1.04 | 1.20 | 1.45 | 1.42 | 0.95 | 1.18 |
| **Tg (°C)** | −20 | 105 | 150 | 50 (dry) | −60 | −120 | 105 |
| **Tm (°C)** | 165 | — (amorphous) | — (amorphous) | 265 | 175 | 130 | — (amorphous) |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 25 | 40 | 65 | 180 | 70 | 30 | 70 |
| **Elongation at Break (%)** | 400 | 30 | 110 | 3 | 40 | 600 | 5 |
| **Modulus (GPa)** | 1.7 | 2.3 | 2.4 | 10.0 | 3.0 | 0.8 | 3.2 |
| **HDT @ 0.45 MPa (°C)** | 130 | 100 | 140 | 250 | 170 | 80 | 95 |
| **Moisture Absorption** | Very Low | Low | Low | High | Low | Very Low | Low |
| **UV Resistance** | Poor | Fair | Good | Good | Fair | Fair | Excellent |
| **Recyclability** | High | High | High | High | High | High | High |
| **Cost (₹/kg, indicative)** | 90 | 130 | 220 | 320 | 280 | 100 | 180 |
| **Typical Vehicle Use** | Bumper fascia, dashboard carrier | Interior trim, grilles | Headlamp lens, sunroof | Intake manifold, engine cover | Fuel system clips, gears | Fuel tank, battery case | Tail lamp lens |

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important KTU revision artifact** for the polymer section. Memorize the densities, Tg values, and primary applications — these are the most frequently tested items.

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Linear Thermal Expansion Dimensional Check

**Problem Statement:** A polycarbonate (PC) sunroof panel of length $L_0 = 1200 \text{ mm}$ is installed in an aluminum roof frame at 25 °C. The car's paint-bake oven reaches 140 °C. The CTE values are: $\alpha_{PC} = 65 \times 10^{-6} \text{/°C}$, $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{/°C}$. Find the **differential expansion** between the PC and the Al frame opening.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Compute the expansion of the PC panel.

$$\Delta L_{PC} = \alpha_{PC} \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T = 65 \times 10^{-6} \times 1200 \times (140 - 25)$$

$$\Delta L_{PC} = 65 \times 10^{-6} \times 1200 \times 115$$

$$\Delta L_{PC} = 65 \times 10^{-6} \times 138000 = 8.97 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the expansion of the Al frame opening (same $L_0$).

$$\Delta L_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 1200 \times 115 = 3.17 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the differential expansion.

$$\Delta L_{diff} = \Delta L_{PC} - \Delta L_{Al} = 8.97 - 3.17 = 5.80 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

The PC panel expands **5.80 mm more** than the Al frame. This **mismatch must be absorbed by the rubber seal and the mounting clips**; otherwise, the panel would buckle or crack. This is why **multi-material body (MMB)** design always includes **CTE matching analysis** and **flexible mounting systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Molecular Architecture: Thermoplastic vs Thermoset

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TP["THERMOPLASTIC: Linear Chains with Weak Bonds"]
        A1["Chain 1: -C-C-C-C-C-C-"] -.->|Van der Waals| A2["Chain 2: -C-C-C-C-C-C-"]
        A2 -.->|Van der Waals| A3["Chain 3: -C-C-C-C-C-C-"]
        A1 -.->|Heating| H1["Softens, flows, can be remolded"]
        H1 -.->|Cooling| A1
    end
    subgraph TS["THERMOSET: Crosslinked Network with Covalent Bonds"]
        B1["Chain A"] ---|Covalent Crosslink| B2["Chain B"]
        B2 ---|Covalent Crosslink| B3["Chain C"]
        B1 ---|Covalent Crosslink| B3
        B1 -.->|Heating| H2["Chars and degrades; cannot be remelted"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The solid lines `---` represent strong covalent bonds (in thermosets). The dashed lines `-.->` represent weak secondary bonds (in thermoplastics). This single distinction explains all thermal, mechanical, and recyclability differences.

### 4.2 Classification Flow Chart of Automotive Thermoplastics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Automotive Thermoplastics"] --> Struct{"Internal Structure?"}
    Struct -->|Amorphous| Amor["Amorphous TPs"]
    Struct -->|Semi-Crystalline| Semi["Semi-Crystalline TPs"]
    Amor --> AmorEx["ABS, PMMA, PC, PS, PVC"]
    Semi --> SemiEx["PP, PE, PA Nylon, POM, PET, PBT"]
    AmorEx --> AmorUse["Interior trim, lenses, headlamps, sunroofs"]
    SemiEx --> SemiUse["Bumpers, fuel tanks, manifolds, gears"]
    Start --> Perf{"Performance Class?"}
    Perf -->|Commodity| Com["PE, PP, PVC, PS"]
    Perf -->|Engineering| Eng["ABS, PA, POM, PC, PBT"]
    Perf -->|High-Performance| HiP["PEEK, PPS, PSU"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Material Selection Workflow for a Bumper

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Req["Design Requirements: Bumper Beam + Fascia"] --> Step1["Step 1: Define Functional Loads"]
    Step1 --> S1Out["Impact: 4 km/h no damage\nWeight Target: less than 4 kg\nService T: minus 40 to plus 80 C\nCost: less than Rs. 1500"]
    S1Out --> Step2["Step 2: Screen by Specific Strength"]
    Step2 --> S2Out["Ashby Plot: Specific Modulus vs Specific Strength"]
    S2Out --> Step3["Step 3: Identify Candidate Materials"]
    Step3 --> Mat1["GF-PA66 (beam)"]
    Step3 --> Mat2["Talc-filled PP EPDM (fascia)"]
    Mat1 --> Proc1["Process: Injection Molding"]
    Mat2 --> Proc2["Process: Injection Molding"]
    Proc1 --> Test1["Test: Crash Simulation CAE"]
    Proc2 --> Test2["Test: Pedestrian Protection"]
    Test1 --> OK["Design Validated"]
    Test2 --> OK
```

### 4.4 Processing Methods Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Thermoplastic Granules"] --> IM["Injection Molding"]
    P --> BM["Blow Molding"]
    P --> EXT["Extrusion"]
    P --> TH["Thermoforming"]
    P --> RIM["Rotational Molding"]
    IM --> App1["Bumpers, dashboards, grilles"]
    BM --> App2["Fuel tanks, air ducts"]
    EXT --> App3["Profiles, tubes, weatherstrips"]
    TH --> App4["Door panels, headliner substrates"]
    RIM --> App5["Large hollow parts, fenders"]
```

### 4.5 Multi-Material Body (MMB) Integration Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MMB["Modern Vehicle Body: Multi-Material Architecture"] --> Zone1["Front Zone: Crumple, Plastics and Aluminum"]
    Zone1 --> Z1Mat["PP fascia, GF-PA beam, Al hood"]
    MMB --> Zone2["Passenger Cell: High Strength Steel"]
    Zone2 --> Z2Mat["Hot-stamped boron steel, UHSS"]
    MMB --> Zone3["Rear Zone: Mixed, Plastics and Steel"]
    Zone3 --> Z3Mat["SMC tailgate, PP bumper, MS rails"]
    MMB --> Zone4["Roof: Lightweight, Glass and PC"]
    Zone4 --> Z4Mat["PC sunroof, glass panel, Al frame"]
    MMB --> Zone5["Underbody: Composite, Shield"]
    Zone5 --> Z5Mat["Glass fiber PP, PA66 battery tray"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams above are not literal photographs of the molecular structure (which would require electron microscopy images). Instead, they map the **functional relationships** between molecular architecture, classification, processing, and real-world vehicle body application — which is the **KTU Board Examiner's** expected level of schematic analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
**"Define thermoplastics. Why are they preferred over thermosetting plastics for modern vehicle body applications?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Bloom's Level: Remembering + Understanding):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** Thermoplastics are linear or slightly branched chain polymers that **soften on heating and harden on cooling** without any chemical change. The process is **reversible and repeatable**.
- **Reasons for preference over thermosets (2 Marks):**
  1. **Recyclable** — meet ELV Directive requirements for 85% end-of-life recoverability. Thermosets cannot be remelted.
  2. **Higher impact resistance** — better crash energy absorption in bumpers and fascias.
  3. **Faster cycle time** in injection molding → higher productivity.
  4. **Lower density (≈ 0.9–1.4 g/cm³ vs 1.5–2.0 g/cm³)** → lightweighting.
  5. **Weldable** using ultrasonic, hot plate, and vibration welding.

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**"Distinguish between amorphous and semi-crystalline thermoplastics. Give two examples of each used in vehicles."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Bloom's Level: Understanding):**

| Feature | Amorphous | Semi-Crystalline |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Random chain arrangement | Ordered crystalline + disordered regions |
| Thermal behavior | Gradual softening at Tg | Sharp melting at Tm |
| Transparency | Often transparent | Opaque |
| Shrinkage | Low, isotropic | Higher, anisotropic |
| Chemical resistance | Lower | Higher |
| **Vehicle example 1** | **PMMA** (tail lamp lens) | **PP** (bumper fascia) |
| **Vehicle example 2** | **PC** (headlamp lens) | **PA66** (intake manifold) |

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **molecular structure** of thermoplastics and differentiate it from thermosetting plastics. Discuss the **bonding mechanisms** and their effect on the **recyclability and processing** of these materials. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **properties, advantages, and automotive applications** of the following thermoplastics:
   (i) Polypropylene (PP) — bumper fascia
   (ii) Polycarbonate (PC) — headlamp lens
   (iii) Polyamide 66 (PA66) — air intake manifold
   
   Compare their **density, Tg, tensile strength, and typical processing method**. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Molecular Structure and Bonding (7 Marks):**

1. **Thermoplastic Structure (2 Marks):**
   - Linear/slightly branched long-chain molecules.
   - Held together by **weak secondary bonds** — Van der Waals forces, dipole interactions.
   - No primary covalent crosslinks between chains.
   - **Neat sketch:** Draw parallel wavy lines (chains) connected by dashed lines (secondary bonds). Label: Chain, Secondary Bond, Heat Arrow showing reversible softening.

2. **Thermoset Structure (2 Marks):**
   - Three-dimensional **network** of crosslinked chains.
   - Held together by **strong covalent crosslinks** (formed by curing/irreversible chemical reaction).
   - **Neat sketch:** Draw a network with solid lines connecting all chains at junction points. Label: Chain, Covalent Crosslink, Permanent Curing Arrow.

3. **Effect on Properties (3 Marks):**
   - **Thermoplastic:** Reversible softening, weldable, recyclable, soluble in some solvents, reprocessable.
   - **Thermoset:** Irreversible curing, infusible, insoluble, better creep resistance, better thermal stability up to decomposition point, **not remeltable** — only mechanically ground for filler use.
   - **Processing implications:** Thermoplastics → Injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, thermoforming. Thermosets → Compression molding (SMC), resin transfer molding (RTM), reaction injection molding (RIM).

**Part (b) — Three Thermoplastics Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Property | **PP (Bumper Fascia)** | **PC (Headlamp Lens)** | **PA66 (Intake Manifold)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 0.91 (unfilled) → 0.97 (talc-filled) | 1.20 | 1.13 (dry) → 1.45 (GF 30%) |
| **Tg (°C)** | −20 | 150 | 50 (dry) / 30 (conditioned) |
| **Tm (°C)** | 165 | — (amorphous) | 265 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 25 (unfilled) → 35 (talc-filled) | 65 | 85 (unfilled) → 180 (GF 30%) |
| **Structure** | Semi-crystalline | Amorphous | Semi-crystalline |
| **Key Property** | Lightweight, hinge property | Optical clarity, impact | High strength, heat resistance |
| **Main Limitation** | Poor UV, paint adhesion | Notch sensitive, scratches | Hygroscopic (must dry) |
| **Processing Method** | Injection molding | Injection molding + coating | Injection molding (with drying) |
| **Vehicle Application** | Bumper fascia, dashboard carrier, door panel | Headlamp lens, sunroof, instrument cluster | Intake manifold, radiator end tank, engine cover |

**Application Justification (1 Mark):**
- **PP** for bumper fascia: Lowest density gives 30–40% mass reduction over steel; talc filling gives the stiffness needed for fascia retention; EPDM rubber phase absorbs minor impacts.
- **PC** for headlamp: 90%+ light transmission; survives under-hood temperatures; can be metalized for reflector housings.
- **PA66 (GF 30%)** for intake manifold: Withstands continuous 120–150 °C exhaust manifold heat; glass fibers carry structural load; replaces aluminum for 40% mass savings.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice for KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a flow diagram the **classification of thermoplastics** used in vehicle body engineering based on their **structure and performance grade**. Give **at least three examples** for each class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's polycarbonate sunroof has length $L_0 = 1000 \text{ mm}$ at 25 °C. The car's paint-bake oven temperature is 130 °C. The CTE of polycarbonate is $\alpha_{PC} = 65 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$ and the aluminum frame is $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$.
   (i) Calculate the differential expansion between the PC panel and Al frame. **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) Discuss **two design strategies** to accommodate this CTE mismatch in a multi-material body. **(4 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Classification (7 Marks):**

**Structure-Based Classification (3.5 Marks):**
- **Amorphous Thermoplastics** — Random chain arrangement. Examples: **ABS, PC, PMMA, PS, PVC**.
- **Semi-Crystalline Thermoplastics** — Mix of crystalline lamellae and amorphous regions. Examples: **PP, PE, PA, POM, PET**.

**Performance-Based Classification (3.5 Marks):**
- **Commodity Plastics** — High volume, low cost. Examples: **PE (HDPE), PP, PVC, PS**.
- **Engineering Plastics** — Higher performance. Examples: **ABS, PC, PA, POM, PBT, PET**.
- **High-Performance Plastics** — Specialty, extreme conditions. Examples: **PEEK, PPS, PSU, PEI, LCP**.

(Flow diagram should clearly show branching and example linkages.)

**Part (b) — Numerical and Design (7 Marks):**

**(i) Differential Expansion (3 Marks):**

$$\Delta T = 130 - 25 = 105 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta L_{PC} = 65 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 105 = 6.825 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta L_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 105 = 2.415 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta L_{diff} = 6.825 - 2.415 = 4.41 \text{ mm}$$

The PC panel grows **4.41 mm more** than the Al frame opening.

**(ii) Design Strategies (4 Marks):**

**Strategy 1 — Flexible Mounting System (2 Marks):**
Use **elastomeric rubber gaskets and slotted mounting holes** that allow the panel to slide ±3 mm in the frame. This is industry standard for all sunroof and windshield installations. EPDM rubber seals with bell-mouth geometry absorb the differential strain without transmitting stress to the polycarbonate.

**Strategy 2 — Geometry Compensation (2 Marks):**
Design the frame opening with **ovalized mounting holes** in the direction of maximum CTE mismatch. Use **sliding clips** instead of fixed fasteners. Provide adequate **edge clearance (≥ 5 mm)** between the PC edge and the Al frame to allow for both thermal expansion and manufacturing tolerances.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Tg and Tm** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if a student uses Tg where Tm is required (or vice versa) in a numerical.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C → K in the Fox equation** — full marks lost if temperatures are used in Celsius.
> 3. **Naming a material but not justifying the selection** — e.g., "ABS is used for dashboards" is **incomplete**; you must say "ABS is used because it offers good impact strength (15 kJ/m²), high surface gloss for Class-A finish, and easy electroplating for decorative trims."
> 4. **Sketching without labels** — unlabeled molecular structures get 0 out of the 2 sketch marks.
> 5. **Spelling "thermoplastic" as "thermoplastic" with one 'l'** — examiners mark spelling errors in the definition question.
> 6. **Mixing up PP and PE** — PP has Tg ≈ −20 °C, PE has Tg ≈ −120 °C. Examiners test this explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 2 (Thermoplastics)**

✅ **Thermoplastic = linear polymer + weak secondary bonds = recyclable + remeltable + reversible softening**. This is the single most-tested line in the syllabus.

✅ **Tg (Glass Transition)** — softens; **Tm (Melting)** — for semi-crystalline only. Amorphous polymers have **no Tm**, only Tg.

✅ **Vehicle Body Application Triad to Memorize:**
   - **Bumper fascia** → PP (talc/EPDM filled)
   - **Headlamp lens** → PC (polycarbonate)
   - **Fuel tank** → HDPE (with EVOH barrier)
   - **Dashboard** → ABS or PC/ABS blend
   - **Intake manifold** → GF-PA66 (glass-filled nylon 66)
   - **Tail lamp lens** → PMMA (acrylic)
   - **Wire insulation** → PVC
   - **Fuel system clips** → POM (acetal)
   - **EV battery enclosure** → GF-PA66 or PP composite

✅ **Three Density Values to Memorize:**
   - **PP ≈ 0.91 g/cm³** (lightest)
   - **PC ≈ 1.20 g/cm³**
   - **GF-PA66 (30%) ≈ 1.45 g/cm³** (heaviest common automotive TP)

✅ **Three Numerical Formulas for Exam:**
   - **Crystallinity:** $\%X_c = (\Delta H_f / \Delta H_f^0) \times 100$
   - **Fox Tg:** $1/T_g = \sum w_i / T_{g,i}$ (always use Kelvin)
   - **Linear Expansion:** $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$

✅ **Why thermoplastics win:** Lightweighting (1/8 the density of steel), Corrosion-free, Design freedom, Mass production, **Recyclable (ELV Directive compliance)**.

✅ **Why NOT thermoplastics:** Lower modulus, High CTE (5–10× steel), UV degradation, Creep, Flammability.

✅ **Three KTU Buzzwords to Use in Answers:**
   1. "Multi-material body (MMB) architecture"
   2. "Class-A surface finish"
   3. "End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) Directive compliance"

✅ **Difference from thermoset (always mention in comparison answers):** Thermosets have **covalent crosslinks** (irreversible, not remeltable); thermoplastics have **secondary bonds** (reversible, remeltable, recyclable).

✅ **Always justify material selection** in answers using the **triangle of: Property + Process + Cost**.

---

**End of Module 2 — Thermoplastics | KTU 2024 Scheme PEAUT741**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Bearing Plastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Load Bearing Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Load Bearing Plastics** are high-performance polymer-based composite materials engineered to sustain significant structural and mechanical loads in a vehicle body, replacing conventional metals (steel and aluminium) in primary and secondary load-carrying applications. They are typically **fiber-reinforced thermosetting or thermoplastic composites** in which a polymer **matrix** is fortified with high-strength **reinforcements** (glass, carbon, aramid, or natural fibers) to enhance specific strength, specific stiffness, fatigue endurance, and impact absorption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** A load-bearing plastic is *not* simply any plastic used in a car — it must contribute to the structural integrity of the vehicle (load transfer, energy absorption, or stiffness provision). Examples include a composite leaf spring, a SMC engine hood, a CFRP roof panel, and a reinforced plastic bumper beam.

### 1.2 Classification of Load Bearing Plastics

```
Load Bearing Plastics
├── Based on Matrix Type
│   ├── Thermosetting (e.g., Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Epoxy, Phenolic)
│   └── Thermoplastic (e.g., Nylon, PBT, PP, PPS, PEEK)
│
├── Based on Reinforcement
│   ├── Glass Fiber Reinforced Plastics (GFRP)
│   ├── Carbon Fiber Reinforced Plastics (CFRP)
│   ├── Aramid Fiber Reinforced Plastics (AFRP)
│   └── Natural Fiber Reinforced Plastics (NFRP)
│
└── Based on Manufacturing Process
    ├── Sheet Molding Compound (SMC)
    ├── Bulk Molding Compound (BMC)
    ├── Reaction Injection Molding (RIM / RRIM / SRIM)
    ├── Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)
    └── Compression / Injection Molding
```

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bamboo-Reinforced Mud Wall:** Imagine a mud wall (weak in tension) reinforced with bamboo strips (strong in tension). The mud holds the bamboo together and transfers load, while the bamboo carries the heavy tensile and bending stresses. The combination gives strength far greater than either alone. **A load-bearing plastic works exactly like this wall:** the **polymer matrix** is the "mud" (binds, transfers load, protects fibers), and the **fibers** are the "bamboo" (carry the actual mechanical load). In a car, this allows a composite part to weigh **40–60 % less than steel** while offering comparable strength — directly improving **fuel efficiency** and **crash energy absorption**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Density of GFRP:** **1.5 – 2.0 g/cm³**
- **Density of CFRP:** **1.5 – 1.8 g/cm³**
- **Density of Mild Steel (for comparison):** **7.85 g/cm³**
- **Standard Tensile Strength of SMC:** **70 – 150 MPa**
- **Standard Tensile Modulus of CFRP:** **100 – 250 GPa**
- **Rule of Mixtures Weight Limit:** fiber volume fraction typically **30 % – 60 %** for structural composites.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are Plastics Used as Load-Bearing Materials in Vehicles?

1. **Weight Reduction:** Specific gravity ~ **1/5** of steel → reduced fuel consumption & EV range extension.
2. **Corrosion Resistance:** Polymer matrices do not rust → extended service life.
3. **Design Freedom:** Complex curved shapes moldable in one piece (e.g., bumper fascia with integrated grille).
4. **Energy Absorption:** Progressive crushing behaviour yields excellent crashworthiness.
5. **Vibration Damping:** Higher internal damping coefficient reduces NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness).
6. **Tooling Cost vs Volume:** High initial tooling cost, but low per-unit cost at mass production (> 30,000 units/year).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-limitation:** Plastics have **lower absolute stiffness** than steel, so unreinforced plastics cannot replace steel in primary safety structures (e.g., A-pillars, B-pillars) without reinforcement.

### 2.2 Mechanics of Load Bearing Composites — Structured Logic

- **Step 1 — Load Transfer:** External load is transferred from the polymer matrix to the high-strength fibers through the **fiber-matrix interface** (shear-lag mechanism).
- **Step 2 — Stress Distribution:** Fibers carry the majority of tensile load (fibers are ~ 10–100× stronger in tension than the matrix).
- **Step 3 — Matrix Role:** The matrix binds fibers, protects them from environmental damage, and carries compressive and shear loads.
- **Step 4 — Failure Criterion:** Fiber-dominated failure → tensile fracture; matrix-dominated failure → delamination and shear failure.
- **Step 5 — Anisotropic Behaviour:** Properties depend on **fiber orientation angle (θ)** relative to load direction.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Formula / Relation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|-------|------------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$ | **Rule of Mixtures** (Tensile Strength, Voigt Model) | MPa |
| 2 | $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | **Rule of Mixtures** (Young's Modulus, longitudinal) | GPa |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** (transverse modulus) | GPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}$ | **Specific Strength** (Strength-to-Weight Ratio) | $\text{N·m/kg}$ |
| 5 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | **Specific Stiffness** (Modulus-to-Weight Ratio) | $\text{kN·m/kg}$ |
| 6 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | **Volume Fraction Conservation** | — |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_c^{\text{actual}}}{\sigma_c^{\text{theoretical}}}$ | **Reinforcement Efficiency Factor** | — |
| 8 | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | **Density of Composite** (Rule of Mixtures) | $\text{g/cm}^3$ |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{\pi d L}$ | **Fiber-Matrix Interface Shear Stress** | MPa |
| 10 | $\sigma_{fiber} = \dfrac{E_f}{E_c} \cdot \sigma_c$ | **Fiber Stress from Composite Strain** (iso-strain) | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU derivations, $V_f$ is fiber volume fraction, $V_m$ is matrix volume fraction, $E$ denotes Young's modulus, and $\sigma$ denotes tensile stress. Subscript $c$ = composite, $f$ = fiber, $m$ = matrix.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why KTU Emphasizes This Topic

Load-bearing plastics form the **backbone of modern lightweight automotive design**, directly mandated by:
- **CAFE / Bharat Stage VI fuel-economy regulations**
- **EV range extension targets** (every 10 % weight reduction → ~ 6–8 % range gain)
- **Crash safety norms** (AIS-008, ECE R94) which plastics meet through controlled energy absorption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Tensile Strength (Iso-Strain Model)

**Assumption:** Fibers and matrix experience the **same strain** $\epsilon_c = \epsilon_f = \epsilon_m$ (valid when fibers are continuous, aligned, and perfectly bonded — Voigt Model).

**Step 1:** Apply force equilibrium on the composite cross-section:

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

**Step 2:** Express each force as stress times area:

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

**Step 3:** Divide throughout by the total area $A_c$:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f \left(\dfrac{A_f}{A_c}\right) + \sigma_m \left(\dfrac{A_m}{A_c}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** For uniform cross-section, $A_f / A_c = V_f$ and $A_m / A_c = V_m$:

$$
\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $V_m = 1 - V_f$ for completeness:

$$
\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + (1 - V_f) \sigma_m
$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures for tensile strength** under iso-strain conditions, used universally in KTU problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Strength Comparison (Steel vs CFRP)

**Given Data (typical values):**

| Material | Density $\rho$ ($\text{g/cm}^3$) | Tensile Strength $\sigma$ (MPa) |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Mild Steel | **7.85** | **400** |
| CFRP (unidirectional) | **1.60** | **1500** |

**Step 1:** Convert density to $\text{kg/m}^3$ by multiplying by 1000:

$$
\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7850 \; \text{kg/m}^3, \quad \rho_{\text{CFRP}} = 1600 \; \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Specific Strength of Steel:

$$
\sigma_{sp,\text{steel}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{steel}}}{\rho_{\text{steel}}} = \dfrac{400 \times 10^6}{7850} = 50{,}955 \; \text{N·m/kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Specific Strength of CFRP:

$$
\sigma_{sp,\text{CFRP}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{CFRP}}}{\rho_{\text{CFRP}}} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^6}{1600} = 937{,}500 \; \text{N·m/kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_{sp,\text{CFRP}}}{\sigma_{sp,\text{steel}}} = \dfrac{937{,}500}{50{,}955} \approx 18.4
$$

**Interpretation:** CFRP is **~18× stronger per unit weight** than mild steel — this single result explains why CFRP is used in F1 monocoques, supercar chassis, and modern EV battery enclosures.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fiber Stress at Composite Failure (Critical Fiber Volume Fraction)

A composite is **fiber-dominated** only above a **critical fiber volume fraction** $V_{f,\text{crit}}$. Below this, the matrix fails before the fibers reach their capacity.

**Step 1:** At composite failure under iso-strain, the matrix strain equals fiber strain:

$$
\epsilon_c^* = \epsilon_m^* = \epsilon_f^*
$$

**Step 2:** Fiber stress at composite failure:

$$
\sigma_{f}^* = E_f \cdot \epsilon_c^*
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Rule of Mixtures at composite failure:

$$
\sigma_c^* = V_f \sigma_f^* + (1 - V_f) \sigma_m^*
$$

**Step 4:** To ensure fibers carry the load (matrix failure strain $\epsilon_m^*$ is lower than fiber failure strain $\epsilon_f^*$), set:

$$
V_{f,\text{crit}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{m,u} - \sigma_{m}^*}{\sigma_{f,u} - \sigma_{m}^*}
$$

where $\sigma_{m,u}$ = ultimate matrix strength, $\sigma_{f,u}$ = ultimate fiber strength.

**Typical result:** For E-glass / polyester, $V_{f,\text{crit}} \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$; commercial structural composites use $V_f = 0.30$ to $0.60$ to stay comfortably above this threshold.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Material Selection for Vehicle Body

| **Property** | **Mild Steel** | **Aluminium (6061)** | **GFRP (SMC)** | **CFRP** |
|--------------|----------------|----------------------|----------------|----------|
| Density ($\text{g/cm}^3$) | 7.85 | 2.70 | 1.80 | 1.60 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 400 | 310 | 150 | 1500 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 210 | 70 | 12 | 140 |
| Specific Strength ($\text{kN·m/kg}$) | 51 | 115 | 83 | 938 |
| Specific Stiffness ($\text{MN·m/kg}$) | 26.7 | 25.9 | 6.7 | 87.5 |
| Corrosion Resistance | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Tooling Cost | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Recyclability | Excellent | Good | Difficult | Difficult |
| Typical Vehicle Use | Body-in-White | Bonnet, Doors | Bumper, Hood | Roof, Battery Enclosure |

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU viva and Part A questions — memorize the **density and specific strength values** for at least steel, aluminium, and CFRP.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Load Transfer Mechanism in a Fiber-Reinforced Plastic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["External Mechanical Load"] --> B["Polymer Matrix Receives Load"]
    B --> C["Shear Stress at Fiber Matrix Interface"]
    C --> D["Load Transferred to High Strength Fibers"]
    D --> E["Fibers Carry Tensile Load Along Axis"]
    E --> F["Composite Demonstrates Enhanced Strength and Stiffness"]
    B --> G["Matrix Carries Compressive and Shear Loads"]
    G --> F
    F --> H["Engineering Application in Vehicle Body"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification Hierarchy for KTU Viva

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Load Bearing Plastics in Vehicles"] --> B["Matrix Type"]
    A --> C["Reinforcement Type"]
    A --> D["Process Type"]
    B --> B1["Thermosetting - Polyester, Epoxy, Vinyl Ester"]
    B --> B2["Thermoplastic - Nylon, PBT, PP, PPS"]
    C --> C1["Glass Fiber - GFRP - Low Cost"]
    C --> C2["Carbon Fiber - CFRP - High Performance"]
    C --> C3["Aramid Fiber - AFRP - Impact Resistant"]
    C --> C4["Natural Fiber - NFRP - Eco Friendly"]
    D --> D1["SMC - Sheet Molding Compound"]
    D --> D2["BMC - Bulk Molding Compound"]
    D --> D3["RTM - Resin Transfer Molding"]
    D --> D4["RIM - Reaction Injection Molding"]
    D --> D5["Compression Molding"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Selection Logic for Vehicle Body Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start["New Vehicle Body Component Required"] --> Q1{"Load Bearing Yes or No"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes - Structural"| Q2{"Production Volume"}
    Q1 -->|"No - Trim"| Trim["Use Non Structural Plastic"]
    Q2 -->|"Low Volume - Under 5000 units"| CFRP1["Use CFRP with Autoclave or RTM"]
    Q2 -->|"Medium Volume - 5000 to 30000 units"| GFRP1["Use GFRP with RTM or Compression Molding"]
    Q2 -->|"High Volume - Above 30000 units"| SMC1["Use SMC with Compression Molding"]
    CFRP1 --> App1["F1 Monocoque, Supercar Roof, EV Battery Enclosure"]
    GFRP1 --> App2["Hood, Door Panel, Leaf Spring"]
    SMC1 --> App3["Bumper Beam, Engine Cover, Fender"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Representation — Composite Leaf Spring (Genuine KTU Exam Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Composite Leaf Spring Assembly"] --> B["Eye at Fixed End"]
    A --> C["Tapered Composite Leaf Body"]
    C --> D["Unidirectional Glass Fiber Epoxy Layup"]
    D --> E["Fiber Orientation Along Length for Maximum Bending Load"]
    A --> F["Center Bolt for Axle Clamping"]
    A --> G["Eye at Free End"]
    E --> H["Weight Reduction 50 to 70 Percent vs Steel"]
    E --> I["Life Double that of Steel Spring"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** The **glass-fiber/epoxy leaf spring** designed by GKN and demonstrated on the Chevrolet Corvette in the 1980s is the **classic KTU exam example** of load-bearing plastics. Highlight the **unidirectional fiber lay-up** because bending stress is maximum along the spring's longitudinal axis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define load-bearing plastics. Give two examples of their use in vehicle body engineering.*

**Model Answer:**

Load-bearing plastics are fiber-reinforced polymer composites designed to carry significant structural loads in a vehicle, replacing metals like steel or aluminium in primary and secondary structural components.

They possess high specific strength and stiffness due to the synergistic action of the polymer matrix (which binds and protects the fibers) and the reinforcing fibers (which carry most of the load).

**Two Examples:**
1. **GFRP leaf spring** replacing conventional steel leaf spring in commercial vehicles.
2. **SMC (Sheet Molding Compound) engine hood** in passenger cars for weight reduction.

(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark mechanism + 1 mark examples)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State the Rule of Mixtures for composite tensile strength and explain its significance.*

**Model Answer:**

The Rule of Mixtures (Voigt Model, iso-strain) states:

$$
\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m
$$

**Significance:**
- It predicts the tensile strength of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite.
- It shows that **increasing fiber volume fraction linearly increases composite strength** (provided $V_f > V_{f,\text{crit}}$).
- It is the foundation for material selection and design of composite structural components like automotive leaf springs and bumper beams.

(3 marks: 1.5 marks equation + 1.5 marks significance)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] – Module 2 Choice A**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of load-bearing plastics** used in vehicle body engineering, with examples for each class. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Load-bearing plastics are classified based on **three criteria**:

**(i) Based on Matrix Type:**

| Type | Examples | Typical Use |
|------|----------|-------------|
| Thermosetting | Polyester, Vinyl ester, Epoxy, Phenolic | Body panels, leaf springs (cannot be remelted) |
| Thermoplastic | Nylon (PA), PBT, PP, PPS, PEEK | Under-hood brackets, connectors (recyclable) |

**(ii) Based on Reinforcement:**

| Type | Composition | Vehicle Application |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| GFRP | Glass fiber + polymer | Bumper, hood, leaf spring (most common, low cost) |
| CFRP | Carbon fiber + epoxy/polymer | Roof, chassis, battery enclosure (high cost, premium) |
| AFRP | Aramid (Kevlar) + polymer | Crash structures, armor |
| NFRP | Jute/Flax/Hemp + polymer | Interior trims (eco-friendly) |

**(iii) Based on Manufacturing Process:**

- **SMC (Sheet Molding Compound):** Chopped glass fiber + polyester resin; compression molded; used in **hoods, deck lids, fenders**.
- **BMC (Bulk Molding Compound):** Bulk dough; injection/compression molded; used in **headlamp housings**.
- **RTM (Resin Transfer Molding):** Dry fiber preform placed in closed mold, resin injected; used in **door panels, floor pans**.
- **RIM (Reaction Injection Molding):** Two reactive liquids mixed and injected; used in **flexible bumper fascia**.
- **Compression Molding:** High-pressure, high-temperature forming of SMC/BMC; high-volume production.

**(7 marks distributed: 2 + 3 + 2 across the three classifications with examples)**

---

**(b)** A **glass-fiber reinforced epoxy** composite has the following properties:
- Fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.40$
- Fiber tensile strength $\sigma_f = 2500 \; \text{MPa}$
- Matrix tensile strength $\sigma_m = 80 \; \text{MPa}$

Calculate the **composite tensile strength** using the Rule of Mixtures and the **specific strength** if the composite density is $1.95 \; \text{g/cm}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify matrix volume fraction:

$$
V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Rule of Mixtures:

$$
\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m
$$

$$
\sigma_c = (0.40)(2500) + (0.60)(80)
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 1000 + 48 = 1048 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert density:

$$
\rho_c = 1.95 \; \text{g/cm}^3 = 1950 \; \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Specific Strength:

$$
\sigma_{sp,c} = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{1048 \times 10^6}{1950}
$$

$$
\sigma_{sp,c} = 537{,}435 \; \text{N·m/kg} \approx 537.4 \; \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with steel ($\sigma_{sp,\text{steel}} \approx 51 \; \text{kN·m/kg}$):

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_{sp,c}}{\sigma_{sp,\text{steel}}} = \dfrac{537.4}{51} \approx 10.5
$$

**Conclusion:** The GFRP composite is **~10.5× stronger per unit weight** than mild steel.

**(7 marks: 2 for RoM, 2 for specific strength, 2 for comparison, 1 for conclusion)**

---

### **OR**

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] – Module 2 Choice B**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **manufacturing process of SMC (Sheet Molding Compound)**. List **three advantages** of SMC over steel for vehicle body panels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Manufacturing Process of SMC:**

1. **Resin Paste Preparation:** Unsaturated polyester resin is mixed with filler (calcium carbonate), catalyst, release agent, and thickener to form a viscous paste.
2. **Carrier Film:** The paste is sandwiched between two polyethylene carrier films.
3. **Fiber Chopping:** Continuous glass fiber rovings are chopped into ~25 mm lengths and dropped onto the lower paste-coated film.
4. **Compounding:** The upper film sandwiches the fibers; the assembly passes through compaction rollers that impregnate fibers with resin paste.
5. **Maturation:** The SMC sheet is stored at 25–40 °C for 1–5 days to allow thickening (B-stage), increasing viscosity for easy handling.
6. **Molding:** Cut SMC blanks are placed in a heated (140–160 °C) compression mold; pressure (5–10 MPa) is applied for 60–180 seconds, curing the thermoset part.

**Three Advantages of SMC over Steel for Body Panels:**

| # | Advantage | Explanation |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Weight Reduction (40–60 %)** | $\rho_{\text{SMC}} \approx 1.8 \; \text{g/cm}^3$ vs steel $7.85 \; \text{g/cm}^3$ |
| 2 | **Corrosion Resistance** | Polymer matrix does not rust; longer service life, no paint chipping |
| 3 | **Design Flexibility & Part Integration** | Complex curved shapes moldable in one piece; reduces assembly operations |

(7 marks: 4 for process steps, 3 for advantages)

---

**(b)** Explain the **load transfer mechanism** in a fiber-reinforced plastic with reference to the **shear-lag theory**. Why is the **fiber-matrix interface** critical? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Load Transfer Mechanism — Shear-Lag Theory (Cox, 1952):**

In an aligned short-fiber composite under tensile load:

1. The **polymer matrix** first receives the external load because it is in continuous contact with the load-introducing fixture.
2. Load is transferred from matrix to fiber through **shear stresses** at the cylindrical fiber-matrix interface.
3. At the **fiber ends**, stress in the fiber is zero; stress builds up gradually along the fiber length and reaches a maximum at the **fiber mid-length**.
4. The **interfacial shear stress** $\tau$ is given by:

$$
\tau(x) = \dfrac{n \, \sigma_f}{2} \left[ \dfrac{\cosh(\beta x)}{\cosh(\beta L/2)} \right]
$$

where $\beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, G_m}{E_f \, r^2 \ln(R/r)}}$ and $n$, $R$ are geometric parameters.

5. If the fiber length $L > L_c$ (**critical fiber length**), the fiber can be loaded to its ultimate strength $\sigma_{f,u}$. Critical length:

$$
L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{f,u} \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}
$$

where $d$ = fiber diameter, $\tau_i$ = interface shear strength.

**Why the Interface is Critical:**

- The interface is the **only path** for load transfer between matrix and fiber.
- Poor bonding → fiber "pull-out" failure → composite strength drops sharply.
- Good bonding → fiber fracture dominates → high composite strength.
- Surface treatments (silanes for glass, sizing for carbon) are therefore mandatory.

(7 marks: 3 for mechanism, 2 for critical length equation, 2 for interface criticality)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Load-Bearing Plastics Questions:**
>
