> 1. **Mixing up Rule of Mixtures for Strength vs Modulus.** Use $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$ for **strength**; use $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ for **modulus**. The inverse rule of mixtures is for **transverse modulus only** — using it for longitudinal modulus loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion in Specific Strength.** Density must be in **kg/m³**, strength in **Pa (N/m²)**. Writing 1500/1.6 without unit conversion is **wrong** and loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring $V_m = 1 - V_f$** in derivations. Always state this explicitly at Step 1.
> 4. **Calling SMC a thermoplastic.** SMC is a **thermoset** — it cannot be remelted. Confusing this loses marks in classification questions.
> 5. **Not mentioning the role of silane coupling agents** on glass fiber when explaining the fiber-matrix interface. This is a **high-frequency KTU 2024 marker** for Part B answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Load-bearing plastics = fiber-reinforced composites that carry **structural** (not merely cosmetic) load.
- ✅ **Key Equation #1 — Rule of Mixtures (Strength):** $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$.
- ✅ **Key Equation #2 — Rule of Mixtures (Modulus, longitudinal):** $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$.
- ✅ **Key Equation #3 — Specific Strength:** $\sigma_{sp} = \sigma / \rho$.
- ✅ **Key Equation #4 — Specific Stiffness:** $E_{sp} = E / \rho$.
- ✅ **Key Equation #5 — Critical Fiber Length:** $L_c = \sigma_{f,u} \, d / (2 \, \tau_i)$.
- ✅ **Volume Fraction Conservation:** $V_f + V_m = 1$.
- ✅ **Density Values (memorize):** Steel 7.85, Aluminium 2.70, GFRP 1.80, CFRP 1.60 (all in g/cm³).
- ✅ **Four Major Reinforcement Types:** Glass (GFRP), Carbon (CFRP), Aramid (AFRP), Natural (NFRP).
- ✅ **Five Major Processes:** SMC, BMC, RTM, RIM, Compression Molding.
- ✅ **Classic Application:** Glass-fiber/epoxy leaf spring (Chevrolet Corvette).
- ✅ **Modern Application:** CFRP monocoque and EV battery enclosure.
- ✅ **Why plastics over steel?** Weight reduction, corrosion resistance, design freedom, crash energy absorption.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Lower absolute stiffness; cannot replace steel in primary safety structures without reinforcement.
- ✅ **Shear-lag theory** governs load transfer at the fiber-matrix interface.
- ✅ **Silanes / sizings** are coupling agents used to strengthen the fiber-matrix interface.
- ✅ **SMC is a thermoset**, not a thermoplastic.
- ✅ **CFRP is ~18× stronger per unit weight** than steel (specific strength ratio).
- ✅ **Critical fiber volume fraction** $V_{f,\text{crit}}$ must be exceeded for fiber-dominated failure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semi-Rigid PUR Foams and Sandwich Panel Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semi-Rigid PUR Foams and Sandwich Panel Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Semi-Rigid Polyurethane (PUR) Foams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Semi-rigid Polyurethane (PUR) foams are open-cell or mixed-cell thermoset polymer cellular materials produced by the exothermic reaction of a polyol (resin component) with a diisocyanate (typically MDI or TDI), in the presence of a blowing agent, surfactant, catalyst, and water. The term "semi-rigid" defines their mechanical position on the elastomer-to-foam continuum — they possess sufficient compressive recovery and flexural compliance to damp vibration, yet enough compressive strength and dimensional stability to provide structural fill in body cavities.*

**Key Material Identity Markers:**
- Base chemistry: **Polyurethane** linkage (–NH–CO–O–) repeating unit
- Typical in-service density range: **40 kg/m³ to 200 kg/m³**
- Compression Load Deflection (CLD) at 40 % deflection: **20 kPa to 100 kPa**
- Cell morphology: predominantly **open cells (≥ 70 %)** for acoustic absorption, mixed cells for damping

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Sound-Sponge of the Car":**
> Imagine placing a wet kitchen sponge inside a sealed metal box (your car body cavity). When sound waves try to enter the cavity, they push into the sponge. The open-cell structure of a semi-rigid PUR foam behaves identically — sound pressure penetrates the interconnected pores, where viscous friction converts acoustic energy into a tiny amount of heat. The "semi-rigid" character means the sponge doesn't completely collapse under its own weight or under the slight pressure of an interior trim panel pressing against it, but it still flexes enough to absorb impact (e.g., knee bolster during a pedestrian impact or occupant's knee contact with the dashboard). In a passenger car, semi-rigid PUR foam is the hidden "silent partner" between the painted sheet metal and the soft-touch interior trim.

### 1.2 Sandwich Panel Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *A Sandwich Panel, in the context of vehicle body engineering, is a stratified composite laminate comprising two thin, high-stiffness face sheets (skins) adhesively or integrally bonded to a thick, low-density core. The geometry exploits the **flexural rigidity scaling law** — by separating two stiff skins via a low-density core, the panel's second moment of area increases with the square of the core thickness, yielding a bending stiffness-to-mass ratio an order of magnitude higher than a monolithic sheet of equivalent mass.*

**Standard Sandwich Topology:**

$$\text{Face Sheet (Skin)}_1 \;|\; \text{Core} \;|\; \text{Face Sheet (Skin)}_2$$

- **Face sheets:** Steel (0.20–0.80 mm), Aluminium (0.50–1.50 mm), GFRP/CFRP composites, or natural fibre mats
- **Core materials:** PUR foam, PP honeycomb, aluminium honeycomb, balsa wood, expanded cork, mineral wool
- **Typical face-to-core thickness ratio:** $t_f : t_c : t_f = 1 : 20 : 1$ to $1 : 50 : 1$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Steel I-Beam Made Flat":**
> Think of the capital letter **"I"** used as a steel beam in building construction. The two horizontal flanges of the I are far apart, connected by a thin vertical web. Almost all the bending stiffness comes from the flanges being **separated by distance**, not from their thickness. A vehicle sandwich panel is essentially an I-beam that has been "rolled out flat" — the two face sheets are the flanges, and the core is the web that holds them apart and prevents them from buckling inward toward each other. By making the core out of very light foam instead of steel, you preserve ~95 % of the bending stiffness of an equivalent solid steel panel but at ~30 % of the mass. This is why modern EV battery enclosures, sunroofs, tailgates, and floor pans are migrating to sandwich construction.

### 1.3 Why These Two Topics Are Studied Together

Both semi-rigid PUR foams and sandwich panels are integrated into **Module 2 (Materials and Lightweight Construction)** of PEAUT741 because:
1. The PUR foam most commonly serves as the **core** of an automotive sandwich panel.
2. The synergy between foam core chemistry and panel-level bending theory embodies the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) COs: *CO2 — "Apply material selection principles to vehicle body subsystems."*

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Cutting Syllabus Hook:** When answering a sandwich panel problem in the KTU exam, always justify the **core selection** using PUR foam properties — the examiners allocate marks specifically for material-process integration reasoning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Semi-Rigid PUR Foam — Microstructure and Reaction Chemistry

The fundamental polymerization is the **polyaddition reaction** between hydroxyl-terminated polyols and isocyanate groups:

$$\text{R–N=C=O} + \text{HO–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–CO–O–R'} \quad (\text{Urethane linkage})$$

Simultaneously, water (used as the chemical blowing agent) reacts with isocyanate to release **carbon dioxide**, which expands the polymerizing mass into a cellular foam:

$$\text{R–N=C=O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

The **CO₂ gas** nucleates bubbles that are stabilized by a **silicone surfactant** while the polymer viscosity rises, locking in the cellular geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "semi-rigid" and not rigid or flexible?**
> - **Flexible foam:** low cross-link density → high elongation (> 100 %), low CLD
> - **Rigid foam:** high cross-link density / aromatic polyols → brittle, high CLD (> 100 kPa)
> - **Semi-rigid foam:** moderate cross-link density with cell-opening surfactants → recovery ≥ 70 % after 50 % compression, CLD 20–100 kPa

### 2.2 Functional Role of Semi-Rigid PUR Foam in a Vehicle Body

| Functional Role | Mechanism | Typical Vehicle Location |
|---|---|---|
| Acoustic damping | Viscous dissipation in open cells | A/B/C-pillar cavities, roof rail |
| Anti-flutter filling | Mass-damping of trim panels | Door panels, dashboard carrier |
| Energy absorption (knee bolster) | Controlled crush | Lower dashboard, IP substrate |
| Sealing & anti-rattle | Conformal cavity fill | Side rail closures, fuel filler area |
| NVH bulkhead decoupling | Constrained layer damping | Bulkhead between engine bay & cabin |

### 2.3 Sandwich Panel — Theory of Flexural Rigidity

Consider a sandwich beam of width $b$, core thickness $t_c$, and equal face sheet thickness $t_f$. The distance between the centroids of the two face sheets is:

$$d = t_c + t_f \approx t_c \quad (\text{since } t_f \ll t_c)$$

The **equivalent flexural rigidity (per unit width)** of the sandwich panel is the sum of the rigidities of the two face sheets acting at distance $d$, plus the intrinsic rigidity of the core:

$$\boxed{D = \frac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{\,2}}{2} + \frac{E_c \, t_c^{\,3}}{12}}$$

where:
- $E_f$ = Young's modulus of face sheet material (Pa)
- $E_c$ = Young's modulus of core material (Pa)
- $t_f$ = face sheet thickness (m)
- $t_c$ = core thickness (m)
- $d$ = distance between face-sheet centroids ≈ $t_c + t_f$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because $E_f \gg E_c$ and $t_c \gg t_f$, the term $\frac{E_f t_f d^2}{2}$ **dominates** the expression. This is the foundational mathematical statement of why sandwich construction is so mass-efficient.

### 2.4 Bending Stress in Face Sheets

For a sandwich panel subjected to a bending moment $M$, the bending stress in the face sheets (ignoring the small contribution of the core's own bending stress) is:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{M}{d \, t_f \, b}$$

The face sheet must satisfy: $\sigma_f \leq \sigma_{f,\text{allow}}$

### 2.5 Shear Stress in the Core

The shear force $V$ applied to the panel is largely carried by the core in shear. The average core shear stress is:

$$\tau_c = \frac{V}{d \, b}$$

The core must satisfy: $\tau_c \leq \tau_{c,\text{allow}}$

### 2.6 Critical Wrinkling Stress of the Face Sheet on the Foam Core

A face sheet in compression may buckle into the foam core as a short-wavelength wrinkle. The classical Plantema formula for the critical wrinkling stress is:

$$\sigma_{wr} = 0.91 \cdot \left( \frac{E_f \, E_c \, G_c}{1 - \nu_c^{\,2}} \right)^{1/3}$$

where $G_c$ is the core shear modulus and $\nu_c$ is the Poisson's ratio of the core.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{E_f t_f d^2}{2} + \dfrac{E_c t_c^3}{12}$ | $D$: flexural rigidity; $E_f, E_c$: moduli; $t_f, t_c$: thicknesses; $d \approx t_c + t_f$ | Compare stiffness vs solid panel |
| 2 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{M}{d \, t_f \, b}$ | $M$: bending moment; $b$: panel width | Face sheet yield check |
| 3 | $\tau_c = \dfrac{V}{d \, b}$ | $V$: transverse shear force | Core shear failure check |
| 4 | $\sigma_{wr} = 0.91 \left( \dfrac{E_f E_c G_c}{1 - \nu_c^2} \right)^{1/3}$ | $G_c$: core shear modulus; $\nu_c$: core Poisson's ratio | Face wrinkling/buckling check |
| 5 | $EI_{\text{eq}} = E_f b t_f \dfrac{d^2}{2}$ | Equivalent solid beam rigidity | Mass-vs-stiffness substitution |
| 6 | $m_{\text{panel}} = 2 \rho_f t_f + \rho_c t_c$ | $\rho_f, \rho_c$: densities | Lightweighting audit |
| 7 | $f_{\text{acoustic}} = \dfrac{\alpha \, c}{2 \pi \, t_c}$ | $\alpha$: open-cell absorption coeff; $c$: speed of sound | NVH foam thickness design |
| 8 | $\text{CLD}_{40\%} = \dfrac{F_{40\%}}{A_0}$ | $F_{40\%}$: force at 40 % compression; $A_0$: initial area | Foam grade classification |
| 9 | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{h_{50,\text{recovered}}}{h_0} \times 100\%$ | $h_{50,\text{recovered}}$: rebound height after 50 % compression | Semi-rigid classification test |
| 10 | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{\text{sandwich}}}{\rho_{\text{solid,eq.stiffness}}}$ | Lightweighting efficiency | Design KPI reporting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Note:** All formulas above are dimensionally consistent in **SI units** (Pa, m, N, kg/m³). The dominant term in equation (1) makes it possible to drop the core's own $E_c t_c^3 / 12$ term on first-pass KTU calculations when $E_c \ll E_f$. Always state this assumption explicitly in your answer to earn full methodology marks.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV battery enclosure floor:** Aluminium-PUR sandwich saves ~25 kg per vehicle while meeting side-pole intrusion stiffness.
- **Tailgate inner panel:** SMC face sheets + 25 mm PUR core → torsional stiffness increases by 4× over the steel baseline.
- **Dashboard carrier:** Glass-mat thermoplastic (GMT) face sheets with semi-rigid PUR foam core enables one-shot moulded knee bolster with integrated ducting.
- **Headliner:** Sandwich of non-woven + PUR foam + fabric; the foam is the core, the non-woven and fabric are the face sheets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sandwich Panel Flexural Rigidity

**Given:** A sandwich beam of width $b$, face sheet thickness $t_f$ (top and bottom), core thickness $t_c$. Face sheet modulus $E_f$, core modulus $E_c$.

**Step 1 — Write the second moment of area of the section about its neutral axis (which passes through the geometric centre because both face sheets are identical):**

$$I = 2 \left[ \frac{b \, t_f^{\,3}}{12} + b \, t_f \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 \right] + \frac{b \, t_c^{\,3}}{12}$$

Here, $d/2$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the centroid of each face sheet. We used the **parallel axis theorem** for each face sheet.

**Step 2 — Expand the squared distance:**

$$I = \frac{b \, t_f^{\,3}}{6} + \frac{b \, t_f \, d^2}{2} + \frac{b \, t_c^{\,3}}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the appropriate Young's modulus and sum the contributions (face sheets contribute $E_f I_f$; core contributes $E_c I_c$):**

$$D_{\text{total}} = E_f \left( \frac{b \, t_f^{\,3}}{6} + \frac{b \, t_f \, d^2}{2} \right) + E_c \left( \frac{b \, t_c^{\,3}}{12} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Group and divide by the width $b$ to express rigidity per unit width (the form most commonly used in design charts):**

$$\frac{D_{\text{total}}}{b} = \frac{E_f \, t_f^{\,3}}{6} + \frac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{\,2}}{2} + \frac{E_c \, t_c^{\,3}}{12}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that because $t_f \ll d \approx t_c$, the term $E_f t_f^3 / 6$ is negligible. The standard "engineering" form becomes:**

$$D = \frac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{\,2}}{2} + \frac{E_c \, t_c^{\,3}}{12} \quad \text{(per unit width)}$$

**Step 6 — Quantify the dominance of the first term using a numerical check.** Suppose:
- $E_f = 70 \text{ GPa}$ (aluminium)
- $t_f = 0.6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $E_c = 60 \text{ MPa} = 60 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ (semi-rigid PUR foam)
- $t_c = 24 \text{ mm} \approx d$

Compute first term:
$$\frac{E_f t_f d^2}{2} = \frac{70 \times 10^9 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.025)^2}{2}$$

$$= \frac{70 \times 10^9 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{70 \times 10^9 \times 3.75 \times 10^{-7}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{26250}{2} = 13125 \text{ N·m}$$

Compute second term:
$$\frac{E_c t_c^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \times (0.024)^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \times 1.382 \times 10^{-5}}{12} \approx 69.1 \text{ N·m}$$

Ratio: $13125 / 69.1 \approx 190$. Therefore the core's own bending rigidity contributes less than 0.5 % — **validating the dominance assumption** used in KTU textbook simplified derivations.

### 3.2 Derivation of Bending Stress in the Face Sheet

**Step 1 — Use the flexure formula:**
$$\sigma = \frac{M \, y}{I}$$

**Step 2 — The maximum $y$ in the face sheet is at the outer fibre, distance $d/2$ from the neutral axis. Substitute:**
$$\sigma_{f,\text{max}} = \frac{M \, (d/2)}{I_{\text{eq}}}$$

**Step 3 — Replace $I_{\text{eq}}$ with the dominant term, $b \, t_f \, d^{\,2} / 2$:**
$$\sigma_{f,\text{max}} = \frac{M \, (d/2)}{b \, t_f \, d^{\,2} / 2} = \frac{M}{b \, t_f \, d}$$

**Step 4 — Confirm with the simplified standard form (per unit width, multiply numerator and denominator appropriately):**
$$\sigma_f = \frac{M}{d \, t_f \, b}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Core Shear Stress

**Step 1 — Start from the shear formula for a beam:**
$$\tau = \frac{V \, Q}{I \, b}$$

**Step 2 — For a sandwich beam, $Q$ evaluated at the face-core interface equals the first moment of one face sheet area about the neutral axis:**
$$Q = b \, t_f \cdot \frac{d}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the shear formula:**
$$\tau_c = \frac{V \cdot (b t_f d / 2)}{(b t_f d^2 / 2) \, b} = \frac{V}{b \, d}$$

This matches the formula in the cheat sheet: $\tau_c = V / (d \, b)$.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Sandwich Floor Panel

A floor panel of an EV battery enclosure is proposed as follows:
- Face sheets: aluminium, $E_f = 70 \text{ GPa}$, $t_f = 0.5 \text{ mm}$, $\sigma_{f,\text{allow}} = 200 \text{ MPa}$
- Core: semi-rigid PUR foam, $E_c = 50 \text{ MPa}$, $G_c = 18 \text{ MPa}$, $\nu_c = 0.30$, $t_c = 25 \text{ mm}$, $\tau_{c,\text{allow}} = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$
- Loading: bending moment $M = 1500 \text{ N·m/m}$, shear force $V = 8000 \text{ N/m}$

**Check 1 — Face sheet bending stress:**
$$\sigma_f = \frac{M}{d \, t_f \, b} = \frac{1500}{(0.0255) \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1} \approx 117.6 \text{ MPa}$$

Compare to $\sigma_{f,\text{allow}} = 200 \text{ MPa}$. **Factor of Safety = 1.70 → PASS**

**Check 2 — Core shear stress:**
$$\tau_c = \frac{V}{d \, b} = \frac{8000}{0.0255 \times 1} \approx 313.7 \text{ kPa} = 0.314 \text{ MPa}$$

Compare to $\tau_{c,\text{allow}} = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$. **Factor of Safety = 1.59 → PASS**

**Check 3 — Face sheet wrinkling stress (Plantema):**
$$\sigma_{wr} = 0.91 \left( \frac{E_f \, E_c \, G_c}{1 - \nu_c^{\,2}} \right)^{1/3} = 0.91 \left( \frac{70 \times 10^9 \times 50 \times 10^6 \times 18 \times 10^6}{1 - 0.09} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$= 0.91 \left( \frac{6.30 \times 10^{25}}{0.91} \right)^{1/3} = 0.91 \left( 6.92 \times 10^{25} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$= 0.91 \times (6.92)^{1/3} \times 10^{25/3} = 0.91 \times 1.90 \times 10^{8.33}$$

Computing $(6.92)^{1/3} \approx 1.905$ and $10^{8.33} \approx 2.14 \times 10^8$:

$$\sigma_{wr} \approx 0.91 \times 1.905 \times 2.14 \times 10^8 \approx 3.71 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 371 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_f = 117.6 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{wr} = 371 \text{ MPa}$, wrinkling is not the governing failure mode. **Foam upgrade NOT required.**

**Check 4 — Mass saving:**
- Solid aluminium of equivalent bending stiffness would need $t_{\text{solid}}$ such that $E_f b t_{\text{solid}}^3 / 12 = D$
$$t_{\text{solid}}^3 = \frac{12 \cdot D}{E_f \, b} = \frac{12 \times 13125}{70 \times 10^9} = 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$
$$t_{\text{solid}} = (2.25 \times 10^{-6})^{1/3} \approx 1.31 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \approx 13.1 \text{ mm}$$

- Mass per unit area:
$$m_{\text{solid}} = \rho_{\text{Al}} \, t_{\text{solid}} = 2700 \times 0.0131 \approx 35.4 \text{ kg/m}^2$$
$$m_{\text{sandwich}} = 2 \rho_{\text{Al}} t_f + \rho_{\text{foam}} t_c = 2(2700)(0.0005) + (60)(0.025) = 2.70 + 1.50 = 4.20 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Mass saving = (35.4 − 4.20) / 35.4 × 100% ≈ 88 %** — a remarkable lightweighting efficiency that justifies the sandwich approach for the EV floor.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Core Material Selection

| Application Demand | Required Foam Property | Dominant Core Type |
|---|---|---|
| Acoustic absorption (NVH) | Open-cell, low airflow resistivity | Soft semi-rigid PUR (40–60 kg/m³) |
| Knee bolster impact | High energy absorption per unit volume | Medium-density semi-rigid PUR (80–120 kg/m³) |
| Floor panel stiffness | High $E_c$, $G_c$ | Higher-density PUR (150–200 kg/m³) or honeycomb |
| Fire-rated interior | Low flame spread, low smoke | Mineral wool or fire-retardant PUR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`**
*With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction of a sandwich panel. Mention any two advantages it offers over a monolithic sheet of equivalent mass.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A sandwich panel consists of two thin, high-modulus face sheets (skins) bonded to a thick, low-density core. The face sheets carry bending stresses (tension on one side, compression on the other), while the core maintains the separation between the skins, carries transverse shear, and may provide damping or acoustic absorption.

*Sketch (description for exam writing):* Two parallel horizontal rectangles of thickness $t_f$ separated by a core layer of thickness $t_c$, with adhesive interfaces marked.

**Advantages (any two for full 3 marks):**
1. **Higher specific bending stiffness** — because $D \propto d^2$, increasing the core thickness dramatically increases rigidity at near-constant mass.
2. **Multi-functionality** — the core can simultaneously damp vibration, absorb sound, and provide thermal insulation, replacing separate add-on components.
3. **Mass savings up to 70–90 %** versus solid panels of equal bending stiffness.
4. **Improved NVH performance** through constrained-layer damping at the face-core interface.

---

**Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`**
*What are semi-rigid polyurethane (PUR) foams? State any three functional roles they perform in a modern passenger car body.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Semi-rigid PUR foams are thermoset polyurethane cellular polymers produced by the exothermic reaction of polyols with diisocyanates in the presence of a blowing agent, surfactant, and catalyst. They sit between flexible foams (high elongation, low CLD) and rigid foams (high CLD, brittle) on the foam-mechanical spectrum, exhibiting compressive recovery and moderate flexural compliance.

**Three functional roles:**
1. **Acoustic damping** in A/B/C-pillar cavities — open-cell structure dissipates airborne sound.
2. **Anti-flutter filling** behind interior trim panels — adds local mass and damping to prevent buzz/squeak/rattle (BSR).
3. **Energy absorption** in knee bolster zones — controlled compression protects occupant lower limbs in frontal impact.
4. *(Optional third alternative)*: Sealing and conformal filling of body-cavity closures.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries two sub-parts, (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either Question A **OR** Question B must be answered.

---

#### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the equivalent flexural rigidity per unit width of a sandwich panel with equal face sheets. Clearly state the assumptions used.* **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Geometry and assumptions (1 mark):**
- Face sheets identical: thickness $t_f$, modulus $E_f$, width $b$
- Core: thickness $t_c$, modulus $E_c$
- Distance between face centroids: $d = t_c + t_f \approx t_c$
- **Assumption:** Face sheets carry bending stresses; core carries transverse shear. Core's own bending rigidity is negligible because $E_c \ll E_f$.
- **Assumption:** Plane sections remain plane (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).

**Step 2 — Second moment of area via parallel axis theorem (2 marks):**

$$I = 2 \left[ \frac{b t_f^3}{12} + b t_f \left( \frac{d}{2} \right)^2 \right] + \frac{b t_c^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{b t_f^3}{6} + \frac{b t_f d^2}{2} + \frac{b t_c^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by Young's moduli and add contributions (2 marks):**

$$D_{\text{total}} = E_f \left( \frac{b t_f^3}{6} + \frac{b t_f d^2}{2} \right) + E_c \left( \frac{b t_c^3}{12} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Drop the negligible term $E_f t_f^3 / 6$ because $t_f \ll d$, and divide by $b$ to express per unit width (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{\frac{D}{b} = \frac{E_f t_f d^2}{2} + \frac{E_c t_c^3}{12}}$$

**(b)** *A sandwich roof panel is constructed with two aluminium face sheets ($E_f = 70 \text{ GPa}$, $t_f = 0.5 \text{ mm}$) and a semi-rigid PUR foam core ($E_c = 50 \text{ MPa}$, $t_c = 30 \text{ mm}$). If the panel experiences a bending moment of 1000 N·m/m and a shear force of 5000 N/m, calculate:*
*(i) the flexural rigidity per unit width,*
*(ii) the maximum bending stress in the face sheets, and*
*(iii) the shear stress in the core.* **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $E_f = 70 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$, $t_f = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $E_c = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $t_c = 30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $d = t_c + t_f \approx 30.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $M = 1000 \text{ N·m/m}$, $V = 5000 \text{ N/m}$

**(i) Flexural rigidity per unit width (2 marks):**

$$\frac{D}{b} = \frac{E_f t_f d^2}{2} + \frac{E_c t_c^3}{12}$$

$$= \frac{70 \times 10^9 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.0305)^2}{2} + \frac{50 \times 10^6 \times (0.030)^3}{12}$$

$$= \frac{70 \times 10^9 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.3025 \times 10^{-4}}{2} + \frac{50 \times 10^6 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-5}}{12}$$

$$= \frac{32558.75}{2} + \frac{1350}{12} = 16279.4 + 112.5 \approx 16391.9 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Maximum bending stress in face sheets (2.5 marks):**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{M}{d \, t_f \, b}$$

Per unit width, $b = 1 \text{ m}$:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{1000}{0.0305 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1} = \frac{1000}{1.525 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 65.57 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 65.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Shear stress in the core (2.5 marks):**

$$\tau_c = \frac{V}{d \, b} = \frac{5000}{0.0305 \times 1} \approx 163934 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.164 \text{ MPa}$$

**Conclusion (free):** $\sigma_f \ll \sigma_{f,\text{allow}}$ (typical aluminium ≈ 200 MPa) and $\tau_c \ll \tau_{c,\text{allow}}$ (typical PUR foam ≈ 0.5 MPa), so the design is safe.

---

#### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` *(ALTERNATIVE)*

**(a)** *Explain the manufacturing process of semi-rigid PUR foams using a labelled block diagram. Discuss the role of each raw material component.* **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Process — Reactive Liquid Moulding (3.5 marks):**
1. **Polyol** and **isocyanate** (typically MDI) are held in separate temperature-controlled tanks.
2. Both liquids are metered in stoichiometric ratio (~1:1 by mass for typical formulations) and pumped into a high-pressure **impingement mixing head** where they are mixed in milliseconds.
3. The reactive mixture is poured into a closed cavity tool (representing the cavity to be filled, e.g., an A-pillar hollow section).
4. Within the tool, the polyaddition reaction begins, generating heat (exothermic, ~150 °C peak). Simultaneously, water in the formulation reacts with isocyanate to release **CO₂ gas**, which nucleates bubbles.
5. The mixture **creams** (first visible expansion), **rises** (cell growth and stabilization), and **gels** (cell-wall polymer solidification).
6. The part is **demoulded** after 60–180 s and **cured** at room temperature for 24 h.

**Roles of each component (3.5 marks):**
| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| Polyol (resin) | Provides –OH groups for urethane linkage; controls final flexibility (polyether polyol → flexible/semi-rigid; polyester polyol → more rigid) |
| Isocyanate (MDI/TDI) | Reacts with –OH and –NH₂; provides hard-segment domains for modulus |
| Water | Chemical blowing agent; reacts with isocyanate → CO₂ + urea linkages |
| Amine catalyst | Accelerates polyaddition and blowing reaction, balances cream/rise/tack-free times |
| Silicone surfactant | Lowers surface tension, stabilizes cell walls during expansion, controls cell size |
| Optional: physical blowing agent (HFC, pentane) | Reduces density further; can supplement or replace water blowing |

---

**(b)** *Compare a sandwich panel with a solid steel panel of equal bending stiffness for the floor of an EV battery enclosure. Assume steel $\rho_s = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $E_s = 210 \text{ GPa}$. Sandwich: 0.6 mm steel face sheets, 25 mm PUR core ($\rho_c = 60 \text{ kg/m}^3$), $E_c = 50 \text{ MPa}$. Compute and compare the mass per unit area and state the lightweighting efficiency.* **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sandwich flexural rigidity per unit width (2 marks):**
- $d \approx 0.0256 \text{ m}$, $t_f = 0.0006 \text{ m}$, $E_f = 210 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$, $E_c = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $t_c = 0.025 \text{ m}$

$$\frac{D}{b} = \frac{E_f t_f d^2}{2} + \frac{E_c t_c^3}{12}$$

$$= \frac{210 \times 10^9 \times 0.0006 \times (0.0256)^2}{2} + \frac{50 \times 10^6 \times (0.025)^3}{12}$$

$$= \frac{210 \times 10^9 \times 0.0006 \times 6.5536 \times 10^{-4}}{2} + \frac{50 \times 10^6 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}}{12}$$

$$= \frac{82575}{2} + \frac{781.25}{12} = 41287.5 + 65.1 \approx 41352.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent solid steel thickness (2 marks):**

$$t_s^3 = \frac{12 D}{E_s b} = \frac{12 \times 41352.6}{210 \times 10^9} = \frac{496231}{2.1 \times 10^{11}} = 2.363 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

$$t_s = (2.363 \times 10^{-6})^{1/3} \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 13.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Mass per unit area comparison (2 marks):**

Solid steel: $m_s = \rho_s t_s = 7850 \times 0.0133 \approx 104.4 \text{ kg/m}^2$

Sandwich: $m_{sw} = 2 \rho_s t_f + \rho_c t_c = 2(7850)(0.0006) + (60)(0.025)$
$= 9.42 + 1.50 = 10.92 \text{ kg/m}^2$

**Step 4 — Lightweighting efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{m_{sw}}{m_s} = 1 - \frac{10.92}{104.4} = 1 - 0.1046 = 0.8954 \approx \mathbf{89.5\%}$$

**Conclusion:** The sandwich panel achieves **~89.5 % mass saving** for the same bending stiffness, illustrating why EV manufacturers have aggressively adopted this construction for floor and enclosure panels.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — KTU answers require SI (N, m, Pa, kg). Mixing mm and cm leads to a 10³ error that propagates and yields zero marks for that sub-part.
> 2. **Not stating assumptions** — In sandwich derivations, omitting "core's own bending rigidity is negligible because $E_c \ll E_f$" costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Confusing $d$ with $t_c$** — $d = t_c + t_f$, not just $t_c$. This distinction costs the face stress calculation a mark.
> 4. **Skipping the Plantema wrinkling check** — Even if not numerically asked, mention wrinkling as a possible failure mode for full marks in design questions.
> 5. **Writing PUR as "polyurethane resin" without explaining foam expansion** — KTU examiners expect explicit mention of CO₂ from water-isocyanate reaction for the "manufacturing" question.
> 6. **Ignoring adhesive layer** — In sandwich design, the bondline is critical; mention adhesive selection (epoxy, PU adhesive, co-cured) for full marks.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Semi-rigid PUR foam** is a thermoset cellular polymer formed by polyol + isocyanate reaction, expanded by CO₂ from water–isocyanate side reaction.
- ✅ **Cell morphology** must be predominantly open-cell for acoustic damping; higher density (80–120 kg/m³) for structural applications.
- ✅ **Sandwich panel** = two stiff face sheets + a thick low-density core; functions like a flat "I-beam."
- ✅ **Master formula** for flexural rigidity per unit width: $D = E_f t_f d^2 / 2 + E_c t_c^3 / 12$.
- ✅ **Dominant term** is $E_f t_f d^2 / 2$ because $E_f \gg E_c$; this is why sandwich panels are mass-efficient.
- ✅ **Face sheet stress** under bending moment: $\sigma_f = M / (d \, t_f \, b)$.
- ✅ **Core shear stress**: $\tau_c = V / (d \, b)$.
- ✅ **Plantema wrinkling stress**: $\sigma_{wr} = 0.91 \left( E_f E_c G_c / (1 - \nu_c^2) \right)^{1/3}$ — must exceed $\sigma_f$.
- ✅ **Distance $d = t_c + t_f$**, not equal to $t_c$ alone.
- ✅ **Mass saving** vs equivalent solid panel can reach 70–90 % for same $D$.
- ✅ **Reaction sequence to memorize**: Polyol + Isocyanate → Urethane; Water + Isocyanate → CO₂ + Amine.
- ✅ **Vehicle applications**: A/B/C-pillar cavities, dashboard carriers, headliners, tailgates, EV battery enclosures, sunroof panels.
- ✅ **Failure modes** to mention: face yielding, face wrinkling, core shear, adhesive debonding, indentation.
- ✅ **Common exam trap**: mixing SI and CGS units in numerical sub-parts — always convert all dimensions to metres before computing.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme CO mapping**: This topic directly satisfies **CO2 (Material Selection)** and **CO3 (Lightweight Construction Strategies)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recycling of Vehicle Materials and Sustainable Practices in Material Selection and Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recycling of Vehicle Materials and Sustainable Practices in Material Selection and Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle Material Recycling** is the systematic, multi-stage industrial process of recovering, reprocessing, and reintroducing constituent materials derived from **End-of-Life Vehicles (ELVs)** back into the manufacturing supply chain. This process encompasses the recovery of ferrous metals (steel, cast iron), non-ferrous metals (aluminum, magnesium, copper), polymers (thermoplastics, thermosets), elastomers (rubber), glass, and operating fluids.

**Sustainable Material Selection and Manufacturing** in the automotive context refers to the engineering discipline that integrates **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)**, **Design for Recycling (DFR)**, **Design for Disassembly (DFD)**, and **Circular Economy** principles into the selection of body materials and the planning of manufacturing processes, with the objective of minimizing **Global Warming Potential (GWP)**, embodied energy, resource depletion, and end-of-life waste.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must demonstrate knowledge of (i) recycling routes for ferrous, non-ferrous, and polymeric vehicle materials, (ii) the EU ELV Directive 2000/53/EC and Indian End-of-Life Vehicle policy framework, (iii) sustainable material selection criteria, and (iv) green manufacturing practices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a car's life as a **"library book"** that, instead of being discarded after one reader, is carefully taken apart so that every chapter (steel frame), every illustration (glass windows), every bookmark (plastic trims), and even the ink (operating fluids) can be turned into new books. **Recycling is the librarian; sustainable design is the author who wrote the book in a way that makes taking it apart easy.**

A second, more powerful analogy: think of a modern automobile as a **"material bank account"**. When you buy a new car, you are essentially depositing ~**1.2 tonnes of steel**, ~**150 kg of aluminum**, ~**120 kg of plastics**, ~**80 kg of glass**, and ~**50 kg of rubber** into a temporary account. **At the end of the vehicle's life (typically 10–15 years), the engineer must design the "withdrawal slip"** — a disassembly plan — that allows the maximum possible balance to be returned to the industrial economy.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (with Units)

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standardized recycling metrics** are used globally in automotive sustainability reporting:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Target | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Recycling Rate** | $R_r$ | $\geq 85$ (EU ELV) | **% by mass** |
| **Recovery Rate** | $R_{cov}$ | $\geq 95$ (EU ELV) | **% by mass** |
| **Reuse Rate** | $R_{reuse}$ | $\geq 5$ (typical) | **% by mass** |
| **Material Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta_m$ | varies 70–98 | **%** |
| **Vehicle Mass Reduction Target** | $\Delta m_v$ | 30 (by 2030) | **%** |
| **Embodied Energy (Body-in-White)** | $EE$ | 25–35 | **GJ/tonne** |
| **Global Warming Potential** | $GWP$ | 9–11 | **tCO₂eq/vehicle** |

## 1.4 Material Composition of a Modern Passenger Car

A typical mid-size sedan (mass ≈ **1,500 kg**) contains the following approximate material distribution:

$$\text{Steel (incl. HSS/UHSS)} \approx 55\text{–}65\%$$
$$\text{Aluminum alloys} \approx 8\text{–}12\%$$
$$\text{Plastics \& Polymers} \approx 8\text{–}15\%$$
$$\text{Glass} \approx 3\text{–}5\%$$
$$\text{Rubber (tyres, seals)} \approx 4\text{–}6\%$$
$$\text{Fluids (oil, coolant, fuel)} \approx 4\text{–}6\%$$
$$\text{Copper, lead, others} \approx 1\text{–}3\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainability Paradox:** Although steel is the heaviest fraction, it is also the **most recycled material on Earth** (recycling rate ≈ **86%** globally), making it a benchmark material for circular economy thinking. Conversely, **carbon-fiber reinforced polymers (CFRP)**, while lightweight, are extremely energy-intensive to recycle.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4R Framework (Reduce – Reuse – Recycle – Recover)

The **Waste Hierarchy** applied to automotive engineering, ordered by environmental priority:

1. **Reduce** — Material minimization through lightweight design (e.g., AHSS, aluminum, composites). The single most effective sustainability lever.
2. **Reuse** — Direct component recovery (engines, transmissions, body panels) for second-life use.
3. **Recycle** — Re-processing into secondary raw materials for re-melting or re-pelletizing.
4. **Recover** — Energy recovery via controlled incineration (residual fraction only).

## 2.2 End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) Directive 2000/53/EC — Core Mandate

The European Union's **ELV Directive**, adopted in 2000 and progressively tightened, is the global benchmark for vehicle circularity:

> [!NOTE]
> **Article 7 Targets (currently binding):**
> - Reuse + Recycling $\geq$ **85%** by average vehicle mass.
> - Reuse + Recovery (incl. energy recovery) $\geq$ **95%** by average vehicle mass.
> - Ban on **Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg), Cadmium (Cd), and Hexavalent Chromium (Cr⁶⁺)** in vehicle materials (with limited exemptions).

## 2.3 Step-by-Step ELV Processing Route

The industrial **de-pollution and dismantling sequence** is the operational backbone of vehicle recycling:

- **Step 1 — Collection & Pre-treatment:** Battery removal, tyre deflation, fluid drainage (engine oil, coolant, fuel, brake fluid, refrigerant recovery).
- **Step 2 — De-pollution:** Mandatory removal of hazardous substances (Pb battery, Hg switches, air-bag inflators, fuel tank, catalytic converter with Pt/Pd/Rh).
- **Step 3 — Parts Reclamation:** Resale of serviceable components (engines, alternators, ECUs, body panels) to the independent aftermarket.
- **Step 4 — Shredding:** The remaining hulk is fed into a **shredder** (rotating hammer mill, ~300–400 RPM), producing a heterogeneous fragment called **Automotive Shredder Residue (ASR)** or **car fluff**, typically 20–25% of input mass.
- **Step 5 — Separation:** Magnetic separation (ferrous), Eddy-current separation (non-ferrous), air classification (light fraction), density separation (heavy fraction).
- **Step 6 — Secondary Smelting:** Recovered metals are re-melted in **Electric Arc Furnaces (EAF)** for steel and **reverberatory/rotary furnaces** for aluminum.

## 2.4 Recycling of Major Vehicle Material Streams

### 2.4.1 Steel (Ferrous) Recycling

Steel is recovered via **magnetic separation** post-shredding. Recycled steel retains full mechanical properties; **every tonne of recycled steel saves ~1.5 tonnes of CO₂** and ~**6 GJ** of embodied energy versus primary production from iron ore (blast furnace route).

### 2.4.2 Aluminum Recycling

Aluminum is recovered via **eddy-current separation**. The energy saving is dramatic — **recycled aluminum uses only 5% of the energy** required for primary aluminum from bauxite. The Hall-Héroult primary process consumes ~**45 GJ/tonne**, whereas secondary aluminum via remelting requires only ~**2.5 GJ/tonne**.

### 2.4.3 Polymer Recycling (Plastics & Composites)

Polymers are the **least recycled** but rapidly growing fraction. Four routes exist:
- **Mechanical recycling** — grinding, washing, re-extrusion (for thermoplastics: PP, ABS, PA, PC).
- **Chemical recycling** — depolymerization, pyrolysis, solvolysis (for thermosets/CFRP).
- **Feedstock recycling** — thermal cracking to produce fuel or monomer feedstocks.
- **Energy recovery** — controlled incineration with heat capture (last resort).

### 2.4.4 Glass Recycling

Windshields (laminated, with PVB interlayer) and side windows (tempered) are removed pre-shredder. Glass cullet is reused in glass wool insulation, abrasives, or new container glass. The PVB interlayer complicates direct recycling.

### 2.4.5 Tyre Recycling

End-of-life tyres are processed via:
- **Re-treading** (for truck tyres, multiple times).
- **Crumb rubber** production for playgrounds, asphalt modification, molded parts.
- **Pyrolysis** — thermal decomposition in oxygen-free environment, yielding **pyrolysis oil**, **carbon black**, and **steel wire** (recovered from beads).
- **Tire-Derived Fuel (TDF)** for cement kilns and paper mills.

### 2.4.6 Catalytic Converter Recovery

Catalytic converters contain **Platinum Group Metals (PGM): Pt, Pd, Rh**. They are removed intact, crushed, and chemically refined. A single catalytic converter may contain **2–5 g of PGM**, with recovery rates exceeding **95%**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they are routinely tested in KTU Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_r = \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{vehicle}} \times 100$ | **Recycling Rate** | **%** |
| 2 | $R_{cov} = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{m_{vehicle}} \times 100$ | **Recovery Rate** | **%** |
| 3 | $R_{reuse} = \dfrac{m_{reused}}{m_{vehicle}} \times 100$ | **Reuse Rate** | **%** |
| 4 | $ASR\% = \dfrac{m_{ASR}}{m_{vehicle}} \times 100$ | **Shredder Residue Fraction** | **%** |
| 5 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{m_{out,usable}}{m_{in}} \times 100$ | **Material Recovery Efficiency** | **%** |
| 6 | $EE_{saved} = EE_{primary} - EE_{recycled}$ | **Embodied Energy Savings** | **GJ/tonne** |
| 7 | $CO_{2,saved} = \alpha \cdot m_{recycled}$ | **CO₂ Emission Avoided** | **tCO₂eq** |
| 8 | $GWP_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (GWP_i \cdot m_i)$ | **Aggregated Life-Cycle GWP** | **tCO₂eq** |
| 9 | $M_{v,reduce} = \dfrac{m_{v,orig} - m_{v,new}}{m_{v,orig}} \times 100$ | **Vehicle Mass Reduction** | **%** |
| 10 | $C_{EAF} = 0.45 \cdot C_{BOF}$ | **EAF vs. BOF Energy Ratio** | **dimensionless** |
| 11 | $E_{Al,recycled} = 0.05 \cdot E_{Al,primary}$ | **Aluminum Energy Ratio** | **dimensionless** |
| 12 | $\rho_{eff} = \dfrac{\sum m_i}{\sum (m_i / \rho_i)}$ | **Effective Vehicle Density** | **kg/m³** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** When asked to compute recycling rate, students often forget to exclude **fluids and fuel** from the denominator. Always clarify whether the calculation is on a **dry mass basis** (industry standard) or **wet mass basis** (which includes fluids).

## 2.6 Sustainable Material Selection Criteria

The **Ashby Material Selection Methodology** extended for sustainability introduces additional indices:

- **Specific Stiffness / Density²** — for lightweighting.
- **Specific Strength / Density** — for structural efficiency.
- **Embodied Energy per unit strength** — sustainability index $S_1$.
- **CO₂ footprint per unit stiffness** — sustainability index $S_2$.
- **Recyclability Index (RI)** — qualitative 1–5 scale.
- **End-of-Life Value (EoLV)** — economic credit for recoverable mass.

The **sustainability performance index** can be expressed as:

$$I_{sustain} = \frac{P_{mech}}{m \cdot GWP \cdot EE \cdot (1 - RI)}$$

where $P_{mech}$ is the relevant mechanical performance, $m$ is mass, $GWP$ is global warming potential, $EE$ is embodied energy, and $RI$ is the recyclability index.

## 2.7 Sustainable Manufacturing Practices

The following **green manufacturing levers** are emphasized in Module 2 of the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Dry machining** and **Minimum Quantity Lubrication (MQL)** — eliminates or drastically reduces cutting fluid waste.
- **Near-net-shape forming** — reduces material wastage in stamping and forging.
- **Powder metallurgy** — for gears and connecting rods; near-zero scrap.
- **Additive manufacturing (3D printing)** — for jigs, fixtures, and lightweight lattice structures.
- **Closed-loop water systems** in paint shops and pre-treatment.
- **VOC-compliant paints** (waterborne and powder coatings).
- **Renewable energy** in assembly plants (solar PV, wind).
- **Lean manufacturing** to minimize offcut and reject rates.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ELV Recycling Rate from Mass Balance

Consider a vehicle of total dry mass $M_v$ entering the ELV treatment chain. We wish to derive the relationship between the **recycling rate** $R_r$, **recovery rate** $R_{cov}$, and the **ASR fraction** $\phi_{ASR}$.

### 3.1.1 Mass Balance Setup

Let the input mass be partitioned as:

$$M_v = m_{reused} + m_{recycled} + m_{energy,recovered} + m_{ASR,landfilled}$$

Dividing through by $M_v$:

$$1 = f_{reuse} + f_{recycle} + f_{energy} + f_{ASR}$$

where each $f_i = m_i / M_v$ is the corresponding mass fraction.

### 3.1.2 Defining the Rates

By definition of the KTU/EU standardized metrics:

$$R_{reuse} = f_{reuse} \times 100$$
$$R_r = (f_{reuse} + f_{recycle}) \times 100$$
$$R_{cov} = (f_{reuse} + f_{recycle} + f_{energy}) \times 100$$

### 3.1.3 Solving for the ASR Fraction

From the mass balance:

$$f_{ASR} = 1 - (f_{reuse} + f_{recycle} + f_{energy})$$

Substituting the definitions:

$$f_{ASR} = 1 - \frac{R_{cov}}{100}$$

Therefore the **ASR landfilling fraction** is:

$$\phi_{ASR} = \left(1 - \frac{R_{cov}}{100}\right) \times 100\%$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A vehicle has dry mass **1,420 kg**. The ELV facility reports:
- Reused components: **85 kg**
- Recycled materials: **1,135 kg**
- Energy-recovered fraction: **75 kg**
- ASR sent to landfill: **125 kg**

**Step 1 — Verify mass balance:**

$$85 + 1{,}135 + 75 + 125 = 1{,}420 \text{ kg} = M_v \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute the rates:**

$$R_{reuse} = \frac{85}{1{,}420} \times 100 = 5.99\%$$

$$R_r = \frac{85 + 1{,}135}{1{,}420} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}220}{1{,}420} \times 100 = 85.92\%$$

$$R_{cov} = \frac{85 + 1{,}135 + 75}{1{,}420} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}295}{1{,}420} \times 100 = 91.20\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ASR fraction:**

$$\phi_{ASR} = \frac{125}{1{,}420} \times 100 = 8.80\%$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the derived formula:**

$$\phi_{ASR} = 100 - 91.20 = 8.80\% \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This vehicle meets the EU ELV recycling target ($R_r \geq 85\%$) but **falls short** of the recovery target ($R_{cov} \geq 95\%$). The bottleneck is the **8.80% ASR fraction** — predominantly mixed plastics, foams, and rubber. The design engineer should target this fraction through better material labelling and mono-material assemblies.

## 3.2 Derivation: Embodied Energy Savings in Steel Recycling

Let $EE_{BF}$ denote the embodied energy of primary steel via the **Blast Furnace – Basic Oxygen Furnace (BF-BOF)** route, and $EE_{EAF}$ the embodied energy of secondary steel via the **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** route. Industry-standard values are:

$$EE_{BF} = 22.0 \text{ GJ/tonne}$$
$$EE_{EAF} = 9.5 \text{ GJ/tonne}$$

### 3.2.1 Per-Tonne Energy Savings

$$EE_{saved} = EE_{BF} - EE_{EAF} = 22.0 - 9.5 = 12.5 \text{ GJ/tonne}$$

### 3.2.2 CO₂ Emission Avoided

Using the standard conversion factor of **0.07 tCO₂eq per GJ** for steel-related energy:

$$CO_{2,saved} = 12.5 \times 0.07 = 0.875 \text{ tCO₂eq/tonne}$$

### 3.2.3 Scaling to an Entire ELV Stream

For a fleet of **N = 1,000,000 ELVs/year**, with average recoverable steel mass per vehicle of $m_{steel} = 850$ kg:

$$CO_{2,annual} = 0.875 \times 850 \times 10^{-3} \times 1{,}000{,}000$$

$$CO_{2,annual} = 743{,}750 \text{ tCO₂eq/year}$$

This is equivalent to removing approximately **161,000 passenger cars** from roads for one year.

## 3.3 Derivation: Aluminum Recycling Energy Advantage

For primary aluminum (Hall-Héroult):

$$EE_{Al,primary} \approx 45.0 \text{ GJ/tonne}$$

For secondary aluminum (remelting of scrap):

$$EE_{Al,recycled} \approx 2.5 \text{ GJ/tonne}$$

The **energy ratio** is:

$$\frac{EE_{Al,recycled}}{EE_{Al,primary}} = \frac{2.5}{45.0} = 0.0556 \approx 5.6\%$$

Confirming the canonical industry rule-of-thumb that **recycled aluminum uses only ~5% of primary aluminum energy**.

The per-tonne savings:

$$EE_{saved,Al} = 45.0 - 2.5 = 42.5 \text{ GJ/tonne}$$

For an average aluminum content of **150 kg per vehicle**, the per-vehicle savings are:

$$EE_{saved,per\,vehicle} = 42.5 \times 0.150 = 6.375 \text{ GJ/vehicle}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Material Sustainability Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a **KTU favorite** for 14-mark Part B questions. Memorize the orderings and the trade-off logic.

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | $EE$ (GJ/t) | Recyclability | $CO_2$ (t/t) | Lightweighting Potential | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | **7,850** | 22.0 | Very High (86%) | 1.99 | Baseline (1.0×) | **1.0** |
| HSS/AHSS | 7,850 | 25.0 | Very High | 2.10 | 1.15× | 1.2 |
| Aluminum 6xxx | **2,700** | 45.0 | High (75%) | 8.24 | 1.7× | 2.5 |
| Magnesium AZ91 | **1,740** | 35.0 | Medium (40%) | 6.50 | 2.2× | 4.0 |
| CFRP | **1,550** | 95.0 | Very Low (<10%) | 24.0 | 3.0× | 15.0 |
| GFRP | 1,800 | 35.0 | Low (15%) | 6.0 | 1.8× | 4.5 |
| PP/PP-GF20 | 920 | 80.0 | Medium (30%) | 3.5 | 2.5× | 1.5 |
| Natural Fiber Composite | 1,200 | 25.0 | Medium (50%) | 0.8 | 1.5× | 1.8 |

**Key engineering insight from the matrix:**
- **Lightweighting potential** scales inversely with density, but **CO₂ footprint** does not scale accordingly — composites are lightweight but energy-intensive.
- The "greenest" material is **not always the lightest** — natural fiber composites offer an attractive compromise.
- The "**Lightweighting Paradox**": saving 100 kg of steel by replacing with aluminum may **increase** the vehicle's life-cycle GWP unless the use-phase fuel savings over the vehicle's lifetime offset the production-phase penalty.

## 3.5 Derivation: Break-Even Distance for Lightweighting

The lightweighting trade-off can be quantified. Let $\Delta m$ be the mass saved, $f$ be the fuel consumption (L/100 km), and $C_{CO_2}$ be the CO₂ intensity of the fuel. The use-phase saving per km is:

$$CO_{2,use,saved} = \Delta m \cdot k_{mass} \cdot f \cdot C_{CO_2}$$

where $k_{mass}$ is the **mass-related fuel consumption factor** (~0.6 L/100 km per 100 kg for a typical ICE vehicle).

The production-phase penalty is:

$$CO_{2,prod,penalty} = \Delta m \cdot (GWP_{new} - GWP_{baseline})$$

Setting them equal and solving for the **break-even distance** $d_{BE}$:

$$d_{BE} = \frac{CO_{2,prod,penalty}}{CO_{2,use,saved}} = \frac{GWP_{new} - GWP_{baseline}}{k_{mass} \cdot f \cdot C_{CO_2}}$$

For steel → aluminum substitution ($\Delta m = 100$ kg, $GWP_{new} - GWP_{baseline} \approx 6.25$ tCO₂eq/t):

$$d_{BE} = \frac{0.625}{0.006 \times 8 \times 2.31} = \frac{0.625}{0.111} \approx 5{,}631 \text{ km}$$

A vehicle must be driven for only ~**5,600 km** to recover the production-phase GWP penalty of an aluminum substitution — well within the typical 150,000 km lifetime. Hence aluminum substitution is **life-cycle beneficial** for ICE vehicles.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the terms: (a) End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV), (b) Automotive Shredder Residue (ASR), and (c) Design for Disassembly (DFD).**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV):** A vehicle that has reached the end of its useful service life and is destined for treatment at an Authorised Treatment Facility (ATF). It is the regulatory unit of the EU ELV Directive 2000/53/EC, which mandates recovery and recycling rates.

**(b) Automotive Shredder Residue (ASR):** The heterogeneous, lightweight residual fraction remaining after the shredding and separation stages of ELV processing. Also called "car fluff," it consists of plastics, foams, textiles, rubber, glass fines, and dirt, and typically represents **20–25%** of the input vehicle mass.

**(c) Design for Disassembly (DFD):** A design philosophy in which a vehicle is engineered so that its components and materials can be efficiently and non-destructively separated at end of life, using reversible joining methods (bolts, clips, snap-fits) instead of permanent welds or adhesive bonds.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**List any six hazardous substances whose use in vehicle materials is restricted or banned under the EU ELV Directive.**

**Model Answer:** The four primary restricted substances are:
1. **Lead (Pb)** — historically used in wheel balancing weights, solder, and battery terminals; phased out progressively.
2. **Mercury (Hg)** — used in small amounts in switches and display backlights; effectively banned.
3. **Cadmium (Cd)** — used in stabilizers, coatings, and some battery chemistries.
4. **Hexavalent Chromium (Cr⁶⁺)** — used in anti-corrosion coatings on fasteners and body panels.

Additional substances restricted by other regulations (e.g., REACH) that students may cite for completeness: **Polybrominated biphenyls (PBB)**, **Polybrominated diphenyl ethers (PBDE)**, and certain **phthalates** in interior plastics.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the typical **End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) treatment process** from collection to secondary material recovery. Discuss the role of de-pollution, shredding, and post-shredder separation technologies.

**(b)** A passenger car of total dry mass **1,380 kg** enters an ELV facility. After processing, the recorded mass flows are: Reuse = 78 kg, Material recycling = 1,098 kg, Energy recovery = 62 kg, and ASR = 142 kg.
**Compute:** (i) Reuse rate, (ii) Recycling rate, (iii) Recovery rate, (iv) ASR fraction. **Comment** on whether the vehicle meets the EU ELV Directive targets.

---

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — ELV Treatment Flow (7 Marks)

The answer should present a labelled flowchart (draw the architecture from SECTION 4.1) and explain each stage:

- **Collection and Pre-treatment** (2 marks): Receipt at Authorised Treatment Facility (ATF), identification, hazardous component inventory.
- **De-pollution** (2 marks): Mandatory removal of battery, fuel, oils, coolant, brake fluid, refrigerant, airbags, catalytic converter, and Hg switches. This step is legally required **before** any further processing.
- **Shredding** (1.5 marks): Hammer mill at 300–400 RPM, fragment size 50–100 mm. Produces a bulk flow of mixed fragments.
- **Separation and Recovery** (1.5 marks): Magnetic drum for ferrous (steel, cast iron); Eddy-current separator for non-ferrous (aluminum, copper, zinc); air classification for light fraction (plastics, foams); density tables for heavy fraction. Output streams are sent to EAF, aluminum remelt, and ASR processing.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $M_v = 1{,}380$ kg, $m_{reuse} = 78$ kg, $m_{recycled} = 1{,}098$ kg, $m_{energy} = 62$ kg, $m_{ASR} = 142$ kg.

**Step 1 — Verify mass balance:**

$$78 + 1{,}098 + 62 + 142 = 1{,}380 \text{ kg} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute Reuse rate (1.5 marks):**

$$R_{reuse} = \frac{78}{1{,}380} \times 100 = 5.65\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute Recycling rate (2 marks):**

$$R_r = \frac{78 + 1{,}098}{1{,}380} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}176}{1{,}380} \times 100 = 85.22\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute Recovery rate (1.5 marks):**

$$R_{cov} = \frac{78 + 1{,}098 + 62}{1{,}380} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}238}{1{,}380} \times 100 = 89.71\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute ASR fraction (1 mark):**

$$\phi_{ASR} = \frac{142}{1{,}380} \times 100 = 10.29\%$$

**Step 6 — Compliance comment (1 mark):**

- $R_r = 85.22\% \geq 85\%$ → **MEETS recycling target** (marginally).
- $R_{cov} = 89.71\% < 95\%$ → **FAILS recovery target** by **5.29 percentage points**.
- **Engineering recommendation:** Reduce ASR (currently 10.29%) by adopting DFD principles, mono-material plastic assemblies, and labelling of polymer components. Targeting an ASR reduction to 5% would bring $R_{cov}$ above 95%.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Discuss the **sustainable material selection** methodology for automotive body-in-white (BIW) applications. Compare the use of **Advanced High-Strength Steel (AHSS)**, **Aluminum alloys**, and **Carbon-Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** in terms of density, embodied energy, recyclability, and life-cycle CO₂ footprint. Use a comparative table.

**(b)** A vehicle manufacturer replaces **250 kg of mild steel** with **aluminum alloy** in a body-in-white. Calculate the embodied energy saved and CO₂ emission avoided per vehicle. Use the following data:

$$EE_{steel,primary} = 22.0 \text{ GJ/t}, \quad EE_{Al,primary} = 45.0 \text{ GJ/t}, \quad EE_{Al,recycled} = 2.5 \text{ GJ/t}$$
$$CO_2 \text{ intensity} = 0.07 \text{ tCO₂eq/GJ} \text{ (steel)}, \quad 0.10 \text{ tCO₂eq/GJ} \text{ (Al)}$$

Comment on whether the substitution is environmentally justified for a vehicle with a **lifetime mileage of 150,000 km**.

---

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Material Comparison (7 Marks)

The answer must present a **comparative table** (3 marks) and an **engineering discussion** (4 marks).

**Comparative Table (representative values):**

| Property | AHSS | Aluminum 6xxx | CFRP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 7,850 | 2,700 | 1,550 |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 1,200–1,800 | 250–350 | 600–1,500 |
| Embodied energy (GJ/t) | 25.0 | 45.0 | 95.0 |
| Recyclability (%) | 86 | 75 | <10 |
| Life-cycle CO₂ (t/t) | 2.10 | 8.24 | 24.0 |
| Relative cost | 1.2 | 2.5 | 15.0 |

**Discussion points (4 marks):**
- **AHSS** dominates in **recyclability** and **cost**; offers 15–25% mass reduction versus mild steel; current default for BIW.
- **Aluminum** offers **40–50% mass reduction** at acceptable recyclability; widely used in luxury and EV applications (closures, body panels, battery enclosures).
- **CFRP** offers **>50% mass reduction** but suffers from **very low recyclability**, high embodied energy, and prohibitive cost; currently limited to supercars and racing.
- The **"lightweighting paradox"** must be addressed — material choice must be justified by use-phase fuel/energy savings, not by mass alone.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\Delta m = 250$ kg; $EE_{steel} = 22.0$ GJ/t; $EE_{Al,recycled} = 2.5$ GJ/t; $CO_2$ intensities as above.

**Step 1 — Mass replaced and substituted:**

$$m_{steel} = 250 \text{ kg} = 0.250 \text{ t}$$
$$m_{Al} = 250 \times \dfrac{2{,}700}{7{,}850} = 250 \times 0.344 = 86.0 \text{ kg} = 0.0860 \text{ t}$$

(For equal volume substitution; for equal stiffness, use mechanical equivalence.)

**Step 2 — Embodied energy comparison (2 marks):**

$$EE_{steel} = 22.0 \times 0.250 = 5.500 \text{ GJ}$$
$$EE_{Al,recycled} = 2.5 \times 0.086 = 0.215 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 3 — Energy saved (1 mark):**

$$EE_{saved} = 5.500 - 0.215 = 5.285 \text{ GJ/vehicle}$$

**Step 4 — CO₂ calculation (2 marks):**

$$CO_{2,steel} = 5.500 \times 0.07 = 0.385 \text{ tCO₂eq}$$
$$CO_{2,Al} = 0.215 \times 0.10 = 0.0215 \text{ tCO₂eq}$$

$$CO_{2,saved} = 0.385 - 0.0215 = 0.3635 \text{ tCO₂eq/vehicle}$$

**Step 5 — Use-phase benefit (1 mark):**

For an ICE vehicle, **mass reduction of ~164 kg** (assuming equal-volume substitution) yields a fuel saving of:

$$\Delta f = 0.6 \times \frac{164}{100} = 0.984 \text{ L/100 km}$$

Over 150,000 km:

$$\Delta F_{total} = 0.984 \times 1{,}500 = 1{,}476 \text{ L of fuel}$$

At 2.31 kg CO₂eq/L (petrol), this saves:

$$CO_{2,use} = 1{,}476 \times 2.31 \times 10^{-3} = 3.41 \text{ tCO₂eq}$$

**Step 6 — Final comment (1 mark):**

Use-phase CO₂ savings (**3.41 tCO₂eq**) vastly exceed production-phase savings (**0.36 tCO₂eq**), confirming that aluminum substitution is **strongly environmentally justified** for a 150,000 km vehicle life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse **embodied energy** with **life-cycle energy**. Always state the boundary of your calculation (cradle-to-gate vs. cradle-to-grave).
> - Do **not** omit the use-phase benefit — it is the **single largest term** in a life-cycle assessment of an automotive material substitution.
> - Always specify the CO₂ intensity factor used; examiners penalize vague statements like "some CO₂ is saved."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2):**

- **ELV Directive 2000/53/EC** mandates $R_r \geq 85\%$ and $R_{cov} \geq 95\%$; banned substances: **Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr⁶⁺**.
- **Reuse + Recycle + Energy Recovery + ASR = 100%** (mass balance on dry basis).
- **Steel recycling** uses **EAF route**; saves **~12.5 GJ/tonne** and **~0.88 tCO₂eq/tonne** vs. BF-BOF.
- **Aluminum recycling** uses only **~5% of primary energy**; the canonical "5% rule" is a KTU favorite.
- **Plastics** are the **weakest link** in vehicle recycling — 4 routes: mechanical, chemical, feedstock, energy recovery.
- **Tyre recycling** routes: re-tread, crumb rubber, pyrolysis (oil + carbon black + steel), TDF.
- **PGM recovery** from catalytic converters exceeds **95%**; Pt, Pd, Rh are critical secondary resources.
- **ASR / car fluff** is the **key design target** for reduction — typically 20–25% of input mass.
- **Design for Recycling (DFR)** and **Design for Disassembly (DFD)** start at the **concept sketch** stage, not at end of life.
- **Lightweighting paradox:** mass reduction is beneficial only if the **use-phase savings exceed the production-phase penalty**. For ICE vehicles, the break-even distance for steel→aluminum is **~5,000–6,000 km**.
- **Natural fiber composites** (flax, jute, hemp) offer a **bio-based, lower-CO₂** alternative for non-structural interior panels.
- **Sustainable manufacturing** levers: dry machining, MQL, near-net-shape forming, powder metallurgy, additive manufacturing, closed-loop water systems, waterborne paints, renewable energy.
- **Circular economy** in automotive = closed-loop material flow from **raw material → manufacturing → use → EoL → secondary material → manufacturing** (the famous "cradle-to-cradle" loop).
- **Ashby charts** with sustainability axes (embodied energy vs. stiffness, CO₂ vs. strength) are the standard tool for sustainable material selection.
- **EU ELV Directive Article 7** percentages — memorize: **85% recycling, 95% recovery** (by mass, dry basis).
- **Authorised Treatment Facility (ATF)** is the legal entity responsible for ELV de-pollution and dismantling.
- **Eddy-current separation** is the workhorse for non-ferrous automotive recycling (aluminum, copper, brass).
- **Battery recycling** (especially for EVs) is an emerging critical stream: Li, Co, Ni, Mn recovery via hydrometallurgical and pyrometallurgical routes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paints and Adhesives - Paints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Paints and Adhesives - Paints ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Vehicle Body Engineering (PEAUT741)**, paint is defined as a **chemically engineered, multicomponent liquid coating system** that is applied to the surface of an automobile body and subsequently converted into a solid, continuous, adherent film through a controlled drying and curing mechanism. It is designed to perform three simultaneous engineering functions: **corrosion protection, surface decoration, and functional identification**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Paint is a homogeneous dispersion of **pigment** (color + opacity) in a **vehicle** (binder + solvent), which, when applied in a thin layer to a substrate, forms an opaque, protective, and decorative film through oxidation, evaporation, or polymerization. The **four mandatory constituents** of any automotive paint formulation are: 1. **Pigment** – solid coloring and opacifying particles 2. **Binder (Resin/Vehicle)** – film-forming polymeric matrix 3. **Solvent (Thinner)** – viscosity regulator for application 4. **Additives** – property modifiers (anti-settling, UV stabilizers, etc.) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Four-Ingredient Sandwich"** > Imagine paint as a **layered sandwich**: > - The **bread (Binder)** holds everything together and sticks to the body. > - The **filling (Pigment)** gives it color, taste, and texture. > - The **water sprayed to make the filling spreadable (Solvent)** makes the mixture thin enough to spread evenly with a knife. > - The **salt and pepper (Additives)** preserve it longer and make it look more appetizing. > > When you toast the sandwich (Curing), the water evaporates, the bread hardens, and you get a **crisp, durable protective film** that resists the environment — exactly like an automotive paint film drying on a car body. ### 1.3 Physical Standards & Performance Metrics The performance of an automotive paint film is quantified by universally accepted industrial parameters: - **Film Thickness (DFT – Dry Film Thickness):** 80–120 µm (total multi-coat system) - **Gloss (60° Gloss Units):** > 90 GU for OEM topcoats - **Adhesion Strength:** Cross-hatch adhesion rating **ASTM D3359 – 5B (no peeling)** - **Hardness:** Pencil hardness **≥ H to 2H** - **Salt Spray Resistance:** **> 1000 hours** (ASTM B117) - **MEK (Methyl Ethyl Ketone) Rub Test:** **> 100 double rubs** without film breakdown > [!IMPORTANT] > These numerical benchmarks are **board-favorite** figures. KTU examiners frequently ask for the **standard thickness range of an automotive paint film** and the **typical corrosion resistance duration**. ### 1.4 Primary Functions of Automotive Paint An automotive paint system is **not a single layer** but a **multi-layered functional stack** performing distinct engineering roles: | Layer | Function | Typical DFT | |-------|----------|------------| | **Electrodeposition (E-Coat)** | Primary anti-corrosion primer | 18–25 µm | | **Primer/Surfacer** | Smoothness, stone-chip resistance, adhesion promotion | 25–35 µm | | **Base Coat** | Color, metallic effect, aesthetics | 15–25 µm | | **Clear Coat** | Gloss, UV protection, chemical resistance | 35–45 µm | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common question is *"Why are multiple coats used instead of a single thick coat?"* — the answer lies in **defect tolerance, cure uniformity, and functional segregation** (corrosion vs. color vs. gloss). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Breakdown of Paint Film Formation The transformation from a liquid paint layer to a solid protective film occurs via one of **three physico-chemical mechanisms** (or their combinations): **Mechanism 1 — Evaporation Drying (Lacquers)** - The solvent evaporates, leaving behind the binder-pigment matrix. - Film formation is **purely physical** — no chemical reaction. - Example: **Nitrocellulose lacquers** used in vintage and refinish applications. **Mechanism 2 — Oxidation Drying (Alkyd Enamels)** - Binder reacts with atmospheric oxygen via oxidative crosslinking. - Produces a hard, glossy film over **6–24 hours**. - Example: **Traditional synthetic enamel paints**. **Mechanism 3 — Polymerization/Crosslinking (Modern OEM Coatings)** - Two reactive components (resin + hardener) undergo **thermosetting polymerization**. - Requires **elevated temperature (140–160°C)** for **20–30 minutes** in an oven. - Examples: **2K Polyurethane, Acrylic-Melamine, Epoxy systems**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for paint calculations. Master these for direct 3-mark and 14-mark derivations. | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Standard Value | Engineering Application | |---|---------------------|------------|------------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Spreading Rate (SR)** | $SR = \dfrac{A}{V} = \dfrac{A \times \rho_{solids} \times 10}{NVM\% \times DFT}$ | m²/L | Paint quantity estimation for a vehicle body | | 2 | **Volume Solids (NVM)** | $NVM\% = \dfrac{V_{solids}}{V_{paint}} \times 100$ | % | Determines wet film thickness required | | 3 | **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{NVM\% / 100}$ | µm | Application parameter | | 4 | **Coverage per Coat (C)** | $C = \dfrac{10 \times NVM\%}{DFT}$ | m²/L | Material planning | | 5 | **Transfer Efficiency (TE)** | $TE = \dfrac{Mass\ deposited}{Mass\ sprayed} \times 100$ | % (Electrostatic ≈ 70–85%) | Process economics | | 6 | **Paint Consumption (M)** | $M = \dfrac{A \times DFT}{TE \times NVM\% / 100 \times \rho_{paint}}$ | kg | Body shop material balance | | 7 | **VOC Emission** | $VOC = \dfrac{(1 - NVM\%) \times \rho_{solvent}}{10}$ | g/L | Environmental compliance | | 8 | **Viscosity (Ford Cup #4)** | $\eta = t_{efflux}$ | seconds @ 25°C | Spray parameter control | | 9 | **Adhesion (Pull-off)** | $\sigma_{adhesion} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | MPa (≥ 5 MPa typical) | Substrate bonding strength | | 10 | **Total DFT (Multi-coat)** | $DFT_{total} = DFT_{primer} + DFT_{base} + DFT_{clear}$ | µm (80–120 µm total) | OEM specification | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** In all formulas above, vertical bar symbols have been replaced with the LaTeX `\vert` or `/` operator to preserve markdown table integrity. The expression `\vert x \vert` denotes **absolute value** without breaking the table parser. ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is DFT (Dry Film Thickness) the master variable?** Every coating property — corrosion resistance, gloss, hiding power, mechanical strength — is a **non-linear function of film thickness**. Too thin ⇒ corrosion failure; too thick ⇒ sagging, solvent popping, and economic waste. The KTU exam frequently tests the calculation of WFT given a target DFT. **Why multiple layers?** - **Single thick layer** suffers from internal stress, solvent entrapment, and curing gradient. - **Multi-layer system** distributes functions: each layer is optimized for **one primary job**. **Why the clear coat is mandatory in modern cars?** - The base coat contains the **pigment and metallic flakes**, which are UV-sensitive. - The clear coat acts as a **sacrificial UV-transparent armor**, taking the brunt of weathering. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In a modern automotive OEM plant, the **Paint Shop is the largest energy consumer (≈ 60% of total plant energy)** and the **largest VOC emitter**. Understanding paint chemistry directly impacts: - **Corrosion warranty periods** (typically **6–12 years** in modern vehicles) - **Resale value** (paint condition is a top-3 quality perception metric) - **Environmental compliance** (Euro 6/BS-VI norms cap VOC at **< 35 g/m²** for OEM lines) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Wet Film Thickness from Dry Film Thickness **Problem Statement:** A vehicle body panel requires a base coat of **25 µm DFT** using a paint with **40% volume solids**. Calculate the **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** that the operator must apply. **Step 1 — Define the Governing Relationship** The volume of solids deposited per unit area is **conserved** between wet and dry states. $$V_{solids} = WFT \times NVM\% = DFT$$ Wait — the rigorous form is: $$DFT = WFT \times \dfrac{NVM\%}{100}$$ **Step 2 — State the Known Quantities** - Target $DFT = 25\ \mu m$ - Volume solids $NVM\% = 40$ **Step 3 — Algebraic Isolation of WFT** $$WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{NVM\%/100} = \dfrac{DFT \times 100}{NVM\%}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Substitution** $$WFT = \dfrac{25 \times 100}{40} = \dfrac{2500}{40}$$ **Step 5 — Final Result** $$WFT = 62.5\ \mu m$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** The operator must lay down a wet film **2.5× thicker** than the final dry film, because **60% of the wet volume (the solvent) will evaporate** during flashing and oven curing. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Paint Consumption for a Vehicle **Problem Statement:** A sedan body has a total paintable surface area of **80 m²**. The paint system comprises: - Primer: **30 µm DFT, 55% NVM, density 1.4 g/cm³** - Base Coat: **20 µm DFT, 35% NVM, density 1.0 g/cm³** - Clear Coat: **40 µm DFT, 45% NVM, density 1.05 g/cm³** The transfer efficiency is **75%** for all coats. Calculate the **total paint mass (kg) required per car**, assuming **no losses** other than transfer efficiency. **Step 1 — General Consumption Formula** $$M = \dfrac{A \times DFT}{TE \times NVM\%/100 \times \rho_{paint}}$$ **Step 2 — Primer Calculation** $$M_{primer} = \dfrac{80 \times 30}{0.75 \times 0.55 \times 1.4 \times 10^3}$$ Convert DFT to compatible units: $30\ \mu m = 30 \times 10^{-6}\ m$. Working in $\mu m \cdot m^{2} = 10^{-6}\ m^{3}$ for clarity, and dividing by $\rho$ in $g/cm^3$: $$M_{primer} = \dfrac{80 \times 30}{0.75 \times 0.55 \times 1.4} = \dfrac{2400}{0.5775} = 4155.84\ g$$ $$M_{primer} = 4.156\ kg$$ **Step 3 — Base Coat Calculation** $$M_{base} = \dfrac{80 \times 20}{0.75 \times 0.35 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{1600}{0.2625} = 6095.24\ g$$ $$M_{base} = 6.095\ kg$$ **Step 4 — Clear Coat Calculation** $$M_{clear} = \dfrac{80 \times 40}{0.75 \times 0.45 \times 1.05} = \dfrac{3200}{0.354375} = 9029.42\ g$$ $$M_{clear} = 9.029\ kg$$ **Step 5 — Total Mass** $$M_{total} = M_{primer} + M_{base} + M_{clear}$$ $$M_{total} = 4.156 + 6.095 + 9.029$$ $$M_{total} = 19.28\ kg\ per\ vehicle$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Validation:** A typical automotive OEM paint shop consumes **18–22 kg of paint per car body**. Our result of **19.28 kg** falls exactly in this range — confirming the calculation methodology. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: VOC Emission Calculation **Problem Statement:** A water-borne base coat has **35% volume solids, density 1.05 g/cm³**, and contains only water as solvent (water density = 1.0 g/cm³). Calculate **VOC in g/L** for regulatory compliance. **Step 1 — Identify VOC Components** In a **water-borne system**, water is **not** a VOC (exempt per EU Directive 2004/42/EC). However, the problem assumes VOC = non-solid content for simplicity. **Step 2 — Volume of VOC per Litre** $$V_{VOC} = 1 - NVM\% = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65\ L$$ **Step 3 — Mass of VOC per Litre** $$VOC = V_{VOC} \times \rho_{solvent} \times 1000$$ $$VOC = 0.65 \times 1.0 \times 1000 = 650\ g/L$$ > [!NOTE] > In **real water-borne systems**, co-solvent content is typically 5–10%, so actual VOC drops to **< 150 g/L**, satisfying EU Directive limits of **< 420 g/L** for base coats. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Paint Types > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is a **high-yield KTU comparison tool**. Board questions often ask: *"Compare enamel, lacquer, and polyurethane paints"* or *"Which paint is preferred for OEM application and why?"* | Property | Nitrocellulose Lacquer | Alkyd Enamel | 2K Polyurethane | Acrylic-Melamine (OEM) | |----------|------------------------|--------------|------------------|--------------------------| | **Drying Mechanism** | Evaporation | Oxidation | Polymerization (2K) | Thermoset crosslink | | **Cure Temperature** | RT (25°C) | RT | RT to 60°C | **140–160°C (oven)** | | **Gloss Retention** | Moderate (2 yrs) | Good (3–4 yrs) | Excellent (**8+ yrs**) | **Excellent (10+ yrs)** | | **Hardness** | Soft (HB) | Medium (F–H) | Hard (2H–3H) | **Very Hard (3H–4H)** | | **Chemical Resistance** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | **Excellent** | | **Application** | Refinish / Vintage | Industrial | Refinish, premium OEM | **OEM mass production** | | **VOC Level** | Very High | High | Medium | **Low (water-borne variants)** | | **Cost** | Low | Low | High | **High (capital-intensive)** | | **Yellowing Tendency** | Yes | Yes | No | **No** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Automotive Paint Layer Architecture This diagram shows the **functional cross-section of a modern multi-coat automotive paint system** applied on a steel substrate. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["ENVIRONMENT
UV, Rain, Stone Chips"] B["CLEAR COAT
35-45 µm
Gloss and UV Shield"] C["BASE COAT
15-25 µm
Color and Metallic Flakes"] D["PRIMER SURFACER
25-35 µm
Smoothness and Adhesion"] E["E-COAT
18-25 µm
Cathodic Anti-Corrosion"] F["PHOSPHATE LAYER
5-10 µm
Zn/Fe Conversion Coating"] G["STEEL SUBSTRATE
Body-in-White Panel"] H["ROAD SIDE
Salt, Moisture, Abrasion"] A --> B H --> F B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000 style G fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style H fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The diagram flows from the **aggressive external environment (UV, salt, stones)** at the top down through the protective layers to the **steel substrate**. Each layer **sacrifices itself** to protect the layer below, with the **clear coat taking the most weathering load**. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Paint Film Formation Mechanisms This flowchart maps the **three curing mechanisms** based on binder chemistry. ```mermaid flowchart LR Start(["LIQUID PAINT
APPLIED ON PANEL"]) S1{"BINDER
CHEMISTRY?"} P1["NITROCELLULOSE
ACRYLIC LACQUER"] P2["ALKYD ENAMEL
OIL-BASED"] P3["2K POLYURETHANE
EPOXY
ACRYLIC-MELAMINE"] M1["MECHANISM A
PHYSICAL
EVAPORATION"] M2["MECHANISM B
CHEMICAL
OXIDATION"] M3["MECHANISM C
THERMOSET
POLYMERIZATION"] E1["FILM FORMS
RT 10-30 MIN
REVERSIBLE"] E2["FILM HARDENS
RT 6-24 HRS
IRREVERSIBLE"] E3["FILM CURES
140-160 C
20-30 MIN OVEN"] Out(["SOLID PROTECTIVE
