PAINT FILM"]) Start --> S1 S1 -->|Thermoplastic| P1 S1 -->|Oxidative| P2 S1 -->|Reactive 2K or 1K| P3 P1 --> M1 --> E1 --> Out P2 --> M2 --> E2 --> Out P3 --> M3 --> E3 --> Out style Start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style M1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style M2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 style M3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The decision tree branches based on **binder chemistry** into three distinct curing pathways, all converging into the same final **solid paint film** — but with vastly different durability, gloss retention, and process requirements. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Paint Application Process Flow in OEM Plant This block diagram maps the **end-to-end process flow** of a typical automotive paint shop. ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["STAGE 1 PRE-TREATMENT
Degrease, Rinse, Phosphate, DI Rinse"] S2["STAGE 2 E-COAT
Cathodic Electrodeposition
CED at 28-32 C, 250-350 V"] S3["STAGE 3 SEALER AND PVC
Anti-Chip, Seam Sealant, Underbody PVC"] S4["STAGE 4 PRIMER SURFACER
Robotic Spray, 25-35 µm DFT"] S5["STAGE 5 BASE COAT
Rotary Bell Applicator, 15-25 µm DFT"] S6["STAGE 6 CLEAR COAT
Wet-on-Wet or After Flash, 35-45 µm"] S7["STAGE 7 OVEN CURING
140-160 C, 20-30 Minutes"] S8["STAGE 8 INSPECTION
DFT, Gloss, Color, Defects, Polish"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S7 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S8 fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Application Note:** Memorize the **sequence (PDI → E-Coat → Sealer → Primer → Base → Clear → Bake → Inspection)**. A common 14-mark question is: *"Explain the complete painting process of an automobile body, listing the function of each stage."* ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define paint and list its four essential constituents with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Paint** is a homogeneous liquid mixture that, when applied as a thin layer on a substrate, dries to form a continuous, opaque, protective, and decorative film. The four essential constituents are: 1. **Pigment** (e.g., Titanium Dioxide TiO₂) – provides color, opacity, and corrosion inhibition. 2. **Binder/Resin** (e.g., Acrylic-Melamine) – the film-forming polymer that binds pigment and adheres to the substrate. 3. **Solvent/Thinner** (e.g., Xylene, Toluene, or Water) – reduces viscosity for spray application and evaporates during drying. 4. **Additives** (e.g., UV stabilizers, anti-settling agents, flow promoters) – modify specific properties like durability, leveling, and shelf life. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** A common mistake is listing "vehicle" as a separate component. The **"vehicle" in paint terminology is binder + solvent together**, not a fifth constituent. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State any three functions of paint applied on an automobile body.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Corrosion Protection:** The paint film acts as a barrier against moisture, oxygen, road salt, and industrial pollutants, thereby preventing rust formation on the steel body. **(1 mark)** 2. **Aesthetic Decoration:** Paint provides color, gloss, and visual appeal (metallic, pearlescent, matte finishes) which directly influences the perceived quality and resale value of the vehicle. **(1 mark)** 3. **Functional Identification:** Different colors are used for fleet identification, taxi/emergency vehicle coding, and model differentiation in the marketplace. **(1 mark)** *(Other valid answers: UV protection, chemical resistance, heat reflection, scratch resistance, and noise dampening.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation**, every Module 2 question offers an **internal choice**. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each with sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**. --- ### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a)** Explain in detail the **functions of each constituent** (pigment, binder, solvent, additive) used in an automotive paint formulation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **complete painting process of an automobile body** in a modern OEM plant, listing the function of each stage. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer (a) — Functions of Each Constituent (7 Marks):** 1. **Pigment (2 marks):** - Provides **color, opacity, and hiding power**. - Types include **inorganic (TiO₂, Iron Oxide, Carbon Black)** and **organic (Phthalocyanine Blue, Azo Pigments)**. - **Metallic pigments** (aluminum flakes) and **pearl/mica pigments** (coated mica) provide special effects. - Some pigments act as **corrosion inhibitors** (e.g., zinc chromate, red lead — now restricted due to toxicity). 2. **Binder/Resin (2 marks):** - The **film-forming matrix** that holds pigment particles together and adheres the film to the substrate. - Determines **gloss, hardness, flexibility, and chemical resistance**. - Examples: **Alkyd, Acrylic, Polyurethane, Epoxy, Melamine-Formaldehyde**. - In OEM: **Acrylic-Melamine** and **2K Polyurethane** dominate. 3. **Solvent (1.5 marks):** - Reduces viscosity to enable **spray application** (typically **15–25 seconds Ford Cup #4**). - Evaporates during flash-off and oven curing. - Types: **Aromatic hydrocarbons (Xylene, Toluene), Aliphatic hydrocarbons, Ketones, Esters, Water (in water-borne systems)**. - **Water-borne systems** are mandated by **EU Directive 2004/42/EC** to reduce VOC. 4. **Additives (1.5 marks):** - **UV stabilizers** (HALS — Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers): prevent polymer degradation. - **Anti-settling agents**: prevent pigment sedimentation in the can. - **Flow and leveling agents**: eliminate brush/spray marks, prevent orange peel. - **Catalysts/Hardeners**: accelerate crosslinking (e.g., isocyanate hardeners in 2K PU). - **Anti-foam agents, biocides, slip additives, conductivity additives** for electrostatic application. --- **Model Answer (b) — Complete Painting Process (7 Marks):** Refer to **Section 4.3 (Mermaid Block 3)** for the process flow diagram. The 8 stages are: 1. **Pre-Treatment (Degrease + Phosphate):** Removes oils, dirt, mill scale; zinc/iron phosphate layer (5–10 µm) improves adhesion and corrosion resistance. *(1 mark)* 2. **Electrodeposition (E-Coat):** Cathodic epoxy primer deposited at 250–350 V, 28–32°C; provides uniform anti-corrosion coverage including internal box sections. *(1 mark)* 3. **Sealer and PVC Application:** Seam sealants prevent water ingress; underbody PVC coating resists stone chips and abrasion. *(1 mark)* 4. **Primer/Surfacer Spray:** Applied by **robotic rotary bell atomizers**; provides surface smoothness, sandability, and stone-chip resistance (25–35 µm DFT). *(1 mark)* 5. **Base Coat Application:** Contains color pigments and effect pigments (metallic/pearl); applied in **wet-on-wet** process; 15–25 µm DFT. *(1 mark)* 6. **Clear Coat Application:** Transparent 2K polyurethane or acrylic-melamine; provides gloss, depth, and UV protection (35–45 µm DFT). *(1 mark)* 7. **Oven Curing:** **140–160°C for 20–30 minutes** triggers thermoset crosslinking; unifies all coats into a single integrated film. *(0.5 mark)* 8. **Inspection and Rectification:** DFT measurement (magnetic/eddy current gauges), gloss meter (60°), color spectrophotometer, defect detection, polishing for minor imperfections. *(0.5 mark)* --- ### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **(a)** Compare the following paint types with respect to drying mechanism, durability, gloss retention, and typical application: **(i)** Nitrocellulose lacquer, **(ii)** Alkyd enamel, **(iii)** 2K Polyurethane, **(iv)** Acrylic-Melamine. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sedan body has a **total paintable surface area of 75 m²**. The OEM paint system specifications are: - **Primer:** 30 µm DFT, 50% NVM, density 1.5 g/cm³ - **Base Coat:** 20 µm DFT, 40% NVM, density 1.0 g/cm³ - **Clear Coat:** 40 µm DFT, 45% NVM, density 1.05 g/cm³ The transfer efficiency is **70%** for all coats. Calculate the **total paint mass required per vehicle** in **kg**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks):** | Property | Nitrocellulose Lacquer | Alkyd Enamel | 2K Polyurethane | Acrylic-Melamine (OEM) | |----------|------------------------|--------------|------------------|--------------------------| | **Drying** | Evaporation (RT) | Oxidation (RT) | Polymerization (RT/60°C) | **Thermoset (140–160°C)** | | **Durability** | 1–2 years | 3–5 years | 8–10 years | **10+ years** | | **Gloss Retention** | Poor (yellows) | Moderate | Excellent | **Excellent** | | **Application** | Refinish, vintage | Industrial, agricultural | Premium refinish, OEM | **OEM mass production** | *(Allocate ~1.75 marks per paint type, covering all four parameters.)* > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Preferred Answer Note:** Mention that **Acrylic-Melamine is the industry standard for OEM** due to its **superior weathering, color retention, and process compatibility** with robotic application lines. --- **Model Answer (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Formula Used (1 mark):** $$M = \dfrac{A \times DFT}{TE \times NVM\%/100 \times \rho_{paint}}$$ **Given Data:** - $A = 75\ m^{2}$ - $TE = 0.70$ **Step 1 — Primer (1.5 marks):** $$M_{primer} = \dfrac{75 \times 30}{0.70 \times 0.50 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{2250}{0.525} = 4285.71\ g$$ $$M_{primer} = 4.286\ kg$$ **Step 2 — Base Coat (1.5 marks):** $$M_{base} = \dfrac{75 \times 20}{0.70 \times 0.40 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{1500}{0.28} = 5357.14\ g$$ $$M_{base} = 5.357\ kg$$ **Step 3 — Clear Coat (1.5 marks):** $$M_{clear} = \dfrac{75 \times 40}{0.70 \times 0.45 \times 1.05} = \dfrac{3000}{0.33075} = 9070.30\ g$$ $$M_{clear} = 9.070\ kg$$ **Step 4 — Total Mass (1.5 marks):** $$M_{total} = 4.286 + 5.357 + 9.070$$ $$\boxed{M_{total} = 18.71\ kg\ per\ vehicle}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Validation:** Result of **18.71 kg** aligns with industry standard of **18–22 kg per car**, confirming dimensional and methodological correctness. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks — Critical Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch in Paint Calculations:** The most common error is mixing **µm with m²** without consistent volume unit conversion. Remember: $1\ \mu m \times 1\ m^{2} = 10^{-6}\ m^{3} = 1\ L \times 10^{-3}$. Always keep $\rho$ in $g/cm^{3}$ and $DFT$ in $\mu m$ for compact arithmetic. > > 2. **Confusing DFT with WFT:** Students often interchange these. **WFT ≥ DFT** always; the ratio is governed by **NVM%**. > > 3. **Omitting Transfer Efficiency:** A 70% TE means you need to spray **1/0.70 = 1.43×** the theoretically calculated mass. Many students forget this and lose 1–2 marks. > > 4. **Listing "Vehicle" as a Constituent:** The term "vehicle" in paint chemistry is the **binder + solvent combination**, NOT a separate ingredient. > > 5. **Skipping Diagram in Process Question:** A **7-mark process question without a flow diagram loses 1–2 marks** even if the text is perfect. Always include the Mermaid process flow. > > 6. **Wrong Process Sequence:** Memorize: **Pre-treatment → E-Coat → Sealer → Primer → Base → Clear → Bake → Inspection**. Writing them out of order invites partial deduction. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Paint Module 2** - ✅ **Paint = Pigment + Binder + Solvent + Additives** (4 mandatory constituents, no fifth). - ✅ **Total automotive paint film thickness = 80–120 µm** distributed over **3–4 functional layers**. - ✅ **Clear coat is 35–45 µm**, base coat is 15–25 µm, primer is 25–35 µm, e-coat is 18–25 µm. - ✅ **OEM oven cure temperature = 140–160°C for 20–30 minutes.** - ✅ **Acrylic-Melamine is the OEM industry standard; 2K Polyurethane is the premium refinish standard.** - ✅ **Master formula:** $WFT = \dfrac{DFT \times 100}{NVM\%}$ - ✅ **Master formula:** $M = \dfrac{A \times DFT}{TE \times NVM\%/100 \times \rho_{paint}}$ - ✅ **Transfer efficiency order:** Conventional spray (30–40%) < HVLP (60–70%) < **Rotary bell + Electrostatic (70–85%)** < Disk + ESTA (90%+). - ✅ **Salt spray resistance benchmark: > 1000 hours** (ASTM B117). - ✅ **Cross-hatch adhesion: 5B (best) to 0B (worst)** as per ASTM D3359. - ✅ **VOC limits (EU Directive 2004/42/EC):** < 420 g/L for base coats; < 35 g/m² for OEM lines. - ✅ **Process sequence:** Pre-treatment → E-Coat → Sealer → Primer → Base → Clear → Bake → Inspection. - ✅ **Three drying mechanisms:** Evaporation (lacquer), Oxidation (alkyd), Polymerization (2K PU, Acrylic-Melamine). - ✅ **Common paint defects to remember:** Orange peel, runs/sags, fish eyes, solvent popping, pinholes, dieback, mottling (metallics), and color mismatch. - ✅ **Adhesion testing standards:** ASTM D3359 (cross-hatch), ASTM D4541 (pull-off), ASTM D523 (gloss).

topics.title: Adhesives and Their Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Adhesives and Their Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Adhesives and Their Properties

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Adhesive):**
> An **adhesive** is a non-metallic substance capable of joining two similar or dissimilar materials (adherends) together by surface attachment, resisting separation through the development of internal cohesive forces and interfacial adhesive forces. In vehicle body engineering, adhesives are critical for structural bonding, sealing, vibration damping, and corrosion prevention.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sticking two pieces of paper together using glue. The glue does not "melt" the paper into one solid block. Instead, it forms a thin, strong "bridge" layer between the two paper surfaces. This bridge holds them together because the glue molecules grab onto the paper molecules on both sides, and the glue itself is strong enough not to tear in the middle. That thin bridge is essentially the **adhesive layer**, the two paper pieces are the **adherends**, and the grip on either side is the **adhesive force**, while the glue's internal strength is the **cohesive force**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Adhesives in Car Bodies?**
> Modern automobiles use adhesives to:
> - **Reduce weight** (replacing heavy spot welds) — directly improving **fuel efficiency**.
> - **Increase crash safety** by distributing stress over a larger bonded area.
> - **Prevent corrosion** in hem-flanges and roof panels where welding leaves gaps.
> - **Reduce NVH** (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) through damping properties.

### 1.2 Fundamental Terminology (KTU Standardized)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Adherend** | The substrate (metal, plastic, composite) being bonded. |
| **Adhesive** | The bonding material that forms the joint. |
| **Cohesive Failure** | Failure *within* the adhesive layer itself. |
| **Adhesive Failure** | Failure at the interface between adhesive and adherend. |
| **Substrate Failure** | Failure *within* the adherend (ideal bond strength). |
| **Open Time** | Time between adhesive application and joining. |
| **Cure Time** | Time required for the adhesive to reach full strength. |
| **Pot Life** | Working time of a mixed two-part adhesive before it hardens. |
| **Green Strength** | Early handling strength before full cure. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** Adhesive bond strength is typically reported in **MPa (MegaPascals)** or **psi (pounds per square inch)**, where **1 MPa ≈ 145 psi**.

### 1.3 Classification of Adhesives (Vehicle Body Perspective)

```
ADHESIVES
├── Structural Adhesives (Load-bearing)
│   ├── Epoxy
│   ├── Polyurethane (PU)
│   ├── Phenolic
│   └── Acrylic
├── Non-Structural / Sealants
│   ├── Silicones
│   ├── PVC Plastisols
│   └── Butyl Rubber
├── Pressure-Sensitive
│   ├── Tapes (foam, acrylic)
└── Specialty
    ├── Hot-melt
    ├── Anaerobic
    └── Cyanoacrylates
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Adhesion — The "Five Theories"

For an adhesive bond to form and sustain load, several physical and chemical phenomena must occur simultaneously. The **five classical theories of adhesion** together explain the bond:

#### Theory 1: Mechanical Interlocking
- The adhesive penetrates the microscopic pores and roughness of the adherend surface.
- After curing, it locks mechanically into these features.
- **Why it works:** A rough surface increases the effective contact area, much like Velcro.

#### Theory 2: Adsorption (Wetting) Theory
- For adhesion, the adhesive must **wet** the adherend surface (contact angle $\theta \to 0$).
- Molecules of the adhesive come into intimate molecular contact (within **0.5 nm**) with the adherend.
- Secondary forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonds) hold them together.

#### Theory 3: Chemical Bonding Theory
- Primary chemical bonds (covalent, ionic) form across the interface.
- Example: Silane coupling agents create **Si–O–Si** covalent bridges between glass and polymer.
- This is the **strongest** form of adhesive force.

#### Theory 4: Diffusion Theory
- Adhesive polymer molecules diffuse into the adherend polymer surface.
- Common with **TPE/TPO bumper bonding** where both surfaces are plastics.
- Requires mutual solubility and chain mobility.

#### Theory 5: Electrostatic Theory
- Contact between dissimilar materials creates a **double electrical layer** (like a capacitor).
- This generates attractive coulombic forces across the interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Explain the mechanism of adhesion," list all five theories with **one-line examples** for each. Full marks require at least 4 theories with diagrams.

### 2.2 Key Properties of Adhesives for Vehicle Body

#### (a) Mechanical Properties
- **Tensile / Shear Strength** — ability to resist pulling or sliding forces.
- **Peel Strength** — resistance to peeling (important for laminated windshields).
- **Impact Resistance** — crucial for crash zones.
- **Fatigue Strength** — resistance to cyclic loading on road vibrations.
- **Elongation at Break** — flexibility under deformation.

#### (b) Physical Properties
- **Viscosity** — flow behavior during application.
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** — temperature above which the adhesive softens.
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** — must closely match the adherend.

#### (c) Chemical Properties
- **Chemical Resistance** — to fuels, oils, coolants, road salts.
- **UV Resistance** — for exterior body panels.
- **Hydrolytic Stability** — resistance to water/moisture degradation.

#### (d) Processing Properties
- **Open Time, Cure Time, Pot Life** (defined earlier).
- **Cure Temperature** — **room-temperature cure** (epoxy PU) vs. **oven cure** (≥ 160 °C for e-coat compatibility).
- **Gap-Filling Ability** — automotive body tolerances can be **±0.5 mm**; adhesive must bridge these gaps.

### 2.3 Adhesive Properties Required Specifically for Automotive Body

A modern car body uses adhesives in the **Body-in-White (BIW)** stage. The adhesive must therefore survive:
- **E-coat (electrodeposition) bath** at **180–220 °C**.
- **Phosphate treatment** chemicals.
- **Primer/Paint oven** cure at **140–160 °C**.

Hence the most critical property is **"e-coat compatibility"** — the adhesive must not outgas, soften excessively, or lose bond strength after passing through these ovens.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit |
|---|-------------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Basic tensile/shear stress (Force / Bonded Area) | **MPa** |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Shear stress in lap joint | **MPa** |
| 3 | $\theta = 0^\circ \Rightarrow$ complete wetting | Young's equation condition for good adhesion | degrees |
| 4 | $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | **Dupré Work of Adhesion** | **N/m** or **J/m²** |
| 5 | $W_c = 2\gamma_{lv}$ | **Work of Cohesion** (within adhesive itself) | **N/m** |
| 6 | $S = W_a / W_c$ | **Spreading Coefficient** — must be $\geq 0$ for spontaneous wetting | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | Elongation at break (ductility) | % |
| 8 | $T_g$ — Glass transition temp. | Max service temperature guideline | **°C** |
| 9 | Lap shear ASTM D1002 | Standard test for adhesive strength | **MPa** |
| 10 | Peel test ASTM D1876 | Standard T-peel test | **N/mm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols Used:**
> - $\gamma_{sv}$ = Solid–Vapor surface energy
> - $\gamma_{lv}$ = Liquid–Vapor surface energy (≈ surface tension of adhesive)
> - $\gamma_{sl}$ = Solid–Liquid interfacial energy
> - $W_a$ = Work of Adhesion (energy to separate unit area of interface)
> - $W_c$ = Work of Cohesion (energy to separate unit area within adhesive)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In automotive design, the engineer selects adhesive chemistry based on:
1. **Substrate** — steel, aluminum, CFRP, GFRP, SMC, PP, ABS.
2. **Joint geometry** — lap, butt, hem-flange, T-joint.
3. **Service temperature** — under-hood ($>$150 °C) vs. body panel ($<$100 °C).
4. **Production rate** — 60 cars/hour demands fast cure (PU 30–60 s induction).
5. **Crash performance** — must exhibit **crash-tough** ductile failure, not brittle.

For example, **Benz "S-Class"** uses **2 kg of adhesive** per car, replacing **~150 spot welds**, increasing torsional rigidity by **~20%** and reducing weight by **~7 kg**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Young–Dupré Equation for Work of Adhesion

The **Work of Adhesion** $W_a$ is the reversible thermodynamic work required to separate a unit area of the adhesive-adherend interface into two free surfaces.

**Step 1 — Setup the system:**
Consider a liquid adhesive droplet of surface tension $\gamma_{lv}$ resting on a solid adherend with surface energy $\gamma_{sv}$ in a vapor atmosphere. The interface has energy $\gamma_{sl}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Young's Equation (force balance at contact line):**
The horizontal forces acting on the contact line must balance:

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of Work of Adhesion:**
When the interface is separated, two new surfaces of energy $\gamma_{lv}$ (new liquid surface) and $\gamma_{sv}$ (new solid surface) are created, while the interface $\gamma_{sl}$ is destroyed. By definition:

$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $\gamma_{sl}$ using Young's Equation:**
Rearranging Young's Equation:

$$
\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

Substitute into the $W_a$ equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \bigl(\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\bigr) \\
&= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta \\
&= \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Young–Dupré Equation** — a cornerstone of adhesion science.

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- If $\theta = 0^\circ$ (complete wetting): $W_a = 2\gamma_{lv}$ — maximum possible work of adhesion.
- If $\theta = 180^\circ$ (no wetting): $W_a = 0$ — no adhesion occurs.
- For good bonding, we need $\theta < 90^\circ$ and ideally $\theta \to 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Insight:** For a typical epoxy on steel, $\gamma_{lv} \approx 42$ mN/m and $\theta \approx 25^\circ$. So $W_a \approx 42(1 + 0.906) \approx 80$ mJ/m², indicating strong bonding.

### 3.2 Derivation: Spreading Coefficient Condition

For an adhesive to spontaneously spread over the adherend, the **Spreading Coefficient** $S$ must satisfy:

$$
S = W_a - W_c \geq 0
$$

**Step 1 — Define Work of Cohesion** (energy to split a bulk liquid adhesive into two parts):

$$
W_c = 2\gamma_{lv}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $W_a$ and $W_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= W_a - W_c \\
&= \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) - 2\gamma_{lv} \\
&= \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta - 2) \\
&= \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Analyze the inequality:**

- Since $\cos\theta \leq 1$ for any real contact angle, $S \leq 0$ mathematically.
- However, in practical engineering, **pretreatments** (plasma, corona, primers) **lower** $\gamma_{sv}$ of the substrate, effectively making $\cos\theta \to 1$, hence $S \to 0$, allowing effective spreading.
- **Conclusion:** True spontaneous spreading is rare; engineered surface preparation makes it possible.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Adhesive Types vs. Properties

| Property | Epoxy | Polyurethane (PU) | Silicone | PVC Plastomelt | Phenolic |
|----------|-------|-------------------|----------|----------------|----------|
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 30–90 | 10–40 | 2–7 | 5–15 | 20–40 |
| **Elongation (%)** | 1–8 | 100–600 | 100–700 | 100–300 | 1–3 |
| **Service Temp (°C)** | -40 to +150 | -40 to +120 | -60 to +200 | -30 to +90 | -50 to +250 |
| **Cure Type** | 2-part or heat | 1- or 2-part moisture | Moisture / RTV | Heat (oven) | Heat + pressure |
| **E-coat Compatible** | Excellent | Good | Fair | Excellent | Good |
| **Substrates** | Metals, composites | Metals, plastics | Metals, glass | Metals (hem flanges) | Metals, brake linings |
| **Automotive Use** | CFRP chassis, FRP | Windshield, roof panel | Sealing gaskets | Hem flange bonding | Brake/clutch bonding |
| **Failure Mode** | Brittle | Tough/ductile | Flexible | Moderate | Brittle |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Bond Strength Calculation (Lap Joint)

**Problem:** A single-lap joint of a car door inner panel is bonded with epoxy. The bond overlap is **$L = 25$ mm** wide and **$w = 1.5$ mm** thick. The epoxy shear strength is **$\tau = 25$ MPa**. If the door is subject to a closing load of **$F = 900$ N**, find the factor of safety.

**Step 1 — Compute the bonded area:**

$$
A = L \times w = 25 \text{ mm} \times 1.5 \text{ mm} = 37.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the induced shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{induced} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{900 \text{ N}}{37.5 \text{ mm}^2} = 24.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the factor of safety:**

$$
FoS = \dfrac{\tau_{allowable}}{\tau_{induced}} = \dfrac{25}{24.0} = 1.04
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Judgment:**
A factor of safety of **1.04** is **unsafe** for an automotive structural component. The engineer must:
- Increase overlap length to **$L = 30$ mm** (giving $A = 45$ mm², $\tau_{induced} = 20$ MPa, $FoS = 1.25$).
- Or select a higher-strength epoxy (e.g., $\tau = 35$ MPa).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Adhesive Joint Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Adherend Top Substrate Steel Aluminum Composite] --> B1[Adhesive Interface Zone Molecular Contact lt 0.5 nm]
    B1 --> C1[Adhesive Bulk Layer Cohesive Zone]
    C1 --> D1[Adhesive Interface Zone Molecular Contact lt 0.5 nm]
    D1 --> E1[Adherend Bottom Substrate Steel Aluminum Composite]
    C1 --> F1[Failure Modes]
    F1 --> F2[Cohesive Failure inside adhesive]
    F1 --> F3[Adhesive Failure at interface]
    F1 --> F4[Substrate Failure ideal strongest]
    C1 --> G1[Forces Resisted]
    G1 --> G2[Tensile Force Normal to plane]
    G1 --> G3[Shear Force Parallel to plane]
    G1 --> G4[Peel Force Edge concentration]
    G1 --> G5[Clevage Prising apart]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Adhesive Selection for Vehicle Body

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Adhesive Selection]) --> Q1{Substrate Type}
    Q1 -- Steel Steel --> P1[Epoxy or PU]
    Q1 -- Aluminum Aluminum --> P2[Modified Epoxy with corrosion inhibitor]
    Q1 -- CFRP CFRP --> P3[Toughened Epoxy Crash grade]
    Q1 -- PP TPO Plastic --> P4[PU with primer or plasma treat]
    P1 --> Q2{Service Condition}
    P2 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q2
    P4 --> Q2
    Q2 -- High Temp under hood --> R1[Phenolic or high Tg Epoxy]
    Q2 -- Crash Zone --> R2[Toughened PU crash tough]
    Q2 -- Hem Flange --> R3[PVC Plastomelt oven cure]
    Q2 -- Windshield --> R4[PU moisture cure black primerless]
    R1 --> End([End Adhesive Chosen])
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Stress Distribution in a Single-Lap Joint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Single_Lap_Joint
    A1[Adherend 1 Under Tension] --> B1[Adhesive Layer Thickness 0.1 to 0.5 mm]
    B1 --> A2[Adherend 2 Under Tension]
    end
    B1 --> S1[Stress Distribution Along Overlap]
    S1 --> S2[Peak Shear at Ends]
    S1 --> S3[Minimum Shear at Center]
    S2 --> S3
    S2 --> Note1[Stress concentration causes early failure at overlap ends]
    S3 --> Note2[Center is underutilized only 20 to 30 percent of capacity]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Surface Preparation Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP1[Stage 1 Degreasing Alkaline Wash] --> SP2[Stage 2 Rinsing Deionized Water]
    SP2 --> SP3[Stage 3 Phosphating Zinc or Iron Phosphate]
    SP3 --> SP4[Stage 4 Drying Hot Air or IR]
    SP4 --> SP5[Stage 5 Adhesive Application Robot Dispensing]
    SP5 --> SP6[Stage 6 E coat Bath 180 to 220 degree C]
    SP6 --> SP7[Stage 7 Paint Oven Cure]
    SP7 --> DONE([Bonded Assembly Ready])
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Adhesive Bonding Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PRE[Pretreatment Tunnel] --> APP[Robotic Adhesive Applicator Bead or Spray]
    APP --> ASY[Body in White Assembly Station]
    ASY --> IND[Induction or IR Curing Zone]
    IND --> ECO[E coat Oven]
    ECO --> TOP[Top Coat Paint Oven]
    TOP --> FIN[Finished Vehicle Body]
    APP --- CTRL[PLC and Vision System]
    CTRL --- APP
    APP --- QAC[Quality Assurance Camera and Ultrasonic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from Diagram 4.5:** The adhesive is applied *before* the e-coat bath in modern BIW assembly lines. The adhesive must therefore withstand the high pH (≈ 11) and high temperature of the e-coat oven — a property unique to **crash-toughened epoxies** and **high-modulus PUs**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**Define an adhesive. List the four essential requirements an adhesive must satisfy for effective bonding.**

**Model Answer:**

An **adhesive** is a non-metallic substance that can join two materials (adherends) by surface attachment, resisting separation through cohesive and adhesive forces.

**Four essential requirements:**
1. **Wetting** — must spread spontaneously on the substrate (low contact angle).
2. **Curing** — must harden by chemical reaction, cooling, or solvent loss.
3. **Adhesion** — must form strong interfacial bonds (chemical/physical).
4. **Cohesion** — the bulk adhesive must itself be strong enough to transfer load.

> *(Word count: ~70 words = ideal 3-mark answer. Add a small sketch of the contact angle to score the 1-mark diagram mark.)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — 3 Marks
**Differentiate between "Cohesive Failure" and "Adhesive Failure" in a bonded joint.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cohesive Failure | Adhesive Failure |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| **Location** | Within the bulk adhesive layer | At the adhesive–adherend interface |
| **Indication of** | Adhesive is weaker than the bond to substrate | Surface preparation is poor / weak interface |
| **Appearance** | Adhesive residue visible on both substrates | Clean substrate surface on one side, adhesive on the other |
| **Remedy** | Use stronger / tougher adhesive | Improve surface prep, use primer, or change adhesive chemistry |

> *(Tabular answer = full 3 marks under KTU valuation scheme.)*

---

### 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]
**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of adhesives** used in vehicle body engineering, with suitable examples for each category. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **mechanical, physical, and chemical properties** that an automotive adhesive must possess to survive Body-in-White processing. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

##### (a) Classification of Adhesives (7 Marks)

**1. Structural Adhesives** — load-bearing joints (crash, chassis, roof):
- **Epoxy:** highest strength (30–90 MPa), excellent chemical resistance, used in CFRP–metal hybrid joints.
- **Polyurethane (PU):** flexible, crash-tough, used in windshield bonding, roof panel.
- **Phenolic:** high-temperature resistant, used in brake/clutch bonding.
- **Acrylic:** fast-cure, used in plastic bumper repair.

**2. Non-Structural / Sealants** — sealing, NVH, anti-corrosion:
- **Silicone RTV:** temperature resistant, flexible gaskets.
- **PVC Plastisols:** hem-flange bonding in doors/bonnets.
- **Butyl Rubber:** sealing seams, sound deadening.

**3. Pressure-Sensitive Adhesives (PSAs)** — pre-applied tapes:
- **Foam tapes** for emblem mounting, badge fixing.
- **Acrylic foam tapes** for side molding attachment.

**4. Specialty / Reactive Adhesives:**
- **Hot-melt:** fast production, used in headliner attachment.
- **Anaerobic:** thread-locking in mechanical joints.
- **Cyanoacrylate:** superglue, used in trim repair.

> *(A clean table or classification tree with 4 categories and 2 examples each = full 7 marks.)*

##### (b) Required Properties for BIW (7 Marks)

**Mechanical Properties:**
- Lap-shear strength $\geq$ 20 MPa (BIW structural demand).
- High elongation (≥ 100% for crash zones to absorb energy).
- T-peel strength ≥ 8 N/mm for laminated windshields.

**Physical Properties:**
- **$T_g$** of crash adhesives ≈ 80–100 °C, must not soften in e-coat oven.
- Viscosity 30,000–80,000 cP for robotic bead application.
- Cure time 30–60 seconds induction to support line speed of **60 cars/hr**.

**Chemical Properties:**
- **E-coat compatibility** — no outgassing at pH ≈ 11 and 200 °C.
- **Galvanic compatibility** — must not promote corrosion at aluminum–steel interfaces.
- **UV resistance** — exterior trim adhesives must not yellow or embrittle.

**Processing Properties:**
- **Robotic dispensability** — thixotropic (non-sag) rheology.
- **Wash-off resistance** — must not be displaced by alkaline degreasing.
- **Long pot life / short open time** — for mixed two-part systems.

> *(Tip: Organize under 4 sub-headings — Mechanical, Physical, Chemical, Processing. KTU examiner allocates 1.5–2 marks per heading.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **Young–Dupré equation** for the work of adhesion. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Explain the **five classical theories of adhesion** with one automotive example each. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

##### (a) Young–Dupré Derivation (7 Marks)

*Sketch:* A sessile adhesive droplet on a substrate showing the contact angle $\theta$, with the three interfacial tensions acting on the contact line.

1. Write Young's Equation by force balance at the contact line:

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

2. Define work of adhesion as the energy required to separate unit area of interface:

$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
$$

3. Eliminate $\gamma_{sl}$ from the two equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sl} &= \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta \\
W_a &= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta) \\
&= \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Conclusion:** This is the **Young–Dupré Equation**. For complete wetting ($\theta = 0$), $W_a = 2\gamma_{lv}$ (maximum). For poor wetting ($\theta \to 180°$), $W_a \to 0$.

5. **Automotive application:** For epoxy on steel ($\gamma_{lv} = 42$ mN/m, $\theta = 25°$): $W_a = 42(1 + \cos 25°) \approx 80$ mJ/m², indicating excellent bond formation.

##### (b) Five Theories of Adhesion (7 Marks)

| # | Theory | Mechanism | Automotive Example |
|---|--------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical Interlocking** | Adhesive flows into pores/roughness | Phosphated steel surface gives 10× bond strength vs. smooth steel |
| 2 | **Adsorption / Wetting** | Molecular contact + van der Waals | Epoxy wetting clean aluminum hood |
| 3 | **Chemical Bonding** | Covalent/ionic bond at interface | Silane coupling on glass windshield |
| 4 | **Diffusion** | Polymer chain interdiffusion | PU primer on PP bumper |
| 5 | **Electrostatic** | Double layer / coulombic attraction | Adhesion between dissimilar metals (steel + aluminum) |

> *(Each theory = 1.4 marks. Mention 5 names + 1 example each = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "cohesive" and "adhesive" failure** — KTU examiners specifically test this. A clean substrate side = *adhesive failure*; residue on both sides = *cohesive failure*.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the formula sheet (e.g., writing $W_a$ without "mJ/m²") is the **#1 cause of half-mark deductions**.
> 3. **Skipping the sketch** in Young–Dupré derivation costs **2 marks** at minimum. Always draw the contact angle and label all three interfacial tensions.
> 4. **Listing "five theories" without examples** = only 4/7 marks. Each theory **must** have one automotive application.
> 5. **Beware the "e-coat compatibility" question** — students who omit this in Part B answers for BIW adhesives lose **2–3 marks** because examiners treat it as a key 2024-scheme keyword.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **adhesive** is a non-metallic bonding material; an **adherend** is the substrate being joined.
- **Five theories** of adhesion: Mechanical, Adsorption, Chemical, Diffusion, Electrostatic — at least 4 should be memorised.
- **Young–Dupré equation**: $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — complete wetting at $\theta = 0°$.
- **Spreading Coefficient** $S = W_a - W_c \geq 0$ governs spontaneous spreading.
- **Key automotive adhesives**: Epoxy, PU, PVC Plastomelt, Silicone, Phenolic.
- **Critical BIW property**: e-coat compatibility (withstand 180–220 °C, pH ≈ 11).
- **Mechanical properties to know**: tensile/shear strength, peel strength, elongation, impact resistance, fatigue.
- **Standard tests**: ASTM D1002 (lap shear), ASTM D1876 (T-peel), ISO 11339 (wedge test).
- **Failure modes**: Cohesive (inside adhesive), Adhesive (at interface), Substrate (in adherend — best).
- **Modern car** uses ~1.5–2 kg adhesive, replacing ~150 spot welds, reducing weight and increasing stiffness.
- **Hem-flange bonding** is dominated by **PVC plastisol** oven-cured adhesives.
- **Windshield bonding** uses **moisture-cure black PU** (high modulus, crash-retention).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "MACE-D"** for theories: **M**echanical, **A**dsorption, **C**hemical, **E**lectrostatic, **D**iffusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion and Their Prevention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT741 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Corrosion and Their Prevention # Corrosion and Their Prevention ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Corrosion** is the **electrochemical or chemical degradation of a metal** caused by its reaction with the surrounding environment (moisture, oxygen, salts, acids, or industrial pollutants), leading to the formation of stable compounds such as oxides, hydroxides, sulfides, or carbonates. In the context of vehicle body engineering, corrosion refers primarily to the **rusting of ferrous body panels** and the **oxidation of aluminium, magnesium, and zinc-based alloys** used in modern automotive structures. According to ISO 8049 (NACE/ASTM equivalent), corrosion is the *physicochemical interaction between a metal and its environment which results in changes in the properties of the metal, and which may often lead to impairment of the function of the metal, the environment, or the technical system of which these form a part.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a freshly painted iron gate left in your backyard during monsoon. Over weeks, you see reddish-brown spots bubbling up, eventually the paint peels, and the iron becomes flaky and weak. This is exactly what happens to a car body — but in slow motion. The metal is essentially **"returning to its original ore state"** because refining iron from iron oxide (Fe₂O₃) required enormous energy, and nature wants to reclaim it. Corrosion is the metal **"undoing" the smelting process**. Think of corrosion as a tiny **leak in a battery** that has been unintentionally built on the surface of your car. Tiny regions of the metal act as the **positive terminal (anode)**, other regions act as the **negative terminal (cathode)**, and rainwater/moisture acts as the **electrolyte**. A current flows, metal dissolves at the anode, and you get rust. ### 1.3 Why Corrosion is Critical in Vehicle Body Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024:** > The automotive industry loses **billions of dollars annually** to corrosion-related failures. Modern vehicles must guarantee **anti-corrosion warranties of 6 to 12 years** (OEM standard). Structural integrity, passenger safety, resale value, and aesthetic appeal all depend on robust corrosion prevention. ### 1.4 The Electrochemical Cell (Basic Setup) Corrosion is fundamentally an **electrochemical process** requiring **four essential elements**: 1. **Anode** – the metal that corrodes (loses electrons, e.g., Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻) 2. **Cathode** – the site where reduction occurs (gains electrons, e.g., O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻) 3. **Electrolyte** – a conductive medium (water, salt solution, moisture film) 4. **Metallic path** – electrical connection between anode and cathode (the metal itself) The **standard reference electrode** is the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** with a potential of **0.000 V** at **25 °C**. All metal corrosion tendencies are compared against this reference using the **Galvanic Series**. | Term | Symbol / Value | Significance | |------|----------------|--------------| | Faraday's constant | **F = 96,485 C/mol** | Charge per mole of electrons | | Standard Hydrogen Electrode | **E° = 0.000 V** | Universal reference | | Iron oxidation potential | **E°(Fe²⁺/Fe) = –0.44 V** | Tendency to corrode | | Oxygen reduction potential | **E°(O₂/OH⁻) = +0.40 V** | Cathodic reaction driving force | | Cell potential (rusting) | **E°cell = +0.84 V** | High thermodynamic driving force | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Electrochemical Mechanism of Corrosion (Step-by-Step) > [!NOTE] > The **rusting of iron** is the most studied corrosion reaction. It proceeds through **anodic dissolution**, **cathodic reduction**, and **subsequent hydrolysis/oxidation** to form hydrated iron(III) oxide (Fe₂O₃·H₂O) — the familiar red-brown rust. **Step 1 — Anodic Reaction (Oxidation, at anodic sites):** Iron atoms on the surface lose electrons and enter the electrolyte as ferrous ions. $$\text{Fe (s)} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$ **Step 2 — Cathodic Reaction (Reduction, at cathodic sites):** In neutral or slightly alkaline aerated water, dissolved oxygen is reduced. $$\text{O}_2 \text{(g)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O (l)} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}\text{(aq)}$$ **Step 3 — Hydrolysis of Ferrous Ion:** The ferrous ions react with hydroxide ions to form insoluble ferrous hydroxide. $$\text{Fe}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{OH}^{-}\text{(aq)} \longrightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2\text{(s)}$$ **Step 4 — Oxidation to Rust:** In the presence of oxygen and water, ferrous hydroxide oxidizes to hydrated ferric oxide (rust). $$4\text{Fe(OH)}_2\text{(s)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O (l)} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O (s)}$$ The **overall rusting reaction** becomes: $$4\text{Fe (s)} + 3\text{O}_2\text{(g)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O (l)} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O (s)}$$ ### 2.2 Why and How of Each Step - **Why anode dissolves:** Metals are thermodynamically unstable in their refined state; oxidation lowers their free energy. - **Why oxygen reduction:** Dissolved O₂ provides an inexhaustible electron acceptor in aerated environments. - **Why rust is voluminous:** Fe₂O₃·H₂O occupies **6 to 10 times** the volume of the parent iron, causing paint blistering and panel distortion. - **Why different regions of a panel behave differently:** Compositional variations, cold-worked zones, weld areas, and grain boundaries have different electrochemical potentials, creating microscopic galvanic cells. ### 2.3 Galvanic Series (Practical, Seawater Reference) | Region | Metals (Most Active → Least Active) | |--------|-------------------------------------| | **Anodic (Most Corroded)** | Magnesium → Zinc → Aluminium → Steel/Iron → Lead → Tin → Copper → Bronze → Stainless Steel (passive) → Gold → Platinum | | **Cathodic (Most Noble)** | Cobalt, Nickel, Silver | > [!WARNING] > When two dissimilar metals are coupled in a vehicle (e.g., aluminium hood with steel hinges), the **more anodic metal (Al) corrodes preferentially**. Designers must either isolate them electrically with gaskets/plastic bushings or ensure the cathodic metal is the fastener. ### 2.4 Major Types of Corrosion in Vehicle Bodies | Type | Mechanism | Typical Location on Vehicle | |------|-----------|-----------------------------| | **Uniform / General Corrosion** | Even metal loss over the entire surface | Body panels, bonnets, doors, outer skin | | **Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion** | Dissimilar-metal electrolytic attack | Aluminium-steel joints, copper-brass radiators | | **Pitting Corrosion** | Localized deep cavities at coating defects | Scratched paint areas, stone-chip damage | | **Crevice Corrosion** | Oxygen-starved zone under deposits | Box sections, hem flanges, bolt heads | | **Intergranular Corrosion** | Attack along grain boundaries | Welded HSS (High-Strength Steel) zones | | **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** | Tensile stress + corrosive media | Suspension arms, coil springs | | **Filiform Corrosion** | Thread-like underfilm growth | Under paint on aluminium, coated steel | | **Exfoliation** | Layered flaking of grains | Heavily worked aluminium extrusions | | **Cavitation Corrosion** | Bubble collapse at high velocity | Water pump impellers | | **Erosion–Corrosion** | Mechanical wear + chemical attack | Wheel hubs, brake dust regions | ### 2.5 Factors Affecting Corrosion Rate > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU 2024, students must remember the **"Eight Variables"** controlling vehicle corrosion: 1. **Relative Humidity** — Critical threshold is **> 60% RH** for atmospheric corrosion 2. **Temperature** — Rate doubles approximately every **10 °C** rise 3. **Atmospheric Pollutants** — SO₂, NOₓ, Cl⁻ (coastal), H₂S 4. **Chloride Concentration** — Road salts cause 5–10× acceleration 5. **Time of Wetness (TOW)** — Hours per year with RH > 80% 6. **Surface Condition** — Roughness, mill scale, weld spatter 7. **Stress State** — Residual stress from forming/welding 8. **Microbial Activity** — Fungus/bacteria in fuel tanks ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical equations to memorize for KTU 2024 examination:** | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---------|-------------|------| | 1 | **m = (I · t · M) / (n · F)** | Faraday's law of electrolysis (mass of metal dissolved) | kg | | 2 | **E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode** | Standard cell potential (driving force) | V | | 3 | **ΔG = −n · F · E°cell** | Gibbs free energy (spontaneity criterion) | J/mol | | 4 | **Nernst: E = E° − (0.0591 / n) · log₁₀ Q** | Electrode potential at non-standard conditions | V | | 5 | **CR = (K · W) / (A · T · D)** | Corrosion rate (mass loss method) | mm/yr (mpy) | | 6 | **CR (mm/yr) = 87.6 · (W / (A · T · D))** | Tafel-derived constant for steel | mm/yr | | 7 | **i_corr = B / R_p** | Stern–Geary relation (LPR method) | A/cm² | | 8 | **Pitting Factor = (deepest pit depth) / (avg. penetration)** | Severity index | Dimensionless | | 9 | **R_p = (β_a · β_c) / (2.303 · i_corr · (β_a + β_c))** | Polarization resistance | Ω·cm² | | 10 | **Crevice pH = pH_0 − log(C_{Cl⁻}/C_0)** | Local acidification (Rosenberg's) | pH units | **Where:** - *m* = mass of metal dissolved, *I* = current (A), *t* = time (s), *M* = molar mass (kg/mol), *n* = electrons transferred, *F* = Faraday constant - *K* = 87.6 (mm/yr conversion constant), *W* = mass loss (mg), *A* = exposed area (cm²), *T* = time (hr), *D* = density (g/cm³) - *β_a, β_c* = anodic/cathodic Tafel slopes (V/decade), *R_p* = polarization resistance ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive OEMs** specify accelerated corrosion tests per **ASTM B117** (salt spray) and **SAE J2334** (cyclic corrosion). - **Bridges and pipelines** use **cathodic protection (CP)** to extend life from 20 to 100+ years. - **Aerospace and marine** applications mandate **alloy selection + coatings + inhibitors** as a *defense-in-depth* strategy. - **EV battery enclosures** require novel corrosion control because aluminium and copper busbars coexist in humid packs. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law for Corrosion Mass Loss Starting from the fundamental relation that **1 Faraday of charge (96,485 C) liberates 1 gram-equivalent of metal**: **Step 1:** Charge passing through the corroding cell. $$Q = I \cdot t$$ where *I* is in amperes and *t* in seconds, giving *Q* in coulombs. **Step 2:** Number of moles of electrons transferred. $$n_{e^{-}} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$ **Step 3:** Moles of metal dissolved (1 mol metal requires *n* mol electrons). $$n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{n_{e^{-}}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$ **Step 4:** Mass of metal dissolved. $$m = n_{\text{metal}} \cdot M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ $$\boxed{m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$$ This is the **cornerstone formula** of electrochemical corrosion rate estimation. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Corrosion Rate in mm/year (Mass-Loss Method) **Step 1:** Convert mass loss *W* in mg to density-based volume. $$V = \frac{W}{D}$$ where *W* is in mg, *D* is in g/cm³ (or kg/m³ with consistent units). **Step 2:** Calculate average penetration depth (linearised rate). $$CR = \frac{V}{A \cdot T}$$ with *A* in cm², *T* in hours. **Step 3:** Introduce unit-conversion constant for the commonly used **mm/yr** output. If *W* is in mg, *A* in cm², *T* in hours, *D* in g/cm³, the magic number that converts this to mm/yr is: $$K = \frac{24 \cdot 365 \cdot 10}{1000 \cdot 1} = 87.6$$ Thus: $$\boxed{CR\ \left(\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{yr}}\right) = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A mild-steel body panel specimen of **exposed area 10 cm × 5 cm = 50 cm²** is subjected to a 240-hour salt-spray test. The mass loss measured is **420 mg**. The density of steel is **7.85 g/cm³**. Determine the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** and the equivalent in **mils per year (mpy)**. **Given:** W = 420 mg, A = 50 cm², T = 240 h, D = 7.85 g/cm³ **Step 1 — Apply the formula:** $$CR = \frac{87.6 \times 420}{50 \times 240 \times 7.85}$$ **Step 2 — Numerator evaluation:** $$87.6 \times 420 = 36{,}792$$ **Step 3 — Denominator evaluation:** $$50 \times 240 \times 7.85 = 50 \times 1884 = 94{,}200$$ **Step 4 — Final division:** $$CR = \frac{36{,}792}{94{,}200} = 0.3906\ \text{mm/yr}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to mpy (1 mm/yr = 39.37 mpy):** $$CR_{mpy} = 0.3906 \times 39.37 = 15.38\ \text{mpy}$$ **Interpretation:** This is a **moderate corrosion rate**. Modern automotive specifications demand **< 0.025 mm/yr (1 mpy)** for 10-year warranty components. ### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Nernst Equation for Half-Cell Potential For a general reaction *aA + ne⁻ → bB*, the electrode potential under non-standard conditions is: **Step 1:** Start from the Gibbs free energy change. $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 2:** Use the electrical work relation *ΔG = −nFE*: $$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 3:** Solve for E: $$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ **Step 4:** Convert natural log to log base 10 (*ln x = 2.303 log x*) and substitute **T = 298 K, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96,485 C/mol**: $$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{n \times 96{,}485} \log_{10} Q$$ **Step 5:** The factor simplifies at 25 °C to: $$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q$$ $$\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{Oxidised}]}{[\text{Reduced}]}}$$ ### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Galvanic Series vs. EMF Series The **EMF Series** lists metals under *standardised, thermodynamically pure* conditions. The **Galvanic Series** ranks them in *real seawater*, where oxide films, passivity, and kinetic effects dominate. The galvanic series is what the automotive engineer uses; the EMF series is what underpins theoretical calculations. ### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Prevention Method | Method | Best Suited For | Initial Cost | Maintenance | Vehicle Application | |--------|-----------------|--------------|-------------|---------------------| | **Galvanizing (HDG/EG)** | Steel sheet | Medium | None | Body-in-white panels | | **E-Coat (Cathodic ED)** | Complex box sections | Medium | None | Entire body structure | | **Powder Coating** | Wheels, chassis parts | High | None | Alloy wheels | | **Sacrificial Anode (CP)** | Underground tanks, hulls | Low | Periodic replacement | Uncommon on cars | | **Impressed Current CP** | Large structures | High | Power supply | Bridge decks, pipelines | | **Inhibitors (VCI)** | Enclosed spaces | Low | Renewal | ECU housings, spare-wheel wells | | **Paint + Wax / Sealant** | Underbody | Medium | Re-application | Wheel arches, sills | | **Cladding (Plastic)** | Lower sills | Medium | None | Rocker panels | | **Alloy Selection (Al, SS)** | Premium structures | High | None | Audi A8, Tesla battery packs | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Electrochemical Corrosion Cell on a Steel Panel This diagram illustrates how a microscopic galvanic cell forms on a single piece of steel due to surface heterogeneity. ```mermaid graph TD A["Anode Site
Fe oxidized
Fe = Fe2+ + 2e-"] -->|Electron flow| B["Cathode Site
O2 + 2H2O + 4e- = 4OH-"] B -->|Hydroxide migration| A A -->|Fe2+ + 2OH-| C["FeOH2 Layer
Intermediate product"] C -->|Oxidation by O2| D["Fe2O3 · H2O
Reddish Brown Rust"] E["Electrolyte Film
Moisture Rain Dew"] --- A E --- B F["Steel Substrate
Metallic Conduction Path"] --- A F --- B ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Defense-in-Depth Corrosion Protection Strategy (Modern Vehicle) ```mermaid graph TD L1["Layer 1
Base Steel or Aluminium
Substrate Selection"] --> L2["Layer 2
Surface Pretreatment
Phosphate or Zirconium"] L2 --> L3["Layer 3
E Coat Cathodic Electrodeposition
20 to 25 microns"] L3 --> L4["Layer 4
Anti Chip Primer
PVC or Epoxy"] L4 --> L5["Layer 5Basecoat and Clearcoat
Total 40 to 60 microns"] L5 --> L6["Layer 6
Seam Sealant and PVC Underbody
Wax Injection"] L6 --> L7["Layer 7
Cavity Wax and Drain Plugs
Closed Box Sections"] ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Cathodic Protection Schematic (Sacrificial Anode Method) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Protected S["Steel Body Panel
Cathode

Steel protected
Sacrificial anode replaced periodically"] -.-> Z ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Corrosion Type Decision Tree ```mermaid graph TD Start["Corrosion Observed
on Vehicle Body"] --> Q1{"Uniform

metals

Add isolation gaskets"] Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Localised
deep

Lower residual stress"] Q5 -->|No| T6["Erosion or

Clean and replace"] ``` ### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Automotive Corrosion Test Cycle (SAE J2334) ```mermaid graph LR A["Phase 1
Humid Stage
50 C 100 percent RH
6 hours"] --> B["Phase 2
Salt Application
0.5 percent NaCl Spray
15 minutes"] B --> C["Phase 3

17.75 hours"] C --> D["Cycle Repeats
